topics.title: Introduction to Big Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT746 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Big Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Big Data** is a term that describes the **large, diverse, and complex datasets** that require specialized techniques, technologies, and architectures to store, manage, process, and analyze in order to extract meaningful patterns, insights, and value within a tolerable elapsed time.

According to the **Gartner Definition (Updated)**: *Big Data is high-volume, high-velocity, and/or high-variety information assets that demand cost-effective, innovative forms of information processing that enable enhanced insight, decision making, and process automation.*

According to **McKinsey Global Institute**: *Big Data refers to datasets whose size is beyond the ability of typical database software tools to capture, store, manage, and analyze.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the definition "relative" and not absolute?**
> Big Data is not defined by a specific number in **terabytes (TB)** or **petabytes (PB)**. The threshold of "what is Big" keeps shifting as technology evolves. What was considered "Big Data" in **2008** (a few TB) is trivially handled by a **smartphone** today. Hence, Big Data is best characterized by its **challenges** (Volume, Velocity, Variety) rather than by a fixed storage number.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Flooded Library"

Imagine a small village library that holds **10,000 books**, neatly cataloged, indexed, and arranged alphabetically. A librarian can find any book in seconds.

Now, imagine a **modern digital scenario**:
- Every second, **millions of WhatsApp messages**, **YouTube uploads**, **Facebook posts**, **credit card swipes**, **satellite images**, and **IoT sensor readings** are being generated globally.
- This is like a library that is being **flooded with billions of new books every hour**, written in **thousands of languages** (text, images, video, audio), and arriving at a pace so fast that the old librarian (your traditional RDBMS) **physically cannot keep up**.

**The Reality of 2025–2026:**
- **500+ million tweets** posted per day
- **720,000+ hours** of video uploaded to YouTube daily
- **~402.74 million terabytes** of data generated per day globally
- **5 billion+ internet users** constantly contributing data

> [!TIP]
> **The Core Shift:** Traditional databases were designed for **transactional integrity** (ACID properties). Big Data systems are designed for **scalability, fault tolerance, and parallel processing** across commodity hardware.

## 1.3 What is NOT Big Data

It is equally important to understand what does **not** qualify as Big Data:

| **Aspect** | **Traditional Data** | **Big Data** |
|---|---|---|
| Data Size | Megabytes to Gigabytes | Terabytes to Zettabytes |
| Data Structure | Highly Structured (tables) | Mixed (structured, semi, unstructured) |
| Processing | Sequential / OLTP | Parallel / Distributed |
| Hardware | Single high-end server | Cluster of commodity machines |
| Schema | Schema-on-Write | Schema-on-Read |
| Velocity | Batch (hours/days) | Real-time / Stream |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** A dataset is "Big Data" only if it exhibits **at least one** of the core challenges (Volume, Velocity, or Variety) to such an extent that **traditional systems fail** to process it within acceptable time bounds.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 5 V's of Big Data – The Foundation Framework

The **5 V's** form the universally accepted characterization framework. Every KTU question on "Introduction to Big Data" is essentially testing your understanding of these dimensions.

### 2.1.1 Volume (The Size Dimension)
- Refers to the **sheer magnitude** of data generated and stored.
- Modern enterprises deal with data ranging from **Terabytes (10¹² bytes)** to **Zettabytes (10²¹ bytes)**.
- **Example:** Facebook stores **~500 petabytes** of data; Walmart handles **~2.5 petabytes** of customer data hourly.

### 2.1.2 Velocity (The Speed Dimension)
- Refers to the **rate at which data is generated, collected, and processed**.
- Two sub-aspects:
  - **Data Ingestion Velocity:** How fast data arrives (e.g., **6 million tweets per minute**).
  - **Data Processing Velocity:** How fast insights must be derived (e.g., fraud detection must occur in **milliseconds**).
- Real-time processing frameworks (Apache Storm, Apache Flink) operate at sub-second latencies.

### 2.1.3 Variety (The Format Dimension)
- Refers to the **different types and formats** of data.
- Three categories:
  - **Structured Data:** Tabular, relational, with fixed schema (RDBMS, SQL tables).
  - **Semi-Structured Data:** Has some organizational properties but no rigid schema (JSON, XML, CSV, log files).
  - **Unstructured Data:** No predefined model (~80–90% of all data): text, images, audio, video, social media posts.

### 2.1.4 Veracity (The Trust Dimension)
- Refers to the **quality, accuracy, and trustworthiness** of data.
- Addresses: noise, bias, inconsistency, missing values, data lineage.
- Critical in **healthcare**, **finance**, and **autonomous systems** where a wrong data point can be catastrophic.

### 2.1.5 Value (The Outcome Dimension)
- Refers to the **worth** of the data extracted.
- The ultimate goal of Big Data analytics is to convert raw data into **actionable business value**.
- **Example:** Netflix saves **\$1 billion per year** in customer retention through Big Data-driven recommendations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Some industries extend this to 7 V's or more:** Variability, Visualization, Vulnerability, etc. However, for **KTU 2024 Scheme exams**, the **5 V's model (Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value)** is the **expected standard answer**.

## 2.2 The 3 V's vs 5 V's – Examination Perspective

The original **3 V's** were proposed by **Doug Laney (Gartner, 2001)**: Volume, Velocity, Variety.

The **5 V's** extended this by IBM (~2012 onwards) to include Veracity and Value.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU 14-mark answers:** Always present the **3 V's first** (as the original framework), then add **Veracity and Value** as the **IBM extension** to demonstrate depth of knowledge.

## 2.3 Structured vs Unstructured vs Semi-Structured Data

| **Property** | **Structured** | **Semi-Structured** | **Unstructured** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Schema | Fixed, predefined | Flexible, partial | No schema |
| Format | Tables (rows/columns) | JSON, XML, Email | Text, Image, Audio, Video |
| Storage | RDBMS (MySQL, Oracle) | NoSQL, Document DBs | Data Lakes, Object Storage |
| Searchability | High (SQL) | Moderate | Very Low |
| % of Total Data | ~10–20% | ~10–20% | ~70–80% |
| Example | Bank transaction records | Tweet metadata, JSON logs | CCTV footage, doctor notes |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes the **essential formulas, units, and conversion factors** that are high-yield for KTU numerical or short-answer questions on Big Data Introduction.

| **Quantity** | **Formula / Definition** | **Typical Unit** | **Engineering Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data Size (Base 2) | $1 \text{ KB} = 2^{10} \text{ B}$ | Bytes (B) | Storage capacity planning |
| Data Size (Base 10) | $1 \text{ KB} = 10^3 \text{ B}$ | Bytes (B) | Marketing / ISP speeds |
| Throughput | $T = \frac{D}{t}$ | MB/s, GB/s | Network bandwidth estimation |
| Total Daily Data | $D_{day} = N_{users} \times R_{per\_user} \times 86400$ | Bytes/day | Infrastructure sizing |
| Annual Growth | $D_{n} = D_0 \times (1 + r)^n$ | Bytes | Capacity planning over years |
| Doubling Time (Moore-like) | $t_{2x} = \frac{\ln(2)}{\ln(1+r)}$ | Years | Storage evolution forecasting |
| Data Reduction Ratio | $R = \frac{D_{input}}{D_{output}}$ | Dimensionless | Compression efficiency |
| Hadoop Block Scan Time | $t_{scan} = \frac{\text{Block Size}}{\text{Read Bandwidth}}$ | Seconds | I/O performance analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Reference (Base 2, IEC Standard):**
> 1 KB = 1024 B, 1 MB = 1024 KB, 1 GB = 1024 MB, 1 TB = 1024 GB, 1 PB = 1024 TB, 1 EB = 1024 PB, 1 ZB = 1024 EB, 1 YB = 1024 ZB.

## 2.5 Why Traditional RDBMS Fails for Big Data

Traditional **Relational Database Management Systems (RDBMS)** like Oracle, MySQL, and SQL Server are built on **ACID properties** (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) and assume:
1. Data fits on a single server.
2. Schema is fixed before write (Schema-on-Write).
3. Vertical scaling (bigger machines) is the answer.

**Big Data breaks all three assumptions:**

- **Distributed nature:** Data must be spread across hundreds/thousands of nodes.
- **Schema flexibility:** With 80% unstructured data, rigid schemas are impossible.
- **Horizontal scaling:** Adding more **commodity machines** is cheaper than buying one supercomputer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The CAP Theorem (Anderson, 2000; Brewer):** In any distributed system, you can guarantee **at most two** of the following three simultaneously: **Consistency (C), Availability (A), Partition tolerance (P)**. Big Data systems (e.g., HDFS, Cassandra) typically sacrifice **strict consistency** for **availability and partition tolerance** (AP systems).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

| **Domain** | **Use Case** | **Big Data Challenge** |
|---|---|---|
| Healthcare | Genomics, disease prediction | Volume (petabases of DNA data) |
| Finance | Fraud detection, algorithmic trading | Velocity (millisecond decisions) |
| Retail | Recommendation engines, inventory | Variety (clickstream + transactions) |
| Smart Cities | Traffic, pollution, surveillance | Volume + Velocity |
| Astronomy | Sky surveys (SKA, LSST) | Volume (zettabytes of images) |
| IoT | Sensor networks, predictive maintenance | Velocity + Volume |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Total Data Generated Per Day by a Platform

**Problem Statement:** A microblogging platform has **300 million daily active users**. Each user posts an average of **2 messages per day**, where each message has an average size of **250 bytes (metadata + text)**. The platform also processes **150 million image uploads per day** with an average size of **2.5 MB per image**, and **50 million short video uploads per day** with an average size of **15 MB per video**. Calculate the total daily data volume generated by the platform.

### Step 1 – Identify Each Data Stream

We have three independent data streams:
- Text messages (structured metadata + semi-structured text)
- Image uploads (unstructured binary)
- Video uploads (unstructured binary)

### Step 2 – Compute Text Message Volume

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{text} &= 300 \times 10^6 \text{ messages/day} \\
S_{text} &= 250 \text{ bytes/message} \\
D_{text} &= N_{text} \times S_{text} \\
&= 300 \times 10^6 \times 250 \\
&= 75{,}000 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/day} \\
&= 7.5 \times 10^{10} \text{ bytes/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert to Gigabytes (using 1 GB = $2^{30}$ = 1,073,741,824 bytes):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{text\_GB} &= \frac{7.5 \times 10^{10}}{1.07374 \times 10^9} \\
&\approx 69.85 \text{ GB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 – Compute Image Upload Volume

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{img} &= 150 \times 10^6 \text{ images/day} \\
S_{img} &= 2.5 \text{ MB/image} = 2.5 \times 2^{20} \text{ bytes} = 2.62144 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes} \\
D_{img} &= 150 \times 10^6 \times 2.5 \text{ MB} \\
&= 375 \times 10^6 \text{ MB} \\
&= 3.75 \times 10^8 \text{ MB} \\
&= 3.75 \times 10^{11} \text{ bytes/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 – Compute Video Upload Volume

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{vid} &= 50 \times 10^6 \text{ videos/day} \\
S_{vid} &= 15 \text{ MB/video} \\
D_{vid} &= 50 \times 10^6 \times 15 \text{ MB} \\
&= 750 \times 10^6 \text{ MB} \\
&= 7.5 \times 10^{11} \text{ bytes/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 – Sum the Total Daily Volume

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{total} &= D_{text} + D_{img} + D_{vid} \\
&= 7.5 \times 10^{10} + 3.75 \times 10^{11} + 7.5 \times 10^{11} \\
&= (0.75 + 3.75 + 7.50) \times 10^{11} \\
&= 12.0 \times 10^{11} \text{ bytes/day} \\
&= 1.20 \times 10^{12} \text{ bytes/day} \\
&= 1.20 \text{ TB/day (decimal)} \\
D_{total\_TB} &= \frac{1.20 \times 10^{12}}{1.0995 \times 10^{12}} \approx 1.091 \text{ TiB/day (binary)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The platform generates over **1 TB of raw data per day** — purely from user content. Add to this the **backups (typically 3x replication)**, **logs**, **intermediate processing artifacts**, and **analytics outputs**, and the storage infrastructure must handle **5–10 TB/day** — a clear Big Data scenario.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Annual Capacity Growth Planning

**Problem Statement:** A hospital's medical imaging system starts the year with **500 TB** of stored data. Data grows at a steady annual rate of **r = 35%** (typical for healthcare imaging). Calculate the projected storage requirement after **5 years**.

### Step 1 – State the Compound Growth Formula

$$
D_{n} = D_{0} \times (1 + r)^{n}
$$

where:
- $D_{0}$ = initial data size
- $r$ = annual growth rate (as decimal)
- $n$ = number of years
- $D_{n}$ = projected data size after $n$ years

### Step 2 – Substitute Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{0} &= 500 \text{ TB} \\
r &= 0.35 \\
n &= 5
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 – Compute $(1 + r)^n$ Step by Step

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.35)^1 &= 1.3500 \\
(1.35)^2 &= 1.35 \times 1.35 = 1.8225 \\
(1.35)^3 &= 1.8225 \times 1.35 = 2.4604 \\
(1.35)^4 &= 2.4604 \times 1.35 = 3.3215 \\
(1.35)^5 &= 3.3215 \times 1.35 = 4.4840
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 – Multiply by Initial Size

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{5} &= 500 \times 4.4840 \\
&= 2242.0 \text{ TB} \\
&\approx 2.242 \text{ PB}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 – Apply the 3x Replication Factor for HDFS

Big Data systems like **HDFS** replicate data **3 times by default** for fault tolerance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{5,replicated} &= 3 \times D_{5} \\
&= 3 \times 2.242 \text{ PB} \\
&= 6.726 \text{ PB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Decision:** With 6.7 PB of replicated data, the hospital needs approximately **45–50 commodity nodes** (each with ~150 TB raw) and an **exabyte-tier** backup strategy. This is the kind of capacity-planning reasoning expected in KTU design problems.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: Reading Throughput Estimation

**Problem Statement:** A Hadoop cluster has **20 DataNodes**, each with a disk read bandwidth of **150 MB/s**. The HDFS block size is configured at **128 MB**. A file of size **1 TB** is being scanned. Calculate:
1. Total cluster read bandwidth.
2. Number of blocks.
3. Theoretical minimum scan time (assuming perfect parallelism).

### Step 1 – Total Cluster Read Bandwidth

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{cluster} &= N_{nodes} \times B_{per\_node} \\
&= 20 \times 150 \text{ MB/s} \\
&= 3000 \text{ MB/s} \\
&= 3.0 \text{ GB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 – Number of HDFS Blocks

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{blocks} &= \frac{\text{File Size}}{\text{Block Size}} \\
&= \frac{1 \text{ TB}}{128 \text{ MB}} \\
&= \frac{1024 \times 1024 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB}} \\
&= \frac{1{,}048{,}576}{128} \\
&= 8192 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 – Theoretical Minimum Scan Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{scan} &= \frac{\text{File Size}}{B_{cluster}} \\
&= \frac{1 \text{ TB}}{3.0 \text{ GB/s}} \\
&= \frac{1024 \text{ GB}}{3.0 \text{ GB/s}} \\
&\approx 341.33 \text{ seconds} \\
&\approx 5 \text{ minutes } 41 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Reality check:** Actual scan time is typically **20–40% higher** than the theoretical minimum due to network overhead, NameNode coordination, JVM warmup, and task scheduling. KTU questions that include a "practical overhead factor" of 1.3 are common.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Comparison Summary

| **Dimension** | **Traditional RDBMS** | **Big Data Systems** |
|---|---|---|
| Data Scale | GB–TB | TB–ZB |
| Data Type | Mostly Structured | Structured + Semi + Unstructured |
| Processing Mode | Sequential / Batch | Parallel / Distributed |
| Hardware | Single high-spec server | Cluster of commodity servers |
| Scaling Model | Vertical (scale-up) | Horizontal (scale-out) |
| Schema Approach | Schema-on-Write | Schema-on-Read |
| Consistency Model | ACID (strong) | BASE (eventual) |
| Fault Tolerance | Hardware RAID | Software replication (e.g., 3x in HDFS) |
| Cost Model | High (proprietary) | Low (open-source, commodity) |
| Latency Goal | Milliseconds (OLTP) | Varies (ms for stream, hours for batch) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
