# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define Big Data. List and briefly explain any three characteristics (V's) of Big Data.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Big Data refers to extremely large and complex datasets that cannot be efficiently processed by traditional database management systems. It requires advanced storage, management, and analytical techniques to extract value from data characterized by high Volume, Velocity, and Variety.

**Three Characteristics (2 marks — 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.5 for explanation):**

1. **Volume:** Refers to the massive size of data, typically ranging from terabytes to zettabytes. Example: Google processes over 20 petabytes of data per day.

2. **Velocity:** Refers to the speed at which data is generated and must be processed. Example: Stock market data must be analyzed within milliseconds for algorithmic trading.

3. **Variety:** Refers to the different formats of data — structured (tables), semi-structured (JSON, XML), and unstructured (images, videos, text).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected]`
**Differentiate between Structured, Semi-Structured, and Unstructured data with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per type with example):**

| **Type** | **Description** | **Example** |
|---|---|---|
| **Structured** | Data organized in a predefined schema with rows and columns; easily searchable using SQL. | A bank's MySQL customer table with fields like name, account number, balance. |
| **Semi-Structured** | Data with partial organizational properties; uses tags or markers but no fixed schema. | JSON file containing tweets with fields like user_id, timestamp, text. |
| **Unstructured** | Data with no predefined model or schema; constitutes ~80% of all data. | A 4K surveillance video recording or a doctor's handwritten clinical notes. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the **5 V's of Big Data** in detail with real-world examples. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** Compare and contrast **Traditional RDBMS** with **Big Data Systems** across any **seven key parameters**. Discuss why traditional systems fail to handle Big Data scenarios. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Part (a): The 5 V's of Big Data (7 marks)**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The 5 V's framework, popularized by **IBM**, is the industry-standard characterization model for Big Data. It extends Doug Laney's original 3 V's (Volume, Velocity, Variety) with two additional dimensions: **Veracity** and **Value**.

**1. Volume (1.5 marks):**
- Refers to the **sheer size/quantity** of data.
- Data sizes range from **terabytes to zettabytes**.
- **Example:** Facebook stores over **500 petabytes** of user data; the **Square Kilometre Array (SKA)** telescope generates ~**1 zettabyte per day**.

**2. Velocity (1.5 marks):**
- Refers to the **speed** of data generation and processing requirements.
- Two aspects: **data ingestion rate** and **data processing latency**.
- **Example:** High-frequency stock trading systems require decisions in **microseconds**; autonomous vehicles process sensor data at **100+ frames/second**.

**3. Variety (1.5 marks):**
- Refers to the **diversity of data types and formats**.
- Includes structured (RDBMS), semi-structured (JSON, XML, logs), and unstructured (images, video, audio, social media).
- **Example:** A single hospital generates structured EHR records, semi-structured HL7 messages, and unstructured MRI scans, endoscopy videos, and doctor dictations.

**4. Veracity (0.75 mark):**
- Refers to the **quality, accuracy, and trustworthiness** of data.
- Addresses inconsistencies, noise, missing values, and bias.
- **Example:** Social media sentiment data is highly noisy (sarcasm, bots, multilingual content).

**5. Value (0.75 mark):**
- Refers to the **business worth** derived from Big Data analytics.
- The ultimate goal: convert raw data into actionable insights.
- **Example:** Amazon's recommendation engine drives **35% of total revenue**; Uber uses Big Data for dynamic pricing and route optimization.

---

### **Model Solution — Part (b): Traditional RDBMS vs Big Data (7 marks)**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Traditional **Relational Database Management Systems (RDBMS)** like Oracle, MySQL, and SQL Server have served enterprises for decades. However, the **explosion of data** in the 2000s exposed fundamental limitations in handling Big Data scenarios.

**Comparison Table (3 marks — 0.5 mark per parameter, at least 6 parameters):**

| **Parameter** | **Traditional RDBMS** | **Big Data Systems** |
|---|---|---|
| Data Scale | GB to few TB | TB to ZB |
| Data Type | Mostly Structured | All three types |
| Processing | Sequential / Vertical | Parallel / Distributed |
| Scaling | Vertical (bigger server) | Horizontal (more servers) |
| Schema | Schema-on-Write | Schema-on-Read |
| Fault Tolerance | Hardware RAID | Software replication |
| Cost | High (proprietary licenses) | Low (open-source, commodity) |
| Consistency | Strong ACID | Eventual (BASE) |

**Why Traditional Systems Fail (3 marks):**

1. **Storage Ceiling:** A single high-end server maxes out at ~**100 TB** even with top-tier SAN storage. Zettabyte-scale data is impossible to fit.

2. **Single Point of Failure:** RDBMS relies on one server; if it crashes, the entire system halts. Big Data systems replicate data across nodes.

3. **Cost Inefficiency:** Vertical scaling requires exponentially expensive hardware (a **\$1 million** server is ~10x faster than a **\$100K** one, not 100x).

4. **Schema Rigidity:** Modern data (logs, JSON, images) is **schema-less or schema-flexible**. RDBMS demands a fixed schema before write, causing massive ETL overhead.

5. **Processing Latency:** MapReduce/Spark on 1000 nodes can process 1 PB in **hours**; a single RDBMS would take **months**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write only the comparison table and skip the **"Why Traditional Systems Fail"** explanation in Part (b). This typically causes a **2–3 mark deduction**. Always provide **at least 3 substantive reasons** for the failure, not just generic statements like "RDBMS is slow."

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected]`

**Q: (a)** Define Big Data. Explain in detail the **evolution of Big Data** from traditional data processing to the modern Hadoop-era. Mention key milestones and technologies. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** A social media analytics company stores **2 TB of raw data daily**. Data grows at **40% per year**. The infrastructure uses **3x replication** on HDFS. Each DataNode has **20 TB raw capacity** with **150 nodes** currently. Calculate:
   (i) The total raw capacity of the cluster. **(2 marks)**
   (ii) The data volume after **4 years** (with replication). **(3 marks)**
   (iii) Whether the existing cluster is sufficient after 4 years; if not, how many **additional DataNodes** are required? **(2 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Part (a): Evolution of Big Data (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Big Data is data whose volume, velocity, and variety exceed the capacity of conventional database systems to capture, store, manage, and analyze within acceptable time bounds.

**Evolution Timeline (5 marks):**

| **Era** | **Period** | **Key Characteristics** | **Technology Milestone** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pre-2000s** | Early Databases | OLTP, batch processing, single-server RDBMS | Oracle, IBM DB2 |
| **2000–2005** | Web Era Begins | Search engines indexing billions of pages | Google File System (GFS) paper, 2003 |
| **2006–2008** | Hadoop Revolution | Open-source distributed processing | Hadoop (Yahoo!), MapReduce, HDFS |
| **2009–2012** | NoSQL Boom | Schema-less, horizontal scaling | MongoDB, Cassandra, HBase |
| **2013–2015** | In-Memory Era | Faster processing via RAM | Apache Spark, Strom |
| **2016–2019** | Cloud Big Data | Managed services, serverless | AWS EMR, Azure HDInsight, GCP Dataproc |
| **2020–2026** | AI + Lakehouse Era | Unified analytics + ML integration | Delta Lake, Apache Iceberg, ML pipelines |

**Key Milestones (1 mark):**
- **2003:** Google publishes **GFS (Google File System)** paper.
- **2004:** Google publishes **MapReduce** paper.
- **2006:** **Doug Cutting** creates Hadoop; Yahoo! deploys it at scale.
- **2014:** **Apache Spark** emerges as a faster in-memory alternative to MapReduce.
- **2018+:** **Lakehouse architecture** unifies data lakes and warehouses.

---

### **Model Solution — Part (b): Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**
- Daily raw data: $D_{daily} = 2 \text{ TB/day}$
- Annual growth rate: $r = 40\% = 0.40$
- Replication factor: $k = 3$
- Raw capacity per DataNode: $C_{node} = 20 \text{ TB}$
- Current number of nodes: $N = 150$

**Part (i) — Total Raw Capacity (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{total} &= N \times C_{node} \\
&= 150 \times 20 \text{ TB} \\
&= 3000 \text{ TB} \\
&= 3 \text{ PB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (ii) — Data Volume After 4 Years With Replication (3 marks):**

Yearly cumulative volume (assuming 365 days/year):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_0 &= 2 \text{ TB/day} \times 365 \text{ days/year} = 730 \text{ TB/year} \quad \text{(Year 1 base)} \\
D_1 &= 730 \times 1.40 = 1022 \text{ TB} \\
D_2 &= 1022 \times 1.40 = 1430.8 \text{ TB} \\
D_3 &= 1430.8 \times 1.40 = 2003.12 \text{ TB} \\
D_4 &= 2003.12 \times 1.40 = 2804.37 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply 3x replication:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{4,replicated} &= 3 \times 2804.37 \\
&= 8413.11 \text{ TB} \\
&\approx 8.41 \text{ PB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (iii) — Cluster Sufficiency (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Additional Capacity Needed} &= D_{4,replicated} - C_{total} \\
&= 8413.11 - 3000 \\
&= 5413.11 \text{ TB} \\
&\approx 5.41 \text{ PB}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Additional Nodes Required} &= \frac{5413.11 \text{ TB}}{20 \text{ TB/node}} \\
&= 270.66 \\
&\approx 271 \text{ additional DataNodes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The existing 150-node cluster is **insufficient** after 4 years. The company must add approximately **271 more DataNodes** (bringing the total to **421 nodes**) to handle 8.41 PB of replicated data.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark Loss:** Students often forget to apply the **replication factor** in the final comparison or compute the year-over-year growth incorrectly. Always show **each intermediate year** explicitly, and clearly distinguish between **raw vs. replicated** storage.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Introduction to Big Data**

- [x] **Definition:** Big Data is defined by its **challenges (V's)**, not by a fixed size in bytes.
- [x] **3 V's (Doug Laney, 2001):** Volume, Velocity, Variety — these are the **original and mandatory** V's.
- [x] **5 V's (IBM extension):** Add **Veracity** (data quality) and **Value** (business outcome).
- [x] **Volume** → Size: TB → PB → EB → ZB → YB (Base-2: 1024 multiples).
- [x] **Velocity** → Speed of generation AND processing (real-time vs batch).
- [x] **Variety** → Structured (~20%), Semi-structured (~10%), Unstructured (~70–80%).
- [x] **Veracity** → Trust, accuracy, lineage, bias handling.
- [x] **Value** → The **end goal** — actionable insights, ROI.
- [x] **Traditional RDBMS fails** because of: storage ceiling, single point of failure, schema rigidity, vertical scaling cost, and inability to process unstructured data.
- [x] **CAP Theorem:** Distributed systems can guarantee only **2 of 3** (Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance).
- [x] **ACID vs BASE:** RDBMS uses ACID (strong); Big Data uses BASE (eventual consistency).
- [x] **Schema-on-Write** (RDBMS) vs **Schema-on-Read** (Hadoop/Data Lake).
- [x] **Vertical Scaling** (bigger machine) vs **Horizontal Scaling** (more machines).
- [x] **Replication Factor:** HDFS default is **3x** — always include in capacity planning.
- [x] **Growth Formula:** $D_{n} = D_{0} \times (1 + r)^{n}$ for capacity planning problems.
- [x] **Throughput Formula:** $T = D/t$ for bandwidth and scan-time calculations.
- [x] **Key Conversion:** 1 TB = 1024 GB = $2^{40}$ bytes (binary) or $10^{12}$ bytes (decimal).
- [x] **Real-world data scale examples to memorize:** Google ~20 PB/day, Facebook ~500 PB total, YouTube ~720K hours/day.
- [x] **Block size in HDFS:** Default 128 MB (older versions: 64 MB).
- [x] **Milestones:** GFS (2003), MapReduce (2004), Hadoop (2006), Spark (2014), Lakehouse (2018+).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conventional Data vs Big Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT746 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Conventional Data vs Big Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Conventional Data (Traditional Data)

**Conventional Data** refers to data that is **structured, organized, and stored in relational database management systems (RDBMS)** such as Oracle, MySQL, PostgreSQL, or Microsoft SQL Server. It typically fits neatly into rows and columns of a table, follows a **predefined schema**, and is managed using **ACID (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability)** properties. Conventional data is generally limited in size (ranging from **Gigabytes to a few Terabytes**), processed using **batch processing** techniques, and is best suited for **OLTP (Online Transaction Processing)** and **OLAP (Online Analytical Processing)** workloads.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECNT746 Module 1):**
> Conventional data is the data processed by traditional Enterprise Data Warehouse (EDW) systems — limited in volume, velocity, and variety — handled by centralized, vertically scalable, schema-on-write architectures.

### 1.2 Definition of Big Data

**Big Data** refers to datasets whose **size, speed, or complexity exceeds the capability of traditional data processing systems** to capture, manage, and process them within tolerable elapsed time. According to Gartner (industry-standard definition), Big Data is high-volume, high-velocity, and/or high-variety information assets that demand **cost-effective, innovative forms of information processing** that enable enhanced insight, decision-making, and process automation. Big Data is typically measured in **Petabytes, Exabytes, or Zettabytes** and requires distributed computing frameworks like **Hadoop, Spark, NoSQL databases, and cloud-scale architectures**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Big Data = The "5 V's" Paradigm (Industry Standard, KTU 2024):**
> 1. **Volume** — Scale of data (TB → PB → EB)
> 2. **Velocity** — Speed at which data is generated (real-time streams)
> 3. **Variety** — Different data formats (structured, semi-structured, unstructured)
> 4. **Veracity** — Trustworthiness/quality of data
> 5. **Value** — Business worth extracted from data

### 1.3 Real-World Analogy: The Library vs. The Internet

Imagine **Conventional Data** as a **traditional library**:
- A library has a fixed number of books, neatly catalogued using the **Dewey Decimal System** (schema).
- A librarian (the RDBMS) knows exactly where every book is located and can retrieve it quickly using structured queries (SQL).
- You must visit the library, fill out a card, and wait for retrieval — this is like **batch processing**.
- The library's capacity is physically limited by the building's size (vertical scaling limit).

Now imagine **Big Data** as the **entire Internet**:
- The Internet holds an ever-growing, never-ending supply of information — text, videos, images, social media posts, sensor readings (variety).
- New content is uploaded every second at lightning speed (velocity).
- The total volume is incomprehensible — billions of websites, trillions of files (volume).
- The data is often messy, contradictory, or incomplete (veracity).
- Yet hidden within it lies immense value — patterns, predictions, insights (value).
- To process this, you cannot use a single librarian — you need a **distributed team of workers** (Hadoop cluster with thousands of nodes) working in parallel.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Conventional Data is like storing water in a **bucket** (limited, structured, easy to handle). Big Data is like storing water in the **ocean** (vast, complex, requires distributed systems to navigate).

### 1.4 Where Does the Boundary Lie?

| Metric | Conventional Data | Big Data |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Size** | MB → GB → few TB | TB → PB → EB → ZB |
| **Data Sources** | Operational DBs, ERP, CRM | Social media, IoT sensors, logs, web |
| **Schema** | Fixed, predefined (Schema-on-Write) | Dynamic, flexible (Schema-on-Read) |
| **Processing Mode** | Batch | Batch + Real-time / Stream |
| **Architecture** | Centralized, monolithic | Distributed, parallel |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (KTU 2024):** A dataset is generally classified as "Big Data" when its volume exceeds **1 Terabyte (TB)** and requires distributed storage/computation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Deep Analysis: Why Conventional Data Systems Fail at Scale

The fundamental limitations of conventional data systems stem from three architectural bottlenecks:

1. **Vertical Scalability Ceiling (Scale-Up Architecture):**
   - Conventional RDBMS rely on a **single, powerful server** (scale-up). To handle more data, you must buy a bigger, more expensive machine.
   - There is a **physical and economic ceiling** — you cannot keep adding CPU, RAM, or disk to one machine indefinitely.
   - High-end servers cap out at certain configurations, after which costs grow **exponentially** while performance gains diminish.

2. **Schema Rigidity (Schema-on-Write):**
   - In RDBMS, the schema (table structure) must be **defined BEFORE data is loaded**.
   - This makes accommodating new data types (e.g., images, JSON, video) extremely cumbersome — every new field requires schema migration.
   - It enforces strict data typing, which works for transactions but fails for unstructured sources.

3. **ACID vs. BASE Trade-off:**
   - Conventional systems prioritize **ACID** (strict consistency). In a distributed environment, achieving ACID across thousands of nodes is prohibitively expensive.
   - Big Data systems often relax this to **BASE (Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency)** to achieve horizontal scale and fault tolerance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (Why Big Data emerged):** As the world moved to web-scale platforms (Google, Facebook, Amazon) in the early 2000s, the limitations of RDBMS became critical. Google's **GFS (Google File System)** paper (2003) and **MapReduce** paper (2004) laid the foundation for Hadoop, which solved these limitations via **horizontal scaling (scale-out)**.

### 2.2 The 5 V's of Big Data — Deep Dive

**V1 — Volume:**
- Refers to the **sheer quantity** of data generated.
- Data sources: social media posts (~500 million tweets/day on Twitter), IoT sensors (billions), transactional logs, scientific experiments (LHC generates ~1 PB/sec).
- Measured in **Terabytes (TB = 10¹² bytes), Petabytes (PB = 10¹⁵), Exabytes (EB = 10¹⁸), Zettabytes (ZB = 10²¹)**.

**V2 — Velocity:**
- Refers to the **speed** at which data is produced, captured, and must be processed.
- Examples: high-frequency stock trading (microsecond latency), autonomous vehicle sensor data, real-time fraud detection.
- Conventional systems operate in **batch windows (hourly/daily ETL)**; Big Data requires **stream processing (millisecond-level)**.

**V3 — Variety:**
- Refers to the **diversity of data types and sources**.
- Structured (relational tables), Semi-structured (JSON, XML, CSV, logs), Unstructured (images, video, audio, text, social media).
- Conventional DBs handle **structured only**; Big Data frameworks natively support all three.

**V4 — Veracity:**
- Refers to the **quality, accuracy, and trustworthiness** of data.
- Big Data sources are often noisy — sensor errors, duplicate records, missing values, conflicting entries.
- Requires **data cleansing, validation, and lineage tracking**.

**V5 — Value:**
- Refers to the **business or analytical worth** that can be extracted from data.
- Value is the **end goal** — the other V's (Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity) are inputs; Value is the output.
- Achieved through ML, AI, predictive analytics, and statistical modeling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Some KTU references include a 6th V: Variability** (changes in data rate/format over time) and **Visualization** — but the canonical 5 V's (Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value) are the KTU 2024 standard.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Comparison Metrics)

| Aspect | Conventional Data Formula/Metric | Big Data Formula/Metric | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Growth (Compound)** | $D_{n} = D_{0} \cdot (1 + r)^{n}$ | $D_{n} = D_{0} \cdot (1 + r)^{n}$ | bytes |
| **Storage Requirement** | $S_{c} = V \cdot (1 + O_{h})$ | $S_{b} = V \cdot R_{rep} \cdot (1 + O_{h})$ | bytes |
| **Processing Throughput** | $T_{c} = \dfrac{D}{R_{single}}$ | $T_{b} = \dfrac{D}{N \cdot R_{node}}$ | seconds |
| **Latency Bound (Real-time)** | $L_{c} \geq \Delta t_{batch}$ | $L_{b} \leq \Delta t_{real\text{-}time}$ | ms / s |
| **Cost per TB (Annual)** | $C_{c} \approx \$100$–\$1000 | $C_{b} \approx \$10$–\$100 | USD |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Single point of failure | $P_{fail} = 1 - (1 - p)^{N}$ | probability |
| **Scalability Type** | Vertical (Scale-Up) | Horizontal (Scale-Out) | nodes |

> Where: $D_0$ = initial data, $r$ = growth rate, $n$ = years, $V$ = volume, $O_h$ = overhead, $R_{rep}$ = replication factor (typically 3 in HDFS), $R_{single}$ = single-server rate, $N$ = number of nodes, $R_{node}$ = per-node rate, $p$ = per-node failure probability.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of This Comparison

- **Industry Use Case (Banking):** Conventional DBs handle customer accounts and transactions. Big Data systems analyze millions of transactions per second for fraud detection.
- **Healthcare:** Conventional systems store patient records. Big Data systems analyze genomic data, clinical trial outcomes, and wearable device streams.
- **E-commerce:** Conventional systems manage the product catalog. Big Data systems drive recommendation engines (Amazon, Flipkart) and dynamic pricing.
- **Smart Cities:** Big Data enables real-time traffic management, pollution monitoring, and emergency response.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Data Growth Projection (Compound Model)

Both conventional and big data environments experience exponential data growth. The compound growth model is:

$$
D(n) = D_0 \cdot (1 + r)^{n}
$$

**Derivation Steps:**

Let $D_0$ be the initial data size at year 0, and $r$ be the annual growth rate (decimal). After 1 year, the new data size is:

$$
D(1) = D_0 + D_0 \cdot r = D_0 \cdot (1 + r)
$$

After 2 years, the previous year's size also grows by $r$:

$$
D(2) = D(1) + D(1) \cdot r = D(1) \cdot (1 + r) = D_0 \cdot (1 + r)^2
$$

By mathematical induction, after $n$ years:

$$
D(n) = D_0 \cdot (1 + r)^{n}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU Exam Standard):**

A company starts with $D_0 = 100 \text{ GB}$ of data. The annual growth rate is $r = 0.60$ (60% per year). Find the data size after 5 years, and determine whether it has crossed the Big Data threshold (1 TB).

Substituting into the formula:

$$
D(5) = 100 \cdot (1 + 0.60)^{5} \text{ GB}
$$

Step-by-step evaluation:

$$
D(5) = 100 \cdot (1.6)^{5} \text{ GB}
$$

$$
D(5) = 100 \cdot 10.48576 \text{ GB}
$$

$$
D(5) = 1048.576 \text{ GB} = 1.048 \text{ TB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** After 5 years, the data has just **crossed the 1 TB threshold**, transitioning the system from a **Conventional Data** regime to a **Big Data** regime. This single numerical demonstration is a **high-yield KTU question**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Storage Requirement with Replication (HDFS Model)

In Big Data systems (Hadoop HDFS), files are split into blocks and replicated across nodes (default replication factor $R_{rep} = 3$). The actual physical storage required is:

$$
S_{b} = V \cdot R_{rep} \cdot (1 + O_h)
$$

**Derivation Logic:**

The **logical data volume** $V$ is the actual amount of business data stored. However, the system stores $R_{rep}$ copies for fault tolerance. So the **raw storage** is $V \cdot R_{rep}$. Additionally, the file system has metadata, logs, and block overhead totaling a fraction $O_h$ of the raw storage. The total physical storage becomes:

$$
S_{b} = V \cdot R_{rep} \cdot (1 + O_h)
$$

**Numerical Example:**

Suppose $V = 10 \text{ TB}$ of business data, $R_{rep} = 3$, and $O_h = 0.20$ (20% overhead).

$$
S_{b} = 10 \cdot 3 \cdot (1 + 0.20) \text{ TB}
$$

$$
S_{b} = 30 \cdot 1.20 \text{ TB}
$$

$$
S_{b} = 36 \text{ TB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Compare with Conventional Data:** A conventional system storing 10 TB of data typically requires $S_c = 10 \cdot 1.20 = 12 \text{ TB}$ (no replication; backups are separate). The Big Data system uses 3× more storage but gains **fault tolerance** (can survive 2 simultaneous node failures).

### 3.3 Derivation: Processing Time Comparison (Speed-Up Analysis)

**Conventional System (Single Server):**

A single server can process data at rate $R_{single}$ (bytes/sec). The time to process data of size $D$ is:

$$
T_{c} = \frac{D}{R_{single}}
$$

**Big Data System (Distributed, N nodes):**

When the same data is distributed across $N$ nodes, each processing at rate $R_{node}$:

$$
T_{b} = \frac{D}{N \cdot R_{node}}
$$

**Speed-Up Factor:**

$$
S = \frac{T_{c}}{T_{b}} = \frac{D / R_{single}}{D / (N \cdot R_{node})} = N \cdot \frac{R_{node}}{R_{single}}
$$

Assuming $R_{node} \approx R_{single}$ (commodity hardware, same performance per node):

$$
S \approx N
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (Amdahl's Law context):** The theoretical maximum speed-up is $N$ (linear). In practice, overhead from data shuffling, coordination, and network bottlenecks reduces this. The **realized speed-up** with MapReduce is typically $S_{real} \approx 0.7 \cdot N$ to $0.9 \cdot N$ for well-designed jobs.

**Numerical Example (KTU Exam Standard):**

A 100 TB dataset takes 10 hours to process on a single server. If we use a Hadoop cluster of 50 nodes, what is the theoretical processing time?

$$
T_{c} = 10 \text{ hours}, \quad D = 100 \text{ TB}, \quad N = 50
$$

$$
T_{b} = \frac{T_{c}}{N} = \frac{10}{50} \text{ hours} = 0.2 \text{ hours} = 12 \text{ minutes}
$$

Accounting for ~30% overhead (network, coordination, I/O):

$$
T_{b, real} \approx 0.2 \cdot 1.3 = 0.26 \text{ hours} \approx 15.6 \text{ minutes}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comprehensive KTU Comparison Table

| Parameter | Conventional Data | Big Data |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Size** | GB to few TB (typically < 10 TB) | TB to PB to EB (typically > 1 TB) |
| **Data Type** | Structured (relational tables) | Structured + Semi-structured + Unstructured |
| **Schema** | Schema-on-Write (rigid) | Schema-on-Read (flexible) |
| **Processing** | Batch (ETL, OLAP) | Batch + Real-time / Stream |
| **Architecture** | Centralized, monolithic | Distributed, parallel (cluster) |
| **Scaling** | Vertical (Scale-Up) | Horizontal (Scale-Out) |
| **Hardware** | High-end servers, proprietary | Commodity hardware (commodity clusters) |
| **Cost Model** | CapEx-heavy (buy servers) | OpEx-friendly (cloud, pay-as-you-go) |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Manual backup/restore | Built-in replication (HDFS factor = 3) |
| **Consistency Model** | ACID (strong) | BASE (eventual) |
| **Throughput** | Low–Medium | Very High (parallel I/O) |
| **Latency** | Seconds to hours | Milliseconds to seconds |
| **Examples** | Oracle, MySQL, SQL Server | Hadoop, Spark, Cassandra, MongoDB |
| **Use Case** | Banking transactions, ERP | Social media analytics, IoT, ML |
| **Data Sources** | ERP, CRM, OLTP | Web logs, sensors, social, mobile |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** When drawing this comparison, **always include the Schema-on-Write vs. Schema-on-Read distinction** — it is a frequently asked 3-mark and 7-mark concept.

### 3.5 Migration Threshold: When to Move from Conventional to Big Data

A common KTU question: *"When should an organization migrate from conventional to big data infrastructure?"*

The decision can be quantified using the **Data Gravity Principle**:

$$
F_{data} = k \cdot \frac{M_{1} \cdot M_{2}}{d^{2}}
$$

Where $F_{data}$ is the "pull" of data on services, $M_1$ and $M_2$ are data masses, and $d$ is the distance (latency). As $M$ grows, services are pulled closer to data, justifying Big Data infrastructure.

**Empirical Rule (Industry Standard):**
- < 1 TB → Conventional RDBMS is sufficient
- 1 TB – 10 TB → Hybrid (RDBMS + data warehouse)
- > 10 TB → Big Data (Hadoop/Spark) is justified

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Conventional Data Pipeline (EDW)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Systems ERP CRM OLTP] --> B[ETL Extract Transform Load]
    B --> C[Staging Area]
    C --> D[Data Warehouse RDBMS]
    D --> E[OLAP Cube]
    E --> F[BI Reports Dashboards]
    D -.Backup.-> G[Tape Backup]
    G -.Restore.-> D
```

**Explanation of the Conventional Pipeline:**

- **Source Systems** generate structured transactional data (e.g., order entries, customer records).
- **ETL (Extract, Transform, Load)** processes the data in scheduled batches (typically nightly).
- **Staging Area** is a temporary holding zone where data is cleansed and transformed.
- **Data Warehouse (RDBMS)** stores the cleaned, integrated data in a star/snowflake schema.
- **OLAP Cube** pre-aggregates data for fast analytical queries.
- **BI Reports/Dashboards** are generated for business users.
- **Tape Backup** provides disaster recovery (manual, slow, off-site).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architectural Limitation:** Every component is a **single point of failure**. The pipeline is **synchronous, batch-oriented, and not horizontally scalable**.

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Big Data Pipeline (Hadoop Ecosystem)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Web Logs] --> I[Ingestion Layer Flume Kafka Sqoop]
    A2[IoT Sensors] --> I
    A3[Social Media APIs] --> I
    A4[Databases MySQL Oracle] --> I
    I --> J[Distributed Storage HDFS HBase]
    J --> K[Processing Layer MapReduce Spark Tez]
    K --> L[Analytics Layer Hive Pig Impala]
    L --> M[Machine Learning Mahout MLlib]
    L --> N[Visualization Tableau D3.js]
    J -.Replication 3x.-> J
```

**Explanation of the Big Data Pipeline:**

- **Ingestion Layer (Flume/Kafka/Sqoop):** Captures streaming (Flume, Kafka) and batch (Sqoop) data from heterogeneous sources.
- **Distributed Storage (HDFS/HBase):** Stores data across commodity nodes with built-in **3× replication** for fault tolerance.
- **Processing Layer (MapReduce/Spark):** Performs parallel computation in a distributed fashion.
- **Analytics Layer (Hive/Pig/Impala):** Provides SQL-like query capabilities over the distributed store.
- **Machine Learning (Mahout/MLlib):** Extracts predictive models and patterns.
- **Visualization (Tableau/D3.js):** Presents insights to end users.

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Strength:** The system is **horizontally scalable** (add more nodes), **fault-tolerant** (replication), and **schema-flexible** (Schema-on-Read). It can process both **batch (MapReduce)** and **real-time (Spark Streaming, Flink)** workloads.

### 4.3 Comparison Flow: Decision Tree for Data Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Data Requirement] --> Q1{Data Size Estimate}
    Q1 -- Less than 1 TB --> Conv[Use Conventional RDBMS]
    Q1 -- Greater than 1 TB --> Q2{Real time Processing Needed}
    Q2 -- No --> Batch[Use Hadoop MapReduce Hive]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Variety of Data Types}
    Q3 -- Mostly Structured --> StreamSQL[Use Spark Streaming with RDBMS]
    Q3 -- Mixed Structured Unstructured --> StreamNoSQL[Use Spark Kafka NoSQL]
    Conv --> Review[Periodically Review Growth]
    Batch --> Review
    StreamSQL --> Review
    StreamNoSQL --> Review
    Review -.Data exceeds 10 TB.-> Migrate[Migrate to Big Data Stack]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- Start by estimating the data size.
- If size is small (< 1 TB), a **conventional RDBMS** is sufficient and cost-effective.
- If size is large, decide whether real-time processing is required.
- For batch-only workloads with large data, **Hadoop MapReduce + Hive** is ideal.
- For real-time needs, the choice depends on data variety:
  - **Mostly structured** → Spark Streaming + RDBMS hybrid.
  - **Mixed (structured + unstructured)** → Spark + Kafka + NoSQL.
- Periodically review the architecture as data grows; **migrate to Big Data stack** when data exceeds ~10 TB.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*Define Big Data. Explain any three of its characteristics.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Big Data refers to datasets whose size, speed, or complexity exceeds the capability of traditional data processing systems to capture, manage, and process them within a tolerable elapsed time.

**Three Characteristics (2 marks – 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.5 structure):**

1. **Volume:** Big Data involves massive amounts of data, typically measured in **Petabytes (PB) to Zettabytes (ZB)**. For example, social media platforms like Facebook generate over **4 Petabytes of new data per day**.

2. **Velocity:** Data is generated and must be processed at extremely high speeds. Real-time applications (e.g., stock trading, fraud detection) require sub-second processing, which is impossible with traditional batch-oriented RDBMS.

3. **Variety:** Big Data comes in multiple formats — structured (databases), semi-structured (JSON, XML, logs), and unstructured (images, video, audio, text). Traditional systems handle only structured data.

4. *(Alternative for 3rd characteristic)* **Veracity:** Data quality is variable; sources may be noisy, incomplete, or inconsistent, requiring robust cleansing mechanisms.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]**
*Differentiate between Conventional Data and Big Data in terms of schema, processing, and scalability.*

**Model Answer (3 marks – 1 mark per dimension):**

| Dimension | Conventional Data | Big Data |
|---|---|---|
| **Schema** | Schema-on-Write (schema defined before data is loaded; rigid, requires migration for changes) | Schema-on-Read (schema applied at query time; flexible, accommodates any format) |
| **Processing** | Batch processing (ETL jobs run on schedules — hourly/daily; not suitable for real-time) | Batch + Real-time/Stream processing (Spark Streaming, Flink for sub-second latency) |
| **Scalability** | Vertical (Scale-Up) — increase server capacity (CPU, RAM, disk); physical/economic ceiling | Horizontal (Scale-Out) — add more commodity nodes to the cluster; near-linear scaling |

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each – Module Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified Model Paper]**
**(a)** Explain the **5 V's of Big Data** with suitable examples for each characteristic. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a **neat block diagram**, compare the **architecture of a Conventional Data Warehouse** and a **Big Data (Hadoop) system**. Discuss at least **five key differences**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer for (a) — The 5 V's of Big Data (7 marks):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The 5 V's — Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, and Value — are the defining characteristics of Big Data, first popularized by IBM and now the industry standard (Gartner, KTU 2024 syllabus).

**1. Volume (1.5 marks):**
- Refers to the **sheer quantity** of data being generated and stored.
- Measured in **Terabytes, Petabytes, Exabytes, Zettabytes**.
- **Example:** Google processes over **20 Petabytes of data per day**. Facebook stores over **300 Petabytes** of user data. The Large Hadron Collider at CERN generates **1 Petabyte per second** during experiments.

**2. Velocity (1.5 marks):**
- Refers to the **speed** at which data is produced, transmitted, and processed.
- Modern applications demand **real-time or near-real-time** responses.
- **Example:** High-frequency stock trading systems must process market data within **microseconds**. Uber's dynamic pricing updates every few seconds based on ride demand streams. IoT sensors in autonomous vehicles generate data at **100 Hz** that must be processed in real time.

**3. Variety (1 mark):**
- Refers to the **diverse formats and sources** of data.
- Three types: **structured** (relational tables, CSV), **semi-structured** (JSON, XML, log files, emails), and **unstructured** (images, video, audio, social media posts).
- **Example:** A single e-commerce platform handles customer profiles (structured), product reviews (unstructured text), product images (unstructured), clickstream logs (semi-structured), and inventory feeds (semi-structured).

**4. Veracity (1 mark):**
- Refers to the **quality, accuracy, and trustworthiness** of data.
- Big Data sources are often **noisy, inconsistent, or incomplete**.
- **Example:** Twitter data has typos, slang, sarcasm, and spam. Sensor data may have missing values due to network outages. Veracity requires data cleansing, validation, and quality checks.

**5. Value (1 mark):**
- Refers to the **business or analytical worth** that can be derived from data.
- Value is the **ultimate goal** of Big Data analytics — the other V's are inputs; Value is the output.
- **Example:** Netflix's recommendation engine (analyzing viewing patterns) is valued at over **\$1 billion per year** in customer retention. Walmart's real-time pricing optimization increased sales by **10–15%** during Hurricane Frank (using Twitter sentiment + weather data).

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Mnemonic:** "**V³V²**" = Volume, Velocity, Variety (the original 3) + Veracity, Value (the added 2). This ordering is the **standard KTU answer structure**.

---

**Model Answer for (b) — Architecture Comparison (7 marks):**

**Conventional Data Warehouse Architecture (3 marks):**

The traditional EDW architecture consists of:
1. **Source Systems** (ERP, CRM, OLTP databases) — produce structured transactional data.
2. **ETL Layer** — Extracts, transforms, and loads data in scheduled batches (typically nightly).
3. **Staging Area** — temporary holding zone for raw data.
4. **Data Warehouse (RDBMS)** — stores cleaned, integrated, historical data in star/snowflake schemas.
5. **OLAP Cube / Data Marts** — pre-aggregated views for fast analytical queries.
6. **BI Tools** — generate reports, dashboards, and ad-hoc queries.

**Big Data (Hadoop) Architecture (3 marks):**

The Hadoop ecosystem architecture consists of:
1. **Ingestion Layer** (Flume, Kafka, Sqoop) — captures data from diverse sources (streaming and batch).
2. **Distributed Storage (HDFS)** — stores data across commodity nodes with **3× replication** for fault tolerance.
3. **Processing Layer** (MapReduce, Spark, Tez) — performs parallel computation.
4. **Analytics Layer** (Hive, Pig, Impala) — SQL-like query interfaces over distributed data.
5. **Machine Learning Layer** (Mahout, MLlib) — predictive analytics and pattern mining.
6. **Visualization Layer** (Tableau, D3.js) — presents insights to end users.

**Five Key Differences (1 mark – 0.2 × 5):**

| # | Aspect | Conventional | Big Data |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Storage | Centralized RDBMS | Distributed HDFS (commodity nodes) |
| 2 | Processing | Batch (ETL, OLAP) | Batch + Real-time (Spark Streaming) |
| 3 | Scalability | Vertical (scale-up) | Horizontal (scale-out) |
| 4 | Data Types | Structured only | Structured + Semi + Unstructured |
| 5 | Fault Tolerance | Manual backup | Built-in replication (3× default) |

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified Model Paper]**
**(a)** Define **Conventional Data** and **Big Data**. List any **five limitations** of Conventional Data systems that necessitate the shift to Big Data. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A company has **500 GB of data** today, growing at a rate of **80% per year**. Calculate the data size after **6 years** and determine whether the company must migrate to a Big Data infrastructure (threshold = 1 TB). Also compute the **physical storage required** in a Hadoop cluster with replication factor 3 and 15% overhead. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer for (a) — Definitions & Limitations (7 marks):**

**Definitions (2 marks — 1 + 1):**

**Conventional Data (1 mark):**
Conventional data is structured data that is stored, managed, and processed using traditional Relational Database Management Systems (RDBMS) such as Oracle, MySQL, or SQL Server. It typically ranges from **Megabytes to a few Terabytes** and follows a **predefined schema (Schema-on-Write)**.

**Big Data (1 mark):**
Big Data is a term for datasets that are so large, fast, or complex that traditional data processing applications are inadequate to handle them. It is characterized by the **5 V's — Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, and Value** — and is typically processed using distributed frameworks like **Hadoop, Spark, and NoSQL databases**.

**Five Limitations of Conventional Data Systems (5 marks — 1 × 5):**

1. **Limited Scalability (Vertical-Only):** Conventional RDBMS rely on a single powerful server. Scaling requires upgrading CPU, RAM, and disk on the same machine, which has a **physical and economic ceiling**. Beyond a certain point, costs grow exponentially while performance gains are marginal.

2. **Inability to Handle Unstructured Data:** Traditional databases are designed for structured tabular data. They cannot natively store or process images, videos, audio, social media posts, or sensor data — which constitute the **majority (>80%) of today's data**.

3. **High Cost of Ownership:** Enterprise RDBMS licenses (Oracle, IBM DB2) and high-end server hardware cost **hundreds of thousands to millions of dollars**. This CapEx-heavy model is not feasible for start-ups or for storing rapidly growing data.

4. **Slow Batch Processing:** Conventional systems process data in scheduled batches (hourly, daily, weekly). They cannot deliver **real-time insights**, which are critical for use cases like fraud detection, stock trading, and IoT monitoring.

5. **Single Point of Failure:** A conventional system running on a single server (or even an active-passive cluster) has inherent reliability issues. Hardware failure can cause **downtime and data loss** unless expensive backup/restore procedures are followed. Big Data systems use **built-in replication (default 3× in HDFS)** to overcome this.

6. *(Bonus point)* **Schema Rigidity:** Schema-on-Write forces the structure to be defined before data is loaded. Any change to the data format requires **costly schema migration** and downtime.

---

**Model Answer for (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Initial data: $D_0 = 500 \text{ GB}$
- Annual growth rate: $r = 0.80$ (80%)
- Time period: $n = 6$ years
- Big Data threshold: 1 TB = 1024 GB (using 1 TB ≈ 1000 GB for simplicity, as per KTU convention)
- Replication factor: $R_{rep} = 3$
- Overhead: $O_h = 0.15$ (15%)

**Step 1: Calculate data size after 6 years using compound growth formula (3 marks):**

$$
D(n) = D_0 \cdot (1 + r)^{n}
$$

$$
D(6) = 500 \cdot (1 + 0.80)^{6} \text{ GB}
$$

$$
D(6) = 500 \cdot (1.8)^{6} \text{ GB}
$$

Step-by-step evaluation of $(1.8)^6$:

$$
(1.8)^{2} = 3.24
$$

$$
(1.8)^{3} = 3.24 \cdot 1.8 = 5.832
$$

$$
(1.8)^{6} = (5.832)^{2} = 34.012224
$$

Therefore:

$$
D(6) = 500 \cdot 34.012224 \text{ GB} = 17006.11 \text{ GB} \approx 17.01 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 2: Compare with Big Data threshold (1 mark):**

Since $D(6) = 17.01 \text{ TB} \gg 1 \text{ TB}$, the data has **vastly exceeded** the Big Data threshold. **Migration to a Big Data infrastructure is mandatory.**

**Step 3: Calculate physical storage required in Hadoop (3 marks):**

$$
S_b = V \cdot R_{rep} \cdot (1 + O_h)
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
S_b = 17.01 \cdot 3 \cdot (1 + 0.15) \text{ TB}
$$

$$
S_b = 17.01 \cdot 3 \cdot 1.15 \text{ TB}
$$

$$
S_b = 51.03 \cdot 1.15 \text{ TB}
$$

$$
S_b = 58.68 \text{ TB}
$$

**Final Answer:**

- Data after 6 years ≈ **17.01 TB**
- Migration to Big Data is **necessary** (data has grown ~34×)
- Physical storage in Hadoop ≈ **58.68 TB** (with 3× replication and 15% overhead)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the (1 + r) inside the parenthesis** — many students write $(r)^n$ instead of $(1+r)^n$, causing massive numerical errors.
> 2. **Unit confusion** — the problem states data in GB, threshold in TB; always convert before comparing.
> 3. **Skipping intermediate steps of $(1.8)^6$** — KTU examiners award step marks; always show the squaring/cubing.
> 4. **Forgetting the overhead factor $(1 + O_h)$** in the storage formula — this costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Not concluding with a clear "Yes/No" migration decision** — a 14-mark answer must end with an explicit recommendation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Save this section for last-minute revision before KTU exams!**

**Core Definitions:**
- **Conventional Data:** Structured data in RDBMS, < few TB, Schema-on-Write, batch processing, vertical scaling.
- **Big Data:** Datasets exceeding traditional processing capability, characterized by the **5 V's**, processed via distributed frameworks.

**The 5 V's (Memorize Order — Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value):**
- **Volume** — size of data (TB → PB → EB)
- **Velocity** — speed of generation/processing (real-time streams)
- **Variety** — diversity of formats (structured, semi-structured, unstructured)
- **Veracity** — quality and trustworthiness
- **Value** — business worth extracted

**Architectural Pillars:**
- Conventional: Centralized RDBMS, ETL, OLAP cubes, ACID, schema-on-write.
- Big Data: Distributed HDFS, MapReduce/Spark, BASE, schema-on-read, replication (3× default).

**Key Formulas (Must Memorize):**
- Compound growth: $D(n) = D_0 \cdot (1+r)^{n}$
- Big Data storage: $S_b = V \cdot R_{rep} \cdot (1 + O_h)$
- Processing time (conventional): $T_c = D / R_{single}$
- Processing time (Big Data): $T_b = D / (N \cdot R_{node})$
- Speed-up factor: $S = T_c / T_b \approx N$ (linear, with overhead in practice)

**Numerical Threshold:**
- A dataset is classified as "Big Data" when it **exceeds 1 TB** (KTU standard).
- Data growth of **~60–80% per year** is typical for enterprises entering the Big Data regime within 5 years.

**Comparison Mantras for Exam:**
- Conventional = "**One big server**" vs. Big Data = "**Many small servers**"
- Conventional = "**Schema first, data later**" vs. Big Data = "**Data first, schema when querying**"
- Conventional = "**ACID (strict)**" vs. Big Data = "**BASE (eventual consistency)**"
- Conventional = "**Vertical (scale-up)**" vs. Big Data = "**Horizontal (scale-out)**"

**Common KTU 14-Mark Question Patterns:**
1. Explain the 5 V's with examples (7 marks) + Compare architectures with diagram (7 marks).
2. Define both + List limitations of conventional (7 marks) + Solve numerical on data growth and Hadoop storage (7 marks).
3. Differentiate conventional vs. big data across 8–10 parameters (14 marks) — use a comprehensive table.

**Key Examples to Quote in Exams:**
- Google: ~20 PB/day processed
- Facebook: ~4 PB/day generated, 300+ PB total
- Twitter: ~500 million tweets/day
- CERN LHC: ~1 PB/second during experiments
- Walmart: 2.5 PB of customer data analyzed

**Final Exam Tip:** Always include a **labeled diagram (block-level architecture)** in 14-mark answers — it guarantees **2–3 extra marks** even if the written explanation is incomplete.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Big Data Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT746 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Big Data Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Big Data Architecture** is a distributed, scalable, and fault-tolerant computational blueprint designed to ingest, store, process, analyze, and visualize massive volumes of heterogeneous data that exceed the capacity of traditional single-node database systems.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme PECNT746 syllabus**, Big Data Architecture encompasses the logical organization of:
- **Data Sources** (structured, semi-structured, unstructured)
- **Ingestion Layer** (batch/stream collectors)
- **Storage Layer** (distributed file systems, NoSQL stores, data lakes)
- **Processing Layer** (batch and real-time engines)
- **Analytics & Visualization Layer** (BI tools, dashboards)
- **Orchestration & Governance Layer** (metadata, monitoring, security)

### 1.2 The 5 V's of Big Data (KTU Board-Exam Favorite)

> [!NOTE]
> The **5 V's** framework is mandatory KTU board content. Examiners routinely test this for **3-mark short answers**.

| Dimension | Definition | Standard Metric |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Volume** | Scale of data generated | **Petabytes (PB), Exabytes (EB), Zettabytes (ZB)** |
| **Velocity** | Speed of data generation and movement | **MB/s, GB/s, ms-latency** |
| **Variety** | Different data formats | Structured, semi-structured, unstructured |
| **Veracity** | Trustworthiness/quality of data | Confidence score, error rate (%) |
| **Value** | Business insight extracted | ROI, KPI improvement |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart City Water Grid" Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy:** Imagine Big Data Architecture as a **municipal water supply system** of a smart city.
> - **Data Sources** = Rivers, lakes, rainwater (raw input from households, sensors, industries)
> - **Ingestion Layer** = Collection tanks & intake pumps
> - **Storage Layer** = Underground reservoirs and treatment plants
> - **Processing Layer** = Filtration and chemical treatment units
> - **Analytics Layer** = Smart meters in homes that show usage analytics
> - **Orchestration** = The municipal control room monitoring pressure, leaks, and quality
>
> Just as the water grid must handle **varied flow rates** (showers vs. dry season), handle **different water qualities** (clean vs. muddy), and **scale with population growth**, Big Data Architecture must handle diverse data at high speeds with elastic scalability.

### 1.4 Why Traditional RDBMS Fails — The Architectural Inflection Point

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU question: *"Why can't traditional databases handle Big Data?"* The answer must hit these three points: **vertical scaling ceiling, ACID overhead at scale, and lack of horizontal partitioning**.

**Traditional Systems Limitation Curve:**
- Single-server CPU bound
- Storage bound by disk size
- Network bound at the choke point

**Big Data Architectural Response:**
- Horizontal scaling (add commodity nodes)
- Distributed file systems (HDFS, S3)
- Eventual consistency (BASE vs. ACID)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five-Layer Big Data Architecture (Reference Model)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **canonical KTU-recommended architecture** derived from NIST SP 1500-03 and the syllabus. Examiners expect students to name and explain each layer.

**Layer 1 — Data Sources Layer**
- **Structured**: RDBMS, OLTP systems (rows/columns)
- **Semi-Structured**: JSON, XML, CSV, logs
- **Unstructured**: Images, video, audio, social media posts
- **Streaming Sources**: IoT sensors, clickstreams, server logs (Kafka topics)

**Layer 2 — Data Ingestion Layer**
- **Batch Ingestion**: Sqoop, Flume, SFTP, scheduled ETL jobs
- **Stream Ingestion**: Apache Kafka, Apache Pulsar, Amazon Kinesis, RabbitMQ
- **Hybrid Ingestion**: NiFi for complex event flows

**Layer 3 — Data Storage Layer**
- **Distributed File Systems**: HDFS, Amazon S3, Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2
- **NoSQL Stores**: HBase (columnar), Cassandra (wide-column), MongoDB (document), Neo4j (graph)
- **Data Lakes**: Raw storage in native format (parquet, ORC, Avro)
- **Data Warehouses**: Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift (for analytical queries)

**Layer 4 — Data Processing Layer**
- **Batch Processing**: Apache Hadoop MapReduce, Apache Spark (batch mode)
- **Stream Processing**: Apache Flink, Apache Spark Streaming, Apache Storm
- **Hybrid Engines**: Apache Spark Structured Streaming, Apache Beam
- **Query Engines**: Apache Hive, Presto, Trino, Apache Impala

**Layer 5 — Analytics & Visualization Layer**
- **Analytics**: Machine Learning (Spark MLlib, TensorFlow on Spark), SQL-on-Hadoop
- **Visualization**: Tableau, Power BI, Grafana, Kibana
- **Reporting**: Scheduled report generation, KPI dashboards

**Cross-Cutting Layer — Orchestration, Governance & Security**
- **Orchestration**: Apache Airflow, Oozie, Kubernetes
- **Monitoring**: Prometheus, Grafana, Ambari
- **Security**: Kerberos, Apache Ranger, encryption-at-rest/in-transit
- **Metadata**: Apache Atlas, DataHub

### 2.2 The Lambda Architecture (Nathan Marz, 2011)

> [!NOTE]
> **Lambda Architecture** is a **frequently tested 14-mark KTU question**. Master this block.

Lambda Architecture splits processing into three parallel paths:

1. **Batch Layer (Cold Path)**
   - Stores **all incoming raw data** in master dataset
   - Runs batch computations on complete dataset
   - Produces **batch views** (high-latency, highly accurate)
   - Engine: Hadoop MapReduce, Spark Batch

2. **Speed Layer (Hot Path)**
   - Processes **only recent data** in real-time
   - Compensates for batch layer latency
   - Produces **real-time views** (low-latency, approximate)
   - Engine: Apache Storm, Flink, Spark Streaming

3. **Serving Layer**
   - Merges batch views and real-time views
   - Exposes merged data for low-latency queries
   - Engine: Apache Cassandra, HBase, Druid, Elasticsearch

**Mathematical Formulation (Marz's Equations):**
$$Q = \text{merge}(B, S)$$
where $Q$ is the query result, $B$ is the batch view, and $S$ is the speed layer view.

The complete dataset (master) is represented as:
$$M = \{x_1, x_2, x_3, \dots, x_n\}$$
where each $x_i$ is an immutable data point.

A precomputed view function is:
$$V = \text{function}(M, t)$$
where $t$ is the time of computation.

### 2.3 The Kappa Architecture (Jay Kreps, 2014)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kappa Architecture** simplifies Lambda by removing the batch layer entirely. ALL data flows through a **single stream processing engine**.

**Key Principle:** Treat batch as a special case of streaming. Store data in an **unbounded, replayable log** (Kafka) and rerun the stream processor from the beginning when logic changes.

**Components:**
- **Stream Ingestion**: Kafka (immutable, ordered, replayable log)
- **Stream Processing**: Apache Flink, Kafka Streams
- **Serving Layer**: Same NoSQL stores as Lambda

**Mathematical Equivalence:**
$$\text{Stream Processing} \equiv \text{Batch Processing (when windowed)}$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Big Data has fewer "formulas" than core engineering, but these **capacity & scaling equations** are exam-favorite numerical problems.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $S_{\text{total}} = D \times t \times R$ | Total storage needed (Data × Time × Replication) | TB, PB |
| 2 | $D(t) = D_0 \times (1 + r)^t$ | Data growth (compound) | TB, PB |
| 3 | $S_{\text{cluster}} = N \times C_{\text{node}}$ | Cluster storage capacity | TB |
| 4 | $T = \dfrac{D_{\text{size}}}{B_{\text{bandwidth}}}$ | Data transfer time | seconds |
| 5 | $S_p = \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$ | Amdahl's speedup (parallel fraction $P$, $N$ nodes) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $R_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{R_{\text{raw}}}{C_r}$ | Effective storage after compression ratio $C_r$ | TB |
| 7 | $\text{CAP Theorem: pick 2 of}\{C, A, P\}$ | Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance | trade-off |
| 8 | $\text{Latency}_{\text{E2E}} = L_{\text{ingest}} + L_{\text{process}} + L_{\text{query}}$ | End-to-end latency budget | ms, s |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for CAP Theorem:** "**CA**fe **P**arty" → In distributed systems, you cannot serve Coffee (Consistency) and Avocado-toast (Availability) during a Partition. You must sacrifice one.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare**: Genomic sequencing data (PB-scale) — HDFS + Spark architecture
- **Finance**: Real-time fraud detection — Kappa architecture with Flink
- **E-commerce**: Recommendation engines — Lambda architecture
- **IoT/Smart Cities**: Sensor telemetry — Kappa/Kafka Streams
- **Social Media Analytics**: Twitter firehose — Lambda with HBase serving

### 2.6 Comparison Matrix: Lambda vs. Kappa (Board-Favorite Table)

| Parameter | Lambda Architecture | Kappa Architecture |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Layers** | 3 (Batch + Speed + Serving) | 2 (Stream + Serving) |
| **Complexity** | High (two code paths) | Low (single code path) |
| **Latency** | Minutes to hours (batch) + ms (speed) | ms to seconds (single path) |
| **Reprocessing** | Re-run batch | Replay Kafka log from offset 0 |
| **Storage Cost** | High (master dataset + intermediate) | Lower (only log retention) |
| **Use Case** | Mixed batch+real-time analytics | Pure real-time/event-driven |
| **Tool Stack** | Hadoop + Storm + HBase | Kafka + Flink + Cassandra |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cluster Storage Capacity (Raw + Replication)

**Problem:** A KTU Big Data cluster stores **2 PB of raw data** with a **replication factor of 3** (HDFS default). What is the **total physical disk capacity** required? If the compression ratio achieved is **1.8:1**, what is the **effective usable storage**?

**Step 1 — Identify the variables.**
- $D_{\text{raw}} = 2 \text{ PB}$
- $R = 3$ (replication factor)
- $C_r = 1.8$ (compression ratio)

**Step 2 — Apply the storage equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{physical}} &= D_{\text{raw}} \times R \\[4pt]
&= 2 \text{ PB} \times 3 \\[4pt]
&= 6 \text{ PB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** HDFS stores every block on 3 different DataNodes (default $R=3$) to ensure fault tolerance. So 2 PB of logical data consumes 6 PB of physical disk.

**Step 3 — Apply compression to compute effective usable storage.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{effective}} &= \dfrac{S_{\text{physical}}}{C_r} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{6 \text{ PB}}{1.8} \\[4pt]
&= 3.33 \text{ PB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The cluster needs **6 PB of physical disks** to safely host 2 PB of data, which after 1.8:1 compression reduces to **3.33 PB of logical data**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Data Transfer Time Calculation

**Problem:** A daily log file of **500 GB** must be transferred across an inter-node link of bandwidth **1 Gbps**. Compute the **transfer time in seconds and minutes**. If we use **4 parallel links (shuffle)**, what is the new transfer time?

**Step 1 — Convert units consistently.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 \text{ Gbps} &= 10^9 \text{ bits/second} \\[4pt]
500 \text{ GB} &= 500 \times 8 \times 10^9 \text{ bits} = 4 \times 10^{12} \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute serial transfer time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{serial}} &= \dfrac{D_{\text{size}}}{B_{\text{bandwidth}}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{4 \times 10^{12}}{10^9} \\[4pt]
&= 4000 \text{ seconds} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{4000}{60} \approx 66.67 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply 4-way parallel sharding.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{parallel}} &= \dfrac{T_{\text{serial}}}{N_{\text{links}}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{4000}{4} \\[4pt]
&= 1000 \text{ seconds} \approx 16.67 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** With 4 parallel links, transfer time drops from **66.67 min to 16.67 min** (a 4× speedup, ideal case).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Amdahl's Law for Hadoop Speedup

**Problem:** A Spark job has **85% parallelizable code** ($P = 0.85$) and runs on a cluster of **16 nodes**. What is the **maximum achievable speedup**? How does adding more nodes to 64 affect it?

**Step 1 — Apply Amdahl's Law.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_p &= \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{(1-0.85) + \dfrac{0.85}{16}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{0.15 + 0.053125} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{0.203125} \\[4pt]
&\approx 4.92
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — For 64 nodes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_p &= \dfrac{1}{0.15 + \dfrac{0.85}{64}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{0.15 + 0.01328} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{0.16328} \approx 6.12
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the maximum theoretical speedup (infinite nodes).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\max} &= \lim_{N \to \infty} S_p = \dfrac{1}{1-P} = \dfrac{1}{0.15} \approx 6.67
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Even with infinite nodes, the speedup is **capped at 6.67×** because the 15% serial portion becomes the bottleneck. This is the **famous Amdahl's ceiling** and the reason why Big Data architectures must minimize serial code paths.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Data Growth Projection (Compound)

**Problem:** A hospital's patient records grow at **30% per year** from a baseline of **10 TB**. Project the storage for **5 years** if replication factor is 2 and compression ratio is 2:1.

**Step 1 — Compute raw data growth.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D(t) &= D_0 \times (1+r)^t \\[4pt]
D(5) &= 10 \times (1.3)^5 \\[4pt]
&= 10 \times 3.71293 \\[4pt]
&\approx 37.13 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply replication factor.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{physical}} &= 37.13 \times 2 = 74.26 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply compression.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{usable}} &= \dfrac{74.26}{2} = 37.13 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** After 5 years, the hospital's usable data equals its raw data (compression offsets replication).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Component Selection by Use Case

| Use Case | Ingestion | Storage | Processing | Serving | Architecture |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Daily Sales ETL** | Sqoop | HDFS | Spark Batch | Hive | Lambda (batch-only) |
| **Fraud Detection** | Kafka | Cassandra | Flink | Elasticsearch | Kappa |
| **Log Search** | Flume | HDFS | Spark | Elasticsearch | Lambda |
| **IoT Telemetry** | Kafka | HDFS + HBase | Spark Streaming | Druid | Hybrid |
| **Genomics** | SFTP | S3 | Spark Batch | Parquet + Presto | Lambda (batch-heavy) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.2 Lambda Architecture — Detailed Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    MSG --> BATCH_STORE[MASTER DATASET HDFS IMMUTABLE RAW]

    BATCH_STORE --> BATCH_PROC[BATCH PROCESSOR HADOOP SPARK RECOMPUTES PERIODICALLY]
```
