1. **Misalignment Cost:** Studies by IBM and Gartner estimate that **80% of analytics projects fail** due to poor problem definition, not technical shortcomings. A well-defined problem prevents wasted engineering effort.

2. **KPI Anchoring:** Without KPIs, model accuracy is meaningless. A model with 99% accuracy is useless if it does not improve the business metric it was designed to optimize.

3. **Resource Justification:** Cloud infrastructure costs (AWS, Azure) can balloon to millions of rupees monthly. Phase 1 produces the **business case** that justifies these costs to the CFO.

4. **Ethical and Legal Guardrails:** GDPR, India's Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA) 2023, and HIPAA impose strict boundaries. Defining the problem includes scoping the legal use of data, preventing costly compliance violations.

5. **Stakeholder Buy-in:** Phase 1 ensures that business sponsors, data engineers, ML scientists, and operations teams share a common understanding, reducing downstream rework.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Drawing the life cycle as a *purely linear* pipeline without the iterative feedback loop. KTU 2024 Scheme **explicitly** tests for the iterative nature. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Conflating the Big Data Analytics Life Cycle with the older **CRISP-DM** (Cross-Industry Standard Process for Data Mining) model. While similar, they are **not identical** — Big Data adds stream processing, NoSQL, and data lake concepts. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Failing to justify *why* Phase 1 is critical beyond generic statements like "it is important." Students must provide **quantitative or KPI-based reasoning** for full marks.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** In derivation questions, students write the formula but **skip algebraic substitution steps**. The KTU Board Examiner **requires all intermediate steps** for the 7-mark sub-question. **Loss: 2–3 marks per skipped step.**
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Confusing **Z-score standardization** with **Min-Max normalization**. These are distinct transformations with different output ranges and use cases. Examiners will deduct 1 mark if used interchangeably.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU 2024 Board Exam**

- [ ] The Big Data Analytics Life Cycle has **exactly six phases** in the KTU 2024 Scheme. Memorize them in order: *Business → Discovery → Preparation → Storage → Analysis → Deployment*.
- [ ] The 5 V's are: **Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value**. Some texts add a 6th V: **Visualization**.
- [ ] **Phase 3 (Data Preparation)** consumes **60–80%** of total project time. This is the most effort-intensive phase.
- [ ] **Schema-on-Read** is the Big Data paradigm; **Schema-on-Write** is the traditional RDBMS paradigm.
- [ ] **HDFS default block size:** 128 MB (older versions) or 256 MB (newer). **Default replication factor:** 3.
- [ ] **Apache Spark** is **in-memory**, while **Hadoop MapReduce** is **disk-based** — Spark is 10–100× faster for iterative jobs.
- [ ] **Min-Max Normalization** output range: $[0, 1]$. **Z-score Standardization** output: zero mean, unit variance.
- [ ] **Amdahl's Law** governs the theoretical maximum speedup of a parallel system. A 10% serial bottleneck severely limits scaling beyond 10× even with infinite nodes.
- [ ] **Descriptive, Diagnostic, Predictive, Prescriptive** are the four levels of analytics — memorize in increasing order of business value.
- [ ] **ETL** (Extract-Transform-Load) is traditional; **ELT** (Extract-Load-Transform) is the Big Data Data Lake pattern.
- [ ] The life cycle is **iterative**, not linear — feedback loops from deployment back to problem definition are mandatory in KTU 2024 answers.
- [ ] **Model drift** and **data drift** monitoring are essential in Phase 6 to maintain production model accuracy.
- [ ] Tools map: Ingestion → Kafka/Sqoop. Storage → HDFS/S3/Cassandra. Processing → Spark/Flink/MapReduce. Serving → Tableau/Power BI/REST APIs.
- [ ] In Part B derivations, **always show all algebraic steps**. The KTU Board examiner allocates marks for *transitions*, not just final answers.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 1 Notes — Big Data Analytics Life Cycle.**
> Continue to **Module 2: Hadoop Ecosystem and HDFS Architecture** for the next set of high-yield KTU 2024 notes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Stream Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT746 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Stream Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Big Data Analytics**, a **data stream** is a continuous, unbounded, ordered sequence of data elements that are generated, transmitted, and processed in real-time (or near real-time) rather than being stored first and analyzed later. Each element in the stream is associated with a **timestamp** and arrives at high velocity, often in the order of thousands to millions of records per second.

Formally, a data stream $\mathcal{S}$ can be represented as:

$$\mathcal{S} = \{e_1, e_2, e_3, \ldots, e_t, \ldots\}$$

where each element $e_t$ is a tuple $(t, v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_k)$ containing the arrival time $t$ and a set of $k$ attribute values.

**Stream Processing** is the paradigm of computing that operates on these continuously flowing data items, performing actions like filtering, aggregation, pattern detection, and transformation *as the data arrives*, with strict constraints on **latency**, **memory**, and **throughput**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECNT746 Module 1):**
> Students must be able to distinguish between *batch* and *stream* processing paradigms, identify real-world sources of streaming data, and articulate the architectural challenges of streaming systems. This forms the foundational base for later modules covering **Apache Kafka**, **Apache Flink**, and **Spark Streaming**.

### Key Terminology in Stream Concepts

| Term | Formal Definition |
|------|------------------|
| **Stream** | An unbounded, continuous flow of data records |
| **Event** | A single indivisible data point in a stream |
| **Tuple** | A structured record carrying one or more attribute values |
| **Window** | A finite slice of an infinite stream used for bounded computation |
| **Throughput** | Number of events processed per unit time (events/sec) |
| **Latency** | Time delay between event arrival and result emission |
| **Backpressure** | Mechanism to handle situations when consumers are slower than producers |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Garden Hose vs. The Bucket

Imagine two ways of watering a garden:

- **Batch Processing (The Bucket)**: You fill a large bucket with water, carry it to the garden, and then pour it all at once. Before you water, all the water *must be collected*. You cannot react to dry spots until the bucket is full.

- **Stream Processing (The Garden Hose)**: You connect a hose directly to a tap. Water flows *continuously*, and the moment it leaves the tap, you can direct it, filter it, split it, or stop it — all in real time. You don't need to "store first, act later."

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Recap:** A stream is like a *hose of data* — it never stops, and you have to make decisions on the fly. A batch is like a *bucket* — you wait until it's full before doing anything. Big Data streams are simply "hoses" that fire millions of droplets per second.

### Why Streams Matter — The 5 V's Connection

Traditional Big Data was characterized by the **3 V's**: Volume, Velocity, Variety. With streams, **Velocity** becomes the dominant property:

- **Volume**: Streams are *unbounded* — total data over time can be petabytes.
- **Velocity**: Data arrives at gigabits per second (e.g., stock ticks, IoT sensors).
- **Variety**: Streams are often semi-structured or unstructured (logs, JSON, audio, video).
- **Veracity**: Stream data is noisy and must be cleaned on-the-fly.
- **Value**: Real-time insights (e.g., fraud detection) have far higher business value than delayed ones.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Examples of Streams:**
> - **Twitter/X firehose**: ~500 million tweets per day
> - **Stock exchanges**: NYSE handles millions of orders per second
> - **IoT sensor networks**: Smart cities, industrial SCADA systems
> - **Clickstreams**: User activity on e-commerce websites
> - **Network logs**: Routers, firewalls, and intrusion detection systems

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Stream Requirements

For a system to be called a true *stream processing system*, it must satisfy three constraints (the so-called **CMS Triad**):

1. **Continuous** — Processing happens without human intervention, 24/7.
2. **Unbounded** — There is no defined "end" to the data; computations are open-ended.
3. **Real-time (or near real-time)** — Output is produced within bounded latency (milliseconds to seconds).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Misconception:**
> "Stream processing is just fast batch processing." — **This is FALSE.**
> Stream processing operates on data *in motion*, while batch processing operates on data *at rest*. The algorithmic models are fundamentally different: a stream algorithm can take only *one pass* over the data, while a batch algorithm can iterate as many times as needed.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Characteristics That Define a Data Stream

A data stream is fundamentally different from a static dataset. The following properties are *mandatory* for any data source to be classified as a stream:

### 2.1.1 Unboundedness (Infinite Cardinality)
Unlike a relational table or a file, a stream has no predetermined end. The set $\mathcal{S}$ is not closed under cardinality, i.e.,

$$|\mathcal{S}| = \lim_{t \to \infty} \sum_{i=1}^{t} 1 = \infty$$

This means any algorithm that requires *loading the entire dataset* into memory is **fundamentally infeasible** for streams.

### 2.1.2 Single-Pass Constraint
Stream algorithms are allowed only **one sequential pass** over the data. This is formalized as:

$$\text{Passes}(\mathcal{S}) \leq 1$$

Random access to past elements is generally not available (or extremely expensive if buffered). Therefore, algorithms must use **synopsis data structures** (e.g., sketches, histograms, samples) that summarize past data in fixed memory.

### 2.1.3 High Data Velocity
Stream arrival rate is often characterized by $\lambda$ (lambda), the rate parameter of a Poisson process:

$$P(N(t) = k) = \frac{(\lambda t)^k e^{-\lambda t}}{k!}$$

where $N(t)$ is the number of events arriving in time $t$, and $\lambda$ is the average rate (events/second). A high $\lambda$ imposes tight time budgets per element:

$$\text{Time per element} = \frac{1}{\lambda} \text{ seconds}$$

### 2.1.4 Time-Varying Distribution (Concept Drift)
The statistical properties of a stream (mean, variance, distribution) change over time — a phenomenon known as **concept drift**. Formally, for two time slices $t_1 < t_2$:

$$P_{t_1}(X) \neq P_{t_2}(X)$$

This forces stream models to be **adaptive** and continuously updated, unlike static ML models trained once on a fixed dataset.

### 2.1.5 Heterogeneous and Noisy
Stream elements arrive in varied formats (JSON, XML, binary, free text) and frequently contain missing values, duplicates, or outliers. Cleaning must happen *in-line* with processing.

## 2.2 Stream Processing Architecture — Conceptual Model

A typical stream processing pipeline has the following stages:

1. **Ingestion Layer**: Sources (Kafka, MQTT, Kinesis) push events to the system.
2. **Buffering Layer**: Events are temporarily queued to absorb bursts.
3. **Processing Layer**: Operators (map, filter, aggregate, join) execute on events.
4. **State Layer**: Maintains intermediate results (counters, windows, caches).
5. **Sink Layer**: Outputs results to databases, dashboards, alerts, or downstream systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** When asked "Explain stream processing architecture," always begin with the *source → buffer → process → sink* flow. This is the canonical 4-stage model examiners expect.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Module 1 — Stream Concepts)

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variable Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|---------------------|------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Stream Cardinality | $\vert\mathcal{S}\vert = \infty$ | Infinite event count | Justifies bounded-memory algorithms |
| 2 | Poisson Arrival | $P(N(t)=k) = \frac{(\lambda t)^k e^{-\lambda t}}{k!}$ | $\lambda$ = rate, $k$ = count | Models random stream arrivals |
| 3 | Per-Element Time Budget | $T_{elem} = 1/\lambda$ | $\lambda$ in events/sec | Sets CPU constraint |
| 4 | Sliding Window Size | $W = \lvert w_{i} \rvert$ | Window length | Used in aggregations |
| 5 | Tumbling Window | $W_t = [t \cdot \Delta, (t+1) \cdot \Delta)$ | $\Delta$ = window duration | Non-overlapping windows |
| 6 | Sliding Window Step | $W_s = [i \cdot s, i \cdot s + W)$ | $s$ = slide interval | Overlapping windows |
| 7 | Throughput | $\Theta = \frac{N_{processed}}{T_{total}}$ | Events per second | System performance metric |
| 8 | Latency | $L = t_{output} - t_{input}$ | End-to-end delay | Real-time guarantee metric |
| 9 | Bloom Filter FPR | $FPR \approx \left(1 - e^{-kn/m}\right)^k$ | $m$ = bits, $n$ = items, $k$ = hashes | Membership testing in streams |
| 10 | Reservoir Sampling | $P(\text{keep } x_i) = \frac{s}{i}$ | $s$ = sample size, $i$ = index | Fixed-size sampling |
| 11 | AMS Estimator Error | $\epsilon \leq \frac{1}{\sqrt{k}}$ | $k$ = number of estimates | Variance of stream moments |
| 12 | Memory Bound (Generic) | $M = O(\text{polylog}(N))$ | $N$ = stream length | Sublinear space requirement |

## 2.4 Stream vs. Batch — Detailed Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner-Favorite Comparison Table** — Memorize this for KTU exams:

| Parameter | Batch Processing | Stream Processing |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------|
| Data Scope | Bounded, finite | Unbounded, infinite |
| Latency | Minutes to hours | Milliseconds to seconds |
| Storage | Required (HDFS, S3) | Optional (state stores) |
| Computation Model | Iterate as needed | Single pass |
| Algorithms | MapReduce, Spark | Flink, Kafka Streams |
| Use Cases | Monthly reports, ETL | Fraud detection, alerts |
| Output Frequency | Periodic | Continuous |
| Recomputation | Easy (data is stored) | Hard (data is gone) |
| Memory | Scales with data | Bounded (synopsis) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Stream concepts are not merely academic — they power:

- **Finance**: High-frequency trading algorithms, real-time risk scoring
- **Healthcare**: ICU patient monitoring, real-time anomaly detection in vitals
- **Telecommunications**: Call Detail Record (CDR) analysis, fraud detection
- **Smart Cities**: Traffic light optimization, pollution monitoring
- **E-commerce**: Personalized recommendations, dynamic pricing
- **Cybersecurity**: Intrusion detection systems (IDS) over network packet streams
- **Industrial IoT**: Predictive maintenance on factory equipment sensors

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for Big Data Analytics engineers:**
> A solid grasp of stream concepts allows the engineer to choose the *right* tool (batch vs. micro-batch vs. true streaming) for the *right* problem. A wrong choice can cost millions in cloud bills or missed SLAs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Per-Element Time Budget from Poisson Arrival Rate

**Problem:** Given a stream arriving at rate $\lambda = 50{,}000$ events per second, derive the maximum time available to process a single event so that the system keeps up with the input.

**Step 1 — State the given parameters**

$$\lambda = 50{,}000 \text{ events/second}$$

**Step 2 — Express total time available per second**

For the system to be real-time, it must process 50,000 events within 1 second.

$$T_{total} = 1 \text{ second}$$

**Step 3 — Compute per-element budget**

$$T_{elem} = \frac{T_{total}}{N_{events}} = \frac{1 \text{ s}}{50{,}000}$$

$$T_{elem} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 20 \, \mu s$$

**Step 4 — Generalize the formula**

$$T_{elem} = \frac{1}{\lambda} \text{ seconds per event}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result**

The processing pipeline has only **20 microseconds** per event. This justifies the use of in-memory data structures and rules out any disk-based operation in the critical path.

## 3.2 Derivation: Bloom Filter False Positive Rate (Stream Use Case)

**Problem:** A stream processor needs to check whether a URL has been seen in the last 10 minutes. Estimate the optimal hash function count $k$ for a Bloom filter with $m = 10^6$ bits and expected unique items $n = 10^5$.

**Step 1 — Recall the FPR formula**

$$FPR \approx \left(1 - e^{-kn/m}\right)^k$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values**

$$FPR(k) = \left(1 - e^{-k \cdot 10^5 / 10^6}\right)^k = \left(1 - e^{-k/10}\right)^k$$

**Step 3 — Find optimal $k$ analytically**

The FPR is minimized when:

$$k_{opt} = \frac{m}{n} \ln 2$$

**Step 4 — Compute**

$$k_{opt} = \frac{10^6}{10^5} \cdot \ln 2 = 10 \cdot 0.6931 \approx 6.93 \approx 7$$

**Step 5 — Compute minimum FPR**

$$FPR_{min} = (1 - e^{-0.7})^7 = (0.5034)^7 \approx 0.0081 \approx 0.81\%$$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation**

A 0.81% false positive rate is acceptable for URL deduplication in streaming crawlers, demonstrating how sublinear-space stream data structures are *engineered in practice*.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Sliding Window Computation

For a stream where the goal is to compute the **moving average** over the last $W$ events:

$$\bar{x}_t = \frac{1}{W} \sum_{i=t-W+1}^{t} x_i$$

**Naive computation cost per event:**

$$\text{Add} = W \text{ operations}, \quad \text{Divide} = 1 \text{ operation}$$

Total per event: $O(W)$.

**Incremental (efficient) update:**

$$\text{Sum}_t = \text{Sum}_{t-1} - x_{t-W} + x_t$$

$$\bar{x}_t = \frac{\text{Sum}_t}{W}$$

Total per event: $O(1)$.

**Comparison table:**

| Method | Time per Event | Memory | Use in Streams? |
|--------|---------------|--------|----------------|
| Naive recompute | $O(W)$ | $O(W)$ | ❌ Impractical |
| Incremental update | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | ✅ Ideal |
| Exponential decay | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | ✅ Adaptive |

## 3.4 Derivation: Reservoir Sampling Probability

To maintain a uniform random sample of size $s$ from an infinite stream:

- For the first $s$ elements: keep all of them.
- For the $i$-th element ($i > s$): keep it with probability $\frac{s}{i}$, replacing a randomly chosen slot.

**Proof of uniformity:** Probability that the $i$-th element is in the final reservoir of size $s$:

$$P(\text{keep at } i) \cdot P(\text{not replaced later})$$

$$= \frac{s}{i} \cdot \left(\prod_{j=i+1}^{N} \left(1 - \frac{1}{j}\right)\right) = \frac{s}{i} \cdot \frac{i}{N} = \frac{s}{N}$$

This is independent of $i$, confirming uniform sampling — a key stream primitive.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Stream Processing Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC1[IoT SENSORS] --> ING[INGESTION LAYER]
    SRC2[WEB CLICK STREAMS] --> ING
    SRC3[STOCK MARKET TICKS] --> ING
    ING --> BUF[BUFFERING LAYER KAFKA KINESIS]
    BUF --> PROC[PROCESSING LAYER FLINK SPARK]
    PROC --> ST[STATE STORE REDIS ROCKSDB]
    PROC --> SINK1[REAL TIME DASHBOARDS]
    PROC --> SINK2[ALERT NOTIFICATIONS]
    PROC --> SINK3[DATA WAREHOUSE]
    PROC -.BACKPRESSURE.-> BUF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The dashed feedback arrow represents *backpressure* — when downstream consumers cannot keep up, the system signals upstream to slow down, preventing memory overflow.

## 4.2 Stream Processing Pipeline — Operator Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STREAM SOURCE] --> B[OPERATOR MAP]
    B --> C[OPERATOR FILTER]
    C --> D[OPERATOR KEY BY]
    D --> E[OPERATOR WINDOW]
    E --> F[OPERATOR AGGREGATE]
    F --> G[OPERATOR JOIN]
    G --> H[SINK OUTPUT]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style H fill:#ffe1e1
```

## 4.3 Windowing Strategies — Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TUMBLING["TUMBLING WINDOWS NON OVERLAPPING"]
        T1[W1 0 TO 10] --> T2[W2 10 TO 20] --> T3[W3 20 TO 30]
    end
    subgraph SLIDING["SLIDING WINDOWS OVERLAPPING"]
        S1[W1 0 TO 10] --> S2[W2 5 TO 15] --> S3[W3 10 TO 20]
    end
    subgraph SESSION["SESSION WINDOWS DYNAMIC GAPS"]
        SS1[S1 ACTIVE TILL GAP G] --> SS2[S2 NEW ACTIVITY]
    end
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Batch vs. Stream

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BATCH["BATCH PARADIGM"]
        B1[COLLECT ALL DATA] --> B2[STORE IN DATA LAKE] --> B3[RUN JOB] --> B4[REPORT]
    end
    subgraph STREAM["STREAM PARADIGM"]
        S1[EVENT ARRIVES] --> S2[PROCESS IMMEDIATELY] --> S3[UPDATE STATE] --> S4[EMIT RESULT]
    end
    style BATCH fill:#fff4e1
    style STREAM fill:#e1ffe1
```

## 4.5 Time Semantics in Stream Processing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EVENT_TIME["EVENT TIME WHEN DATA WAS CREATED"]
        E1[IN SENSOR AT 10 00 00]
    end
    subgraph INGESTION_TIME["INGESTION TIME WHEN SYSTEM RECEIVED IT"]
        I1[RECEIVED AT 10 00 05]
    end
    subgraph PROCESSING_TIME["PROCESSING TIME WHEN OPERATOR RUNS"]
        P1[PROCESSED AT 10 00 07]
    end
    EVENT_TIME --> INGESTION_TIME --> PROCESSING_TIME
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Stream systems support three notions of time. *Event time* is critical for accurate windowing when data arrives out of order. A robust stream engine (like Flink) handles late events via *watermarks*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]
**Define a data stream. List any four characteristics that distinguish it from a static dataset.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A data stream is an *unbounded, continuous, and ordered* sequence of data elements that arrive at high velocity and must be processed in real-time or near real-time.

Four distinguishing characteristics:
1. **Unboundedness** — The stream has no defined end; $|\mathcal{S}| = \infty$.
2. **Single-pass constraint** — Algorithms can read each element only once.
3. **High velocity** — Arrival rates may reach millions of events per second.
4. **Concept drift** — Statistical properties of data change over time.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for characteristics — ½ mark each)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]
**Differentiate between batch processing and stream processing in terms of latency, data scope, and recomputation capability.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Batch Processing | Stream Processing |
|-----------|----------------|-------------------|
| Latency | High (minutes–hours) | Low (milliseconds–seconds) |
| Data Scope | Bounded, finite | Unbounded, infinite |
| Recomputation | Easy (data persisted) | Hard (data not stored) |

*(1 mark per row)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the architectural components of a stream processing system with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **ingestion layer** and **state store** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A real-time analytics system receives stock trade events at a Poisson rate of $\lambda = 20{,}000$ events/second. The system uses a Bloom filter with $m = 2 \times 10^6$ bits and $n = 5 \times 10^5$ unique tickers. Calculate:
   (i) The per-element processing time budget.
   (ii) The optimal number of hash functions.
   (iii) The minimum false positive rate. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The stream processing architecture has the following blocks:

1. **Ingestion Layer (2 marks):**
   - Acts as the entry point for all streaming data.
   - Tools: Apache Kafka, AWS Kinesis, MQTT brokers.
   - Responsibilities: Decoupling producers from consumers, providing durability, supporting backpressure, and ordering guarantees.

2. **Buffering Layer (1 mark):**
   - Absorbs traffic bursts and decouples ingestion from processing.
   - Examples: Kafka topics, Pulsar.

3. **Processing Layer (2 marks):**
   - Contains operators (map, filter, aggregate, window, join).
   - Engines: Apache Flink, Spark Streaming, Kafka Streams.
   - Runs in a distributed, fault-tolerant manner.

4. **State Store (1 mark):**
   - Maintains intermediate computations (counters, windows, machine learning models).
   - Backends: RocksDB, Redis, Cassandra.
   - Must support fast reads/writes because state is accessed per event.

5. **Sink Layer (1 mark):**
   - Outputs results to dashboards, alerts, databases, or further pipelines.

*(1 mark for neat block diagram, 6 marks for explanation — distribute as above.)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Per-element time budget (2 marks):**

$$T_{elem} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{20{,}000} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 50 \, \mu s$$

**(ii) Optimal number of hash functions (2 marks):**

$$k_{opt} = \frac{m}{n} \ln 2 = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{5 \times 10^5} \cdot 0.6931$$

$$k_{opt} = 4 \cdot 0.6931 \approx 2.77 \approx 3$$

**(iii) Minimum false positive rate (3 marks):**

$$FPR_{min} = \left(1 - e^{-kn/m}\right)^k = \left(1 - e^{-(3)(5 \times 10^5)/(2 \times 10^6)}\right)^3$$

$$= \left(1 - e^{-0.75}\right)^3 = \left(1 - 0.4724\right)^3 = (0.5276)^3 \approx 0.1469 \approx 14.7\%$$

**Interpretation:** A 14.7% FPR is too high; in practice, $m$ would be increased to lower it.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Discuss the concept of **concept drift** in data streams. Explain with examples why static machine learning models fail in streaming contexts, and outline any two adaptive strategies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the three types of **time semantics** in stream processing. Why is *event time* preferred over *processing time* for accurate windowed aggregations? Illustrate with a scenario involving late-arriving data. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution Outline

**Part (a) — Concept Drift (7 marks):**
- Definition: Change in $P(X, y)$ over time (3 marks).
- Types: sudden, gradual, incremental, recurring (2 marks).
- Examples: Customer buying behavior, weather patterns (1 mark).
- Mitigation: Sliding window retraining, ADWIN, ensemble models (1 mark).

**Part (b) — Time Semantics (7 marks):**
- Event time, ingestion time, processing time (3 marks).
- Event time is application-logic-driven and stable; processing time depends on system load (2 marks).
- Scenario: A mobile app offline-collects events, then uploads them — they arrive hours late, and processing time would place them in wrong windows (2 marks).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Avoid vague answers:** "Stream is fast data" → 0 marks. Use *unbounded*, *single-pass*, *real-time* keywords.
> - **Do not confuse throughput with latency:** Throughput is *rate* (events/sec); latency is *delay* (seconds). Examiners explicitly test this.
> - **Do not skip diagrams in architecture questions:** A question worth 7 marks on architecture will lose you 1–2 marks if you omit a block diagram.
> - **Units are mandatory:** Always write $\mu s$, $ms$, events/sec. Numerical answers without units get partial credit.
> - **Confusing event time vs. processing time** is a common error — define both clearly in any time-related answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ Data stream = **unbounded, ordered, real-time** sequence of tuples.
- ✅ Cardinality $\vert\mathcal{S}\vert = \infty$; **single-pass** algorithms mandatory.
- ✅ Poisson arrival: $P(N(t)=k) = \frac{(\lambda t)^k e^{-\lambda t}}{k!}$.
- ✅ Per-element time budget: $T_{elem} = 1/\lambda$.
- ✅ **5 V's of Big Data** — especially **Velocity** is the focus in streams.
- ✅ **Three time semantics:** event time, ingestion time, processing time.
- ✅ **Window types:** tumbling, sliding, session.
- ✅ **Synopsis structures:** Bloom filter, reservoir sampling, AMS estimator.
- ✅ **Bloom filter** optimal hash count: $k_{opt} = (m/n) \ln 2$.
- ✅ **Reservoir sampling:** keep $i$-th element with probability $s/i$.
- ✅ **Backpressure** prevents system overload when consumers lag.
- ✅ **Concept drift** requires adaptive, not static, models.
- ✅ Stream processing engines: **Flink, Kafka Streams, Spark Streaming**.
- ✅ Batch = stored + iterated; Stream = in-motion + single-pass.
- ✅ Use **block diagrams** in any architecture question to gain full marks.
- ✅ Always state **units** (events/sec, ms, MB) in numerical answers.
- ✅ **State store** is critical for windowed and stateful computations.
- ✅ Real-world domains: finance, healthcare, IoT, telecom, cybersecurity.
- ✅ Distinguish **throughput** (rate) from **latency** (delay) — common trap.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Streaming Data Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT746 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Streaming Data Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Streaming Data Architecture** is a distributed computing framework designed to ingest, process, store, and analyze continuous, unbounded flows of data records in **real-time** (or near-real-time) as they are produced by source systems. Unlike traditional batch architectures that operate on finite, bounded datasets, a streaming architecture treats data as an *event stream* — an ordered, time-series sequence of events with no predetermined end.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECNT746 Module 1):**
> Streaming Data Architecture refers to the end-to-end blueprint of components, message brokers, stream processors, in-memory stores, and sink systems that collectively enable the continuous transformation of high-velocity, high-volume data into actionable insights with **end-to-end latency** typically measured in **milliseconds (ms) to seconds (s)**.

The architecture must satisfy three foundational guarantees — often called the **Three V's extended for Streaming**:
- **Velocity** (events per second ingested)
- **Volume** (petabytes of state retained)
- **Variability** (schema evolution, out-of-order events, late arrivals)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The River vs. The Lake

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Tap Water vs. Bucket Water**
> - **Batch Processing = The Bucket:** You wait for the bucket to fill (data accumulates), then carry it (process it) all at once. You drink only after the bucket is full.
> - **Stream Processing = The Tap:** Water (data) flows continuously. The instant it leaves the pipe, you can drink, filter, or heat it. There is no "full" state — flow is eternal.
>
> Streaming Data Architecture is the entire *plumbing network* — the source (river), the pump (ingestion layer), the filtration plant (stream processor), and the storage tanks (sink layer) — built to deliver a continuous, clean, low-latency supply of insights.

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Definition | Typical Unit |
|------|------------|--------------|
| **Event** | A single immutable record produced by a source | Tuple / Record |
| **Event Time** | Timestamp when the event actually occurred at the source | Unix epoch / ISO-8601 |
| **Processing Time** | Timestamp when the event is observed by the processor | Unix epoch / ISO-8601 |
| **Skew** | Difference between event time and processing time | ms / s |
| **Throughput** | Number of events processed per unit time | events/sec |
| **Latency** | Time delay between event creation and result emission | **ms** |
| **Watermark** | Logical timestamp indicating progress of event time | Logical clock |
| **Backpressure** | Mechanism where a slow consumer signals upstream to slow down | Pressure signal |
| **Window** | Finite slice of an unbounded stream for aggregation | Tumbling / Sliding / Session |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Highlight (KTU Board Favourite):**
> The triad **Sources → Stream Processor → Sinks** forms the universal backbone of every streaming architecture, regardless of vendor (Apache Kafka, Apache Flink, Spark Streaming, AWS Kinesis, Google Dataflow).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Logical Layers of Streaming Data Architecture

A robust streaming architecture is decomposed into **five decoupled layers**, each with a single responsibility. This separation enables independent scaling, fault tolerance, and technology substitution.

### Layer 1 — Source Layer (Producers)
- Generates unbounded sequences of events.
- Examples: IoT sensors, web clickstreams, application logs, transaction databases (via CDC), social media firehoses.
- Producers are **decoupled** from consumers via the message broker.

### Layer 2 — Ingestion / Message Broker Layer
- Acts as the **durable, ordered, replayable buffer** between producers and consumers.
- Core responsibilities:
  - **Durability** — persist events to disk so they survive crashes.
  - **Partitioning** — split topic into partitions for parallel consumption.
  - **Retention** — store events for a defined time (e.g., 7 days) or size (e.g., 1 TB).
  - **Ordering** — guarantee order *within* a partition (key-based).
- Reference implementations: **Apache Kafka**, RabbitMQ, Amazon Kinesis Data Streams, Pulsar.

### Layer 3 — Stream Processing Layer
- The computational heart of the architecture.
- Performs transformations, enrichments, aggregations, joins, and pattern detections over event streams.
- Operates in one of two execution modes:
  - **Native Streaming (True Streaming):** Processes each event as it arrives — millisecond latency. Example: Apache Flink, Kafka Streams.
  - **Micro-Batching:** Splits stream into tiny batches (e.g., 500 ms) — sub-second latency. Example: Spark Structured Streaming.

### Layer 4 — Storage / State Layer
- Stores results, materialized views, and **operational state** of stream processors.
- Two categories:
  - **Real-time stores:** Key-value or columnar stores for low-latency reads (e.g., Apache Cassandra, Redis, Druid).
  - **Analytical stores (Lakehouse):** Columnar formats like Apache Parquet/ORC on S3/HDFS for historical queries.

### Layer 5 — Sink / Serving Layer
- Delivers processed data to downstream consumers:
  - Dashboards (Grafana, Superset)
  - ML inference pipelines
  - Alerting systems (PagerDuty)
  - Reverse ETL back into operational databases

## 2.2 Core Architectural Patterns

### Pattern A — Lambda Architecture (Hybrid Batch + Stream)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Topic:** Lambda Architecture is the *most frequently examined* streaming pattern.

The Lambda Architecture runs **two parallel pipelines**:
- **Speed Layer (Stream Path):** Processes data in real-time for low-latency views.
- **Batch Layer:** Re-processes all historical data periodically for accurate, comprehensive views.
- **Serving Layer:** Merges both outputs and answers queries by combining them.

**Why two layers?** Real-time systems may have errors, dropped events, or approximate results. The batch layer provides a **correct, eventually-consistent** view that overrides the speed layer's approximations.

### Pattern B — Kappa Architecture (Stream-Only)

Proposed by **Jay Kreps** (creator of Apache Kafka), the Kappa Architecture **eliminates the batch layer entirely**. It uses a single stream processing pipeline that can be re-run from the beginning by rewinding the log (replay).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Kappa = Stream layer + Replayable Log + Same code for real-time and reprocessing.

### Pattern C — Lakehouse Streaming (Modern Hybrid)

Combines the **low-cost object storage** of a data lake (Parquet/Delta/Iceberg) with streaming ingestion and ACID transactions, popularized by **Databricks**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes the **mathematical foundations** of streaming systems that may appear in KTU Part B derivations.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **Throughput** | $\lambda = \dfrac{N_{events}}{T_{window}}$ | events/sec | Higher is better |
| 2 | **End-to-End Latency** | $L_{e2e} = L_{ingest} + L_{queue} + L_{process} + L_{egress}$ | ms | Sum of stage delays |
| 3 | **Watermark Lag** | $W_{lag} = t_{process} - t_{event}$ | ms | Indicates out-of-order delay |
| 4 | **Windowed Aggregation (Tumbling)** | $A_k = \sum_{i=t_k}^{t_{k+1}-1} v_i$ | sum | One event per window |
| 5 | **Windowed Aggregation (Sliding)** | $A(t) = \sum_{i \in W(t, \Delta)} v_i$ | sum | Overlapping windows |
| 6 | **Session Window** | $S_j = \{e_i \vert t_i - t_{i-1} \leq \tau_{gap}\}$ | set | Gap-based segmentation |
| 7 | **Memory of State Backend** | $M_{state} = n_{keys} \times s_{value} \times (1 + \epsilon_{overhead})$ | bytes | $\epsilon$ = RocksDB overhead |
| 8 | **Checkpoint Recovery Time** | $T_{recover} = \dfrac{D_{state}}{B_{disk}}$ | sec | $D$=state size, $B$=bandwidth |
| 9 | **Idempotency Constraint** | $f(x) = f(f(x))$ | logical | Required for replay safety |
| 10 | **Exactly-Once Semantics** | $P_{loss} \cdot P_{dup} = 0$ | probability | Achieved via 2PC + idempotency |

> **Pipeline Note:** Use $\vert$ only in math mode, never inside markdown tables (per protocol).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Streaming Data Architecture is the **backbone of modern digital enterprises**:
- **Finance:** Real-time fraud detection (Visa processes ~**6,000 transactions/sec**).
- **E-commerce:** Live recommendation engines (Amazon, Flipkart).
- **Telecommunications:** Network anomaly detection.
- **IoT & Smart Cities:** Sensor telemetry pipelines.
- **Ride-sharing:** Dynamic pricing and driver allocation (Uber, Ola).
- **Healthcare:** Real-time patient monitoring systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Insight:** Netflix uses Apache Flink to process **billions of events per day** to power its real-time recommendation and A/B testing infrastructure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — End-to-End Latency Decomposition

A KTU-favourite derivation is to show that the total latency of a streaming pipeline is the **sum of the deterministic stage delays**, assuming no backpressure.

**Given:**
- Ingestion (network + serialization) latency: $L_{ingest}$
- Broker queue waiting time: $L_{queue}$
- Stream processing computation time: $L_{process}$
- Egress to sink latency: $L_{egress}$

**To Prove:** Total end-to-end latency is their arithmetic sum.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Consider an event $e$ produced at time $t_0$ at the source. The event traverses the pipeline in four serial stages.

**Stage 1 — Ingestion (Source → Broker):**
The event is serialized (e.g., Avro, Protobuf) and transmitted over the network.

$$t_1 = t_0 + L_{ingest}$$

This accounts for client-side buffering, schema encoding, and TCP round-trip to the broker.

**Stage 2 — Broker Queuing:**
The event enters a partitioned topic. The time spent waiting in the broker depends on the consumer's fetch offset and current lag.

$$t_2 = t_1 + L_{queue}$$

For a well-tuned system, $L_{queue}$ approaches the broker's per-partition commit interval.

**Stage 3 — Stream Processing:**
The processor pulls the event, deserializes it, applies user-defined functions (map, filter, aggregate, join), and updates internal state.

$$t_3 = t_2 + L_{process}$$

For Flink with sub-millisecond processing, $L_{process} \ll L_{ingest}$.

**Stage 4 — Egress to Sink:**
The processed result is emitted to the downstream sink (database, dashboard, or another topic).

$$t_4 = t_3 + L_{egress}$$

**Combining all four stages:**

$$t_4 = t_0 + L_{ingest} + L_{queue} + L_{process} + L_{egress}$$

**End-to-end latency** is defined as $L_{e2e} = t_4 - t_0$, therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{e2e} &= t_4 - t_0 \\
&= (t_0 + L_{ingest} + L_{queue} + L_{process} + L_{egress}) - t_0 \\
&= L_{ingest} + L_{queue} + L_{process} + L_{egress}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** End-to-end latency is **additive** across the four pipeline stages. Optimizing the largest component yields the greatest reduction.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Throughput of a Partitioned Streaming Topic

**Given:** A Kafka-like topic with $P$ partitions, each with a maximum per-partition throughput of $\rho$ events/sec.

**To Derive:** The aggregate cluster throughput $\Lambda_{max}$.

**Step 1 — Per-Partition Bound:**
Each partition is processed by exactly one consumer instance at any time (to preserve ordering). Thus, the throughput of a single partition is bounded by:

$$ \lambda_p \leq \rho $$

**Step 2 — Aggregate Bound:**
With $P$ independent partitions operating in parallel:

$$ \Lambda_{max} = \sum_{p=1}^{P} \lambda_p \leq \sum_{p=1}^{P} \rho = P \cdot \rho $$

**Step 3 — Practical Lower Bound (with consumer lag):**
Real systems rarely saturate partitions. Empirically:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Lambda_{real} &= P \cdot \rho \cdot \eta_{consumer} \cdot \eta_{network} \\
&\approx P \cdot \rho \cdot \eta \quad \text{where } 0 < \eta \leq 1
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (KTU Style):**
> A Kafka topic has 12 partitions, each capable of 50,000 events/sec. What is the maximum aggregate throughput?
>
> $$ \Lambda_{max} = 12 \times 50{,}000 = 600{,}000 \text{ events/sec} = 600 \text{ kEPS} $$
>
> If consumer efficiency is 80%, the real throughput is:
> $$ \Lambda_{real} = 600{,}000 \times 0.8 = 480{,}000 \text{ events/sec} $$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Tumbling vs. Sliding Window Aggregation Cost

**Given:** A stream of $N$ events per second, each carrying a value $v_i$, and a window size of $W$ seconds.

**To Derive:** The number of window updates per second for each window type.

**Tumbling Windows:**
A tumbling window of size $W$ closes every $W$ seconds and does **not** overlap.

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{tumble} &= \frac{N}{W} \quad \text{(updates per window emission)} \\
F_{tumble} &= \frac{1}{W} \quad \text{(emission frequency in Hz)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sliding Windows:**
A sliding window of size $W$ with slide interval $S$ (where $S < W$) creates $\frac{W}{S}$ overlapping windows.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{slide} &= \frac{1}{S} \quad \text{(emission frequency)} \\
U_{slide} &= \frac{N}{S} \quad \text{(updates per emission)} \\
C_{slide} &= \frac{W}{S} \times U_{slide} = \frac{W \cdot N}{S^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cost Ratio (Sliding vs. Tumbling):**

$$ R = \frac{C_{slide}}{C_{tumble}} = \frac{W/S}{1} \cdot \frac{1}{S/W} = \frac{W^2}{S^2} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** If $W = 60$ s and $S = 10$ s, the sliding window performs **36× more computation** than a tumbling window. This is why windowed aggregations are the most expensive operation in stream processing.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Lambda vs. Kappa vs. Lakehouse

| Criterion | Lambda Architecture | Kappa Architecture | Lakehouse Streaming |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|----------------------|
| **Pipeline Count** | 2 (Batch + Speed) | 1 (Stream only) | 1 (Unified) |
| **Codebase** | Two (duplicated logic) | One | One |
| **Latency** | Seconds (speed) + Hours (batch) | Milliseconds to seconds | Seconds to minutes |
| **Reprocessing** | Batch layer | Replay log | Time-travel via Delta/Iceberg |
| **Operational Complexity** | **High** | **Low** | **Medium** |
| **Storage Cost** | High (duplicate data) | Low (log retention only) | Medium |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Strong (batch fallback) | Strong (replay) | Strong (ACID transactions) |
| **KTU Board Frequency** | **★★★★★** | ★★★★ | ★★★ |
| **Best Use Case** | Mixed real-time + historical analytics | Pure event-driven systems | Unified ML + BI workloads |

## 3.5 Throughput-SLA Tradeoff Analysis

**Given:** SLA requirement of $L_{max}$ ms latency, what is the maximum sustainable throughput?

Using Little's Law adapted for streaming systems:

$$ \Lambda = \frac{N_{in\_flight}}{L_{avg}} $$

Where $N_{in\_flight}$ is the number of events concurrently being processed.

**Derivation:** Solving for $N_{in\_flight}$ under SLA constraint $L_{avg} \leq L_{max}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{in\_flight} &= \Lambda \cdot L_{avg} \\
&\leq \Lambda \cdot L_{max}
\end{aligned}
$$

The total memory footprint of the system must satisfy:

$$ M_{required} = N_{in\_flight} \times s_{event} + M_{state} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (KTU):**
> A system must process $\Lambda = 100{,}000$ events/sec with $L_{max} = 50$ ms. Each event is 512 bytes. Compute the minimum in-flight memory.
>
> $$ N_{in\_flight} = 100{,}000 \times 0.050 = 5{,}000 \text{ events} $$
> $$ M_{in\_flight} = 5{,}000 \times 512 = 2.56 \text{ MB} $$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Five-Layer Streaming Data Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1[IoT Sensors]
    src2[Web Clickstream]
    src3[App Logs]
    src4[CDC Databases]
    broker[(Message Broker - Kafka)]
    proc1[Stream Processor A]
    proc2[Stream Processor B]
    state[(State Store)]
    sink1[Real Time Dashboard]
    sink2[Lakehouse Storage]
    sink3[ML Inference Service]
    sink4[Alerting System]
    src1 --> broker
    src2 --> broker
    src3 --> broker
    src4 --> broker
    broker --> proc1
    broker --> proc2
    proc1 --> state
    proc2 --> state
    proc1 --> sink1
    proc1 --> sink3
    proc2 --> sink2
    proc2 --> sink4
```

## 4.2 Lambda Architecture — Two Pipeline View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    source[Event Sources] --> ingest[Ingestion Layer]
    ingest --> speed[Speed Layer - Stream Processing]
    ingest --> batch[Batch Layer - Hadoop Spark]
    speed --> merge[Serving Layer]
    batch --> merge
    merge --> query[Query Clients]
    subgraph SpeedPath
        speed
    end
    subgraph BatchPath
        batch
    end
```

## 4.3 Kappa Architecture — Single Stream with Replay

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Event Sources] --> log[(Durable Log - Replayable)]
    log --> stream1[Stream Processor v1]
    log --> stream2[Stream Processor v2 - New Version]
    stream1 --> out1[Serving Layer]
    stream2 --> out2[Serving Layer]
    log -.replay.-> stream2
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Streaming Pipeline Internals

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    p1[Producer Client 1] -->|Serialize| b1[Broker Partition 0]
    p2[Producer Client 2] -->|Serialize| b2[Broker Partition 1]
    p3[Producer Client 3] -->|Serialize| b3[Broker Partition 2]
    b1 --> c1[Consumer Task 0]
    b2 --> c2[Consumer Task 1]
    b3 --> c3[Consumer Task 2]
    c1 --> op1[Map Filter]
    c2 --> op2[Windowed Aggregate]
    c3 --> op3[Stream Stream Join]
    op1 --> st1[(Key Value State)]
    op2 --> st1
    op3 --> st1
    op1 --> s1[Sink Topic A]
    op2 --> s2[Sink Database B]
    op3 --> s3[Sink Dashboard C]
    ckpt[(Checkpoint Coordinator)] -.checkpoint.-> st1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The above diagrams show that streaming architectures are **physically decoupled but logically sequenced**. Each box is a *separable failure domain* — meaning if one consumer crashes, only its partition is affected, not the entire pipeline.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Define Streaming Data Architecture. List any four characteristics that distinguish it from traditional batch processing.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Streaming Data Architecture is a distributed computing framework designed to ingest, process, and analyze continuous, unbounded flows of data events in real-time, with end-to-end latency typically in milliseconds to seconds.

**Four distinguishing characteristics:**

1. **Unbounded Data:** Streaming systems handle data with no predefined end, whereas batch systems operate on finite datasets.
2. **Low Latency:** Streaming provides millisecond-to-second latency; batch offers minutes-to-hours.
3. **Continuous Processing:** Computation runs perpetually rather than at scheduled job intervals.
4. **Event-Time Semantics:** Streaming handles out-of-order events via watermarks; batch assumes complete data at job start.
5. **Stateful Computation:** Stream processors maintain long-running state (counters, windows) across events.

*(Allocate 1 mark for definition + 0.5 per characteristic × 4 = 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is a watermark in a stream processing system? Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **watermark** is a logical timestamp marker injected into a data stream by the stream processor to indicate that no events with a timestamp less than the watermark value are expected to arrive in the future.

**Necessity:**
1. It enables the system to **trigger windowed computations** even when events arrive out of order.
2. It provides a **progress metric** for event-time-based processing, ensuring correctness of aggregations.
3. Without watermarks, the system would either wait indefinitely (blocking windows) or produce incorrect results (early triggers on incomplete data).

*(1 mark definition + 2 marks necessity breakdown)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Streaming Architecture):**
> - Marks are frequently **lost for failing to draw the architecture diagram** — always include a labeled block diagram in your answer.
> - Students often confuse **Lambda and Kappa architectures**. State clearly: Lambda = TWO pipelines, Kappa = ONE pipeline with replay.
> - Do not use **batch-processing terminology** (like "MapReduce job" or "scheduler") when explaining streaming systems — examiners deduct marks for terminology mismatch.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Lambda vs. Kappa Architecture**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**(a)** Explain the Lambda Architecture for streaming data with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the speed layer and batch layer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare Lambda and Kappa architectures in terms of latency, complexity, reprocessing, and operational overhead. Discuss one real-world scenario where Kappa is preferable over Lambda. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Definition:** The Lambda Architecture, proposed by Nathan Marz, is a hybrid data processing architecture that combines batch and stream processing in parallel to serve both low-latency and high-accuracy queries.

**Block Diagram (Mandatory in Answer):**

```
  Sources  →  [Ingestion Layer]
                ↓           ↓
        [Speed Layer]   [Batch Layer]
        (Stream Proc)   (Hadoop/Spark)
                ↓           ↓
        [Serving Layer  ←  Merges Outputs]
                ↓
        [Query Clients]
```

**Speed Layer (Stream Path):**
- Processes events in real-time as they arrive.
- Produces **approximate, low-latency views** for time-critical queries.
- Uses incremental algorithms (e.g., HyperLogLog for cardinality).
- Example technologies: Apache Storm, Flink, Spark Streaming.

**Batch Layer:**
- Periodically re-processes the **complete historical dataset** stored in a distributed file system (HDFS, S3).
- Produces **accurate, comprehensive batch views** that overwrite any drift in the speed layer.
- Uses idempotent, deterministic batch jobs.

**Serving Layer:**
- Merges outputs from both layers.
- Indexes data for fast random reads (e.g., ElephantDB, HBase, Cassandra).

**Key Benefit:** The batch layer is the **source of truth**; the speed layer exists only to fill the latency gap.

*(Mark Allocation: 2 marks diagram + 3 marks explanation + 2 marks role description)*

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Lambda Architecture | Kappa Architecture |
|-----------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Number of Pipelines** | Two (Batch + Speed) | One (Stream only) |
| **Latency** | Real-time + hours for batch | Milliseconds to seconds |
| **Code Duplication** | Yes (two codebases) | No (single codebase) |
| **Reprocessing** | Re-run batch job | Replay the log |
| **Operational Complexity** | **High** | **Low** |
| **Storage Cost** | Duplicate storage | Single log retention |
| **Fault Recovery** | Rebuild from batch | Rewind log offset |

**Kappa Preferable Scenario:**
Consider a **real-time fraud detection system** in a payment gateway:
- The transaction stream is the *only* source of truth (no need for historical reprocessing).
- Rapidly evolving fraud patterns require frequent re-deployment of detection models — Kappa allows quick reprocessing by replaying the log.
- Maintaining a separate batch pipeline (Lambda) would double engineering cost with no accuracy benefit.

**Conclusion:** Kappa is optimal when **all data can be modeled as an immutable event stream** and the volume of historical data is bounded by log retention (e.g., 30 days).

*(Mark Allocation: 4 marks table + 2 marks real-world example + 1 mark conclusion)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: End-to-End Pipeline Design**

**(a)** Design a Streaming Data Architecture for a real-time ride-hailing application (similar to Uber). Identify the sources, ingestion mechanism, processing layer, state management, and sinks. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the end-to-end latency of the designed system given: $L_{ingest}=8$ ms, $L_{queue}=12$ ms, $L_{process}=25$ ms, $L_{egress}=5$ ms. If the SLA requires latency below 60 ms, does the design meet it? Suggest two optimizations. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Architecture Design for Ride-Hailing Real-Time System:**

**1. Sources (Producers):**
- Mobile app events: ride requests, GPS pings, driver location updates.
- Payment service: transaction completion events.
- Driver app: status changes (online, offline, en-route).

**2. Ingestion Layer (Message Broker):**
- **Apache Kafka** with partitioned topics:
  - `ride-requests` (keyed by rider_id, 16 partitions)
  - `driver-locations` (keyed by driver_id, 32 partitions — high cardinality)
  - `trip-events` (keyed by trip_id)
- Retention: 7 days, replication factor = 3.

**3. Stream Processing Layer:**
- **Apache Flink** jobs:
  - **Job 1 — Matching Engine:** Joins `ride-requests` with `driver-locations` to assign nearest driver. Maintains geospatial index (H3 / S2 cells) in RocksDB state backend.
  - **Job 2 — Dynamic Pricing:** Computes surge multiplier using sliding window (1-minute size, 10-second slide) of demand/supply ratio.
  - **Job 3 — Trip Completion:** Aggregates fare, distance, and time into analytics topic.

**4. State Management:**
- **RocksDB State Backend** in Flink for geospatial and pricing data.
- **Redis** for low-latency read cache of active driver locations (TTL = 30 s).
- **Apache Cassandra** for trip history (wide-column store).

**5. Sinks (Serving Layer):**
- Mobile app push notifications (driver assignment, fare estimate).
- Real-time dashboard for operations (Grafana on Prometheus metrics).
- Apache Parquet files on S3 for offline analytics.
- Alert system for fraudulent trips (PagerDuty).

**Diagram (Mandatory):**

```
  [Rider App]   [Driver App]   [Payment Svc]
        \           |              /
         \          |             /
          →→→ [KAFKA BROKER] ←←←
                ↓        ↓        ↓
         [Flink-Match] [Flink-Price] [Flink-Trip]
                ↓        ↓        ↓
         [RocksDB]   [Redis]    [Cassandra]
                ↓        ↓        ↓
         [Mobile App] [Dashboard] [S3 + Alerts]
```

*(Mark Allocation: 1.5 marks sources + 1.5 marks broker + 2 marks processing + 1 mark state + 1 mark sinks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Apply the Latency Formula:**

The end-to-end latency is the sum of all stage delays (as derived in Section 3.1):

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{e2e} &= L_{ingest} + L_{queue} + L_{process} + L_{egress} \\
&= 8 + 12 + 25 + 5 \\
&= 50 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with SLA:**

$$ L_{e2e} = 50 \text{ ms} \leq L_{SLA} = 60 \text{ ms} $$

**Result:** ✅ The design **meets the SLA** with a 10 ms safety margin.

**Step 3 — Suggested Optimizations (in case SLA tightens):**

**Optimization 1 — Reduce $L_{process}$ via Window Pre-aggregation:**
Instead of computing surge pricing per event, pre-aggregate driver locations in Redis every 5 seconds. This reduces Flink's per-event processing load.

**Optimization 2 — Reduce $L_{queue}$ via Increased Partitioning & Zero-Copy Transfer:**
- Increase partition count to reduce per-partition queue depth.
- Use Kafka's **zero-copy** (sendfile system call) and **batch compression** (lz4/zstd) to lower serialization time.

**Quantitative Impact:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{e2e}^{optimized} &\approx 8 + 6 + 15 + 5 = 34 \text{ ms} \\
\text{Improvement} &= 50 - 34 = 16 \text{ ms (32% reduction)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Mark Allocation: 2 marks formula application + 1 mark SLA comparison + 2 marks each optimization)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Streaming Data Architecture**

✅ **Core Definition:** Continuous processing of unbounded, time-ordered event streams with sub-second latency.

✅ **Five Layers (Memorize Order):** Sources → Ingestion Broker → Stream Processor → State Store → Sinks.

✅ **Lambda Architecture:** Two pipelines (Speed + Batch) for both low-latency and high-accuracy results. **Most-tested pattern in KTU.**

✅ **Kappa Architecture:** Single pipeline + replayable log. Lower complexity, suited for pure event-driven workloads.

✅ **Watermarks:** Logical event-time progress markers; essential for out-of-order event handling.

✅ **Window Types:** Tumbling (no overlap), Sliding (overlap, high cost), Session (gap-based).

✅ **Latency Equation:** $L_{e2e} = L_{ingest} + L_{queue} + L_{process} + L_{egress}$ — always additive.

✅ **Throughput Equation:** $\Lambda_{max} = P \cdot \rho$ — proportional to partition count.

✅ **Little's Law for Streaming:** $N_{in\_flight} = \Lambda \cdot L_{avg}$ — connects throughput, latency, and memory.

✅ **State Backends:** RocksDB (disk-backed, large state), HashMapState (in-memory, fast).

✅ **Fault Tolerance:** Checkpointing (Flink), Write-Ahead Logs (Kafka), Idempotent Sinks for exactly-once semantics.

✅ **Backpressure:** Slow consumer propagates pressure upstream to prevent memory overflow.

✅ **Always draw a labeled diagram** in Part B answers — KTU examiners award 2-3 marks for it.

✅ **Avoid these terms in streaming answers:** "MapReduce job," "ETL scheduler," "overnight batch" — they signal confusion to the examiner.

✅ **Real-world references to memorize:** Netflix (Flink), LinkedIn (Kafka), Uber (Kafka + Flink), AWS Kinesis, Google Dataflow (Beam model).

---

**End of Module 1 Notes — Streaming Data Architecture**
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education framework (CO1–CO2 alignment).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stream Data Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT746 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Stream Data Model # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stream Data Model (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** A *stream* is a data model in which the input arrives not in a fixed, finite batch but as a **continuous, unbounded, real-time sequence of elements** $a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots$ generated at high velocity. Each element $a_i$ is a tuple $(key, value)$ where the key often carries semantic meaning (e.g., timestamp, user-ID) and the value is the measured payload. The model mandates **one-pass, sub-linear-space, near real-time processing** because the volume of data is too large (or too fast) to store, re-scan, or even fully consume before producing analytics. > [!NOTE] > **KTU-2024 Core Distinction:** Unlike the *relational model* (finite relations queried via SQL) or the *data cube* model (multi-dimensional aggregation), the **Stream Data Model** is intrinsically *temporal*, *ordered*, and *append-only*. Memory is the only practical currency, and the algorithm is judged by *throughput*, *latency*, and *approximation error* — not just correctness. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "TAP-WATER" Intuition Imagine a municipal water pipeline versus a tanker-truck: - **Traditional Batch Model** = **Tanker Truck.** A *finite* volume of water is filled, parked, and then analyzed at leisure in a lab. You can re-pump it, re-test it, store it. - **Stream Data Model** = **Continuously Running Tap.** A never-ending flow of water is rushing past your sensor. You can: 1. Either **drink it instantly** (real-time processing), or 2. **Catch a few drops in a cup** (sampling), or 3. **Estimate the "saltiness" without ever tasting every drop** (approximation algorithms like Bloom Filters / Flajolet–Martin). The sensor is your algorithm. The water you have *not* yet seen is *infinity*. You cannot go back upstream and "replay" the flow — once passed, the data is gone. ## 1.3 Key Vocabulary and High-Yield Terminology | Term | KTU-Canonical Meaning | |---|---| | **Element** | A single tuple $(a_i)$ of the stream. | | **Slide / Window** | The most recent $N$ elements considered for an aggregate query. | | **Landmark Window** | Window from a fixed past time $T_0$ up to *now*. | | **Decaying Window** | Window where older elements carry exponentially diminishing weight. | | **Sampling** | A probabilistic technique that retains only a fraction of elements. | | **Synopsis** | A compact data structure (Sketch) summarising the entire stream seen so far. | | **Throughput** | Number of stream elements processed per second. | | **Latency** | Time gap between element arrival and its influence on the output. | | **Filter** | A membership test (e.g., Bloom Filter) that returns *definitely no* or *probably yes*. | > [!TIP] > **Why Streams exist in Industry:** Telecom call-records (CDRs), credit-card transaction monitoring (fraud-detection), click-streams, IoT sensor telemetry, GPS pings, server log scraping, and Twitter/Slack feeds. Every domain generating **velocity + volume + variety** is a candidate for the stream model. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Stream Processing Every stream algorithm in the KTU syllabus can be classified by **which of the five pillars** it emphasises: 1. **Sampling** — selecting a representative subset (Bernoulli, Reservoir, Vitter's Algorithm R). 2. **Filtering** — testing membership without storing the actual set (Bloom Filter, Count-Min Sketch). 3. **Counting** — estimating cardinality of distinct elements (Flajolet–Martin, HyperLogLog). 4. **Moment Estimation** — capturing the frequency-distribution shape (Alon–Matias–Szegedy — AMS). 5. **Windowed Querying** — answering aggregate queries over the recent past (DGIM, Exponential Histograms). ## 2.2 The Mathematical Spine of a Stream Formally, a stream $\mathcal{S}$ is the sequence: $$ \mathcal{S} = \langle a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots \rangle $$ where each $a_i \in \mathcal{U}$ (a *universe* of possible elements, e.g., all IPv4 addresses, $|\mathcal{U}| = 2^{32}$). The **frequency** of an element $x$ at time $t$ is: $$ f_t(x) = \big\vert \{ i \,\vert\, i \le t \;\wedge\; a_i = x \} \big\vert $$ The **count of distinct elements** at time $t$ is: $$ D_t = \big\vert \{ x \in \mathcal{U} \,\vert\, f_t(x) > 0 \} \big\vert $$ > [!NOTE] > **Reality Check:** Naively storing $f_t(x)$ for every $x$ in a single hash-map is $O(\vert\mathcal{U}\vert)$ memory. For IPv4 traces, that is $2^{32} \approx 4.29 \times 10^9$ entries — **impossible in RAM**. This is *exactly* why approximation algorithms were invented. ## 2.3 Bloom Filter — The Workhorse of Filtering A **Bloom Filter** is a bit-array $B$ of length $m$ initialised to all zeros, together with $k$ independent hash functions $h_1, \ldots, h_k$ each mapping $\mathcal{U} \to \{0, 1, \ldots, m-1\}$. **Insertion of element $x$:** Set $B[h_i(x)] \leftarrow 1$ for $i = 1 \ldots k$. **Query "is $x$ in the set?":** Return *YES* iff **all** $B[h_i(x)] = 1$ for $i = 1 \ldots k$. After $n$ insertions, the **False Positive Probability** (FPR) is: $$ \text{FPR} = \left( 1 - e^{-\frac{kn}{m}} \right)^{k} $$ The **optimal $k$** that minimises the FPR for a given $m$ and $n$ is: $$ k_{\text{opt}} = \frac{m}{n} \ln 2 \approx 0.6931 \cdot \frac{m}{n} $$ The **minimum bit-array size** required to achieve a target FPR $\varepsilon$ for $n$ expected insertions is: $$ m_{\min} = -\,\frac{n \ln \varepsilon}{(\ln 2)^{2}} $$ > [!WARNING] > A Bloom Filter **never produces a false negative**, but it **may produce a false positive** because two different keys can hash to the same set of $k$ bit positions. This trade-off is *the* classic KTU derivation. ## 2.4 Flajolet–Martin (FM) — Estimating Distinct Elements To estimate $D_t$ (number of distinct elements seen), each element $x$ is fed through a hash function $h: \mathcal{U} \to \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, 2^{L}-1\}$. The algorithm keeps: $$ R = \max_{x \text{ seen}} \; \rho(h(x)) $$ where $\rho(y)$ is the **position of the rightmost 1-bit** in the binary representation of $y$ (with $\rho(0) = 0$). Then: $$ \hat{D} = 2^{R} $$ The **expected value** of $R$ is the binary-entropy solution: $$ E[R] \approx \log_2 \left( \frac{n}{D} \right) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 2^{R} \approx D $$ In practice, the **stochastic averaging** uses $k$ independent hash functions and the **median** of their $2^{R_i}$ estimates to tame variance. ## 2.5 Alon–Matias–Szegedy (AMS) — Estimating Stream Moments The $k^{\text{th}}$ *frequency moment* of a stream is: $$ F_k = \sum_{x \in \mathcal{U}} \left( f(x) \right)^{k} $$ | Moment | KTU Interpretation | Use Case | |---|---|---| | $F_0$ | Number of distinct elements | Log analytics | | $F_1$ | Total stream length $\approx n$ | Volume monitoring | | $F_2$ | *Repeat-rate*, or *Gini index* basis | Skew detection, JOIN-size estimation | | $F_{\infty}$ | Most frequent element count | Zipfian top-element query | The **AMS estimator** picks a random position $p \in [1, n]$ and defines: $$ X = n \cdot \left( q \cdot \big(2\,q - 1\big) \right) $$ where $q$ is the count of the *same* element $a_p$ from position $p$ up to the end of the stream. It can be shown that: $$ E[X] = F_2 $$ By averaging $m$ independent copies $X_1, \ldots, X_m$, the estimator variance reduces by a factor of $1/m$. ## 2.6 DGIM — Sliding Window Aggregates DGIM (Datar–Gionis–Indyk–Motwani) stores the last $N$ stream bits using **buckets** of exponentially increasing sizes. A bucket is identified by its **timestamp** (most-recent position of a 1-bit) and the number of 1-bits in it follows a power-of-two rule. Query: "How many 1-bits in the last $N$ positions?" is answered in $O(\log^2 N)$ time using at most $O(\log^2 N)$ buckets. ## 2.7 The KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (High-Yield) > [!NOTE] > Memorise these — they appear in **almost every KTU Module-1 numerical**. | # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Stream size | $n = \sum_{x} f(x)$ | $n$ is the count of all elements | | 2 | Distinct elements | $D = \vert\{x : f(x) > 0\}\vert$ | cardinality of unique values | | 3 | Bloom FPR | $(1 - e^{-kn/m})^{k}$ | $k$ hashes, $m$ bits, $n$ items | | 4 | Optimal Bloom $k$ | $(m/n)\ln 2$ | dimensionless | | 5 | Bloom $m$ for FPR $\varepsilon$ | $-n\ln\varepsilon / (\ln 2)^{2}$ | bits | | 6 | FM estimate | $\hat{D} = 2^{R}$ | $R$ is the max tail-of-ones | | 7 | $k^{\text{th}}$ moment | $F_k = \sum_x f(x)^{k}$ | depends on $k$ | | 8 | AMS $F_2$ estimator | $X = n \cdot q(2q-1)$ | $q$ = residual count of sampled element | | 9 | DGIM bucket-count | $O(\log^2 N)$ | $N$ = window width | | 10 | Decay factor | $c = 2^{-1/T}$ | weight halves every $T$ units | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Bloom-Filter False-Positive Probability Let us derive $(1 - e^{-kn/m})^{k}$ from first principles. **Step 1 — Probability a *single* bit is still 0 after one insertion.** A particular bit $B[j]$ stays 0 after inserting element $x$ only if **none** of the $k$ hash outputs of $x$ equals $j$. Assuming a uniform hash distribution: $$ p_{\text{one insert, one bit}} = \Pr[B[j] = 0] = 1 - \frac{1}{m} $$ **Step 2 — Probability the bit is still 0 after $n$ insertions.** Each insertion is independent, so: $$ \Pr[B[j] = 0 \text{ after } n] = \left( 1 - \frac{1}{m} \right)^{kn} $$ **Step 3 — Use the exponential approximation.** For large $m$, $\left(1 - \frac{1}{m}\right)^{m} \approx e^{-1}$. Substituting: $$ \Pr[B[j] = 0] \approx e^{-kn/m} $$ **Step 4 — Probability a *false positive* occurs for a new element $y$.** For $y$ to be declared present, **all** $k$ of its hash positions must already be 1. The probability all of them were flipped by previous inserts is: $$ \text{FPR} = \big( 1 - e^{-kn/m} \big)^{k} $$ **Step 5 — Finding the optimal $k$ that minimises FPR.** Take the natural log of the FPR: $$ \ln(\text{FPR}) = k \cdot \ln\!\left( 1 - e^{-kn/m} \right) $$ Let $u = e^{-kn/m}$. Then $k = -m\ln u / n$. Differentiating and setting $\frac{d}{du} = 0$ yields $u = 1/2$, i.e., $e^{-kn/m} = 1/2$, giving: $$ k_{\text{opt}} = \frac{m \ln 2}{n} $$ **Step 6 — Plug $k_{\text{opt}}$ back to get the minimum achievable FPR.** $$ \text{FPR}_{\min} = \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^{k_{\text{opt}}} = \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^{(m \ln 2)/n} = e^{-(\ln 2)^{2} \cdot m / n} $$ Solving for $m$ in terms of a target $\varepsilon = \text{FPR}_{\min}$: $$ m = -\,\frac{n \ln \varepsilon}{(\ln 2)^{2}} $$ > [!TIP] > **KTU numerical instinct:** If $n = 10^{7}$ and we want $\varepsilon = 0.01$, then > $$m = -\frac{10^{7} \cdot \ln(0.01)}{(\ln 2)^{2}} = \frac{10^{7} \cdot 4.6052}{0.4805} \approx 9.59 \times 10^{7} \text{ bits } \approx 11.43 \text{ MB}.$$ > A Bloom filter of size **~12 MB** can decide set-membership over 10 million elements with 1% false-positive rate. This is the *power* of sketches. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Flajolet–Martin Estimator **Step 1 — Define the hash range.** A hash function $h: \mathcal{U} \to \{0, 1\}^{L}$ gives an $L$-bit string. **Step 2 — Probability of rightmost 1 at position $\rho \geq r$.** An element $x$ has $\rho(h(x)) \geq r$ if the last $r$ bits of $h(x)$ are all 0. Under uniform hashing: $$ \Pr[\rho(h(x)) \geq r] = \frac{1}{2^{r}} $$ **Step 3 — Among $D$ distinct elements, the *maximum* of the $\rho$ values.** Let $M_r$ be the number of elements with $\rho \geq r$. By linearity of expectation: $$ E[M_r] = \frac{D}{2^{r}} $$ **Step 4 — The expected maximum $R$ is the smallest $r$ such that $E[M_r] \leq 1$.** Solving $D / 2^{r} = 1$ gives: $$ r = \log_2 D \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; D = 2^{r} \approx 2^{R} $$ **Step 5 — Stochastic averaging using $k$ independent hash groups and the median.** Averaging $2^{R}$ in log-space reduces variance from exponential to polynomial, so the final estimator is: $$ \widehat{D} = \text{median}_{i=1}^{k} \left( 2^{R_i} \right) $$ ## 3.3 Derivation of the AMS $F_2$ Estimator **Step 1 — Define the position-pick.** Pick $p$ uniformly at random from $\{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ and let $x = a_p$. Define the residual count: $$ q = \big\vert \{ i \geq p \,\vert\, a_i = x \} \big\vert $$ **Step 2 — What is $E[q]$?** By symmetry, the *last* occurrence of $x$ is equally likely to be at any of its $f(x)$ positions, so: $$ E[q \,\vert\, x] = \frac{f(x) + 1}{2} $$ **Step 3 — Define the estimator $X = n (2q - 1)$.** Compute its expectation by unconditioning on $x$: $$ E[X] = n \sum_{x} \Pr[a_p = x] \big(2\,E[q \vert x] - 1\big) $$ Since $\Pr[a_p = x] = f(x)/n$ and $E[q \vert x] = (f(x) + 1)/2$: $$ E[X] = n \sum_{x} \frac{f(x)}{n} \cdot 2 \cdot \frac{f(x) + 1}{2} - n \sum_{x} \frac{f(x)}{n} = \sum_x f(x) (f(x)+1) - n $$ Expand and simplify (note $\sum_x f(x) = n$): $$ E[X] = \sum_x f(x)^{2} + \sum_x f(x) - n = F_2 + n - n = F_2 $$ Hence $X$ is an **unbiased estimator** of the second moment $F_2$. **Step 4 — Variance reduction by averaging $m$ independent copies $X_1, \ldots, X_m$:** $$ \widehat{F_2} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} X_i \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \mathrm{Var}(\widehat{F_2}) = \frac{\mathrm{Var}(X)}{m} $$ By Chebyshev's inequality, the probability of a large relative error is bounded by $O(1/m)$. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What | Stream Task | Best Sketch | Space | Time per Item | Error | |---|---|---|---|---| | Membership test | Bloom Filter | $O(n \log(1/\varepsilon))$ bits | $O(k)$ | FPR $\varepsilon$ | | Distinct count $D$ | Flajolet–Martin | $O(\log n)$ bits | $O(1)$ | $\pm 0.78 / \sqrt{k}$ | | Heavy hitters | Count-Min Sketch | $O(\tfrac{1}{\varepsilon} \log(1/\delta))$ | $O(\log(1/\delta))$ | $\pm \varepsilon n$ | | Second moment | AMS | $O(1/\varepsilon^{2})$ counters | $O(1)$ | $\pm \varepsilon F_2$ w.p. $\geq 1 - \delta$ | | 1-bits in last $N$ | DGIM | $O(\log^{2} N)$ buckets | $O(\log N)$ | $\leq 50\%$ (tunable) | | Frequency decayed | Exponential Histogram | $O(\tfrac{1}{\varepsilon} \log^{2} N)$ | $O(\log N)$ | $\leq \varepsilon$ | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Stream-Processing Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Data Sources
Sensors, Logs, Social] --> B[Ingress Queue
Kafka or Kinesis] B --> C[Stream Processor
Flink or Spark Streaming] C --> D[Synopsis Store
Bloom, FM, AMS] C --> E[Window Manager
DGIM, EH] C --> F[Output Sink
Dashboards, Alerts] D --> G[Query Layer
Approximate Answers] E --> G G --> H[End User
Analyst or App] styleA[fill:#e0f0ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:1px] styleB[fill:#fff4e0,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:1px] styleC[fill:#e8ffe0,stroke:#2a8a2a,stroke-width:1px] styleD[fill:#f0e0ff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:1px] styleE[fill:#ffe0e8,stroke:#cc0066,stroke-width:1px] styleF[fill:#e0f0ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:1px] styleG[fill:#fff4e0,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:1px] styleH[fill:#e8ffe0,stroke:#2a8a2a,stroke-width:1px] ``` ## 4.2 Bloom Filter Internal Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR X1[Element A] --> H1[Hash h1] X1 --> H2[Hash h2] X1 --> H3[Hash h3] H1 --> B0[Bit 0] H2 --> B1[Bit 1] H3 --> B2[Bit 2] B0 --> R[AND Gate] B1 --> R B2 --> R R --> Q{All ones?} Q -- Yes --> Y[Probably in set] Q -- No --> N[Definitely not in set] styleX1[fill:#e0f0ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:1px] styleH1[fill:#fff4e0,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:1px] styleH2[fill:#fff4e0,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:1px] styleH3[fill:#fff4e0,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:1px] styleB0[fill:#f0e0ff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:1px] styleB1[fill:#f0e0ff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:1px] styleB2[fill:#f0e0ff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:1px] styleR[fill:#ffe0e8,stroke:#cc0066,stroke-width:1px] styleQ[fill:#e8ffe0,stroke:#2a8a2a,stroke-width:1px] styleY[fill:#e8ffe0,stroke:#2a8a2a,stroke-width:1px] styleN[fill:#e0f0ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:1px] ``` ## 4.3 DGIM Bucket Structure Over a Sliding Window ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Window[Last N Stream Positions] direction LR W1[Pos 1] W2[Pos 2] W3[Pos 3] W4[Pos 4] W5[Pos 5] W6[Pos 6] W7[Pos 7] W8[Pos 8] end Window --> G1[Bucket Size 1
1 bit] Window --> G2[Bucket Size 2
2 bits] Window --> G4[Bucket Size 4
4 bits] G1 --> Sum[Sum 1s in window
O log squared N] G2 --> Sum G4 --> Sum styleWindow[fill:#e0f0ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:1px] styleG1[fill:#fff4e0,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:1px] styleG2[fill:#f0e0ff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:1px] styleG4[fill:#ffe0e8,stroke:#cc0066,stroke-width:1px] styleSum[fill:#e8ffe0,stroke:#2a8a2a,stroke-width:1px] ``` ## 4.4 Stream Query Lifecycle ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Stream Arrives] --> S2{Query Type} S2 -- Membership --> P1[Use Bloom Filter] S2 -- Cardinality --> P2[Use FM or HyperLogLog] S2 -- Frequency --> P3[Use Count-Min or AMS] S2 -- Recent Window --> P4[Use DGIM] S2 -- Weighted Recency --> P5[Use Exponential Decay] P1 --> O[Return Approximate Answer
with Error Bound] P2 --> O P3 --> O P4 --> O P5 --> O O --> S1 styleS1[fill:#e0f0ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:1px] styleS2[fill:#fff4e0,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:1px] styleP1[fill:#f0e0ff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:1px] styleP2[fill:#f0e0ff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:1px] styleP3[fill:#f0e0ff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:1px] styleP4[fill:#f0e0ff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:1px] styleP5[fill:#f0e0ff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:1px] styleO[fill:#e8ffe0,stroke:#2a8a2a,stroke-width:1px] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]** ### **Q1. Distinguish between the Traditional Data Model and the Stream Data Model.** **Model Answer:** | Property | Traditional Data Model | Stream Data Model | |---|---|---| | Data Volume | Finite, bounded | Unbounded, infinite | | Processing | Multi-pass, batch | Single-pass, online | | Storage | Persistent DB | Synopses in memory | | Latency | High (hours/days) | Low (milliseconds/seconds) | | Updates | Random, on-demand | Append-only | | Memory Budget | Disk-dominant | RAM-dominant | The **Stream Data Model** is required when data is *too fast to store*, *too voluminous to rescan*, and *too time-sensitive* to delay. *(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark comparison + 1 mark use-case)* --- > **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]** ### **Q2. What is a Bloom Filter? Why does it have false positives but never false negatives?** **Model Answer:** A **Bloom Filter** is a space-efficient probabilistic data structure that tests whether an element $x$ belongs to a set $S$. It uses a bit-array of size $m$ and $k$ hash functions. - **No False Negative:** Once a bit is set to 1, it is never reset. If $x$ was inserted, all $k$ of its bits are 1, so the query *must* return YES. ✓ - **False Positive Possible:** If the $k$ bits of a *new* element $y$ happen to be 1 (set by *other* elements), the filter wrongly reports $y$ as present. ✗ False-positive probability is $(1 - e^{-kn/m})^{k}$. *(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark each for the two properties)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, End Semester]** > *Note: Module 1 ESE questions carry 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Either Q.A or Q.B is to be answered.* ### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) #### (a) Explain the Stream Data Model with a neat diagram. Differentiate between Landmark, Sliding, and Decaying Windows. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** The **Stream Data Model** treats input as a *continuous* sequence $\mathcal{S} = \langle a_1, a_2, \ldots \rangle$ of tuples. Queries operate over a *logical view* of the stream known as a **window**. **The three window paradigms:** 1. **Landmark Window** — From a fixed past time $T_0$ (the *landmark*) up to *now*. Used in cumulative analytics (e.g., "all-time unique visitors"). *Static, monotonic.* 2. **Sliding Window** — Only the *last $N$ elements* (or last $T$ seconds) are considered. As a new element arrives, the oldest is evicted. Used in moving averages, real-time dashboards. *Dynamic, fixed-size.* 3. **Decaying Window** — Older elements are *not evicted* but their *weight decays exponentially*.
