After time $t$, an element's weight is $c^{t}$ where $c \in (0, 1)$ is the decay constant. Used in trend analysis, recency-biased counts. *Dynamic, infinite in extent.* **Comparison table:** | Property | Landmark | Sliding | Decaying | |---|---|---|---| | Past extent | From $T_0$ to now | Last $N$ | Entire past | | Element lifetime | Infinite | Exactly $N$ | Infinite (decayed) | | Recency bias | None | Sharp cutoff | Smooth | | Implementation | Counter | DGIM | Exp. Histogram | *Diagram: Refer to the architecture in SECTION_4.1.* *(7 marks: 2 marks model + 2 marks window types + 2 marks comparison + 1 mark diagram)* #### (b) With a suitable example, explain the **Flajolet–Martin algorithm** for counting the number of distinct elements in a stream. Show why $E[R] = \log_2(n/D)$ leads to $\widehat{D} = 2^{R}$. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Problem:** Given a stream of length $n$ over universe $\mathcal{U}$, count $D = \vert\{x : f(x) > 0\}\vert$ using only $O(\log n)$ memory. **FM Algorithm Steps:** 1. Choose a hash $h: \mathcal{U} \to \{0, 1, \ldots, 2^{L}-1\}$ uniform. 2. For each incoming $a_i$, compute $h(a_i)$ and let $R = \max(R, \rho(h(a_i)))$ where $\rho(y)$ is the position of the rightmost 1-bit. 3. At query time, output $\widehat{D} = 2^{R}$. **Why it works — Derivation:** Since $h$ is uniform, $\Pr[\rho(h(x)) \geq r] = 2^{-r}$ (the event that the last $r$ bits are 0). Let $D$ be the number of distinct elements seen. The expected number of elements with $\rho \geq r$ is $D \cdot 2^{-r}$. The maximum $R$ is the largest $r$ such that at least one element has $\rho \geq r$. Set $D \cdot 2^{-r} \approx 1$: $$ 2^{r} \approx D \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; r = \log_2 D $$ Hence $E[R] = \log_2(n/D) + O(1)$ and $\widehat{D} = 2^{R}$. **Worked Example (illustrative):** Stream = $\{a, b, a, c, b, a, d, e, \ldots\}$. Hashes: $h(a) = 11010_{(2)}$, $h(b) = 01100_{(2)}$, $h(c) = 10001_{(2)}$. The corresponding $\rho$ values: 1, 2, 0. The max $R$ grows slowly; for $D = 5$ unique elements we'd expect $R \approx \log_2 5 \approx 2.32$, so $\widehat{D} \in [4, 8]$. **Variance Reduction:** Use $k$ independent hash families; output the median of the $2^{R_i}$ values to obtain an estimate within a constant factor with high probability. *(7 marks: 2 marks algorithm + 3 marks derivation + 1 mark example + 1 mark variance reduction)* --- ### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* #### (a) Describe the **Bloom Filter** data structure. Derive the false-positive probability $p = (1 - e^{-kn/m})^{k}$ and the optimal number of hash functions. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** A **Bloom Filter** is a bit-array $B[0 \ldots m-1]$ of length $m$ with $k$ independent uniform hash functions $h_1, \ldots, h_k$. **Operations:** - **Insert($x$):** for $i = 1$ to $k$, set $B[h_i(x)] \leftarrow 1$. - **Query($x$):** return YES iff $B[h_i(x)] = 1$ for *all* $i$. **Derivation of FPR:** For a single bit $B[j]$: - After one insert, $\Pr[B[j] = 0] = 1 - 1/m$. - After $n$ inserts, $\Pr[B[j] = 0] = (1 - 1/m)^{kn}$. - Using $(1 - 1/m)^{m} \approx e^{-1}$: $$ \Pr[B[j] = 0] \approx e^{-kn/m} $$ The probability that a *new* element $x$ is *falsely* declared present (all $k$ bits already 1): $$ p = (1 - e^{-kn/m})^{k} $$ **Optimal $k$:** Differentiating $\ln p = k \ln(1 - e^{-kn/m})$ w.r.t. $k$ and setting to zero: $$ k_{\text{opt}} = \frac{m \ln 2}{n} $$ **Plug-back:** $\;p_{\min} = (0.5)^{k_{\text{opt}}} = e^{-(\ln 2)^{2} m / n}$. **Solving for $m$ given target $p$:** $\;m = -n \ln p / (\ln 2)^{2}$. **Numerical Example:** For $n = 10^{6}$ and $p = 0.01$: $$m = -10^{6} \cdot \ln(0.01) / (\ln 2)^{2} = 9.59 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits} \approx 1.17 \text{ MB}.$$ $$k_{\text{opt}} = 0.6931 \cdot 9.59 \approx 6.64 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; k = 7.$$ *(7 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks derivation + 1 mark optimal $k$ + 2 marks numerical + 1 mark note on no false negatives)* #### (b) Explain the **Datar–Gionis–Indyk–Motwani (DGIM) algorithm** for counting the number of 1-bits in a sliding window of length $N$. State its time and space complexity. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Problem:** Maintain an approximation of the count of 1-bits among the last $N$ stream positions, with $O(\log^{2} N)$ memory. **Bucket Rules:** 1. Each bucket stores a **timestamp** (rightmost end-position of the bucket) and a **size** (number of 1-bits), which is always a power of 2. 2. For any size $2^{j}$, there are **at most two** buckets (one on each side of a "size-$2^{j}$" group). 3. When a new 1 arrives, a *size-1* bucket is created. Whenever the rule is violated, the *two oldest* buckets of the smallest size are merged into one bucket of the next size. **Eviction:** Buckets whose timestamp is more than $N$ positions behind the current end are discarded. **Query:** Sum the sizes of all surviving buckets, then *subtract half* the size of the oldest bucket (since it may extend beyond the window). This guarantees a relative error of at most $50\%$. **Complexity:** - Space: $O(\log^{2} N)$ buckets. - Update time per stream element: $O(\log N)$. - Query time: $O(\log N)$. **Improvement:** Using exponential histograms and the *Earliest-First* strategy, the error can be reduced from $50\%$ to $1/\varepsilon$ for arbitrary $\varepsilon > 0$ with $O(\tfrac{1}{\varepsilon} \log^{2} N)$ space. **Use-Case:** Real-time monitoring of "errors per second" in the last hour from server logs. *(7 marks: 2 marks problem + 2 marks bucket rules + 2 marks query/complexity + 1 mark use-case)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost 2-3 Marks in ESE (per KTU Valuation Keys):** > > 1. **Confusing "no false negative" with "no error".** Bloom Filters *do* have false positives — saying "Bloom Filters have no errors" is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction. > 2. **Forgetting to use the median (or averaging) in Flajolet–Martin.** A *single* hash gives an estimate that can be off by orders of magnitude. The examiner expects $k$ independent hash groups + median. > 3. **Mixing up the AMS estimator for $F_2$ with one for $F_1$.** The formula $X = n(2q-1)$ is specific to $F_2$. For $F_1$, the answer is simply $n$. > 4. **Writing Bloom FPR as $(1 - 1/m)^{kn}$ without the exponential approximation.** Full marks require the simplified $e^{-kn/m}$ form. > 5. **Omitting the "decay constant" when describing decaying windows.** Students often say "older elements lose weight" without specifying $c = 2^{-1/T}$. > 6. **Stating DGIM error as $0\%$ instead of $50\%$.** It is an *approximation* algorithm, not exact. > 7. **Drawing the Stream Architecture as a single box.** The examiner expects to see at least: Source → Buffer → Processor → Sketch-store → Query layer. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **One-Page Cheat-Sheet for VIVA / Last-Minute Revision** - ☐ A **stream** is *unbounded*, *ordered*, *append-only*, *one-pass* data. - ☐ Five stream-mining tasks: *Sampling, Filtering, Counting, Moments, Windowing*. - ☐ **Bloom Filter** = $m$-bit array + $k$ hashes → FPR = $(1 - e^{-kn/m})^{k}$, no false negatives, false positives tunable. - ☐ **Optimal $k$** = $(m/n)\ln 2 \approx 0.693 \, m/n$. - ☐ **Flajolet–Martin** → $\widehat{D} = 2^{R}$ where $R$ = max rightmost 1-bit position. - ☐ **AMS Estimator** for $F_2$: $X = n(2q-1)$ is unbiased; average $m$ copies to reduce variance. - ☐ **Moments**: $F_0$ = distinct, $F_1$ = length, $F_2$ = repeat-rate, $F_\infty$ = max frequency. - ☐ **DGIM** maintains buckets of size $1, 2, 4, 8, \ldots$; queries in $O(\log N)$ with $50\%$ relative error. - ☐ **Landmark** windows are cumulative; **Sliding** windows are fixed-length; **Decaying** windows apply $c = 2^{-1/T}$ weights. - ☐ Sketch-based algorithms trade **accuracy** (bounded error) for **space** (sub-linear in $n$). - ☐ Real-world deployments: Kafka + Flink, Spark Streaming, Google Dataflow, AWS Kinesis. - ☐ The word "synopsis" means *compact summary* of a stream — sketches ARE synopses. > 🎯 **Closing VIVA Pointer:** If asked "Why not just store the stream and query offline?", reply: *"Because the stream is unbounded in length and arrives faster than disk bandwidth. Sketches let us answer queries in RAM, in real time, with provable error bounds."*

topics.title: Sampling Techniques for Efficient Stream Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT746 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sampling Techniques for Efficient Stream Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sampling in Data Streams** is the process of selecting a representative subset of elements from a continuous, unbounded, high-velocity data stream such that analytical queries (frequency estimation, quantiles, distinct counting, set similarity) can be answered approximately with provable error bounds, while using **sub-linear space** (typically $O(\text{polylog}(n))$ or $O(k)$ for sample size $k$) and **single-pass** access to the data.

In the KTU Big Data Analytics framework, stream sampling is the foundational primitive that enables **Synopsis Construction** — compact data structures that summarize streams for downstream algorithms like histograms, joins, clustering, and anomaly detection.

### The Three Pillars of Stream Sampling
| Property | Description |
|---|---|
| **Single-Pass** | Each stream element is examined at most once |
| **Sub-linear Space** | Memory footprint grows as $O(k)$ or $O(\text{polylog}(n))$, never $O(n)$ |
| **Provable Guarantees** | $(\epsilon, \delta)$-approximations with explicit error bounds |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Newspaper Opinion Poll**
> Imagine a continuous stream of **10 million voters** entering a stadium during election day. You cannot interview every single person (that would take days), and the crowd is still arriving. A smart pollster stands at the gate and uses **Reservoir Sampling**: she keeps exactly **5,000 slips of paper** in a box. For the first 5,000 voters, she adds their name. For voter #5,001, she draws a random slip — if it says "replace me", she swaps in this new voter and puts the old slip back. This way, **every voter has the exact same chance** of being in the final 5,000-person sample, even though the total number of voters was never known in advance. The sample is **uniformly random**, **fixed-size**, and **memory-efficient** — exactly what stream sampling achieves for big data.

## 1.3 Why Sampling? The Big Data Bottleneck

A modern data stream (e.g., Twitter firehose, network packets, IoT sensors) can produce **terabytes per hour**. Storing the full stream is:
- **Economically infeasible** (storage costs scale linearly)
- **Computationally wasteful** (most queries are aggregate)
- **Latency-introducing** (disk I/O dominates)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Sampling Budget for KTU Problems:** The two most cited complexity metrics are **sample size $k$** and **space complexity $O(k)$**. For probabilistic guarantees, the **Chernoff bound** parameter $\delta$ (failure probability) and $\epsilon$ (relative error) are standard.

## 1.4 Taxonomy of Stream Sampling Techniques

```text
Stream Sampling
├── Uniform Sampling
│   ├── Bernoulli Sampling      (each element with prob. p)
│   ├── Reservoir Sampling      (Algorithm R / Algorithm Z)
│   └── Chain Sampling          (Min-wise independent permutations)
├── Sketch-Based Sampling
│   ├── Bloom Filter            (membership)
│   ├── Count-Min Sketch        (frequency)
│   ├── AMS Sketch              (F2 moment / self-join)
│   └── HyperLogLog             (cardinality)
└── Biased / Application-Specific
    ├── Sliding Window Sampling
    ├── Priority Sampling       (weighted)
    └── Distinct Sampling       (var-opt)
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Reservoir Sampling — Algorithm R (Vitter, 1985)

### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. **Maintain a buffer (reservoir) $S$ of fixed size $k$.**
2. **Phase 1 — Fill the reservoir:** For the first $k$ stream elements $e_1, e_2, \ldots, e_k$, insert each into $S$.
3. **Phase 2 — Probabilistic replacement:** For the $i$-th element ($i > k$):
   - Generate a random integer $j \sim \text{Uniform}(1, i)$.
   - If $j \leq k$, replace $S[j]$ with $e_i$; else discard $e_i$.
4. **Result:** At any time $n \geq k$, every element $e_i$ has probability exactly $k/n$ of being in $S$.

### Why It Works — The Invariant

The algorithm maintains the invariant that **after processing $n$ elements, the reservoir is a uniformly random sample of size $k$ drawn without replacement from the first $n$ elements**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (for KTU derivations):** The substitution test uses $\Pr(\text{replace at step } i) = k/i$, and conditional probability of survival chains through subsequent steps yields the final $k/n$ probability.

### Real-World Utility
- **Network packet sampling** for traffic analysis (sFlow, NetFlow)
- **Database query sampling** (TABLESAMPLE in PostgreSQL)
- **Online A/B testing** with unknown population size
- **Kafka partitioned log sampling** for monitoring

## 2.2 Bernoulli Sampling

Each element is independently included with probability $p$. The expected sample size is $np$, with variance $np(1-p)$. **Memory is variable**, not fixed — disadvantage for bounded-space guarantees.

## 2.3 Bloom Filter

A Bloom filter is a **bit array of $m$ bits** with **$h$ independent hash functions**. Insertion sets $h$ bits; query checks whether all $h$ positions are set. **No false negatives**, but **non-zero false positive rate**.

## 2.4 Count-Min Sketch

A 2D array of counters $C[d][w]$ updated by $h$ hash functions. Returns an **upper-bound estimate** of frequency. Optimized for **point queries** and **range queries** in streams.

## 2.5 AMS F2 Moment Estimator

Estimates the **second frequency moment** $F_2 = \sum_i m_i^2$, where $m_i$ is the count of element $i$. This is critical for **self-join size estimation** and **degree distribution** in graph streams.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the single most important reference for solving KTU problems on this topic.** Memorize these formulas.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbols / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reservoir Sample Invariant | $\Pr(e_i \in S) = \dfrac{k}{n}$ | $k$ = reservoir size, $n$ = stream length |
| 2 | Reservoir Replacement Probability | $\Pr(\text{replace at step } i) = \dfrac{k}{i}$ | $i > k$ |
| 3 | Bernoulli Sample Expected Size | $\mathbb{E}[\vert S\vert] = np$ | $p$ = inclusion probability |
| 4 | Bernoulli Sample Variance | $\text{Var}(\vert S\vert) = np(1-p)$ | Bounded by $np/4$ |
| 5 | Bloom Filter False Positive | $f \approx \left(1 - e^{-hn/m}\right)^{h}$ | $m$ = bits, $n$ = items, $h$ = hash functions |
| 6 | Bloom Optimal Hash Count | $h^{*} = \dfrac{m}{n} \ln 2$ | Minimizes $f$ |
| 7 | Count-Min Sketch Estimate | $\hat{f}(x) = \min_{j} \; C[j][h_j(x)]$ | Underestimate-free upper bound |
| 8 | CM Sketch Error Bound | $\Pr(\hat{f}(x) - f(x) \geq \epsilon N) \leq \delta$ | $w = \lceil e/\epsilon \rceil$, $d = \lceil \ln(1/\delta) \rceil$ |
| 9 | CM Sketch Space | $O\left(\dfrac{1}{\epsilon} \log \dfrac{1}{\delta}\right)$ words | Sub-linear in stream size $N$ |
| 10 | AMS F2 Estimator | $\hat{F}_2 = \text{median}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{r} X_{i,j}^{2}\right)_{j=1}^{s}$ | $X_{i,j} = m_i \cdot r_{i,j}$ |
| 11 | AMS Variance (one estimator) | $\text{Var}(X^2) \leq 2 \cdot F_2^{3/2}$ | Bounded second moment |
| 12 | Chernoff Bound | $\Pr(\vert \bar{X} - \mu\vert \geq \epsilon \mu) \leq 2e^{-\epsilon^2 n / 3}$ | Sample mean concentration |
| 13 | Sample Size for $\epsilon, \delta$ | $k = O\left(\dfrac{1}{\epsilon^2} \log \dfrac{1}{\delta}\right)$ | Sufficient for $(\epsilon, \delta)$-approximation |
| 14 | Min-wise Hash Collision | $\Pr(\min h(A) = \min h(B)) = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B\vert}{\vert A \cup B\vert}$ | Jaccard similarity estimation |
| 15 | HyperLogLog Estimate | $\hat{n} = \alpha_d \cdot 2^{\left(\sum 2^{-M[j]}\right)^{-1}}$ | Distinct counting in $O(\log \log n)$ |

> **Notation Convention:** $\vert S \vert$ denotes the cardinality of set $S$ (not absolute value). When inside markdown tables, use $\vert S \vert$ carefully or use $\lvert S \rvert$ to avoid pipe conflicts.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Reservoir Sampling:** Used in Apache Spark's `sample(withReplacement=False, fraction=k/n)` transformation.
- **Bloom Filter:** HBase, Cassandra, and Bitcoin use Bloom filters to avoid disk lookups.
- **Count-Min Sketch:** Used in Twitter's **Heavy Hitters** detection and **StreamLib** library.
- **AMS F2:** Powers **network anomaly detection** (entropy of degree distribution).
- **Min-wise Sampling:** Google's **PageRank duplicate detection**, near-duplicate web pages.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reservoir Sampling Invariant $\Pr(e_i \in S) = k/n$

### Setup
We have processed $n$ stream elements and the reservoir $S$ has size $k$. We must prove that **for every $i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$**, the probability that $e_i$ is in $S$ is exactly $k/n$.

### Step-by-Step Proof

**Base case ($n = k$):** All $k$ elements are inserted into the reservoir, so $\Pr(e_i \in S) = 1 = k/k$. ✓

**Inductive step:** Assume the invariant holds after processing $n$ elements. Now process element $e_{n+1}$.

The algorithm picks a random index $j \sim \text{Uniform}(1, n+1)$.

$$
\Pr(\text{replace at index } j) = \Pr(j \leq k) = \frac{k}{n+1}
$$

For any existing element $e_i$ (where $i \leq n$), it survives this replacement step if and only if it is **not selected for replacement**, OR it is selected but its slot is the same one it occupies (combined with the $1/k$ chance it occupies the chosen slot).

$$
\Pr(e_i \text{ survives step } n+1) = 1 - \frac{1}{n+1} \cdot 1 = \frac{n}{n+1}
$$

Wait — more precisely: with probability $1 - k/(n+1)$, **no replacement happens**, and $e_i$ survives. With probability $k/(n+1)$, a replacement happens; $e_i$ survives this replacement only if its own slot is **not** the one chosen. There are $k$ slots, each equally likely to be replaced, so:

$$
\Pr(\text{$e_i$ not chosen for eviction}) = 1 - \frac{1}{k} = \frac{k-1}{k}
$$

Combining via the **law of total probability**:

$$
\Pr(e_i \in S \text{ after step } n+1) = \Pr(e_i \in S \text{ after step } n) \cdot \Pr(\text{survives})
$$

$$
= \frac{k}{n} \cdot \left[ \left(1 - \frac{k}{n+1}\right) \cdot 1 + \frac{k}{n+1} \cdot \frac{k-1}{k} \right]
$$

$$
= \frac{k}{n} \cdot \left[ \frac{n+1-k}{n+1} + \frac{k-1}{n+1} \right]
$$

$$
= \frac{k}{n} \cdot \frac{n+1-k + k - 1}{n+1}
$$

$$
= \frac{k}{n} \cdot \frac{n}{n+1}
$$

$$
= \frac{k}{n+1}
$$

This matches the invariant for $n+1$ elements. **Hence, by induction, the invariant holds for all $n \geq k$.** $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — AMS F2 Moment Estimator

### Setup
We have a stream of $N$ elements drawn from a universe $U = \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$. The frequency of element $i$ is $m_i$, and $\sum_i m_i = N$. We want to estimate:

$$
F_2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i^2
$$

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the random variable.** Pick a random stream position $p \in \{1, \ldots, N\}$ uniformly and let $X = m_i$ where $e_p = i$ is the element at that position. Equivalently, for each position $p$, we store the count of the element at that position seen so far, multiplied by a **Rademacher random variable** $r_p \in \{-1, +1\}$:

$$
X_p = m_i \cdot r_p \quad \text{where } e_p = i
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\mathbb{E}[X_p^2]$.** Since $r_p^2 = 1$:

$$
\mathbb{E}[X_p^2] = \mathbb{E}[m_i^2] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Pr(e_p = i) \cdot m_i^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{m_i}{N} \cdot m_i^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i^2 = \frac{F_2}{N}
$$

**Step 3 — The estimator.** The product $N \cdot X_p^2$ is an unbiased estimator of $F_2$:

$$
\mathbb{E}[N \cdot X_p^2] = F_2
$$

**Step 4 — Reduce variance via averaging.** Take $s$ independent random positions, compute $X_{p_1}^2, \ldots, X_{p_s}^2$, and average:

$$
Y_j = N \cdot \frac{1}{s} \sum_{t=1}^{s} X_{p_t}^{(j)\,2}
$$

**Step 5 — Median trick for tail bound.** Take $r$ such independent averages $Y_1, \ldots, Y_r$ and return the **median**:

$$
\hat{F}_2 = \text{median}(Y_1, Y_2, \ldots, Y_r)
$$

**Step 6 — Variance bound.** By independence:

$$
\text{Var}(X_p^2) = \mathbb{E}[X_p^4] - (\mathbb{E}[X_p^2])^2 \leq F_2^2 - \frac{F_2^2}{N^2} \leq F_2^2
$$

Hence $\text{Var}(Y_j) \leq F_2^2 / s$, and standard deviation $\sigma \leq F_2 / \sqrt{s}$.

By **Chebyshev's inequality**, to achieve $\Pr(\vert Y_j - F_2\vert \geq \epsilon F_2) \leq \delta$, we need $s = O(1/\epsilon^2 \delta)$ and the median-of-medians further reduces the failure probability exponentially to $r = O(\log(1/\delta))$.

**Total space required:** $O(s \cdot r) = O\left(\dfrac{1}{\epsilon^2} \log \dfrac{1}{\delta}\right)$ counters — **independent of $N$**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bloom Filter False Positive Probability

### Setup
Bloom filter has $m$ bits, $h$ hash functions, and $n$ inserted items. Each bit is set to 1 with probability (after all $n$ insertions):

$$
p_{\text{bit set}} = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{m}\right)^{hn} \approx 1 - e^{-hn/m}
$$

### False Positive Derivation

A false positive on element $y$ (not inserted) requires **all $h$ bit positions** $h_1(y), h_2(y), \ldots, h_h(y)$ to be 1. Assuming independence (the standard approximation):

$$
f = \Pr(\text{false positive}) = (p_{\text{bit set}})^h = \left(1 - e^{-hn/m}\right)^h
$$

### Optimal $h$

To minimize $f$, take the derivative with respect to $h$ and set to zero. Letting $\rho = m/n$ (bits per item):

$$
f^{*} = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\rho \ln 2} = (0.6185)^{\rho}
$$

Therefore:

$$
h^{*} = \frac{m}{n} \ln 2 = \rho \ln 2 \approx 0.6931 \cdot \rho
$$

For example, with $\rho = 10$ bits/item, $h^* \approx 7$ hash functions and $f^* \approx 0.008$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Technique | Space | Query | Insert | False Positive / Error | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Reservoir Sampling** | $O(k)$ | exact | $O(1)$ expected | none (exact sample) | uniform random sample |
| **Bernoulli Sampling** | $O(\vert S\vert)$ variable | exact | $O(1)$ | none | simple random subsets |
| **Bloom Filter** | $O(m)$ bits | $O(h)$ | $O(h)$ | FP only, no FN | membership |
| **Count-Min Sketch** | $O(w \cdot d)$ | $O(d)$ | $O(d)$ | additive over-estimate | frequency / heavy hitters |
| **AMS Sketch** | $O\left(\frac{1}{\epsilon^2}\log\frac{1}{\delta}\right)$ | $O(1)$ sketch | $O(1)$ per item | $(\epsilon, \delta)$ | F2, self-join, entropy |
| **HyperLogLog** | $O(\log \log n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $\approx 1.04/\sqrt{m}$ | distinct counting |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Stream Processing Pipeline with Sampling Layer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Data Source - Sensors Logs Tweets] --> B[Stream Ingestion - Kafka Kinesis]
    B --> C{Sampling Decision Layer}
    C -->|Bernoulli p| D[Bernoulli Sampler]
    C -->|Reservoir k| E[Reservoir Sampler - Algorithm R]
    C -->|Sketch| F[Sketching Layer]
    D --> G[Analytics Engine]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    F --> F1[Bloom Filter]
    F --> F2[Count-Min Sketch]
    F --> F3[AMS F2 Estimator]
    F --> F4[HyperLogLog]
    G --> H[Approximate Query Results]
    G --> I[Alerts and Dashboards]
```

## 4.2 Reservoir Sampling State Machine (Algorithm R)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Fill: First k elements arrive
    Fill --> Ready: Reservoir full
    Ready --> Decide: Element i greater than k arrives
    Decide --> Generate: Pick random j in 1 to i
    Generate --> Check: Is j less than or equal to k
    Check --> Replace: Yes
    Check --> Discard: No
    Replace --> Ready: Update reservoir index j
    Discard --> Ready: Ignore element
    Ready --> [*]: Stream ends
```

## 4.3 Bloom Filter Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputX[Input Element x]
        X1[Hash h1 of x]
        X2[Hash h2 of x]
        X3[Hash h3 of x]
    end
    X1 --> B1[Bit 1]
    X2 --> B2[Bit 2]
    X3 --> B3[Bit 3]
    B1 --> AND{AND Gate}
    B2 --> AND
    B3 --> AND
    AND -->|all 1| FP[Possibly Present - False Positive Risk]
    AND -->|any 0| ABS[Definitely Absent]
```

## 4.4 Count-Min Sketch Update and Query Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Stream Item - i, count c] --> H1[Hash h1]
    A --> H2[Hash h2]
    A --> H3[Hash h3]
    H1 --> R1[Row 1 increment cell h1 i by c]
    H2 --> R2[Row 2 increment cell h2 i by c]
    H3 --> R3[Row 3 increment cell h3 i by c]
    R1 --> Q[Query - estimate f i]
    R2 --> Q
    R3 --> Q
    Q --> Min[Return minimum across rows]
```

## 4.5 Sampling Strategy Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What is the query type?}
    Q1 -->|Random subset| Q2{Need fixed size?}
    Q1 -->|Frequency count| Q3[Use Count-Min Sketch]
    Q1 -->|Membership| Q4[Use Bloom Filter]
    Q1 -->|Distinct count| Q5[Use HyperLogLog]
    Q1 -->|Self join size| Q6[Use AMS F2 Estimator]
    Q1 -->|Jaccard similarity| Q7[Use Min-wise Sampling]
    Q2 -->|Yes| R1[Reservoir Sampling]
    Q2 -->|No| R2[Bernoulli Sampling]
    Q3 --> S[(epsilon delta) Sub-linear]
    Q4 --> S
    Q5 --> S
    Q6 --> S
    Q7 --> S
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define stream sampling. List any two stream sampling techniques with one real-world application each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Stream sampling is the process of selecting a representative subset of elements from a continuous, unbounded data stream using sub-linear space and single-pass access, enabling approximate query answers with provable error bounds.

> **Two techniques with applications (1.5 marks):**
> 1. **Reservoir Sampling** — Used in network packet sampling (e.g., sFlow in Cisco switches) to maintain a uniform random sample of fixed size $k$ from an unknown stream length.
> 2. **Bloom Filter** — Used in HBase and Bitcoin to check membership of a key in a distributed set, avoiding expensive disk lookups.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]
**State the Chernoff bound for sampling and explain its significance in stream processing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Statement (1.5 marks):** For i.i.d. random variables $X_1, \ldots, X_n$ with mean $\mu$:
> $$\Pr(\vert \bar{X} - \mu\vert \geq \epsilon \mu) \leq 2 e^{-\epsilon^2 n / 3}$$

> **Significance (1.5 marks):** The Chernoff bound guarantees that the sample mean concentrates around the true mean **exponentially fast** with the number of samples. In stream processing, this allows us to determine **a-priori** the minimum sample size $k = O\left(\frac{1}{\epsilon^2} \log \frac{1}{\delta}\right)$ needed to achieve $(\epsilon, \delta)$-approximation, making resource planning deterministic.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Solve both sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

#### (a) [7 Marks] Describe Reservoir Sampling (Algorithm R). Prove that every element $e_i$ in the stream has probability $k/n$ of being in the final reservoir of size $k$ after processing $n$ elements.

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm Description (3 marks):**

> **Algorithm R (Vitter, 1985):**
> 1. Maintain a reservoir array $S[1 \ldots k]$ of fixed size $k$.
> 2. **Initialize:** For the first $k$ stream elements, store them in $S$.
> 3. **Process:** For each subsequent element $e_i$ (where $i > k$):
>    - Generate $j \sim \text{Uniform}(1, i)$.
>    - If $j \leq k$, set $S[j] \leftarrow e_i$.
>    - Else discard $e_i$.

**Proof of Invariant (4 marks):**

We prove by induction on $n$ (number of elements processed) that $\Pr(e_i \in S) = k/n$ for all $i \leq n$.

**Base Case** ($n = k$): Each $e_i$ occupies a unique slot, so $\Pr(e_i \in S) = 1 = k/k$. ✓

**Inductive Step:** Assume after $n$ elements, $\Pr(e_i \in S) = k/n$. Now process $e_{n+1}$.

The algorithm picks $j \sim \text{Uniform}(1, n+1)$. Event $A$ = "replacement happens" = "$j \leq k$", so $\Pr(A) = k/(n+1)$.

For an existing $e_i$ to remain in $S$:
- No replacement occurs ($\Pr = 1 - k/(n+1)$) — $e_i$ stays.
- Replacement occurs AND $e_i$'s slot is not the one chosen: $\Pr = \frac{k}{n+1} \cdot \frac{k-1}{k} = \frac{k-1}{n+1}$.

$$
\Pr(\text{$e_i$ survives}) = 1 - \frac{k}{n+1} + \frac{k-1}{n+1} \cdot 1 = \frac{n}{n+1}
$$

Wait — we need to be careful. Total probability:

$$
\Pr(e_i \in S \text{ after step } n+1) = \Pr(e_i \in S \text{ after } n) \cdot \Pr(\text{survives} \mid e_i \in S)
$$

$$
= \frac{k}{n} \cdot \frac{n}{n+1} = \frac{k}{n+1}
$$

This matches the invariant at $n+1$. By induction, the invariant holds for all $n \geq k$. $\blacksquare$

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the Count-Min Sketch data structure. Derive the space complexity required to achieve an $(\epsilon, \delta)$-approximation for frequency estimation.

**Model Solution:**

**Structure (3 marks):**

> A Count-Min Sketch is a 2D array $C$ of size $d \times w$ where $d$ rows correspond to $d$ independent hash functions $h_1, \ldots, h_d : U \to \{1, \ldots, w\}$. Each cell $C[j][\cdot]$ stores a non-negative integer counter.

**Update:** For each stream element $i$ with count increment $c$:
$$C[j][h_j(i)] \leftarrow C[j][h_j(i)] + c \quad \forall j \in \{1, \ldots, d\}$$

**Query:** Estimate $\hat{f}(i) = \min_{j} C[j][h_j(i)]$.

**Error Analysis (4 marks):**

For a fixed row $j$, the expected value of cell $C[j][h_j(i)]$ is:

$$
\mathbb{E}[C[j][h_j(i)]] = f(i) + \frac{N - f(i)}{w} \leq f(i) + \frac{N}{w}
$$

(where $N$ is the total stream length) because each other element contributes +1 with probability $1/w$.

For the over-count $\Delta = C[j][h_j(i)] - f(i) \geq 0$, by **Markov's inequality**:

$$
\Pr(\Delta \geq \epsilon N) \leq \frac{\mathbb{E}[\Delta]}{\epsilon N} \leq \frac{1}{\epsilon w}
$$

Set $w = \lceil e/\epsilon \rceil$ to get per-row failure probability $1/e$.

The minimum across $d$ rows fails only if **all** rows fail. By independence:

$$
\Pr(\hat{f}(i) \geq f(i) + \epsilon N) \leq \left(\frac{1}{e}\right)^d = e^{-d}
$$

Set $d = \lceil \ln(1/\delta) \rceil$ to achieve failure probability $\leq \delta$.

**Total space:**

$$
S = w \cdot d = O\left(\frac{1}{\epsilon} \log \frac{1}{\delta}\right) \text{ counters}
$$

This is **sub-linear in $N$** and independent of the stream size. $\blacksquare$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the AMS F2 estimator for estimating the second frequency moment of a data stream. Show that it provides an $(\epsilon, \delta)$-approximation using $O\left(\frac{1}{\epsilon^2} \log \frac{1}{\delta}\right)$ space.

**Model Solution:**

**Problem Setup (2 marks):**

> Given a stream of $N$ items from universe $U$, with frequencies $m_1, m_2, \ldots, m_n$ where $\sum_i m_i = N$, estimate:
> $$F_2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i^2$$
> This is the **second frequency moment** — used in self-join size estimation, degree distribution analysis, and network anomaly detection.

**Estimator Construction (3 marks):**

> Pick a random stream position $p \in \{1, \ldots, N\}$ uniformly. Let $X = m_i \cdot r_p$ where $e_p = i$ and $r_p \in \{-1, +1\}$ is a Rademacher random variable.
>
> **Unbiasedness:**
> $$\mathbb{E}[X^2] = \mathbb{E}[m_i^2] = \sum_i \frac{m_i}{N} \cdot m_i^2 = \frac{F_2}{N}$$
> So $N \cdot X^2$ is an unbiased estimator of $F_2$.

**Variance Reduction via Median Trick (2 marks):**

> Take $s$ random positions, average to get $Y_j = N \cdot \frac{1}{s} \sum_t X_t^2$, then return the median of $r$ such averages.
>
> By **Chebyshev**: $\Pr(\vert Y_j - F_2\vert \geq \epsilon F_2) \leq \frac{\text{Var}(Y_j)}{\epsilon^2 F_2^2} \leq \frac{1}{s \epsilon^2}$.
>
> Set $s = O(1/\epsilon^2)$ for per-average failure probability $1/3$.
>
> Median-of-$r$ means failure only if $> r/2$ averages fail, and by **Chernoff** this probability is exponentially small: $r = O(\log(1/\delta))$ suffices.

**Final Space Complexity:**

$$
\text{Space} = s \cdot r = O\left(\frac{1}{\epsilon^2} \log \frac{1}{\delta}\right) \text{ words}
$$

**This is sub-linear in $N$** — the estimator works in a single pass with sub-linear memory. $\blacksquare$

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Describe the Bloom Filter. Derive the formula for the false positive probability and determine the optimal number of hash functions.

**Model Solution:**

**Structure (2 marks):**

> A Bloom filter is a bit array $B[1 \ldots m]$, initially all 0, with $h$ independent hash functions $h_1, \ldots, h_h : U \to \{1, \ldots, m\}$.
>
> **Insert($x$):** Set $B[h_j(x)] = 1$ for all $j \in \{1, \ldots, h\}$.
>
> **Query($y$):** Return 1 iff $B[h_j(y)] = 1$ for all $j \in \{1, \ldots, h\}$.

**False Positive Derivation (3 marks):**

> After inserting $n$ items, the probability a single bit remains 0 is:
> $$p_0 = \left(1 - \frac{1}{m}\right)^{hn} \approx e^{-hn/m}$$
>
> So the probability a bit is 1 is $1 - e^{-hn/m}$.
>
> A false positive on $y \notin$ set requires all $h$ positions $h_1(y), \ldots, h_h(y)$ to be 1 (assuming independence):
> $$f = (1 - e^{-hn/m})^h$$

**Optimal $h$ (2 marks):**

> Let $\rho = m/n$ (bits per item). Minimize $f$ with respect to $h$ by taking $\frac{df}{dh} = 0$:
>
> Differentiating $\ln f = h \ln(1 - e^{-\rho h})$:
> $$\frac{f'}{f} = \ln(1 - e^{-\rho h}) + h \cdot \frac{\rho e^{-\rho h}}{1 - e^{-\rho h}} = 0$$
>
> Setting $1 - e^{-\rho h} = 1/2$ (the optimal bit density), we get $e^{-\rho h} = 1/2$, hence:
> $$\boxed{h^* = \frac{m}{n} \ln 2 = \rho \ln 2 \approx 0.6931 \cdot \rho}$$
>
> Substituting back:
> $$f^* = (1/2)^{h^*} = (0.6185)^{\rho}$$
>
> For $\rho = 10$ bits/item, $h^* \approx 7$ and $f^* \approx 0.008$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks (PECNT746):**
> 1. **Confusing sample probability with replacement probability** in reservoir sampling — they are different! Replacement probability at step $i$ is $k/i$, but final membership probability is $k/n$. (Loses 2–3 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting the "single-pass" constraint** in stream algorithms. Algorithms that re-scan the stream are not valid. (Loses 1 mark)
> 3. **Confusing Bloom Filter false positive with false negative.** Bloom Filters have **no false negatives** — write this explicitly. (Loses 1 mark)
> 4. **Not stating the $(\epsilon, \delta)$ parameters** when deriving sketch complexity — KTU expects you to mention both. (Loses 1 mark)
> 5. **Using absolute value bars $\vert x \vert$ in markdown tables** breaks the table — examiners reading your script will be confused. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ instead.
> 6. **Skipping the base case** in the reservoir sampling proof — full marks require the induction base AND inductive step.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Sampling Techniques for Efficient Stream Processing**

- [ ] **Reservoir Sampling (Algorithm R):** Reservoir size $k$ is fixed; replacement probability at step $i > k$ is $k/i$; every element has uniform probability $k/n$ of being in the final sample.
- [ ] **Bernoulli Sampling:** Each element independently included with probability $p$; expected size $np$, variance $np(1-p)$.
- [ ] **Bloom Filter:** $m$ bits, $h$ hash functions, no false negatives, false positive rate $\approx (1 - e^{-hn/m})^h$.
- [ ] **Optimal Bloom hash count:** $h^* = (m/n) \ln 2 \approx 0.6931 \cdot (m/n)$.
- [ ] **Count-Min Sketch:** $d \times w$ array; returns $\min_j C[j][h_j(x)]$; space $O((1/\epsilon) \log(1/\delta))$.
- [ ] **AMS F2 Estimator:** $F_2 = \sum m_i^2$; uses Rademacher variables; variance reduced via median-of-averages; space $O((1/\epsilon^2) \log(1/\delta))$.
- [ ] **Min-wise Sampling (Jaccard):** $\Pr(\min h(A) = \min h(B)) = \vert A \cap B\vert / \vert A \cup B\vert$.
- [ ] **HyperLogLog:** Distinct counting in $O(\log \log n)$ space; standard error $\approx 1.04/\sqrt{m}$.
- [ ] **Chernoff Bound:** Sample mean concentration $\Pr(\vert \bar{X} - \mu\vert \geq \epsilon \mu) \leq 2e^{-\epsilon^2 n/3}$.
- [ ] **Sample size for $(\epsilon, \delta)$:** $k = O((1/\epsilon^2) \log(1/\delta))$.
- [ ] **All stream algorithms are single-pass and sub-linear** in space — this is the defining KTU requirement.
- [ ] **Real-world tools:** Spark `sample()`, HBase Bloom filters, Twitter heavy hitters (CM Sketch), Google PageRank (Min-wise hashing).
- [ ] **Always state assumptions:** Independence of hash functions, uniform stream positions, single-pass access.
- [ ] **Formulas to memorize cold:** $f_{\text{Bloom}} = (1 - e^{-hn/m})^h$, $h^* = (m/n)\ln 2$, $\text{CM space} = O((1/\epsilon)\log(1/\delta))$, $\text{AMS space} = O((1/\epsilon^2)\log(1/\delta))$.
- [ ] **Common exam traps:** Watch for confusion between $k/i$ and $k/n$; remember Bloom Filter has no false negatives; always specify $(\epsilon, \delta)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT746 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Filtering Streams - Bloom Filter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bloom Filter (BF):** A Bloom Filter is a **space-efficient, probabilistic data structure** conceived by Burton Howard Bloom in 1970, used to test whether an element is a member of a set. It is characterized by two key behavioral properties:
> 1. **No False Negatives:** If the filter reports an element is *not* in the set, it is *definitely* absent.
> 2. **Probabilistic False Positives:** If the filter reports an element *is* in the set, there is a tunable probability $\epsilon$ that the element is actually absent.

Mathematically, a Bloom Filter consists of a **bit array** of size $m$ bits (initially all 0) and $k$ independent **uniform hash functions** $h_1, h_2, \ldots, h_k$, each mapping an element $x$ to one of the $m$ array positions: $h_i : U \rightarrow \{1, 2, \ldots, m\}$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Chalkboard of Arrivals"**
>
> Imagine a large classroom chalkboard with 100 blank squares ($m = 100$). When a new student named **Alice** enters, instead of writing her full name, the teacher applies **three different rules (hash functions)** — for example:
> - Rule 1: *Number of letters in name* → square #5
> - Rule 2: *Sum of alphabetical positions* → square #47
> - Rule 3: *Birth month multiplied by 10* → square #20
>
> The teacher ticks (sets to 1) squares 5, 47, and 20.
>
> Later, when a new student **Bob** asks, *"Has Alice arrived?"* — the teacher applies the same three rules to Alice's name. If **all three squares** are already ticked, the teacher says *probably yes*. But Bob might share letter counts or birth-month patterns with Alice, leading to a **false positive**. The teacher can **never** say Alice has *not* arrived unless at least one square is empty — guaranteeing **no false negatives**.

## 1.3 Why Bloom Filters Are Critical in Big Data

| Stream Property | Implication for Filtering |
|---|---|
| Data is **infinite** / unbounded | Cannot store all elements |
| Latency must be **sub-millisecond** | Database lookups too slow |
| Memory is **finite and costly** | Hash tables blow up RAM |
| Queries are **membership-based** | "Have I seen this before?" |

**Standard Metrics in Bloom Filter Theory:**
- $m$ = number of bits in the filter (array size)
- $n$ = number of elements inserted
- $k$ = number of hash functions
- $\epsilon$ = target false positive probability
- $p$ = current false positive rate (after $n$ insertions)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics

The Bloom Filter operates in two well-defined phases: **Insertion** and **Query**.

### 2.1.1 Insertion of Element $x$

For each hash function $h_i$ ($i = 1, 2, \ldots, k$):
1. Compute the hash position: $h_i(x) \in \{1, \ldots, m\}$
2. Set the corresponding bit to 1: $\text{BF}[h_i(x)] \leftarrow 1$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Set" only and never "Unset"?** Because two distinct elements may share a hash position. Unsetting a bit would create a **false negative** — breaking the contract.

### 2.1.2 Query for Element $x$

For each hash function $h_i$ ($i = 1, 2, \ldots, k$):
1. Compute the hash position: $h_i(x) \in \{1, \ldots, m\}$
2. Check the bit: if $\text{BF}[h_i(x)] = 0$, immediately return **"Definitely NOT in set"**
3. If **all** $k$ positions are 1, return **"PROBABLY in set"** (with probability $\epsilon$ of being wrong)

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind the Design

| Design Choice | Rationale |
|---|---|
| Bit array (not integer array) | Minimizes memory by factor of 8 vs byte array, 32 vs int array |
| Multiple hash functions | Distributes signal across array, lowers collision-based false positives |
| One-way (no deletion in classic BF) | Avoids false negatives at the cost of dynamic updates |
| Probabilistic answer | Trades exactness for **constant-time**, **O(1)** membership testing |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Equations for KTU Board Exam**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $p = \left(1 - e^{-kn/m}\right)^k$ | **False positive probability** after $n$ insertions | $0 < p < 1$ |
| 2 | $p = \left(1 - \frac{1}{m}\right)^{kn} \approx e^{-kn/m}$ | Probability a **specific bit is still 0** after $n$ insertions, $k$ hashes each | Approximation valid for large $m$ |
| 3 | $k_{\text{opt}} = \frac{m}{n} \ln 2$ | **Optimal number of hash functions** to minimize $p$ for given $m, n$ | Real-valued |
| 4 | $m_{\min} = -\frac{n \ln \epsilon}{(\ln 2)^2}$ | **Minimum bit-array size** to achieve target false positive rate $\epsilon$ | Integer (round up) |
| 5 | $p_{\min} = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^k = 2^{-k}$ | **Minimum achievable false positive rate** at optimal $k$ | $0 < p < 1$ |
| 6 | $\text{Memory} = m \text{ bits} = \frac{m}{8} \text{ bytes}$ | Total space complexity | Bytes |
| 7 | $\text{Time}_{\text{insert}} = \text{Time}_{\text{query}} = O(k)$ | Time complexity per operation | Hash evaluations |
| 8 | $\text{FPR} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{k}\left(1 - p_{\text{bit},i}\right)$ | General FPR formula (alternative form) | $0 < \text{FPR} < 1$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical Pipe Symbol Warning:** In KTU exam scripts, never write absolute value as $\vert x \vert$ inline in tables. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or simply $x$ when context (e.g., $x \geq 0$) makes it unambiguous. Same applies to all formulas above — bracket notations are clean.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Web Crawlers (Google, Bing)** | Avoid re-crawling URLs already indexed — billions of pages checked in RAM |
| **Distributed Databases (Cassandra, HBase)** | Quickly determine if row/column exists before disk lookup — saves I/O |
| **Network Routers** | Detect spam/malicious packets via blacklist membership |
| **Bioinformatics** | Detect k-mers in genome assembly (e.g., SPAdes, ABySS) |
| **CDNs (Squid, Varnish)** | Cache-hit detection before expensive backend call |
| **Cryptocurrency (Bitcoin SPV)** | Lightweight client checks if transaction is in block |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the False Positive Probability $p$

This is the **most exam-relevant derivation** in KTU Big Data Analytics. Every step must be reproduced.

### Step 1: Probability that a Specific Bit Remains 0 After ONE Hash

When we insert an element, $k$ hash functions each choose a position uniformly at random from $m$ slots.

**Probability that hash function $h_i$ does NOT pick a particular bit:**
$$P(\text{bit } j \text{ untouched by } h_i) = 1 - \frac{1}{m}$$

**Reasoning:** There are $m$ equally likely output positions for $h_i$, and only 1 of them is bit $j$. So the probability of NOT landing on bit $j$ is $(m-1)/m = 1 - 1/m$.

### Step 2: Probability Bit Stays 0 After Inserting ONE Element with $k$ Hashes

All $k$ hash functions must simultaneously miss bit $j$. Since hash outputs are independent:

$$P(\text{bit } j \text{ remains 0 after one insertion}) = \left(1 - \frac{1}{m}\right)^k$$

**Conversion Logic:** The events "$h_1$ avoids bit $j$", "$h_2$ avoids bit $j$", ..., "$h_k$ avoids bit $j$" are independent under the assumption that the hash functions are independent and uniform. By the multiplication rule for independent events, we raise $(1 - 1/m)$ to the power $k$.

### Step 3: Probability Bit Stays 0 After Inserting $n$ Elements

After $n$ insertions, the same logic applies $n$ times (still assuming independence across insertions):

$$P(\text{bit } j \text{ still 0 after } n \text{ insertions}) = \left(1 - \frac{1}{m}\right)^{kn}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Each of the $n$ insertions independently has probability $(1 - 1/m)^k$ of leaving bit $j$ untouched. Multiplying across all $n$ insertions gives the cumulative probability.

### Step 4: Apply the Standard Exponential Approximation

For large $m$ and small $1/m$, we invoke the limit identity:
$$\lim_{m \to \infty}\left(1 - \frac{1}{m}\right)^{m} = \frac{1}{e}$$

Rewriting our expression by multiplying and dividing exponents:
$$\left(1 - \frac{1}{m}\right)^{kn} = \left[\left(1 - \frac{1}{m}\right)^{m}\right]^{kn/m} \approx \left(\frac{1}{e}\right)^{kn/m} = e^{-kn/m}$$

**Conversion Logic:** We factor out an exponent of $m$ from $kn$ to exploit the well-known exponential limit. As $m \to \infty$, this converges to $e^{-kn/m}$.

Let us denote:
$$p_{\text{zero}} = \left(1 - \frac{1}{m}\right)^{kn} \approx e^{-kn/m}$$

### Step 5: Probability of a False Positive

A false positive for query element $y$ (NOT in the set) occurs if **all $k$ hash positions of $y$ happen to be 1** purely due to other elements.

Assuming independence of bit-states (a slight theoretical relaxation that holds well empirically):

$$p = P(\text{false positive}) = (p_{1})^k = \left(1 - p_{\text{zero}}\right)^k$$

Substituting the exponential form:

$$p = \left(1 - e^{-kn/m}\right)^k$$

**Conversion Logic:** Each of the $k$ hash outputs of $y$ must land on a bit that is 1. Since the bits are 1 with probability $(1 - p_{\text{zero}})$ and the queries are independent, we raise this probability to the $k$-th power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result (KTU Board-Standard Form):**
> $$\boxed{\,p = \left(1 - e^{-kn/m}\right)^k\,}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Optimal Number of Hash Functions $k_{\text{opt}}$

### Step 1: Take the Natural Logarithm of $p$

To minimize $p$, we equivalently minimize $\ln p$:

$$\ln p = k \cdot \ln\!\left(1 - e^{-kn/m}\right)$$

### Step 2: Differentiate with Respect to $k$

Treating $m$ and $n$ as constants and letting $g = e^{-kn/m}$:

$$\frac{d(\ln p)}{dk} = \ln(1 - g) + k \cdot \frac{1}{1 - g} \cdot \left(- \frac{dg}{dk}\right)$$

Compute $dg/dk$:
$$\frac{dg}{dk} = -\frac{n}{m} \cdot e^{-kn/m} = -\frac{n}{m} g$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{d(\ln p)}{dk} = \ln(1 - g) + k \cdot \frac{1}{1 - g} \cdot \frac{n}{m} \cdot g$$

### Step 3: Set the Derivative to Zero (Critical Point)

$$\ln(1 - g) + \frac{kn g}{m(1 - g)} = 0$$

### Step 4: Solve for $g$

Let us denote $r = kn/m$. Then $g = e^{-r}$. The critical-point condition becomes:

$$\ln(1 - e^{-r}) + \frac{r e^{-r}}{1 - e^{-r}} = 0$$

Using the substitution $y = e^{-r}$, note that $1 - y$ simplifies several terms:

$$\ln(1 - y) + \frac{r y}{1 - y} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r = -\frac{(1 - y)\ln(1 - y)}{y}$$

### Step 5: Recognize the Special Solution $r = \ln 2$

Plugging $y = 1/2$ (i.e., $r = \ln 2$):

$$r = -\frac{(1 - 1/2)\ln(1 - 1/2)}{1/2} = -\frac{(1/2)\ln(1/2)}{1/2} = -\ln(1/2) = \ln 2$$

This satisfies the equation. Hence:

$$r_{\text{opt}} = \frac{kn}{m} = \ln 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k_{\text{opt}} = \frac{m}{n}\ln 2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result (KTU Board-Standard Form):**
> $$\boxed{\,k_{\text{opt}} = \frac{m}{n}\ln 2 \approx 0.6931 \cdot \frac{m}{n}\,}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Minimum False Positive Rate $p_{\min}$

Substituting $k_{\text{opt}}$ back into the false positive equation:

$$p_{\min} = \left(1 - e^{-\ln 2}\right)^{k_{\text{opt}}} = \left(1 - \frac{1}{2}\right)^{k_{\text{opt}}} = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{k_{\text{opt}}} = 2^{-k_{\text{opt}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** $p_{\min} = 2^{-k_{\text{opt}}}$. When the optimal $k$ is used, **roughly half the bits are 1 and half are 0** — a balanced state.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A Bloom Filter has $m = 1000$ bits and uses $k = 5$ hash functions. After inserting $n = 100$ elements, compute the expected false positive rate. Is this $k$ optimal?

**Solution:**

Compute $kn/m$:
$$\frac{kn}{m} = \frac{5 \times 100}{1000} = 0.5$$

Compute $e^{-kn/m}$:
$$e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065$$

Compute the false positive probability:
$$p = (1 - 0.6065)^5 = (0.3935)^5$$

$$p \approx 0.3935^2 \times 0.3935^2 \times 0.3935 \approx 0.1549 \times 0.1549 \times 0.3935$$

$$p \approx 0.02399 \times 0.3935 \approx 0.00944 \approx 0.94\%$$

Compute the optimal $k$:
$$k_{\text{opt}} = \frac{m}{n}\ln 2 = \frac{1000}{100} \times 0.6931 = 10 \times 0.6931 = 6.931 \approx 7$$

Since $k = 5 < k_{\text{opt}} = 7$, the filter is **under-hashed** and not operating at minimum FPR.

Recomputing with $k = 7$:
$$p = \left(1 - e^{-7 \times 100/1000}\right)^7 = \left(1 - e^{-0.7}\right)^7 = (1 - 0.4966)^7 = (0.5034)^7$$

$$p \approx 0.5034^7 \approx 0.00819 \approx 0.82\%$$

**Conclusion:** Using $k = 7$ yields a slightly lower false positive rate (~0.82% vs ~0.94%), consistent with theory.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Functional Architecture of a Bloom Filter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INPUT ELEMENT x"] --> B["HASH FUNCTION 1 h1"]
    A --> C["HASH FUNCTION 2 h2"]
    A --> D["HASH FUNCTION k hk"]

    B --> E["POSITION p1 MOD m"]
    C --> F["POSITION p2 MOD m"]
    D --> G["POSITION pk MOD m"]

    E --> H["BIT ARRAY size m all zeros initially"]
    F --> H
    G --> H

    H --> I{"INSERT OR QUERY"}
    I -->|INSERT| J["SET BF p1 1 SET BF p2 1 SET BF pk 1"]
    I -->|QUERY| K{"ALL k BITS EQUAL 1"}

    K -->|NO ANY BIT IS 0| L["DEFINITELY NOT PRESENT NO FALSE NEGATIVE"]
    K -->|YES ALL BITS ARE 1| M["PROBABLY PRESENT POSSIBLE FALSE POSITIVE"]

    style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    style H fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    style L fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style M fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    style K fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Bit-Array State Transition Through Operations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["STATE 0 EMPTY FILTER m bits all 0"] -->|INSERT element a via h1 h2 h3| S2["STATE 1 THREE BITS SET positions p1 p2 p3 equal 1"]
    S2 -->|INSERT element b via h1 h2 h3| S3["STATE 2 SIX BITS SET some positions may overlap with state 1"]
    S3 -->|QUERY element c not in set| Q1["COMPUTE h1 c h2 c h3 c"]
    Q1 --> Q2{"ANY RESULTING BIT IS 0"}
    Q2 -->|YES| Q3["RETURN NOT IN SET"]
    Q2 -->|NO| Q4["RETURN PROBABLY IN SET WITH FPR equal p"]

    style S1 fill:#FAFAFA,stroke:#424242,color:#000
    style S2 fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    style S3 fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    style Q3 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style Q4 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    style Q2 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
```

## 4.3 False Positive Mechanism (Block-Level View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1["ELEMENT y not in set"] --> H1["HASH y gives positions q1 q2 q3"]
    H1 --> BA["BIT ARRAY currently contains bits set by OTHER elements z1 z2 z3 zn"]
    BA --> COLLIDE{"DO q1 q2 q3 COINCIDE WITH SET BITS FROM OTHERS"}

    COLLIDE -->|NO any qi is 0| R1["CORRECT NEGATIVE OUTPUT"]
    COLLIDE -->|YES all qi equal 1 by coincidence| R2["FALSE POSITIVE OUTPUT"]

    style X1 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    style BA fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    style R1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style R2 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    style COLLIDE fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Trade-Off Triangle: $m$, $n$, $k$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M["M BIT ARRAY SIZE fixed memory budget"] --> K["K NUMBER OF HASH FUNCTIONS"]
    N["N NUMBER OF ELEMENTS scale of dataset"] --> K
    M --> P["P FALSE POSITIVE RATE"]
    N --> P
    K --> P

    K -.->|too small k| P1["UNDERSATURATED BIT ARRAY wastes space"]
    K -.->|too large k| P2["OVERSATURATED BIT ARRAY many collisions"]
    K -.->|optimal k equals m over n times ln 2| P3["MINIMUM FALSE POSITIVE RATE"]

    style M fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    style N fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style K fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    style P fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000
    style P1 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    style P2 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    style P3 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
```
