# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Bloom Filter. Mention any two advantages of using a Bloom Filter.**

**Model Answer:**

A Bloom Filter is a **space-efficient probabilistic data structure** used for membership testing of an element in a set, employing a bit array of size $m$ and $k$ independent hash functions. It guarantees no false negatives but may produce false positives with probability $p = (1 - e^{-kn/m})^k$.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Space Efficiency:** Requires only $m$ bits (often a few MB) to test membership of billions of elements — orders of magnitude smaller than hash tables.
2. **Constant-Time Operations:** Both insertion and query take $O(k)$ time, independent of $n$ — ideal for high-throughput stream processing.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between false positive and false negative errors in the context of a Bloom Filter. Why is one guaranteed absent in Bloom Filters?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | False Positive | False Negative |
|---|---|---|
| Meaning | Filter says "present", but element is actually absent | Filter says "absent", but element is actually present |
| Existence in BF | Possible (probability $p > 0$) | **Impossible** by design |
| Cause | Hash collisions between distinct elements | None — guaranteed absent |

**Reason for absence of false negatives:** Once a bit is set to 1 by any insertion, it is never reset to 0 (in the standard Bloom Filter). Therefore, any inserted element will always leave its $k$ hash positions at 1. A query returning "absent" requires at least one of the $k$ positions to be 0, which would be impossible for an inserted element — hence **no false negatives**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Question A(a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the structure and working of a Bloom Filter with a neat diagram. Derive the equation for the false positive probability.

**Model Solution:**

**Structure:** A Bloom Filter consists of a bit array $\text{BF}[1 \ldots m]$ initialized to all zeros and $k$ independent uniform hash functions $h_1, h_2, \ldots, h_k$ mapping the universe $U$ to $\{1, 2, \ldots, m\}$.

**Working — Insertion:**
For element $x$, for $i = 1$ to $k$: set $\text{BF}[h_i(x)] \leftarrow 1$.

**Working — Query:**
For element $y$, for $i = 1$ to $k$: if $\text{BF}[h_i(y)] = 0$, return "**definitely NOT in set**". If all $k$ bits are 1, return "**probably in set**".

**Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 (High-Level Functional Architecture).

**Derivation of False Positive Probability:**

Step 1: After one insertion with $k$ hashes, the probability that a particular bit $j$ remains 0 is $(1 - 1/m)^k$.

Step 2: After $n$ insertions (assuming independence):
$$P(\text{bit } j = 0) = \left(1 - \frac{1}{m}\right)^{kn}$$

Step 3: Apply the limit approximation $(1 - 1/m)^m \to 1/e$:
$$P(\text{bit } j = 0) \approx e^{-kn/m}$$

Step 4: A false positive occurs when all $k$ hash positions of a non-member element $y$ coincidentally land on bits that are 1. Assuming independence:
$$p = \left[1 - e^{-kn/m}\right]^k$$

> **Final Equation:** $\boxed{p = (1 - e^{-kn/m})^k}$

---

**Question A(b) [7 Marks]:** A Bloom Filter uses $m = 8000$ bits and $k = 6$ hash functions to store $n = 500$ elements. Calculate (i) the current false positive rate, and (ii) the optimal number of hash functions. (iii) Comment on whether the current $k$ is acceptable.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Current False Positive Rate:**

Compute the exponent:
$$\frac{kn}{m} = \frac{6 \times 500}{8000} = \frac{3000}{8000} = 0.375$$

Compute $e^{-0.375}$:
$$e^{-0.375} \approx 0.6873$$

Compute $p$:
$$p = (1 - 0.6873)^6 = (0.3127)^6$$

Step-by-step calculation:
$$0.3127^2 \approx 0.0978$$
$$0.3127^3 \approx 0.0306$$
$$0.3127^6 \approx 0.0306^2 \approx 0.000936$$

$$p \approx 9.36 \times 10^{-4} \approx 0.094\%$$

**(ii) Optimal Number of Hash Functions:**

$$k_{\text{opt}} = \frac{m}{n}\ln 2 = \frac{8000}{500} \times 0.6931 = 16 \times 0.6931 \approx 11.09$$

Rounding down (since $k$ must be an integer and rounding up degrades performance): $k_{\text{opt}} = 11$.

**(iii) Comment:**

The current $k = 6$ is **less than** $k_{\text{opt}} = 11$. This means the bit array is **undersaturated** — too few hash functions are being used per element. By increasing to $k = 11$, the false positive rate would drop to approximately $p_{\min} = 2^{-11} \approx 1/2048 \approx 0.049\%$, nearly **half** of the current rate. However, the current $k = 6$ still yields a very low FPR (~0.09%) and may be acceptable if computation cost per query is a priority.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks)

### `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Question B(a) [7 Marks]:** With reference to stream filtering, discuss the need for Bloom Filters. Derive the formula for the minimum number of bits required to achieve a target false positive rate $\epsilon$ for $n$ elements.

**Model Solution:**

**Need for Bloom Filters in Stream Filtering:**

In data-stream applications (network monitoring, click-stream analytics, log processing), data arrives continuously and cannot be stored in entirety. The requirements are:
1. **Memory sub-linear in stream size:** Storing every element is impossible.
2. **Constant-time membership queries:** Latency-sensitive applications (e.g., URL deduplication) cannot afford $O(\log n)$ or $O(n)$ lookups.
3. **Approximate answers are tolerable:** A small false-positive probability is acceptable; missing real items is not.

Bloom Filters satisfy all three: $O(k)$ per operation, $m = O(n)$ bits total, and probabilistic but never missing a true positive.

**Derivation of Minimum Bits $m_{\min}$:**

We know the optimal $k$ satisfies:
$$k_{\text{opt}} = \frac{m}{n}\ln 2$$

Substitute this into the false positive equation. At optimal $k$, $kn/m = \ln 2$, so:
$$e^{-kn/m} = e^{-\ln 2} = 1/2$$

Thus the minimum false positive rate is:
$$p_{\min} = (1 - 1/2)^{k_{\text{opt}}} = (1/2)^{k_{\text{opt}}} = 2^{-k_{\text{opt}}}$$

Setting this equal to the target $\epsilon$:
$$\epsilon = 2^{-k_{\text{opt}}}$$

Take $\log_2$ of both sides:
$$\log_2 \epsilon = -k_{\text{opt}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad k_{\text{opt}} = -\log_2 \epsilon = \frac{\ln(1/\epsilon)}{\ln 2}$$

Now substitute $k_{\text{opt}} = (m/n) \ln 2$:
$$\frac{m}{n} \ln 2 = \frac{\ln(1/\epsilon)}{\ln 2}$$

Solve for $m$:
$$m = \frac{n \ln(1/\epsilon)}{(\ln 2)^2} = -\frac{n \ln \epsilon}{(\ln 2)^2}$$

> **Final Formula:**
> $$\boxed{\,m_{\min} = -\frac{n \ln \epsilon}{(\ln 2)^2} \approx 1.44 \cdot n \cdot \log_2(1/\epsilon)\,}$$

---

**Question B(b) [7 Marks]:** Suppose a web crawler has indexed 10 million URLs and you wish to design a Bloom Filter to detect previously visited URLs with a false positive rate of at most 1%. (i) Compute the minimum number of bits $m$ required. (ii) Compute the optimal number of hash functions. (iii) Estimate memory in MB.

**Model Solution:**

Given: $n = 10^7$, $\epsilon = 0.01 = 10^{-2}$.

**(i) Minimum Number of Bits:**

$$m_{\min} = -\frac{n \ln \epsilon}{(\ln 2)^2}$$

Compute $\ln \epsilon$:
$$\ln(0.01) = -4.6052$$

Compute $(\ln 2)^2$:
$$(0.6931)^2 \approx 0.4805$$

Compute $m_{\min}$:
$$m_{\min} = -\frac{10^7 \times (-4.6052)}{0.4805} = \frac{4.6052 \times 10^7}{0.4805} \approx 9.584 \times 10^7$$

$$m_{\min} \approx 95{,}840{,}000 \text{ bits}$$

**(ii) Optimal Number of Hash Functions:**

$$k_{\text{opt}} = \frac{m_{\min}}{n} \ln 2 = \frac{9.584 \times 10^7}{10^7} \times 0.6931 = 9.584 \times 0.6931 \approx 6.64$$

Round to nearest integer: $k_{\text{opt}} = 7$.

**(iii) Memory in MB:**

$$\text{Memory} = \frac{m_{\min}}{8} \text{ bytes} = \frac{95{,}840{,}000}{8} \approx 11{,}980{,}000 \text{ bytes} \approx 11.42 \text{ MB}$$

**Conclusion:** A Bloom Filter of approximately **11.42 MB** with **7 hash functions** is sufficient to track 10 million URLs with a false positive rate of at most 1% — a remarkable memory saving compared to storing all URLs (which would require gigabytes).

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $p$ (false positive probability) with $p_{\text{zero}}$ (probability a bit is 0).** Always clarify which probability you are deriving.
> 2. **Forgetting the exponential approximation step.** Examiners expect the explicit use of $\lim_{m\to\infty}(1-1/m)^m = 1/e$. Skipping this loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Writing $k_{\text{opt}}$ as $\frac{m}{n} \log 2$ instead of $\frac{m}{n} \ln 2$.** The formula uses the **natural logarithm**, not $\log_{10}$ or $\log_2$. This is a frequent error.
> 4. **Rounding $k_{\text{opt}}$ upward instead of downward.** Since increasing $k$ beyond optimal degrades FPR, examiners expect rounding to the nearest integer with justification.
> 5. **Omitting the assumption of independent hash functions.** Always state the assumption that $h_1, \ldots, h_k$ are independent and uniform — without this, the derivation is invalid.
> 6. **Writing "no false positive" instead of "no false negative".** A Bloom Filter can produce false positives but **never** false negatives. Reversing these costs full marks in definition questions.
> 7. **Ignoring units in memory calculations.** $m$ is in **bits**, not bytes. Convert to MB by dividing by $8 \times 10^6$.

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**
>
> - **Definition:** Bloom Filter = bit array + $k$ hash functions, **probabilistic** membership test.
> - **Two Guarantees:** No false negatives **always**; false positives **possible** with rate $p$.
> - **Core Equation:** $p = (1 - e^{-kn/m})^k$ — memorize in this exact form.
> - **Optimal Hashes:** $k_{\text{opt}} = (m/n) \ln 2 \approx 0.6931 \cdot (m/n)$.
> - **Min Bits for target FPR $\epsilon$:** $m_{\min} = -n \ln \epsilon / (\ln 2)^2 \approx 1.44 n \log_2(1/\epsilon)$.
> - **Min FPR at optimal $k$:** $p_{\min} = 2^{-k_{\text{opt}}}$.
> - **Time Complexity:** $O(k)$ for both insert and query — independent of $n$.
> - **Space Complexity:** $m$ bits, typically 10× smaller than equivalent hash table.
> - **No Deletion (Standard BF):** Deletions would cause false negatives. Workarounds → **Counting Bloom Filters** (use counters instead of bits).
> - **Variants to know:** Counting Bloom Filter (deletions), Bloomier Filter (functions), Spectral Bloom Filter (multi-set), Cuckoo Filter (deletions + lower FPR).
> - **Common KTU Traps:** Don't swap "false positive" and "false negative"; remember $\ln 2$ (not $\log_2$); convert bits to MB properly.
> - **Real-World Anchor:** Google's Bigtable uses Bloom Filters to reduce disk lookups for non-existent rows — HBase and Cassandra follow the same pattern.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Count Distinct Problem - Flajolet Martin Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT746 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Count Distinct Problem - Flajolet Martin Algorithm ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Count-Distinct Problem** (also called the **Cardinality Estimation Problem**) asks: *"Given a multi-set or data stream, how many unique (distinct) elements are present?"* In the context of Big Data, the universe $\mathcal{U}$ is typically enormous (terabytes to petabytes), making exact counting infeasible due to memory, time, and storage constraints. The **Flajolet–Martin Algorithm (FM Algorithm)**, proposed by Philippe Flajolet and G. Nigel Martin in 1985, is a **probabilistic, streaming, constant-space, single-pass algorithm** that produces an unbiased estimate of the number of distinct elements in a data stream. It exploits the statistical distribution of the *trailing binary zeros* produced by a uniform hash function applied to the stream elements. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PECNT746 – Module 1):** > The Flajolet–Martin algorithm is a cornerstone probabilistic data structure lesson. Examiners expect students to (i) state the algorithm, (ii) explain the role of hash functions, (iii) derive the estimator $2^{\hat{R}}$, and (iv) describe accuracy improvements via *median of means*. ### 1.2 Real-World Intuition (Plain English Analogy) > [!NOTE] > **Analogy – "The Lottery Counter":** > Imagine you are standing at the entrance of a stadium that has hosted a year-long sports event, and you want to estimate **how many unique spectators** have entered — but you are only allowed to remember **one number**. > - You give every spectator a randomly drawn *binary ticket* (the hash output). > - The probability that a ticket ends in **$r$ consecutive zeros** is exactly $1/2^r$ — which is a *vanishingly small* event. > - If, after observing the entire season, the **rarest ticket** you saw had, say, **12 trailing zeros**, then intuitively, around $2^{12} = 4096$ distinct spectators must have passed through. > - The algorithm does exactly this, but with **bit patterns** instead of paper tickets. This makes the technique ideal for applications like: - **Web analytics:** counting unique visitors, distinct IP addresses, distinct search queries. - **Database query optimisation:** estimating the size of `SELECT DISTINCT` results. - **Network monitoring:** counting unique flows in a router's data stream. - **Bio-informatics:** k-mer counting in DNA sequencing streams. ### 1.3 Why a Probabilistic Approach? | Naïve Approach | Drawback in Big Data | |---|---| | Store every element in a `HashSet` | Memory = $O(N)$ — infeasible for billion-scale streams | | Sort & deduplicate | Time = $O(N \log N)$ and $O(N)$ space | | External DB `COUNT(DISTINCT)` | I/O bottleneck on TB-scale data | The Flajolet–Martin approach uses **$O(\log \log N)$ bits** of memory and a **single pass** over the data — a transformative trade-off. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Building Blocks The Flajolet–Martin algorithm is built on three pillars: 1. **A uniform hash family** $h : \mathcal{U} \rightarrow \{0, 1, 2, \dots, 2^L - 1\}$ - Each element $x \in \mathcal{U}$ is mapped to an integer $h(x)$ in range $[0, 2^L-1]$. - Assumption: outputs are *uniformly distributed* and statistically independent. 2. **The tail-zeros / rightmost-one operator $\rho(\cdot)$** - For a non-zero integer $y$, $\rho(y)$ = position (0-indexed) of the **rightmost 1-bit**. - Examples: $\rho(0) = 0$ (by convention), $\rho(1) = 0$, $\rho(2) = 1$, $\rho(4) = 2$, $\rho(12) = 2$ (binary $1100$). - Notation: $\rho(y) = $ number of trailing zeros in $y$ (with $\rho(0) = 0$). 3. **An estimator built from the maximum trailing-zero count** - Let $R = \max\limits_{x \in \mathcal{M}} \rho(h(x))$, where $\mathcal{M}$ is the multi-set stream. - The **Flajolet–Martin estimator** is $\widehat{N} = 2^{R}$. ### 2.2 Why Does $2^R$ Estimate the Cardinality? (The Core Intuition) For a stream of $N$ **distinct** elements, each of the $N$ hash outputs behaves like a uniform random integer. The probability that any one hash ends in **at least $r$ trailing zeros** is: $$P(\rho(h(x)) \geq r) = \frac{1}{2^{r}}$$ Hence, the probability that **none** of the $N$ elements produces $\geq r$ trailing zeros is: $$P(R < r) = \left(1 - \frac{1}{2^{r}}\right)^{N}$$ For the *typical* maximum, we set $P(R < r) \approx 1/2$, which gives: $$\left(1 - \frac{1}{2^{r}}\right)^{N} = \frac{1}{2}$$ Solving (using $\ln(1-x) \approx -x$ for small $x$): $$-N \cdot \frac{1}{2^{r}} \approx -\ln 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2^{r} \approx \frac{N}{\ln 2}$$ Refining this heuristic yields the expected maximum: $$\mathbb{E}[R] \approx \log_2(N) \quad \Rightarrow \quad N \approx 2^{R}$$ This is the foundational estimator of the Flajolet–Martin algorithm. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorise this table — these equations appear verbatim in KTU ESE problems. | # | Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Typical Value / Range | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $h : \mathcal{U} \to [0, 2^L-1]$ | Uniform hash function | $L = 64$ bits | | 2 | $\rho(y) = $ position of rightmost 1-bit in $y$ | Tail-zeros operator | $\rho(0)=0$ | | 3 | $P(\rho(h(x)) \geq r) = 1/2^{r}$ | Tail probability of $\geq r$ zeros | Geometric | | 4 | $P(R < r) = (1 - 1/2^{r})^{N}$ | CDF of the maximum | Monotonic | | 5 | $\widehat{N} = 2^{R}$ | FM single-hash estimator | One pass | | 6 | $R = \max_{x \in \mathcal{M}} \rho(h(x))$ | Maximum observed $\rho$ | Integer | | 7 | $\mathbb{E}[\widehat{N}] \approx 0.77351 \cdot N$ | Biased (Flajolet's correction adds constant $c$) | Use $2^{R}/\phi$ where $\phi \approx 0.77351$ | | 8 | $\widehat{N}_{\text{corrected}} = 2^{R} / \phi$ | Unbiased estimator | $\phi = 0.77351$ | | 9 | $\widehat{N}_{\text{group}} = \text{mean of medians}$ | Group-median aggregation | Across hash groups | | 10 | $m$ hash functions, grouped into $g$ groups of $k$ | Accuracy vs. memory trade-off | $m = g \cdot k$ | | 11 | Space complexity | Memory used | $O(\log \log N)$ bits | | 12 | Time complexity | Per element | $O(1)$ amortised | ### 2.4 Practical Utility in Engineering - **Google & Twitter** use FM-style sketches for real-time cardinality metrics in dashboards. - **Apache Spark** has `approxCountDistinct` powered by *HyperLogLog* — a direct descendant of FM. - **Network routers** use FM for *distinct-flow* estimation in NetFlow records. - **Database engines** use FM sketches inside the query planner to choose optimal join strategies. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the FM Estimator **Step 1 — Probability model.** Assume $h(x)$ is uniformly distributed on $\{0, 1, \dots, 2^L - 1\}$. For a *single* distinct element $x$, the event that the binary representation of $h(x)$ ends in at least $r$ zeros means $h(x) \bmod 2^{r} = 0$. The fraction of integers in $[0, 2^L-1]$ satisfying this is exactly $2^{L-r} / 2^{L} = 1/2^{r}$. Therefore: $$P(\rho(h(x)) \geq r) = \frac{1}{2^{r}}$$ **Step 2 — Maximum over $N$ independent trials.** Let $R = \max_{x \in \mathcal{M}} \rho(h(x))$. For a stream of $N$ distinct elements, the $N$ hash outputs are i.i.d. uniform. The CDF is: $$P(R < r) = \left(1 - \frac{1}{2^{r}}\right)^{N}$$ **Step 3 — Heuristic median argument.** The *median* $R_{1/2}$ satisfies $P(R < R_{1/2}) = 1/2$. Therefore: $$\left(1 - \frac{1}{2^{R_{1/2}}}\right)^{N} = \frac{1}{2}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $N$ using first-order Taylor expansion.** Recall that $\ln(1 - u) \approx -u$ for small $u$. Here $u = 1/2^{R_{1/2}}$. Taking logarithms: $$\begin{aligned} N \cdot \ln\!\left(1 - \frac{1}{2^{R_{1/2}}}\right) &= \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) \\[4pt] N \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{2^{R_{1/2}}} - \frac{1}{2 \cdot 4^{R_{1/2}}} - \cdots \right) &= -\ln 2 \\[4pt] N \cdot \frac{1}{2^{R_{1/2}}} &\approx \ln 2 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Rearrange to get the estimator.** $$\begin{aligned} 2^{R_{1/2}} &\approx \frac{N}{\ln 2} \\[4pt] N &\approx (\ln 2) \cdot 2^{R_{1/2}} \end{aligned}$$ Because $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$, the *true* $N$ is approximately $(\ln 2) \cdot 2^{R}$. The original Flajolet–Martin paper showed rigorously that: $$\mathbb{E}[2^{R}] = N \cdot \phi, \quad \text{where } \phi \approx 0.77351$$ Therefore, the **unbiased estimator** is: $$\boxed{\widehat{N} = \frac{2^{R}}{\phi} \approx 1.2927 \cdot 2^{R}}$$ ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style) **Problem:** A stream contains the distinct elements $\{a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h\}$. Two hash functions give the following outputs in binary (8-bit): | Element | $h_1(x)$ (binary) | $\rho(h_1)$ | $h_2(x)$ (binary) | $\rho(h_2)$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | a | 00100000 | 5 | 00010000 | 4 | | b | 01000000 | 6 | 00100000 | 5 | | c | 10000000 | 7 | 00001000 | 3 | | d | 00000001 | 0 | 01000000 | 6 | | e | 00000100 | 2 | 00000010 | 1 | | f | 00010000 | 4 | 10000000 | 7 | | g | 00001000 | 3 | 00000001 | 0 | | h | 00100000 | 5 | 00000010 | 1 | **Step 1:** Find the max $\rho$ for each hash function. $$R_1 = \max\{5, 6, 7, 0, 2, 4, 3, 5\} = 7$$ $$R_2 = \max\{4, 5, 3, 6, 1, 7, 0, 1\} = 7$$ **Step 2:** Compute individual estimates. $$\widehat{N}_1 = 2^{7} = 128$$ $$\widehat{N}_2 = 2^{7} = 128$$ **Step 3:** Take the **median** of the two estimates. $$\widehat{N}_{\text{median}} = \text{median}(128, 128) = 128$$ **Step 4:** Apply the bias correction (optional, for higher accuracy). $$\widehat{N}_{\text{corrected}} = \frac{128}{0.77351} \approx 165.5$$ **Step 5:** Group-based aggregation (if more hash functions). If we had $g$ groups each with $k$ medians, the final estimate is the **mean of the medians**. > [!NOTE] > **Sanity check:** The true $N = 8$. The single-hash estimate of 128 is high — this is expected for small datasets. The error $\vert 128 - 8 \vert / 8 = 1500\%$ is normal for a *single* hash trial. Aggregating across many trials dramatically reduces the relative error to within a few percent. ### 3.3 The Variance Reduction Trick: Median of Means **Why median, not mean?** A single $\widehat{N} = 2^{R}$ is *unstable* — it can double or halve with a single unusual hash value. By using $m$ independent hash functions: 1. Partition them into $g$ groups of size $k$. 2. Within each group $j$, compute the **median** $M_j$ of the $k$ estimates. 3. Return the **mean** of the medians: $\widehat{N} = \frac{1}{g} \sum_{j=1}^{g} M_j$. This *median-of-means* estimator has: - **Standard error:** $O(1.04 / \sqrt{m})$ — much tighter than the raw mean. - **Memory:** $m$ integers only — still $O(\log \log N)$ bits. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 High-Level Flow of the Flajolet–Martin Algorithm ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Data Stream
incoming element x"] --> B["Apply Hash Function
h1 x, h2 x ... hm x"] B --> C1["Compute rho h1 x"] B --> C2["Compute rho h2 x"] B --> C3["Compute rho hm x"] C1 --> D1["Max Tracker R1"] C2 --> D2["Max Tracker R2"] C3 --> D3["Max Tracker Rm"] D1 --> E["Group Medians
partition into g groups"] D2 --> E D3 --> E E --> F["Mean of Medians
final N estimate"] F --> G["Output
approx distinct count"] ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of FM Estimator ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"] I1["Stream Element x in U"] end subgraph HASH["HASH LAYER"] H1["Hash Function h1"] H2["Hash Function h2"] H3["Hash Function hk"] end subgraph TAIL["TAIL ZEROS LAYER"] T1["rho h1 x"] T2["rho h2 x"] T3["rho hk x"] end subgraph MAX["MAX TRACKER"] M1["R1 = max rho"] M2["R2 = max rho"] M3["Rk = max rho"] end subgraph AGG["AGGREGATION LAYER"] AG1["Median of k estimates"] AG2["Mean across g groups"] end subgraph OUT["OUTPUT LAYER"] O1["N hat equals 2 raised to R divided by phi"] end I1 --> H1 --> T1 --> M1 --> AG1 I1 --> H2 --> T2 --> M2 --> AG1 I1 --> H3 --> T3 --> M3 --> AG1 AG1 --> AG2 --> O1 ``` ### 4.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use FM? | Scenario | Use FM? | Reason | |---|---|---| | Web unique-visitor count | ✅ Yes | Streaming, repeated queries | | SQL `SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT col)` on TB tables | ✅ Yes (with HLL) | Approximate is acceptable | | Inventory of < 10 000 items | ❌ No | Use exact `HashSet` | | Sliding-window count (last 1 hour) | ⚠️ Partial | Need FM with time decay | | Counting with deletions | ❌ No | FM is insert-only; use Count-Min Sketch variants | | Cardinality must be exact (e.g., billing) | ❌ No | Regulatory / financial accuracy needed | --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]** **Q1. Define the Count-Distinct Problem. Why is exact counting infeasible in big data streams?** *Model Answer:* The **Count-Distinct Problem** seeks the number of unique elements $N$ in a multi-set $\mathcal{M}$ observed as a data stream. Exact counting is infeasible in big data because (i) the universe $\mathcal{U}$ is too large to fit in main memory, (ii) streams cannot be re-read, (iii) storing all distinct elements in a `HashSet` requires $O(N)$ space which is infeasible for billion-scale streams, and (iv) external sorting incurs prohibitive I/O cost. Probabilistic streaming algorithms like **Flajolet–Martin** overcome this with $O(\log \log N)$ memory and a single pass. **Q2. What is the role of the trailing-zeros function $\rho(\cdot)$ in the Flajolet–Martin algorithm?** *Model Answer:* The function $\rho(y)$ returns the position of the **rightmost 1-bit** in the binary representation of $y$ (equivalently, the number of trailing zeros). For a uniform hash $h(x)$, the event $\rho(h(x)) \geq r$ has probability $1/2^{r}$. By tracking the **maximum** $\rho$ over the stream, we obtain a statistical fingerprint whose expected value is $\log_2 N$, giving the estimator $\widehat{N} = 2^{R}$. --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, ESE Pattern: Internal Choice]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Algorithm + Derivation **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Flajolet–Martin algorithm** for estimating the number of distinct elements in a data stream. Discuss the role of multiple hash functions and the *median-of-means* technique. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the Flajolet–Martin estimator $\widehat{N} = 2^{R}$ starting from the probability $P(\rho(h(x)) \geq r) = 1/2^{r}$. Show how the bias-correction constant $\phi \approx 0.77351$ arises. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution **Part (a) — Algorithm Explanation (7 marks)** The **Flajolet–Martin algorithm** proceeds as follows: 1. **Choose $m$ independent uniform hash functions** $h_1, h_2, \dots, h_m : \mathcal{U} \to \{0, 1, \dots, 2^L-1\}$. 2. **Initialise** $m$ registers $R_1, R_2, \dots, R_m$ to $0$. 3. For each stream element $x$: - For $i = 1$ to $m$: - Compute $y_i = h_i(x)$. - Compute $\rho(y_i)$. - Set $R_i = \max(R_i, \rho(y_i))$. 4. **Aggregate** the $m$ registers using *median-of-means*: - Partition into $g$ groups of size $k$ (with $m = g \cdot k$). - For each group $j$, compute median $M_j = \text{median}(2^{R_{(j-1)k+1}}, \dots, 2^{R_{jk}})$. - Return $\widehat{N} = \frac{1}{g} \sum_{j=1}^{g} M_j$. **Role of multiple hash functions:** A single hash function gives high variance. Using many independent hashes gives a *law of large numbers* effect, while the **median** is robust against outliers (a single bad hash cannot corrupt the estimate). **Role of median-of-means:** Taking the **median** within small groups reduces the influence of extreme values, and taking the **mean** across groups further stabilises the estimate. The combined estimator has standard error $O(1.04/\sqrt{m})$ with only $O(m \log \log N)$ bits of memory. The block diagram in **Section 4.2** above illustrates the architecture. **Part (b) — Derivation of Estimator and Bias Constant (7 marks)** *Step 1:* For a uniform hash, the probability that the last $r$ bits are all zero is $1/2^{r}$, so: $$P(\rho(h(x)) \geq r) = \frac{1}{2^{r}}$$ *Step 2:* The maximum $R$ over $N$ i.i.d. elements has CDF: $$P(R < r) = \left(1 - \frac{1}{2^{r}}\right)^{N}$$ *Step 3:* The median $R_{1/2}$ satisfies $P(R < R_{1/2}) = 1/2$. Taking logarithms: $$\begin{aligned} N \cdot \ln\!\left(1 - \frac{1}{2^{R_{1/2}}}\right) &= -\ln 2 \\[4pt] N \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{2^{R_{1/2}}} + O(4^{-R_{1/2}})\right) &\approx -\ln 2 \\[4pt] \frac{N}{2^{R_{1/2}}} &\approx \ln 2 \\[4pt] 2^{R_{1/2}} &\approx \frac{N}{\ln 2} \end{aligned}$$ *Step 4:* Therefore the **raw** FM estimator is $2^{R} \approx N / \ln 2$, which means $2^{R}$ systematically **underestimates** $N$ (it expects only $N \cdot \ln 2 \approx 0.693 N$). *Step 5:* The **exact** expected value (proved by Flajolet & Martin using generating functions) is: $$\mathbb{E}[2^{R}] = N \cdot \phi, \quad \phi = \frac{2 \ln 2 \cdot \Gamma(3/2)}{\sqrt{\pi}} \approx 0.77351$$ *Step 6:* Therefore the **unbiased estimator** is: $$\widehat{N} = \frac{2^{R}}{\phi} \approx 1.2927 \cdot 2^{R}$$ The constant $\phi$ is the famous "Flajolet–Martin bias correction" and arises from the integral evaluation of the gamma function over a Poisson distribution of distinct values. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Numerical + Application **(a)** Consider a stream of $8$ distinct elements whose hash values (8-bit) under $h_1$ are: $\{00100000, 01000000, 10000000, 00000001, 00000100, 00010000, 00001000, 00100000\}$. Compute the FM estimate $\widehat{N}$ using $h_1$ only. Apply the bias correction. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the **Flajolet–Martin algorithm** with the **HyperLogLog** algorithm. List three engineering applications of cardinality estimation in real-world systems. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution **Part (a) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)** Given hash outputs in binary (8-bit): | Element | Hash (binary) | Trailing zeros ($\rho$) | |---|---|---| | 1 | 00100000 | 5 | | 2 | 01000000 | 6 | | 3 | 10000000 | 7 | | 4 | 00000001 | 0 | | 5 | 00000100 | 2 | | 6 | 00010000 | 4 | | 7 | 00001000 | 3 | | 8 | 00100000 | 5 | The maximum $\rho$ is: $$R = \max\{5, 6, 7, 0, 2, 4, 3, 5\} = 7$$ **Raw FM estimate:** $$\widehat{N}_{\text{raw}} = 2^{R} = 2^{7} = 128$$ **Bias-corrected estimate (using $\phi \approx 0.77351$):** $$\widehat{N}_{\text{corrected}} = \frac{2^{R}}{\phi} = \frac{128}{0.77351} \approx 165.5$$ **Conclusion:** With only one hash, the estimate is high (true $N = 8$). The error is large in this case because of small sample size — this is expected. Using many independent hash functions and the median-of-means aggregation would dramatically improve accuracy. **Part (b) — FM vs HyperLogLog & Applications (7 marks)** **Comparison Table:** | Feature | Flajolet–Martin (1985) | HyperLogLog (2007) | |---|---|---| | Memory | $O(m \log \log N)$ | $O(m \log \log N)$ — tighter constant | | Standard error | $\approx 0.78 / \sqrt{m}$ | $\approx 1.04 / \sqrt{m}$ (better!) | | Hash operations | Multiple independent hashes | One hash, register-indexed via leading zeros | | Aggregation | Median of means | Harmonic mean of $2^{R_j}$ | | Industry adoption | Foundational / theoretical | Used in Redis, BigQuery, Druid, Spark | | Complexity | Simple to implement | Slightly more involved | **Three real-world applications:** 1. **Web analytics dashboards (Google Analytics, Mixpanel):** Estimating daily unique visitors, distinct search queries, and unique user-agent strings across billions of page-views. 2. **Database query optimisers (PostgreSQL, BigQuery):** The planner uses HLL sketches (a descendant of FM) to estimate the number of distinct values in a column, deciding whether to use a hash-join or a merge-join. 3. **Network telemetry (Cisco NetFlow, sFlow):** Routers estimate the number of unique source-destination IP pairs and unique flows per second in real-time using sketches derived from FM principles. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do not confuse $\rho(y)$ (rightmost 1-bit position) with $\tau(y)$ (number of trailing zeros).** They are identical in value, but KTU expects the **"position of rightmost 1-bit"** wording. > 2. **The estimator is $2^{R}$, not $R$ alone.** Students who write $\widehat{N} = R$ lose 4–5 marks. > 3. **State the bias correction $\phi \approx 0.77351$ explicitly** when deriving; do not skip it. KTU 2024 papers specifically test the corrected estimator. > 4. **The single-pass claim is essential** — mention that FM uses **one pass** and **$O(\log \log N)$ memory**. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Count-Distinct Problem** = find unique elements in a massive stream. - **Flajolet–Martin (1985)** is a **probabilistic, single-pass, $O(\log \log N)$-space** algorithm. - **Hash function** $h$ is uniform on $\{0, \dots, 2^L-1\}$. - **$\rho(y)$** = position of the **rightmost 1-bit** in $y$ (≡ number of trailing zeros, with $\rho(0) = 0$). - **Key probability:** $P(\rho(h(x)) \geq r) = 1/2^{r}$. - **Maximum tracker:** $R = \max_{x \in \mathcal{M}} \rho(h(x))$. - **Raw estimator:** $\widehat{N} = 2^{R}$. - **Bias constant:** $\phi = 0.77351$ → **unbiased estimator** $\widehat{N} = 2^{R} / \phi$. - **Aggregation:** *Median of means* across $g$ groups of $k$ hashes; standard error $\approx 1.04/\sqrt{m}$. - **Memory:** $m \times \log_2 L$ bits; **Time:** $O(1)$ per element. - **Descendants:** LogLog, SuperLogLog, **HyperLogLog** (used in Redis, BigQuery, Spark's `approxCountDistinct`). - **Use cases:** unique visitors, distinct IPs, k-mer counting, database cardinality estimates. - **Cannot handle deletions** — purely insert-only streaming. - **Single pass** — re-querying the stream is impossible. ---

topics.title: Estimating Moments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT746 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Estimating Moments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Frequency Moments

In the context of **Big Data Stream Processing**, a **data stream** is a sequence of elements arriving one at a time, often so massive that it cannot be stored in main memory. Let the stream be a sequence:

$$S = (a_1, a_2, a_3, \dots, a_n)$$

where each element $a_i$ belongs to a universe $U$ of size $m$. Let $m_i$ denote the **frequency** of the $i$-th distinct element in the stream, i.e., the number of times element $e_i$ appears in $S$. Then the **$k$-th Frequency Moment** $F_k$ is formally defined as:

$$F_k = \sum_{i=1}^{m} (m_i)^k$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The frequency moment is a fundamental statistical measure used to characterize the skewness, distribution, and concentration of data in a stream. It is the cornerstone of the **Alon-Matias-Szegedy (AMS) Sketch**, a landmark 1996 algorithm that revolutionized streaming algorithms.

## 1.2 Special Cases of Frequency Moments

| Moment | Definition | Interpretation | Common Use |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $F_0$ | $\sum_i (m_i)^0$ | Number of **distinct elements** in stream | Data summarization, query optimization |
| $F_1$ | $\sum_i m_i$ | Total **stream length** (count $n$) | Trivial to compute |
| $F_2$ | $\sum_i (m_i)^2$ | **"Surprise Number"** (collision count) | Join size estimation, self-join size |
| $F_\infty$ | $\max_i m_i$ | Most frequent element count | Finding top-$k$ elements |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Library Counter

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Overcrowded Bookstore":**
> Imagine a giant bookstore with **millions of customers** entering every day (the data stream). You want to estimate the popularity of each book using **only a tiny notepad** (limited memory). You cannot keep a list of every book. So you sample a few random moments, count how many times a particular book is bought from that moment onward, and use that sample to estimate the **total sales distribution**.
>
> - $F_1$ = total customers (easy — just count them all)
> - $F_2$ = how concentrated purchases are (are people buying the same book over and over?)
> - $F_k$ for large $k$ = how skewed the distribution is toward a few hot items
>
> **Frequency moments** are the tool that lets us summarize this ocean of data using **sub-linear space**!

## 1.4 Why Estimating Moments is Hard

> [!WARNING]
> **The Streaming Challenge:** The stream length $n$ can be **billions**, while memory is only a few MB. We cannot store the entire stream. We need **sub-linear space** algorithms (ideally $O(\log n)$ or $O(\text{poly}(\log n))$ bits). The AMS estimator achieves exactly this by using **randomness and sampling** combined with clever statistics.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Alon-Matias-Szegedy (AMS) Estimator — Core Idea

The **AMS Estimator** (Alon, Matias, Szegedy, 1996) is the canonical algorithm for estimating $F_k$ in the **data stream model** using only **one pass** and **poly-logarithmic space**.

**Operational Logic (Bulleted Steps):**

- **Step 1 — Initialization:** Maintain a small set of counters. Initially, all counters are inactive.
- **Step 2 — Position Selection:** When the stream begins, choose the *first* element to "activate" all counters (this is the random starting point $r$).
- **Step 3 — Tracking:** As each new stream element $a_i$ arrives, update each active counter if $a_i$ matches the element the counter is tracking.
- **Step 4 — Deactivation (optional):** Some versions allow counters to be deactivated at random times for variance reduction.
- **Step 5 — Estimation:** After processing the stream, combine the counter values using a mathematical formula to produce an unbiased estimate of $F_k$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU question on this topic!**

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Meaning |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $F_k$ | $\sum_{i=1}^{m}(m_i)^k$ | $k$-th frequency moment |
| $X$ (estimator) | $n \cdot \left(c_r^k - (c_r-1)^k\right)$ | AMS estimator for $F_k$ |
| $E[X]$ | $F_k$ | Unbiasedness condition |
| $\text{Var}(X)$ | $\le \dfrac{n^2 \cdot k^2 \cdot F_{2k}}{F_k^2} \cdot F_k^2$ (general bound) | Variance bound |
| $\text{Var}(X_{F_2})$ | $\le 2 \cdot F_2^2$ | Special case for $F_2$ |
| Sample size | $O\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\epsilon^2}\right)$ | Samples needed for error $\epsilon n$ |
| $F_2$ surprise | $\sum_i \binom{m_i}{2} = \dfrac{F_2 - n}{2}$ | Self-join size |

**AMS Estimator for $F_2$ (Simplified Form):**

$$X = n \cdot (2 \cdot c_r - 1)$$

where $c_r$ is the count of the element at random position $r$ from position $r$ to the end of the stream.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where is this used in industry?**
>
> - **Database Query Optimization:** Estimating self-join sizes without materializing tables.
> - **Network Traffic Analysis:** Detecting heavy hitters / DDoS attacks by estimating higher moments of packet frequency.
> - **Search Engines:** Estimating document similarity (e.g., Google's index compression).
> - **Recommendation Systems:** Measuring user-item concentration in clickstreams.
> - **Stream Databases (Apache Flink, Spark Streaming):** Inspired by the AMS sketch family (Count-Min, HyperLogLog, etc.).

## 2.4 Algorithm: AMS Sketch for $F_2$

**Input:** Stream $S = (a_1, \ldots, a_n)$, sample size $s$  
**Output:** Estimate $\hat{F}_2$

- **for** $j = 1$ to $s$ **do**
  - Pick random position $r_j \in \{1, \ldots, n\}$
  - Maintain counter $c_j$ initialized to $1$ (counts position $r_j$)
  - For each stream element $a_i$ (where $i > r_j$):
    - If $a_i = a_{r_j}$, increment $c_j$
  - Compute $X_j = n \cdot (2c_j - 1)$
- **return** $\hat{F}_2 = \dfrac{1}{s} \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{s} X_j$

> **Memory cost:** Only $s$ counters of size $O(\log n)$ bits each ⇒ **$O(s \log n)$ space** — sub-linear!

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Setup for the Derivation

We aim to prove that the AMS estimator $X = n(2c_r - 1)$ is an **unbiased estimator** of $F_2$, and to find a **bound on its variance**.

**Definitions (Recap):**
- Stream $S = (a_1, \ldots, a_n)$ of length $n$
- Random position $r$ chosen uniformly from $\{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$
- Let element at position $r$ be $a_r = v$
- Let $c_r = 1 + \sum_{i=r+1}^{n} \mathbf{1}(a_i = v)$ = count of $v$ from position $r$ to the end
- $F_2 = \sum_{j=1}^{m} m_j^2$ where $m_j$ is the true count of element $e_j$

## 3.2 Derivation of Unbiasedness: $\mathbb{E}[X] = F_2$

**Step 1:** Expand the expectation using the random position $r$.

$$
\mathbb{E}[X] = \mathbb{E}\left[n(2c_r - 1)\right] = n \cdot \left(2\,\mathbb{E}[c_r] - 1\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\mathbb{E}[c_r]$ directly.

$$
\mathbb{E}[c_r] = \mathbb{E}\!\left[1 + \sum_{i=r+1}^{n} \mathbf{1}(a_i = a_r)\right] = 1 + \mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_{i=r+1}^{n} \mathbf{1}(a_i = a_r)\right]
$$

**Step 3:** Swap the order of expectation. By the law of total expectation:

$$
\mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_{i=r+1}^{n} \mathbf{1}(a_i = a_r)\right] = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{r=1}^{n} \sum_{i=r+1}^{n} \mathbf{1}(a_i = a_r)
$$

**Step 4:** Interpret the double sum. It counts every **unordered pair** $(r, i)$ with $r < i$ such that $a_r = a_i$. For each distinct element $e_j$ with true frequency $m_j$, the number of such pairs is $\binom{m_j}{2} = \dfrac{m_j(m_j - 1)}{2}$.

$$
\frac{1}{n} \sum_{r=1}^{n} \sum_{i=r+1}^{n} \mathbf{1}(a_i = a_r) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \binom{m_j}{2} = \frac{1}{n} \cdot \frac{F_2 - n}{2}
$$

> **Reasoning:** We used the identity $\sum_{j=1}^{m} m_j(m_j - 1) = \sum_{j=1}^{m}(m_j^2 - m_j) = F_2 - n$ since $\sum_j m_j = n$.

**Step 5:** Substitute back into the expectation.

$$
\mathbb{E}[c_r] = 1 + \frac{F_2 - n}{2n} = \frac{2n + F_2 - n}{2n} = \frac{F_2 + n}{2n}
$$

**Step 6:** Plug into $\mathbb{E}[X]$.

$$
\mathbb{E}[X] = n \cdot \left(2 \cdot \frac{F_2 + n}{2n} - 1\right) = n \cdot \left(\frac{F_2 + n}{n} - 1\right) = n \cdot \frac{F_2}{n} = F_2 \;\; \blacksquare
$$

**The estimator is unbiased!** $\mathbb{E}[X] = F_2$.

## 3.3 Derivation of Variance: $\text{Var}(X) \le 2F_2^2$

**Step 1:** Expand variance.

$$
\text{Var}(X) = n^2 \cdot \text{Var}(2c_r - 1) = 4n^2 \cdot \text{Var}(c_r) = 4n^2 \left( \mathbb{E}[c_r^2] - (\mathbb{E}[c_r])^2 \right)
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\mathbb{E}[c_r^2]$ using the indicator expansion.

$$
c_r^2 = \left(1 + \sum_{i=r+1}^{n} X_i\right)^2
$$

where $X_i = \mathbf{1}(a_i = a_r)$. Expanding:

$$
c_r^2 = 1 + 2 \sum_{i=r+1}^{n} X_i + 2 \sum_{r < i < j \le n} X_i X_j
$$

> **Note:** We dropped the cross-term cross-coefficient since $X_i \in \{0,1\}$ implies $X_i^2 = X_i$. The sum over $i < j$ with both indicators is what we need.

**Step 3:** Take expectations term by term.

$$
\mathbb{E}[c_r^2] = 1 + \frac{F_2 - n}{n} + 2 \cdot \mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_{r<i<j} X_i X_j\right]
$$

**Step 4:** The triple-indicator term counts **triples** $(r, i, j)$ with $r < i < j$ and $a_r = a_i = a_j$. For element $e_j$ with frequency $m_j$, this count equals $\binom{m_j}{3} = \dfrac{m_j(m_j-1)(m_j-2)}{6}$.

$$
\mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_{r<i<j} X_i X_j\right] = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \binom{m_j}{3} = \frac{1}{6n} \sum_{j=1}^{m} m_j(m_j-1)(m_j-2)
$$

**Step 5:** Bound the triple-count. Note that for non-negative integers $m_j$:

$$
m_j(m_j-1)(m_j-2) \le m_j^3
$$

So:

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{m} m_j(m_j-1)(m_j-2) \le \sum_{j=1}^{m} m_j^3 = F_3
$$

However, the more commonly cited bound uses $F_2$. By Cauchy-Schwarz or direct manipulation:

$$
\sum_j m_j(m_j-1)(m_j-2) \le \sum_j m_j^2(m_j-1) \le F_2 \cdot \max_j m_j
$$

**Step 6:** Combining all terms, after careful algebra (full details in Alon-Matias-Szegedy 1996), we obtain the **classical variance bound** for the $F_2$ estimator:

$$
\text{Var}(X) \le 2 \cdot F_2^2
$$

**Step 7 (Practical interpretation):** The **relative standard deviation** is:

$$
\frac{\sqrt{\text{Var}(X)}}{F_2} \le \frac{\sqrt{2} \cdot F_2}{F_2} = \sqrt{2}
$$

By taking the **average of $s$ independent estimators**, the variance drops by factor $s$. To achieve error $\epsilon \cdot F_2$ with probability $\ge 1 - \delta$, we need:

$$
s = O\!\left(\frac{1}{\epsilon^2} \log \frac{1}{\delta}\right) \quad \text{independent samples}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Property | Formula | Implication |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| Unbiasedness | $\mathbb{E}[X] = F_2$ | The estimator is correct **on average** |
| Variance bound | $\text{Var}(X) \le 2F_2^2$ | Error is $O(F_2)$ |
| Space per sample | $O(\log n)$ bits | Sub-linear! |
| Samples for $\epsilon, \delta$ | $O(\epsilon^{-2} \log \delta^{-1})$ | Polynomial dependence on $1/\epsilon$ |
| Time per stream element | $O(s)$ updates | One pass over the data |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: AMS Estimator Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STREAM INPUT: a1, a2, a3 ... an] --> B[Initialize s Random Positions]
    B --> C1[Counter 1: position r1, value c1]
    B --> C2[Counter 2: position r2, value c2]
    B --> C3[Counter 3: position r3, value c3]
    B --> Cs[Counter s: position rs, value cs]
    A --> C1
    A --> C2
    A --> C3
    A --> Cs
    C1 --> D1[Compute X1 = n * 2c1 - 1]
    C2 --> D2[Compute X2 = n * 2c2 - 1]
    C3 --> D3[Compute X3 = n * 2c3 - 1]
    Cs --> Ds[Compute Xs = n * 2cs - 1]
    D1 --> E[Average all Xj values]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    Ds --> E
    E --> F[FINAL ESTIMATE: F2 hat]
    F --> G{Is F2 hat within epsilon accuracy?}
    G -->|YES| H[Return Estimate to User]
    G -->|NO| I[Increase Sample Size s]
    I --> B
```

## 4.2 Flow Diagram: Stream Processing Pipeline for $F_2$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[STREAM INGESTION]
        S1[Source: Logs, Clicks, Packets]
    end
    subgraph SAMPLE[RANDOM SAMPLING LAYER]
        S2[Pick s random positions r1 ... rs]
        S3[Store element at each position]
    end
    subgraph TRACK[COUNTER MAINTENANCE]
        T1[Update counter on element match]
        T2[Increment cj when aj matches element at rj]
    end
    subgraph COMBINE[ESTIMATION ENGINE]
        C1[Compute per-sample Xj = n * 2cj - 1]
        C2[Average over all s samples]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[DELIVERABLE]
        O1[Estimated F2 with confidence bound]
    end
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> T1
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> O1
```

## 4.3 Data Flow: Estimator Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Stage 1: STREAM ARRIVAL] --> P2[Stage 2: RANDOM POSITION SAMPLING]
    P2 --> P3[Stage 3: COUNTER UPDATE PER ELEMENT]
    P3 --> P4[Stage 4: STREAM ENDS]
    P4 --> P5[Stage 5: COMPUTE PER-SAMPLE ESTIMATE]
    P5 --> P6[Stage 6: AGGREGATE INTO FINAL ESTIMATE]
    P6 --> P7[Stage 7: REPORT WITH ERROR BAR]
    P1 -.uses.-> P3
    P2 -.uses.-> P3
    P5 -.uses.-> P6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - **Top diagram (architecture):** Shows how $s$ independent counters process the same stream in parallel.
> - **Middle diagram (pipeline):** Visualizes the four functional stages: ingestion → sampling → tracking → combination.
> - **Bottom diagram (lifecycle):** Shows the temporal evolution of the estimator from arrival to final report.

---
