# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 YARN Runtime Architecture — Master-Slave Coordination

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    clientApp["Client Application"]
    rm["ResourceManager RM Global Arbiter"]
    scheduler["YARN Scheduler FIFO Fair Capacity"]
    appsManager["ApplicationsManager"]
    nm1["NodeManager NM Node 1"]
    nm2["NodeManager NM Node 2"]
    nm3["NodeManager NM Node 3"]
    am["ApplicationMaster Per Job Master"]
    c1["Container 1 Map Task"]
    c2["Container 2 Map Task"]
    c3["Container 3 Reduce Task"]
    c4["Container 4 Reduce Task"]
    c5["Container 5 Reduce Task"]
    c6["Container 6 Shuffle Task"]
    hdfs["HDFS Distributed Storage"]

    clientApp -->|submit job| rm
    rm --> scheduler
    rm --> appsManager
    appsManager -->|allocate container 0| am
    am -->|register| rm
    am -->|request resources| rm
    rm -->|grant containers| am
    am -->|launch map task| c1
    am -->|launch map task| c2
    am -->|launch reduce task| c3
    am -->|launch reduce task| c4
    am -->|launch reduce task| c5
    am -->|launch shuffle helper| c6
    c1 --> nm1
    c2 --> nm1
    c3 --> nm2
    c4 --> nm2
    c5 --> nm3
    c6 --> nm3
    nm1 -->|heartbeat 3 sec| rm
    nm2 -->|heartbeat 3 sec| rm
    nm3 -->|heartbeat 3 sec| rm
    am -->|heartbeat 1 sec| rm
    c1 -.->|read write| hdfs
    c3 -.->|write final output| hdfs
```

> **Textual annotation:** The diagram shows the **two-level hierarchy** of YARN. The ResourceManager sits at the top and is the *only* component that knows about all NodeManagers. The ApplicationMaster is a *per-job* entity that knows the specifics of its own MapReduce workflow but is unaware of other applications running on the cluster.

## 4.2 Task Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> UNASSIGNED : Task created by job splitter
    UNASSIGNED --> ASSIGNED : Worker slot selected by scheduler
    ASSIGNED --> SCHEDULED : TaskTracker queues task
    SCHEDULED --> RUNNING : JVM launched user code begins
    RUNNING --> COMMIT_PENDING : Reduce shuffle sort reduce phases done
    RUNNING --> FAILED : Exception thrown or timeout
    RUNNING --> KILLED : Speculative duplicate finished first
    COMMIT_PENDING --> SUCCEEDED : Output committed to HDFS
    FAILED --> UNASSIGNED : Retry attempt if attempts remaining
    KILLED --> [*]
    SUCCEEDED --> [*]
    FAILED --> [*] : Max attempts exhausted
```

> **Textual annotation:** The state machine reveals that `FAILED → UNASSIGNED` is a **loop-back** transition — the framework gives the task another chance up to `mapreduce.map.maxattempts` (default 4) before declaring permanent failure.

## 4.3 Speculative Execution Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Task T running on Node A"]
    check1{"Running for at least 1 minute?"}
    check2{"Progress score below mean of peer group?"}
    check3{"Speculation enabled in config?"}
    check4{"A free container available?"}
    launch["Launch speculative duplicate on Node B"]
    monitor["Continue original task T"]
    race["Race both tasks to completion"]
    winner["Whichever finishes first wins"]
    kill["Kill the losing task discard partial output"]
    commit["Commit winners output to HDFS"]

    start --> check1
    check1 -->|No| monitor
    check1 -->|Yes| check3
    check3 -->|No| monitor
    check3 -->|Yes| check2
    check2 -->|No| monitor
    check2 -->|Yes| check4
    check4 -->|No| monitor
    check4 -->|Yes| launch
    launch --> race
    race --> winner
    winner --> kill
    kill --> commit
```

> **Textual annotation:** Speculative execution is a **gated decision process** with four sequential checks. Most tasks in a well-balanced cluster will fail the first or second check and never spawn duplicates, keeping the cluster overhead minimal.

## 4.4 Fault Tolerance Recovery Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a1["Task running on NM1"]
    a2["Heartbeat sent every 3 sec to AM"]
    a3["AM detects missed heartbeats for 10 min"]
    a4["AM marks task as FAILED"]
    a5{"Attempts less than maxattempts?"}
    a6["Re-schedule task on NM2 healthy node"]
    a7["Mark entire job as FAILED"]
    a8["Log failure and notify client"]

    a1 --> a2 --> a3 --> a4 --> a5
    a5 -->|Yes| a6
    a5 -->|No| a7 --> a8
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Runtime Coordination in MapReduce. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Model Answer:**

Runtime coordination in MapReduce refers to the set of distributed mechanisms that the master daemon (JobTracker in MR1, or the combination of ResourceManager and ApplicationMaster in YARN) uses to **schedule, assign, monitor, and recover** Map and Reduce tasks across worker nodes in a Hadoop cluster. It encompasses job submission, input split computation, task assignment based on data locality, heartbeat-based progress monitoring, speculative execution of slow tasks, and fault-tolerant re-execution of failed tasks. The primary goal is to maximize parallelism, minimize data movement across the network, and ensure that the job completes successfully even in the presence of node failures.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing key responsibilities, 1 mark for stating the goal)*

---

### Question 2: What is Speculative Execution in Hadoop MapReduce? Why is it needed? [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Speculative Execution is a runtime optimization in which the ApplicationMaster identifies *straggler* tasks (tasks running significantly slower than their peers) and launches **duplicate copies** of those tasks on other available nodes. Whichever copy finishes first has its output committed; the slower copy is killed.

**Why it is needed:**
1. **Hardware heterogeneity** — Older or slower nodes in a heterogeneous cluster create stragglers.
2. **Resource contention** — A node may be overloaded with other workloads, slowing its map task.
3. **Bad disks or network I/O** — Faulty hardware degrades single-node performance unpredictably.
4. **Improved job completion time** — Eliminating the longest tail of task durations reduces overall job latency, since the job is only as fast as its slowest task.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write that speculative execution is a **fault-tolerance mechanism**. This is **incorrect**. Fault tolerance handles *failed* tasks; speculation handles *slow but successful* tasks. Losing this distinction costs **1 mark**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: Explain the YARN Architecture for Runtime Coordination in MapReduce v2. Discuss the roles of ResourceManager, NodeManager, ApplicationMaster, and Containers. Also explain how task scheduling and fault tolerance are handled in YARN. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]

#### Part (a) — YARN Architecture and Daemon Roles (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)** is the cluster resource management layer introduced in Hadoop 2.x to overcome the scalability and rigidity limitations of the classic MapReduce v1 architecture. It **decouples** resource management from application lifecycle management.

**1. ResourceManager (RM) — Global Master Daemon (2 marks):**

The ResourceManager is the **ultimate authority** that arbitrates resources among all competing applications in the cluster. It runs on the master node and consists of two sub-components:
- **Scheduler**: Implements the resource allocation policy (FIFO, Fair Scheduler, or Capacity Scheduler). It is *pluggable* and only handles allocation, not application monitoring.
- **ApplicationsManager (AsM)**: Accepts job submissions, launches the ApplicationMaster in the first allocated container, and provides restart capability on AM failure.

**2. NodeManager (NM) — Per-Node Slave Daemon (1.5 marks):**

Each worker node runs a NodeManager that:
- Manages the **lifecycles of containers** on that node.
- Monitors **resource usage** (CPU, memory, disk, network) of each container.
- Sends **heartbeat signals** to the ResourceManager every **3 seconds** reporting node health and container status.
- Executes the **ContainerLaunchContext** commands received from the ApplicationMaster to start task JVMs.

**3. ApplicationMaster (AM) — Per-Job Master (2 marks):**

The ApplicationMaster is **one process per job** that:
- Negotiates resources with the ResourceManager on behalf of its job.
- Works with the NodeManager to execute and monitor individual task containers.
- Handles task failures and speculative execution decisions for *its* job.
- Reports job progress to the client.

**4. Container — Resource Abstraction (1.5 marks):**

A Container is a **logical bundle of resources** (RAM in MB + vCores) allocated on a specific node. It is the unit of allocation in YARN. Each task (map or reduce) runs inside one container, which provides:
- An isolated execution environment
- Localized access to HDFS blocks on the same node
- Configurable memory limits enforced by the NM

---

#### Part (b) — Task Scheduling and Fault Tolerance in YARN (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Task Scheduling Flow (3.5 marks):**

The YARN scheduling process follows these steps:

1. **Job Submission**: The client invokes `job.waitForCompletion(true)`, which contacts the ResourceManager and submits an `ApplicationSubmissionContext`.

2. **AM Container Allocation**: The ResourceManager allocates a container (Container 0) on a suitable NodeManager and launches the ApplicationMaster inside it.

3. **AM Registration**: The ApplicationMaster registers itself with the ResourceManager via `RegisterApplicationMasterRequest`.

4. **Resource Request Loop**: The AM sends an `AllocateRequest` to the RM, specifying its resource needs (e.g., 10 containers × 2 GB × 1 vCore for map tasks). The RM responds with an `AllocateResponse` containing granted containers, prioritized by the scheduler's policy.

5. **Container Launch**: The AM constructs a `ContainerLaunchContext` and sends it to the relevant NodeManager, which downloads the JAR and resources from HDFS, then starts the task JVM.

6. **Progress Reporting**: Each running task periodically reports its progress (0.0 to 1.0) to the AM via heartbeats.

7. **Job Completion**: When all tasks succeed, the AM sends `FinishApplicationMasterRequest` to the RM and cleans up.

**Fault Tolerance Mechanisms (3.5 marks):**

| Failure | Detection | Recovery |
|---------|-----------|----------|
| **Task JVM crash** | Task throws exception; AM sees non-zero exit code | Re-attempt task on a new container, up to `mapreduce.map.maxattempts` |
| **NodeManager crash** | RM heartbeat timeout after **10 minutes** | RM marks node as `LOST`; all running containers on it are killed and tasks rescheduled |
| **ApplicationMaster crash** | RM heartbeat timeout | RM launches a new AM; if `yarn.resourcemanager.am.attempt-restart` is enabled, completed map tasks are not re-executed |
| **ResourceManager crash** | ZooKeeper session loss | Standby RM in HA configuration becomes active via leader election |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (7-mark sub-questions):** Students frequently omit the **heartbeat timeout values** (3 sec for NM, 10 min for failure detection). Always quote default values explicitly. Also, do not confuse **AM restart** (preserves completed tasks) with **task retry** (re-runs from scratch). This distinction is worth 1–2 marks.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): Describe the Classic MapReduce v1 Architecture in detail. Explain the JobTracker-TaskTracker coordination model, task lifecycle, and the limitations that led to the development of YARN. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]

#### Part (a) — JobTracker-TaskTracker Coordination Model (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Classic MapReduce v1 (MR1) Architecture (4 marks):**

In MR1, the Hadoop cluster consists of:
- **One JobTracker** (master daemon, runs on the Namenode)
- **Many TaskTrackers** (slave daemons, one per worker node)
- **JobClient** (user-side API)

**Coordination Flow:**

1. **Job Submission**: The user calls `JobClient.runJob(conf)`. The JobClient:
   - Computes input splits (using `InputFormat.getSplits()`)
   - Uploads the JAR file, configuration, and split information to HDFS at `/tmp/hadoop-yarn/...`

2. **JobTracker Job Initialization**: The JobTracker:
   - Receives the job submission
   - Queues the job in the internal scheduler
   - Retrieves split metadata from HDFS

3. **TaskTracker Polling**: Each TaskTracker polls the JobTracker every **3 seconds** via the **Heartbeat RPC** protocol, requesting new task assignments.

4. **Task Assignment by JobTracker**: The JobTracker:
   - Selects an unassigned task (map or reduce)
   - Applies **data locality heuristics** (node-local → rack-local → off-rack)
   - Returns a `TaskAction` (LaunchTask, CommitTask, KillTask, etc.) in the heartbeat response

5. **Task Execution**: The TaskTracker:
   - Downloads the JAR and split files from HDFS
   - Launches a **separate JVM** (or reuses a slot from the pool)
   - Runs the user-defined `Mapper` or `Reducer` code

6. **Progress Reporting**: The task periodically reports its progress to the TaskTracker, which aggregates and forwards to the JobTracker.

**Task Lifecycle in MR1 (3 marks):**

The task transitions through the following states:
- **UNASSIGNED** → **ASSIGNED** (by JT) → **SCHEDULED** (queued at TT) → **RUNNING** (JVM active) → **SUCCEEDED / FAILED / KILLED**

**Key Configuration Parameters:**

| Property | Default Value | Purpose |
|----------|---------------|---------|
| `mapreduce.jobtracker.address` | `localhost:8021` | JT RPC endpoint |
| `mapreduce.tasktracker.address` | `localhost:0` (ephemeral) | TT RPC endpoint |
| `mapreduce.tasktracker.map.tasks.maximum` | **2** | Map slots per node |
| `mapreduce.tasktracker.reduce.tasks.maximum` | **2** | Reduce slots per node |
| `mapreduce.job.maps` | **2** | Default map tasks |
| `mapreduce.job.reduces` | **1** | Default reduce tasks |

---

#### Part (b) — Limitations of MR1 and Motivation for YARN (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

MR1 suffered from **five critical limitations** that constrained Hadoop's evolution:

**1. Scalability Bottleneck (2 marks):**
The JobTracker had to track *both* cluster resources *and* per-task state for every job. At ~**4,000 nodes**, the JT's memory and thread pool became saturated. The heartbeat RPC also became a thundering herd, as thousands of TTs simultaneously polled the JT every 3 seconds.

**2. Single Point of Failure (1 mark):**
If the JobTracker crashed, *every running job* in the cluster was lost. Hadoop had no high-availability mechanism for the JT in MR1.

**3. Rigid Resource Model (2 marks):**
The map-slot and reduce-slot model was **static and coarse-grained**:
- A node with 16 GB RAM and 8 cores was split into 2 fixed map slots + 2 fixed reduce slots.
- A long-running reduce task could not use idle map slots, and vice versa — causing **resource fragmentation**.
- Slots were sized in terms of *tasks*, not *resources*, making it impossible to run non-MapReduce workloads (Spark, Tez, Flink) on the same cluster.

**4. Tight Coupling (1 mark):**
The JobTracker was **hard-coded to understand only MapReduce semantics**. Running a graph processing job (Giraph) or a streaming job (Storm) required running a *separate* cluster.

**5. Poor Multi-tenancy (1 mark):**
There was no native way to guarantee fair sharing of resources across multiple users or organizations sharing the same cluster.

**YARN's Solution:**

YARN addressed all five limitations by:
- **Splitting** the JT role into RM (resource) + AM (lifecycle) → solved scalability
- **Adding RM HA** via ZooKeeper → solved SPOF
- **Replacing slots with containers** (RAM + vCores) → solved rigidity
- **Making AM pluggable** → solved coupling (Spark, Tez, Flink can all run)
- **Plugging in Fair/Capacity schedulers** → solved multi-tenancy

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam (Module 2):**

- ☐ **MR1** = JobTracker + TaskTracker (monolithic, SPOF, ~4k node limit)
- ☐ **MR2 / YARN** = ResourceManager + NodeManager + ApplicationMaster (decoupled, HA, 10k+ nodes)
- ☐ **ApplicationMaster** is a *per-job* container launched by the RM
- ☐ **Container** = bundle of {RAM in MB, vCores} on a single node
- ☐ **Heartbeat interval** = **3 seconds** (NM → RM and TT → JT)
- ☐ **Failure detection timeout** = **10 minutes** of missed heartbeats
- ☐ **Speculative execution** = duplicate slow tasks, *not* for fault tolerance
- ☐ **Data locality order**: Node-local > Rack-local > Off-rack
- ☐ **Default map tasks** = $\lceil \text{Input Size} / \text{Block Size} \rceil$, with **block size = 128 MB**
- ☐ **Default max attempts** = **4** (for both map and reduce)
- ☐ **YARN schedulers** = FIFO, Fair Scheduler, Capacity Scheduler (all pluggable in RM)
- ☐ **ResourceManager HA** = ZooKeeper-based leader election
- ☐ **Task state machine**: UNASSIGNED → ASSIGNED → SCHEDULED → RUNNING → {SUCCEEDED, FAILED, KILLED, COMMIT_PENDING}
- ☐ **Shuffle phase**: reduce tasks copy map outputs via HTTP from completed map tasks
- ☐ **AM restart** preserves completed map task state to avoid re-execution

> **End of Notes — KTU 2024 Scheme | PECNT746 | Module 2**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Designing MapReduce Implementations: Using MapReduce as a Framework for Parallel Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT746 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Designing MapReduce Implementations: Using MapReduce as a Framework for Parallel Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**MapReduce** is a programming model and an associated parallel processing framework for processing and generating large-scale datasets in a distributed computing environment. Introduced by Google in 2004 (Dean & Ghemawat), it abstracts the complexity of distributed parallelism behind two user-defined functions: a **Mapper** and a **Reducer**, orchestrated by a master controller (the *JobTracker* / *ResourceManager*).

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** treatment of this topic, designing a MapReduce implementation is not merely about writing two functions — it requires the engineer to model a computation as a **directed data-flow graph** in which:

- The **map phase** processes one logical input record (key-value pair) at a time and emits an *intermediate* key-value set.
- The framework transparently performs **shuffle, sort, group, and partition** operations.
- The **reduce phase** processes all intermediate values associated with the same intermediate key.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Definition to Memorize**
> *MapReduce is a software framework that allows developers to write programs that process massive amounts of unstructured data in parallel across a distributed cluster of commodity computers, hiding the details of parallelization, fault-tolerance, data distribution, and load balancing behind simple map(k,v) and reduce(k, list[v]) abstractions.*

## 1.2 The Conceptual Analogy — The National Library Sorting Project

Imagine you are the Chief Librarian of a **National Library** with **10 million unsorted books** scattered across 50 branches, and you have been asked to produce a complete frequency count of every word appearing in all books.

You cannot do this alone. So you:

1. **Hire 1,000 assistants** (Mappers) and give each a stack of books. Each assistant reads a page and writes down a slip of paper: `("the", 1)`, `("data", 1)`, etc. — one slip per word. This is the **Map** stage.

2. You install **50 sorting bins** (Reducers), one per letter of the alphabet, pre-labeled. All slips labeled `"a..."` go to bin A, `"b..."` to bin B, and so on. The library's conveyor belt system does this automatically — this is the **Shuffle & Sort** stage.

3. You assign **one senior librarian per bin** (Reducer instance). Each senior librarian piles up all slips for the word *"the"* and adds the numbers. Result: `"the" → 9,876,543`. This is the **Reduce** stage.

> [!NOTE]
> **What MapReduce hides from you** (the librarian chief):
> - How the slips physically travel (network shuffle).
> - What happens if an assistant faints (fault tolerance / task re-execution).
> - How books are stored on different shelves (data locality, HDFS blocks).
> - How the bins stay balanced (partitioning, skew handling).

## 1.3 Key Terminology Quick-Glossary (KTU High-Yield)

| Term | Meaning | KTU Exam Frequency |
|------|---------|-------------------|
| **Job** | The full unit of work — one execution of a MapReduce program. | High |
| **Task** | A single execution of a mapper or reducer on a data slice. | High |
| **Input Split** | A byte-range chunk of input fed to one map task (typically matches an **HDFS** block, **64 MB** or **128 MB**). | High |
| **Intermediate Key** | The key emitted by the mapper, used for grouping at the reducer. | High |
| **Partitioner** | Function deciding which reducer receives an intermediate (k,v) pair — default: `hash(k) mod R`. | Very High |
| **Combiner** | A mini-reducer that runs *locally* on the mapper's output to reduce data shipped to reducers. | Very High |
| **Shuffle** | The transfer of intermediate data from mappers to reducers across the network. | High |
| **Data Locality** | The principle of running map tasks on nodes where the data blocks already reside. | High |
| **Writable** | Hadoop's serialization format (e.g., `IntWritable`, `Text`, `LongWritable`). | Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant to remember:** The default HDFS block size in Hadoop is **64 MB** (Hadoop 1.x) or **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x+). This dictates the maximum granularity of mapper parallelism.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a MapReduce Job — The Five Phases

A MapReduce job, from a black-box perspective, is decomposed into **five logical phases**. KTU examiners expect you to be able to list, define, and draw each:

1. **Input Splits & Map Phase**
   - Input file F is divided into M splits (default size = HDFS block size B).
   - Each split is processed by one `map()` invocation, producing a list of intermediate (k, v) pairs.

2. **Combiner Phase (Optional, Local Aggregation)**
   - Runs *in-memory, on the mapper node*, immediately after the mapper.
   - Reduces the volume of data crossing the network.
   - **Idempotency and associativity requirement** (because combiner is not guaranteed to run).

3. **Shuffle & Sort Phase (Framework-controlled)**
   - All intermediate pairs are partitioned into **R** buckets (one per reducer).
   - Pairs are **sorted** by key within each partition and **grouped** by key.
   - The costliest phase in most real workloads.

4. **Reduce Phase**
   - For each unique key k, the reduce function is called **once** with k and an iterator over its value list.

5. **Output Phase**
   - Reducer outputs are written back to HDFS, typically one file per reducer (named `part-r-00000`, `part-r-00001`, ...).

## 2.2 The MapReduce Cost Model (Lin & Dyer / Leskovec et al.)

The canonical cost model (used in KTU question banks) treats MapReduce as a sequence of distributed operations. Let:

- **M** = number of mappers
- **R** = number of reducers
- **N** = total number of input records
- **|F|** = total input data size (bytes)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The MapReduce Cost Model Formulas — These appear almost every year in KTU ESE:**

| Cost Component | Formula (Big-O terms) | Engineering Meaning |
|----------------|-----------------------|---------------------|
| **Map Cost** | $O(M \cdot t_{m})$ | Per-mapper processing time. |
| **Shuffle Cost (Network)** | $O(\sum_{i=1}^{M} \mid V_i \mid \cdot s_{n})$ | Bytes shipped over network (intermediate output size). |
| **Sort Cost** | $O(\mid V \mid \log \mid V \mid)$ | Framework sorts all intermediate records. |
| **Reduce Cost** | $O(R \cdot t_{r})$ | Per-reducer processing time. |
| **Total Job Time** | $T_{job} \approx \max_{i}\left(T_{map,i}\right) + T_{shuffle} + \max_{j}\left(T_{reduce,j}\right)$ | Bottleneck = slowest map + shuffle + slowest reduce. |

where:
- $V_i$ = intermediate output of mapper $i$ (key-value pairs)
- $|V|$ = total intermediate records across all mappers
- $t_m$ = average per-record map processing time
- $t_r$ = average per-key reduce processing time
- $s_n$ = per-byte network serialization cost

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Combiners

The single most-tested design concept in KTU Module 2 is **when to use a combiner**. The reasoning chain is:

1. Mappers may emit **massive** volumes of (k, v) pairs (e.g., for word count over 10 TB of text: $\sim 10^{12}$ pairs).
2. The network shuffle of these pairs is the **dominant** cost.
3. If the reduce function is **commutative and associative** (e.g., sum, max, min, count), it can be applied *locally* on each mapper's output, collapsing many (k, 1) pairs into one (k, n) pair.
4. This **reduces shuffle volume by an order of magnitude** in word-count-style problems.

> [!WARNING]
> Combiner **cannot** be used for averages, medians, or any non-associative aggregation. The KTU standard trick question: *"Why can't you use a combiner to compute the mean salary per department?"* — Because mean is *not associative*: `mean(a, b, c) ≠ mean(mean(a, b), c)`.

## 2.4 Partitioning — Controlling Data Distribution

The default partitioner is:

$$
\text{partition}(k, R) \;=\; \left( \text{hash}(k) \bmod R \right)
$$

Custom partitioners are written when:
- Output must be **sorted globally** (e.g., secondary sort).
- **Skew** must be mitigated (e.g., hot keys sent to more reducers).
- **Join keys** need to be co-partitioned (reduce-side join pre-condition).

## 2.5 Number of Mappers and Reducers — KTU Numerical Practice

**Mappers (M):** Determined by framework, not user.
- Formula: $M = \lceil |F| / B \rceil$ where $B$ is HDFS block size.

**Reducers (R):** Determined by user, with default = 1.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb from Hadoop production (commonly quoted in KTU):**
> Target reducer input size per task $\approx$ **1 GB** (single reduce) or **256–512 MB** (Hadoop default), so $R \approx \lceil |V| / 1\text{ GB} \rceil$.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

MapReduce-as-a-framework underpins:
- **Batch ETL pipelines** at Google, Facebook, LinkedIn.
- **Search index construction** (inverted indexes, PageRank iterations).
- **Log analytics** at petabyte scale.
- **Training data pre-processing** in early-stage ML pipelines (before Spark dominated).

The framework's value is **not speed** (it's actually disk-heavy and slow), but **scalability, fault tolerance, and programmer productivity** — a single MapReduce job can scale linearly to thousands of nodes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Estimating the Number of Mappers from First Principles

**Given:** A raw input file of size $|F| = 8 \text{ GB}$ stored on HDFS with a default block size $B = 128 \text{ MB}$.

**Goal:** Compute the number of mappers $M$.

$$
M \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{\lvert F \rvert}{B} \right\rceil
$$

**Step-by-step substitution:**

$$
M \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{8 \cdot 1024 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil
$$

$$
M \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{8192}{128} \right\rceil
$$

$$
M \;=\; \lceil 64 \rceil \;=\; 64 \text{ mappers}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ceiling and not floor?** Because a *partial* split must still be assigned to a mapper; you cannot leave any byte of input unprocessed. Hence the upper-bound rounding.

## 3.2 Derivation: Estimating Number of Reducers from Intermediate Volume

**Given:** A word-count job over 1 TB of English text. Assume average word-count density: **100 distinct words per line**, with **10 million lines** per mapper. Each mapper emits, on average, **50,000 unique (word, count) pairs** after local combiner aggregation.

**Step 1 — Total intermediate records (post-combiner):**
Assume $M = 8192$ mappers (since $1 \text{ TB} / 128 \text{ MB} = 8192$).

$$
\lvert V \rvert \;=\; M \cdot \text{(records per mapper post-combiner)}
$$

$$
\lvert V \rvert \;=\; 8192 \cdot 50{,}000 \;=\; 4.096 \times 10^{8} \text{ records}
$$

**Step 2 — Assuming each record is ~50 bytes on the wire:**
$$
\text{Shuffle data} \;\approx\; 4.096 \times 10^{8} \times 50 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\text{Shuffle data} \;\approx\; 2.048 \times 10^{10} \text{ bytes} \;\approx\; 20.48 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the 1 GB-per-reducer rule:**
$$
R \;\approx\; \left\lceil \frac{20.48 \text{ GB}}{1 \text{ GB}} \right\rceil \;=\; 21 \text{ reducers (round up to power of 2)}
$$

$$
R_{\text{chosen}} \;=\; 32 \text{ reducers}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Always round reducer counts to the **next power of 2** (16, 32, 64, …) in production. This optimizes partitioner arithmetic and reduces data skew variance. Examiners often expect this rounding.

## 3.3 Detailed Walkthrough — MapReduce Design for Word Count

We will design a complete MapReduce job for word frequency counting. This is **the** canonical KTU Module 2 example.

### 3.3.1 Mapper Logic (Pseudocode)

```text
FUNCTION map(LongWritable offset, Text line):
    words = line.split("\\s+")
    FOR each w IN words:
        cleaned = w.toLowerCase().trim()
        IF cleaned is not empty:
            EMIT(cleaned, 1)
END FUNCTION
```

**Explanation:**
- Input: byte offset + a line of text (default `TextInputFormat`).
- Output: emits (word, 1) for every word in the line.

### 3.3.2 Combiner Logic (Optional but recommended)

```text
FUNCTION combine(Text word, Iterator<IntWritable> counts):
    sum = 0
    FOR each c IN counts:
        sum = sum + c.get()
    EMIT(word, sum)
END FUNCTION
```

**Explanation:** The combiner aggregates counts *locally* per mapper. Since addition is commutative and associative, the result is mathematically identical to a non-combiner run, but network traffic drops by **~10x to 100x** in practice.

### 3.3.3 Partitioner

The default partitioner applies:

$$
\text{partition}(k, R) \;=\; \left(\text{hash}(k) \bmod R\right)
$$

This guarantees that all occurrences of the same word `k` go to the same reducer, regardless of which mapper emitted them.

### 3.3.4 Reducer Logic

```text
FUNCTION reduce(Text word, Iterator<IntWritable> counts):
    total = 0
    FOR each c IN counts:
        total = total + c.get()
    EMIT(word, total)
END FUNCTION
```

**Explanation:** The framework has *already* grouped and sorted. We simply sum and emit the final (word, total_count) pair.

## 3.4 Detailed Walkthrough — MapReduce Design for Inverted Index (Search Engine Style)

The **inverted index** maps each word to the list of documents containing it. This is a **two-stage** MapReduce design — and KTU frequently tests multi-stage job reasoning.

### 3.4.1 Stage 1: Compute Word-Document Frequencies

**Mapper:**
```text
FUNCTION map(DocID docID, Text content):
    FOR each word w IN content:
        EMIT((w, docID), 1)
END FUNCTION
```

**Reducer:**
```text
FUNCTION reduce((Text word, DocID docID), Iterator<IntWritable> counts):
    total = sum(counts)
    EMIT(word, (docID, total))
END FUNCTION
```

### 3.4.2 Stage 2: Group by Word, Sort by Frequency

**Mapper (Identity):**
```text
FUNCTION map(Text word, Text docID_count):
    EMIT(word, docID_count)
END FUNCTION
```

**Reducer (using a custom comparator for secondary sort):**
```text
FUNCTION reduce(Text word, Iterator<Text> postings):
    sortedPostings = sortByCountDesc(postings)
    EMIT(word, join(sortedPostings, ","))
END FUNCTION
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two stages?** A *single* MapReduce job can only sort by the **intermediate key**. To sort postings *by frequency within each word*, we must promote frequency into the key during Stage 1's reducer output, then use a **key comparator** in Stage 2.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Design Patterns for Common MapReduce Algorithms

| Problem | Design Pattern | Combiner? | Custom Partitioner? | # of Stages |
|---------|----------------|-----------|---------------------|-------------|
| Word Count | Standard Map + Sum-Reduce | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | 1 |
| Inverted Index | Multi-stage with custom grouping | ❌ No (would lose doc IDs) | ❌ No | 2 |
| PageRank | Iterative job chain | ✅ Yes (dangling mass) | ✅ Yes (by node ID) | Many |
| Distributed Sort | Identity Mapper + Sorted Output | ❌ No | ❌ No (default works) | 1 |
| Reduce-Side Join | Co-partitioning on join key | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (essential) | 1 |
| Map-Side Join | Input pre-sorted + composite key | N/A | N/A (no reducers) | 0 (map only) |
| Counting Distinct Items | HyperLogLog sketch or two-stage | ❌ Tricky | ✅ Yes | 2 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU pitfall — Map-Side vs Reduce-Side Join:** A *map-side join* requires that **both** inputs be pre-partitioned and pre-sorted on the join key (e.g., a previous MapReduce output or a small lookup table loaded into the **DistributedCache**). A *reduce-side join* has no such requirement but ships more data over the network. Examiners will ask: *"Given two 1 TB datasets, which join strategy would you use and why?"*

## 3.6 Exhaustive Derivation — Estimating the Total Job Wall-Clock Time

**Scenario:** 10 mappers, each taking 60 seconds. 2 reducers, each taking 90 seconds. Shuffle takes 45 seconds. Negligible combiner time.

**Total job time formula:**
$$
T_{job} \;=\; \max_{i}\left(T_{map,i}\right) \;+\; T_{shuffle} \;+\; \max_{j}\left(T_{reduce,j}\right)
$$

**Step-by-step:**
$$
T_{map} \;=\; \max(60, 60, \ldots, 60) \;=\; 60 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{shuffle} \;=\; 45 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{reduce} \;=\; \max(90, 90) \;=\; 90 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{job} \;=\; 60 \;+\; 45 \;+\; 90 \;=\; 195 \text{ seconds}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** All map tasks run *in parallel* (subject to cluster capacity), so we take the **maximum**, not the sum. The same applies to reducers. This is why **straggler tasks** (slow nodes) are the biggest performance killer in MapReduce.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a MapReduce Job

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HDFS Input File] --> B[InputFormat Splitter]
    B --> C1[Mapper 1]
    B --> C2[Mapper 2]
    B --> C3[Mapper M]
    C1 --> D1[Combiner 1]
    C2 --> D2[Combiner 2]
    C3 --> D3[Combiner M]
    D1 --> E[Shuffle and Sort Framework]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F1[Reducer 1]
    E --> F2[Reducer R]
    F1 --> G1[HDFS Output Part r 00000]
    F2 --> G2[HDFS Output Part r 00001]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Data flows from left (HDFS input) to right (HDFS output). The vertical stacking of mappers and reducers represents **parallel execution** on different cluster nodes. The central `Shuffle and Sort Framework` is a black box controlled by the JobTracker/ResourceManager.

## 4.2 MapReduce Data Flow — Sequence View (Single Mapper-Reducer Pair)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Driver
    participant Mapper
    participant Combiner
    participant Shuffle
    participant Reducer
    participant HDFS

    Driver->>Mapper: invoke map(k1, v1)
    Mapper->>Combiner: emit intermediate list
    Combiner->>Shuffle: local aggregate
    Shuffle->>Reducer: partition + sort + group
    Reducer->>HDFS: write final output
    Driver->>Driver: monitor + fault recovery
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture of a Multi-Stage MapReduce Chain (e.g., Inverted Index)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Stage1[Stage 1 Job - Build Word Doc Pairs]
        S1A[Raw Documents HDFS] --> S1B[Mapper Emit word docID 1]
        S1B --> S1C[Reducer Sum counts per word docID]
        S1C --> S1D[Stage 1 Output HDFS]
    end
    S1D --> Stage2
    subgraph Stage2[Stage 2 Job - Sort Postings by Frequency]
        S2A[Identity Mapper] --> S2B[Secondary Sort Comparator]
        S2B --> S2C[Reducer Emit word postings list sorted]
        S2C --> S2D[Final Inverted Index HDFS]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** When asked "draw the data flow of a MapReduce job," always show:
> 1. **M** mapper boxes in parallel.
> 2. **Combiners** (if used) attached to each mapper.
> 3. A single "Shuffle & Sort" block.
> 4. **R** reducer boxes in parallel.
> 5. Arrow labels for **(k, v) pairs** with sample keys.

## 4.4 Combiner vs No-Combiner — Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NoCombiner[No Combiner Path]
        N1[Mapper emits 1 million pairs] --> N2[Ship all 1 million to network]
        N2 --> N3[Reducer aggregates]
    end
    subgraph WithCombiner[With Combiner Path]
        C1[Mapper emits 1 million pairs] --> C2[Combiner reduces to 50,000]
        C2 --> C3[Ship 50,000 to network]
        C3 --> C4[Reducer aggregates]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Quantitative takeaway:** The combiners path reduces network shuffle by a factor of $\sim 20$ in this illustration. In real word-count over 1 TB, the improvement is often **20x to 100x**, the single most impactful optimization a designer can make.
