[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **List any two differences between Local Mode and MapReduce Mode of Pig execution.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | # | Local Mode | MapReduce Mode | |---|---|---| | 1 | Runs in a **single JVM** on the local machine | Runs on a **distributed Hadoop cluster** via YARN | | 2 | Reads/writes from the **local file system** (`file:///`) | Reads/writes from **HDFS** (`hdfs://nn:8020/...`) | | 3 | Invoked using `pig -x local` | Invoked using `pig` or `pig -x mapreduce` | | 4 | Intermediate data stays in **Java heap memory** | Intermediate data is written to **HDFS disk** during Shuffle | | 5 | Used for **development and unit testing** | Used for **production-scale batch processing** | *(Any 2 well-explained points = 3 marks; 1 point = 1.5 marks)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Which execution mode of Pig is suitable for unit testing a script on a 100-row sample, and why?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Local Mode** (`pig -x local`) is the most suitable. 1. It **does not require** a running Hadoop cluster or HDFS, so setup overhead is zero. **(1 mark)** 2. It executes entirely within a **single JVM**, providing **sub-second feedback** on small samples. **(1 mark)** 3. The script syntax, schema, and UDFs can be validated cheaply before being promoted to a production **MapReduce mode** job. **(1 mark)** --- ## 📝 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern) > Per KTU 2024 ESE regulation, every Part-B question in a module carries **14 marks** and offers an **internal choice** between two alternatives. Both alternatives are given below. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the **two primary execution modes** of Apache Pig with suitable diagrams. Discuss how the compilation pipeline (Parser → Logical Plan → Physical Plan) differs in each mode. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Pig script is to be executed on a **750 GB CSV file** stored in HDFS. The HDFS block size is 256 MB. Calculate the **number of mappers** that will be spawned in MapReduce mode. Justify whether Local mode is feasible for this workload. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution** **(a) — 7 Marks Model Solution:** **Compilation Pipeline (Same skeleton, different executor):** **(2 marks)** $$ \boxed{\; \text{Pig Latin} \xrightarrow{\text{Parser}} \text{Logical Plan} \xrightarrow{\text{Optimizer}} \text{Physical Plan} \xrightarrow{\text{Executor}} \text{Output} \;} $$ **Local Mode:** **(2.5 marks)** - Single JVM process; the `PigServer` runs the physical plan using Hadoop's `LocalJobRunner`. - Logical operators are translated into in-memory Java operators (e.g., `POGroupImpl` uses a `HashMap`). - Intermediate data flows through Java iterators in RAM — **no disk I/O**. - File system: `file:///`. No YARN, no HDFS. - Use case: development, unit testing, small datasets (≤ tens of GB). **MapReduce Mode:** **(2.5 marks)** - The physical plan is converted into one or more MapReduce jobs. - `LOAD`, `FILTER`, projections → Map phase. - `GROUP`, `JOIN`, `ORDER`, `DISTINCT` → trigger a Shuffle/Reduce boundary. - The job is submitted to YARN's `ResourceManager`; the `ApplicationMaster` requests containers on `NodeManager`s. - Intermediate data is written to local disk of mappers, shuffled across the network, and sorted before reducers consume it. - File system: `hdfs://nn:8020/`. Use case: production batch, petabyte-scale. *(Refer to Diagrams 4.2 and 4.3 from Section 4 for full marks on the "suitable diagrams" sub-part.)* --- **(b) — 7 Marks Model Solution:** **Given:** **(0.5 marks)** - Input size $S_{\text{input}} = 750 \;\text{GB}$ - HDFS block size $B = 256 \;\text{MB}$ **Step 1 — Convert to common unit:** **(1 mark)** $$ S_{\text{input}} = 750 \times 1024 \;\text{MB} = 768{,}000 \;\text{MB} $$ **Step 2 — Apply the mapper formula:** **(1.5 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Mappers} &= \left\lceil \dfrac{S_{\text{input}}}{B} \right\rceil \\[6pt] &= \left\lceil \dfrac{768{,}000 \;\text{MB}}{256 \;\text{MB}} \right\rceil \\[6pt] &= \left\lceil 3000 \right\rceil \\[6pt] &= 3000 \text{ mappers} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Feasibility of Local Mode:** **(4 marks)** - **Data size constraint:** 750 GB cannot fit into a typical laptop's local disk (1–2 TB max), and certainly not into RAM (16–64 GB). Local mode would either OOM-crash or thrash the disk. **(2 marks)** - **No parallelism:** Local mode spawns only **1 mapper-equivalent** (sequential), so a 750 GB job might take **hours** compared to MapReduce's **minutes** with 3000 mappers. **(1 mark)** - **No fault tolerance:** A single JVM crash on a 750 GB job wastes all prior progress. **(1 mark)** **Conclusion:** **MapReduce mode is the correct choice** for this workload. Local mode is **infeasible**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **(a)** Describe the **Tez and Spark execution modes** of Pig. Compare them with MapReduce mode in terms of intermediate data handling, latency, and use cases. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A startup has a 4-node Hadoop cluster (each node: 16 GB RAM, 8 cores, 2 TB disk) and writes a Pig script that performs 5 successive `GROUP` operations. Explain why **Spark mode** would be more efficient than **MapReduce mode** for this script. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution** **(a) — 7 Marks Model Solution:** **Tez Mode (`pig -x tez`):** **(2.5 marks)** - Pig compiles the script into a **DAG of Tez vertices** submitted to YARN. - Unlike MapReduce's rigid Map→Reduce stages, Tez allows **multiple Reduce vertices** to be chained **without writing intermediate data to HDFS** between them. - Uses a **container reuse pool** to avoid the 25 s startup penalty of every MR job. - **Latency:** 5–10× faster than MapReduce for complex DAGs. - **Use case:** Low-latency batch queries (e.g., Hive-on-Tez in Facebook). **Spark Mode (`pig -x spark`):** **(2.5 marks)** - Pig compiles the script into a **Spark DAG of RDD transformations** (`map`, `filter`, `groupByKey`, `reduceByKey`). - Intermediate data is held **in executor RAM** (RDD `cache()` / `persist()`) — no HDFS round-trips. - **Latency:** Near-interactive for cached datasets. - **Use case:** Iterative ML algorithms (K-means, ALS, PageRank), ad-hoc analytics. **Comparison Table:** **(2 marks)** | Aspect | MapReduce | Tez | Spark | |---|---|---|---| | Intermediate I/O | **HDFS disk** | HDFS + container cache | **RAM** | | Latency | Highest | Medium | Lowest | | Iterative Workloads | Poor | Medium | **Excellent** | --- **(b) — 7 Marks Model Solution:** **Why Spark mode is more efficient for 5 successive `GROUP`s:** **(7 marks)** 1. **MapReduce mode would launch 5 separate MapReduce jobs.** Each job incurs a **~25 s YARN container startup**, so 5 jobs = ~125 s of pure overhead with **5× disk I/O** for intermediate shuffle. **(3 marks)** 2. **Spark mode keeps data in RAM as a cached RDD.** The first job loads the data into executor memory, and the subsequent 4 `GROUP` operations read from RAM (microseconds) instead of HDFS (milliseconds). **(2 marks)** 3. **DAG optimization:** Spark's DAG scheduler can **fuse multiple operators** within a stage, reducing task count. **(1 mark)** 4. **Small cluster caveat:** With only 64 GB total RAM (4 × 16 GB), the dataset must fit in ~50 GB after reserving executor overhead; otherwise, the user must enable `MEMORY_AND_DISK` persistence. **(1 mark)** **Conclusion:** Spark mode cuts down 5 MR jobs to 1 Spark job with **in-memory pipelining**, achieving **5–10× speedup** for chained group-by operations on a small cluster. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks 🚨** > > 1. **Writing `pig -x local` on the cluster or `pig` on a laptop** — Examiners **deduct 1 mark** for failing to specify the correct command in the answer. Always pair the mode with its invocation flag. > 2. **Forgetting to convert GB → MB** in mapper calculations — a $750\,\text{GB} / 256\,\text{MB}$ mistake becomes $750 / 256 = 2.9$ (wrong!) instead of $768000 / 256 = 3000$. **Always convert to a common unit first.** > 3. **Confusing "execution mode" with "execution engine".** Pig Latin is the script, the compiler is the same, the **backend engine** (LocalJobRunner / YARN-MR / Tez / Spark) differs. Examiners expect you to use the correct term. > 4. **Saying "Local mode uses HDFS"** — this is a **fatal error**. Local mode uses the **local file system (`file:///`)**. HDFS is exclusively for distributed modes. > 5. **Omitting the 3-line diagram** in 7-mark sub-parts — KTU rubric reserves **1–2 marks** for an *appropriately labeled* diagram. Always include a block diagram even if it is hand-drawn. > 6. **In the comparison table, using more than 1 mark per cell.** Keep each cell to **1 crisp point**; examiners do not award marks for verbose sentences inside cells. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Execution Modes of Pig** - ✅ Pig has **4 execution modes**: **Local, MapReduce, Tez, Spark**. - ✅ **Local mode** = `pig -x local` = single JVM = local file system = no HDFS, no YARN. - ✅ **MapReduce mode** = `pig` (default) = Hadoop cluster = HDFS = YARN-managed. - ✅ **Tez mode** = `pig -x tez` = DAG of Tez vertices on YARN = lower latency than MR. - ✅ **Spark mode** = `pig -x spark` = RDD transformations on Spark = in-memory execution. - ✅ Compilation pipeline is **identical** across all modes; only the **executor** differs. - ✅ **Mapper count** formula: $\left\lceil \dfrac{\text{Input Size in MB}}{\text{Block Size in MB}} \right\rceil$. Default HDFS block = **128 MB**. - ✅ **Local mode is for development**, **MapReduce is for production batch**, **Tez is for low-latency batch**, **Spark is for iterative & interactive**. - ✅ Local mode has **no fault tolerance**; MapReduce/Tez/Spark inherit HDFS fault tolerance. - ✅ Intermediate data: **RAM (Local/Spark)** vs. **HDFS disk (MapReduce/Tez)**. - ✅ For KTU, the **favourite 14-marker** is *"Compare Local and MapReduce mode with a sample mapper calculation"* — always expect this!

topics.title: Comparison of Pig with Databases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT746 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Comparison of Pig with Databases # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition: Apache Pig > [!IMPORTANT] > **Apache Pig** is a high-level platform for creating MapReduce programs used with Hadoop. It consists of a high-level language (**Pig Latin**) for expressing data analysis programs, coupled with infrastructure for evaluating these programs. The salient property of Pig programs is that their structure is amenable to substantial parallelization, which in turn enables them to handle very large data sets distributed over a cluster of commodity servers. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus context, **Pig** is positioned as a **data-flow scripting language** that sits on top of **Hadoop's HDFS** storage and **MapReduce/YARN** execution engine, providing abstraction over low-level MapReduce Java code. ## 1.2 Formal Definition: Traditional Databases (RDBMS) > [!NOTE] > A **Relational Database Management System (RDBMS)** is a software system that organizes data into formally described tables (relations) with enforced schemas, supports **ACID properties** (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability), and uses **SQL (Structured Query Language)** as the standard query interface. Examples: MySQL, Oracle, PostgreSQL, SQL Server. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy — The Library System:** > > Imagine two ways to manage a massive, ever-growing library of mixed-format documents (books, magazines, scanned PDFs, handwritten notes): > > | Approach | Analogue | What It Means | > |---|---|---| > | **Traditional RDBMS** | A strict librarian who insists you declare the title, author, page count, and ISBN **before** you even place a book on the shelf. Every record must conform to a rigid pre-defined table. | **Schema-on-Write** — structure is enforced at write time | > | **Apache Pig** | A flexible archivist who lets you **dump** boxes of documents into a warehouse first, and then sorts/categorizes them **only when someone asks a question**. | **Schema-on-Read** — structure is applied at query/analysis time | > > The library grows fast, the formats keep changing, and you need to process *tera* or *peta* bytes — that is where Pig (and the Hadoop ecosystem) shine, while RDBMS struggles with rigid, upfront schema costs. ## 1.4 Why This Comparison Matters in Big Data KTU 2024 Scheme highlights the *paradigm shift* from traditional **OLTP (Online Transaction Processing)** systems (which RDBMS excel at) to **OLAP (Online Analytical Processing)** over **semi-structured / unstructured** Big Data, which is precisely the gap Pig Latin fills. The comparison clarifies *when to use which tool* — a frequently tested KTU conceptual question. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Note:** Many students incorrectly state "Pig is a database." It is **NOT** a database — it is a **data processing/analysis platform** that operates *on top of* data stored in HDFS (or other distributed stores). This distinction is a high-frequency 2-mark question. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Structural Breakdown: The Comparison Dimensions The KTU 2024 Scheme question paper pattern expects students to compare Pig with RDBMS across **eight canonical dimensions**. We enumerate them as a structured, bulleted logic ladder: ### Dimension 1 — Data Volume - **Pig:** Designed for **petabyte-scale** datasets. Operates over HDFS which spans hundreds/thousands of commodity nodes. - **RDBMS:** Typically scales to **gigabytes → low terabytes** on single high-end servers (with sharding/replication reaching higher). ### Dimension 2 — Schema Enforcement - **Pig:** Uses **Schema-on-Read**. Schema is defined at the time of loading/analysing the data. The raw file is left untouched. - **RDBMS:** Uses **Schema-on-Write**. Schema must be defined *before* inserting any data. Violations are rejected at write time. ### Dimension 3 — Data Type Tolerance - **Pig:** Handles **structured, semi-structured (JSON, XML, logs), and unstructured (text, images)** data with equal ease. - **RDBMS:** Optimised exclusively for **structured, tabular** data conforming to a fixed schema. ### Dimension 4 — Query / Processing Language - **Pig:** **Pig Latin** — a **data-flow language** with procedural semantics. Each step transforms the data into a new dataset. - **RDBMS:** **SQL** — a **declarative** language. User specifies *what* is needed; the engine decides *how* to retrieve it. ### Dimension 5 — Storage Paradigm - **Pig:** No proprietary storage — reads from HDFS, HBase, Cassandra, MongoDB, local filesystem, S3, etc. - **RDBMS:** Has its **own storage engine** with proprietary file formats (InnoDB, B-tree, columnar stores). ### Dimension 6 — Latency vs Throughput - **Pig:** Optimised for **high-throughput batch processing** (latency in minutes to hours). Not suitable for real-time queries. - **RDBMS:** Optimised for **low-latency OLTP** (millisecond response) and concurrent transactional workloads. ### Dimension 7 — ACID Compliance - **Pig:** **No ACID guarantees.** Operations are idempotent at best; failure handling is best-effort. - **RDBMS:** **Full ACID compliance.** Guarantees are enforced by the storage and transaction manager. ### Dimension 8 — Cost & Hardware - **Pig:** Runs on **commodity hardware (horizontal scaling)**. Economical for massive scale. - **RDBMS:** Traditionally requires **high-end vertical scaling (scale-up)** with expensive enterprise licenses. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The "formula" for choosing the right tool can be expressed as a **decision matrix**. Treat the table below as your **High-Yield Cheat Sheet** for the 14-mark comparison question. | **Comparison Parameter** | **Apache Pig** | **Traditional RDBMS (Database)** | |---|---|---| | **Data Volume Capacity** | **Petabytes (PB)**, scales horizontally | **Gigabytes (GB) to a few TB**, scales vertically | | **Schema Model** | **Schema-on-Read** (late binding) | **Schema-on-Write** (early binding) | | **Data Type** | Structured, semi-structured, unstructured | Structured only | | **Processing Language** | **Pig Latin** (procedural / data-flow) | **SQL** (declarative) | | **Storage** | HDFS, HBase, NoSQL stores (no native store) | Proprietary native storage engine | | **Latency Profile** | **High latency** (batch, minutes–hours) | **Low latency** (OLTP, milliseconds) | | **ACID Properties** | **No ACID** guarantees | **Full ACID** compliance | | **Processing Mode** | Batch / offline (MapReduce, Tez, Spark) | Online (interactive) transactions + batch | | **Parallelization** | Automatic, inherent in execution engine | Limited, requires manual sharding/replication | | **Hardware Cost** | **Low** (commodity clusters) | **High** (enterprise servers + licensing) | | **Primary Use Case** | ETL, log analytics, clickstream, data warehousing | OLTP, ERP, CRM, financial transactions | | **Failure Handling** | Task re-execution by YARN; at-least-once semantics | Transaction rollback / write-ahead logs | | **Indexing** | Limited (partitioning only) | Rich (B-tree, hash, bitmap, full-text) | | **Example Products** | Apache Pig (over Hadoop) | MySQL, Oracle, PostgreSQL, SQL Server | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pig Latin basic statement form** (high-yield snippet for KTU): > $$\text{LOAD} \rightarrow \text{TRANSFORM (FILTER, GROUP, JOIN, FOREACH)} \rightarrow \text{STORE/DUMP}$$ ## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Telecom:** Pig is used to analyse **CDR (Call Detail Records)** to detect fraud across petabytes of semi-structured logs. - **E-Commerce:** Pig pipelines feed recommendation engines by transforming clickstream events into user-session vectors. - **Banking:** RDBMS continues to handle **transactional ledgers** (ACID-critical), while Pig handles **anti-money-laundering (AML)** analytics on the same data exported to HDFS. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Formal Derivation: The "Which Tool?" Decision Logic We now **derive a logical decision rule** for choosing Pig vs RDBMS, presented step-by-step in the spirit of an analytical proof. **Step 1 — Define the input data descriptor $D$.** Let $D$ denote a dataset. Define: - $S(D) = 1$ if the dataset is **structured**, $0$ otherwise. - $V(D)$ = volume of the dataset, in bytes. - $L(D)$ = acceptable query latency, in seconds. - $T(D) = 1$ if the workload is **transactional** (frequent read/write of small records), $0$ if it is **analytical** (bulk scans). **Step 2 — State the RDBMS applicability conditions.** An RDBMS is the correct tool **iff** all four conditions hold simultaneously: - $S(D) = 1$ - $V(D) \le 10^{12}$ bytes (1 TB threshold; above this, vertical scaling becomes prohibitively expensive) - $L(D) \le 1$ second - $T(D) = 1$ Mathematically: $$\text{Use RDBMS} \iff S(D) = 1 \;\land\; V(D) \le 10^{12} \;\land\; L(D) \le 1 \;\land\; T(D) = 1$$ **Step 3 — State the Pig applicability conditions.** Pig is the correct tool **iff** any of the following hold: - The dataset is semi-structured or unstructured: $S(D) = 0$ - The volume exceeds the practical RDBMS threshold: $V(D) > 10^{12}$ - The workload is analytical (batch scans, not single-row lookups): $T(D) = 0$ $$\text{Use Pig} \iff S(D) = 0 \;\lor\; V(D) > 10^{12} \;\lor\; T(D) = 0$$ **Step 4 — Conversion logic.** If the predicate $\lnot[\text{Use RDBMS}]$ evaluates true, the system migrates the data from the relational store to HDFS via **Sqoop** (SQL-to-Hadoop) and processes it with Pig Latin. The output may then be reverse-loaded back into the RDBMS for downstream reporting. This **extract-transform-load (ETL)** inversion is the defining hybrid pattern of Big Data architectures. > [!TIP] > **Memory Aid:** Remember the **"3 S Rule"** — Pig handles **S**tructured, **S**emi-structured, and **S**calable (unstructured) data. RDBMS handles only the first S optimally. ## 3.2 Comparative Performance Model Let $N$ = number of worker nodes, $B$ = block size on HDFS (default $64$ MB or $128$ MB). The **theoretical scan time** for a Pig job over dataset volume $V$ is: $$T_{\text{pig}} = \frac{V}{N \cdot B \cdot f_{\text{disk}}}$$ where $f_{\text{disk}}$ is the per-disk read throughput in MB/s. For an RDBMS doing a full table scan with index-off: $$T_{\text{rdbms}} = \frac{V}{R_{\text{seq}}}$$ where $R_{\text{seq}}$ is the single-server sequential read bandwidth (typically capped at the storage controller bandwidth, e.g., $500$ MB/s on a high-end SAN). **Derivation of the crossover point $V^*$:** Set $T_{\text{pig}} \le T_{\text{rdbms}}$ and solve for $V$: $$\frac{V}{N \cdot B \cdot f_{\text{disk}}} \le \frac{V}{R_{\text{seq}}}$$ This simplifies to: $$R_{\text{seq}} \le N \cdot B \cdot f_{\text{disk}}$$ The **crossover volume** at which Pig becomes preferable is reached when the aggregate cluster bandwidth exceeds the single-server bandwidth: $$V^{*} = \text{(dataset size at which cluster parallelism saturates single-server capacity)}$$ Numerically, with $N = 20$ nodes, $B = 128$ MB, and $f_{\text{disk}} = 100$ MB/s: $$N \cdot B \cdot f_{\text{disk}} = 20 \times 128 \times 100 = 256{,}000 \text{ MB/s} \approx 250 \text{ GB/s}$$ Compared to a typical RDBMS server $R_{\text{seq}} \approx 0.5$ GB/s$, the cluster is roughly **$500\times$** faster. This is why, beyond a few hundred GB, **Pig-on-Hadoop outperforms RDBMS for batch scans** — an analysis KTU expects students to articulate. ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix (Tabular Form) | **#** | **Criterion** | **Pig** | **RDBMS** | **Winner for Big Data** | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Petabyte handling | ✅ Native | ❌ Sharding needed | Pig | | 2 | Schema flexibility | ✅ Late binding | ❌ Rigid DDL | Pig | | 3 | Semi-structured data | ✅ Native | ❌ JSON/XML hacks | Pig | | 4 | ACID transactions | ❌ None | ✅ Full | RDBMS | | 5 | Sub-second latency | ❌ Batch | ✅ Real-time | RDBMS | | 6 | Horizontal scaling | ✅ Linear | ❌ Vertical only | Pig | | 7 | Hardware cost | ✅ Commodity | ❌ Enterprise | Pig | | 8 | Mature tooling / ecosystem | ⚠️ Growing | ✅ Mature | RDBMS | | 9 | Indexing richness | ❌ Limited | ✅ B-tree, hash, etc. | RDBMS | | 10 | Procedural control | ✅ Pig Latin | ❌ SQL only | Pig | > [!WARNING] > **Examination Trap:** Question 7 of the matrix (Hardware cost) is *relative*. While Pig clusters are cheaper *per node*, total cost of ownership (TCO) including power, cooling, and ops staff may equalise at certain scales. KTU answers should remain precise. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Architecture Comparison: Pig vs RDBMS The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **architectural difference** between Pig-on-Hadoop and a traditional RDBMS stack. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph clusterA["Pig on Hadoop Ecosystem"] nodeA1["Raw Data Sources
Logs JSON XML CSV"] nodeA2["HDFS Storage Layer
Distributed File System"] nodeA3["Pig Latin Script
LOAD TRANSFORM DUMP"] nodeA4["Execution Engine
MapReduce Tez Spark"] nodeA5["Output HDFS or HBase"] nodeA1 --> nodeA2 --> nodeA3 --> nodeA4 --> nodeA5 end subgraph clusterB["Traditional RDBMS Stack"] nodeB1["Application Layer
OLTP Frontend"] nodeB2["SQL Query Engine
Optimizer Parser"] nodeB3["Transaction Manager
ACID Concurrency Control"] nodeB4["Storage Engine
B Tree Heap Files"] nodeB5["Disk Storage
Local SAN or SSD"] nodeB1 --> nodeB2 --> nodeB3 --> nodeB4 --> nodeB5 end ``` ## 4.2 Data-Flow Comparison: Schema-on-Read vs Schema-on-Write ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph soR["Pig Schema on Read"] r1["Raw Unstructured File
in HDFS"] --> r2["LOAD at Query Time
Pig Latin Defines Schema"] r2 --> r3["Transform and Analyze"] r3 --> r4["Output Result"] end subgraph soW["RDBMS Schema on Write"] w1["Define DDL
CREATE TABLE with Types"] --> w2["Enforce Schema
Reject Invalid Inserts"] w2 --> w3["Store Typed Rows"] w3 --> w4["SQL Query at Read Time"] end ``` ## 4.3 ETL vs ELT Paradigm Shift ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph etl["Traditional ETL RDBMS"] e1["Extract from Source"] --> e2["Transform Outside DB
Code or ETL Tool"] e2 --> e3["Load Clean Data into RDBMS"] end subgraph elt["Big Data ELT with Pig"] l1["Extract and Load Raw
into HDFS as Is"] --> l2["Transform In Place
Pig Latin Scripts"] l2 --> l3["Persist Transformed
Dataset in HDFS"] end ``` > [!TIP] > **Reading the diagrams:** The key visual insight is that in Pig, the *storage layer* and *processing layer* are **decoupled** (HDFS + Pig Latin), whereas in RDBMS they are **tightly integrated** into a single binary system. This decoupling is what enables independent horizontal scaling. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]** *Explain the key differences between schema-on-read and schema-on-write with respect to Pig and RDBMS.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | **Aspect** | **Schema-on-Read (Pig)** | **Schema-on-Write (RDBMS)** | |---|---|---| | When schema is applied | At query / load time | At table creation time | | Raw data state | Stored as-is, no transformation | Validated and rejected if non-conforming | | Flexibility | High — same file can be viewed with multiple schemas | Low — schema is rigid | | Overhead | Pay cost on every read | Pay cost on every write | *(1 mark for each valid point × 3 = 3 marks)* ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Pattern]** *List any three scenarios where Apache Pig is preferred over a traditional RDBMS.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. When the dataset is in the **petabyte range** and stored on HDFS, where RDBMS vertical scaling is uneconomical. *(1 mark)* 2. When the input data is **semi-structured or unstructured** (JSON, XML, logs, free text) that does not fit the rigid tabular schema of RDBMS. *(1 mark)* 3. When the workload is **analytical / batch-oriented** (ETL, clickstream, log mining) rather than transactional OLTP, and high latency is acceptable. *(1 mark)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 3 Pattern]** *(a)* Compare Apache Pig with traditional RDBMS across **any seven parameters** with suitable examples. *(7 marks)* *(b)* Discuss why Pig Latin is classified as a **data-flow language** and how it differs from declarative SQL, with a short Pig Latin code snippet illustrating LOAD → FILTER → STORE. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution — Part (a): Seven-Parameter Comparison (7 marks) The seven parameters are presented in the comparison table below. Each parameter is worth 1 mark. | **#** | **Parameter** | **Apache Pig** | **RDBMS** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Data Volume** | Petabytes via HDFS | GB to few TB | | 2 | **Schema** | Schema-on-read (flexible) | Schema-on-write (rigid) | | 3 | **Data Type** | Structured, semi, unstructured | Structured only | | 4 | **Latency** | High (batch, minutes–hours) | Low (ms) | | 5 | **ACID** | Not supported | Fully supported | | 6 | **Scaling** | Horizontal (commodity) | Vertical (enterprise) | | 7 | **Use Case** | ETL, log analytics, warehousing | OLTP, transactions | *(7 × 1 = 7 marks awarded with supporting one-line explanation per row.)* #### Model Solution — Part (b): Data-Flow vs Declarative (7 marks) **1. Data-Flow Nature of Pig Latin (3 marks):** Pig Latin is **procedural / data-flow** because: - Each statement **transforms one dataset into another** (e.g., `filtered = FILTER raw BY score > 50;`). - Execution follows the **sequence of statements**, similar to a pipeline. - The user **explicitly controls the order** of operations. **2. Declarative Nature of SQL (2 marks):** - The user states *what* result is desired, not *how* to compute it. - The query optimiser decides join order, index usage, etc. - Example: `SELECT * FROM students WHERE score > 50;` — the engine chooses the plan. **3. Illustrative Pig Latin Snippet (2 marks):** ``` -- Load the data raw = LOAD '/data/students.txt' USING PigStorage(',') AS (name:chararray, age:int, score:int); -- Filter filtered = FILTER raw BY score > 50; -- Group and aggregate grouped = GROUP filtered BY age; result = FOREACH grouped GENERATE group AS age, COUNT(filtered) AS count; -- Store STORE result INTO '/output/highscorers' USING PigStorage(','); ``` The above shows a complete **LOAD → FILTER → GROUP → FOREACH → STORE** pipeline characteristic of data-flow programming. --- ### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative) **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Module 3 Pattern]** *(a)* Explain with a neat block diagram the **architecture of Apache Pig** and describe the role of the **Parser, Optimizer, and Execution Engine**. *(7 marks)* *(b)* "RDBMS is not suitable for Big Data analytics." Critically evaluate this statement with reference to **volume, variety, and velocity** dimensions of Big Data. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution — Part (a): Pig Architecture (7 marks) **Architecture diagram (in text form for KTU answer book):** ``` +---------------------------------+ | Pig Latin Script (.pig) | +---------------------------------+ | v +---------------------------------+ | Parser | | (Lexical + Syntax Analysis) | +---------------------------------+ | v +---------------------------------+ | Optimizer | | (Logical + Physical Plan) | +---------------------------------+ | v +---------------------------------+ | Execution Engine | | (MapReduce / Tez / Spark) | +---------------------------------+ | v +---------------------------------+ | HDFS / HBase / Result | +---------------------------------+ ``` **Roles (5 marks for explanation + 2 marks for diagram):** - **Parser:** Performs lexical analysis, syntax checking, and produces a **logical plan** (DAG of logical operators). *(1.5 marks)* - **Optimizer:** Applies **projection pushdown, predicate pushdown, and combines operators** to minimise data shuffling. Produces the **physical plan**. *(1.5 marks)* - **Execution Engine:** Submits the physical plan to **Hadoop (MapReduce), Tez, or Spark** for distributed execution. Returns results to the user. *(2 marks)* #### Model Solution — Part (b): Critical Evaluation (7 marks) **Statement:** *RDBMS is not suitable for Big Data analytics.* **Volume dimension (2 marks):** - RDBMS scales vertically; petabyte-scale requires costly SMP hardware. - **Counterpoint:** Modern RDBMS (e.g., Oracle Exadata, SQL Server PDW, Snowflake) handle multi-TB warehouses. - **Verdict:** Pure RDBMS struggles at **PB scale** without architectural changes. **Variety dimension (2 marks):** - RDBMS requires a fixed schema; JSON, XML, logs do not fit cleanly. - **Counterpoint:** Native JSON columns in PostgreSQL/MySQL bridge this gap partially. - **Verdict:** RDBMS is poor for **variety** (unstructured data). **Velocity dimension (2 marks):** - RDBMS offers millisecond OLTP but cannot match stream-processing throughput of Kafka + Spark/Flink. - **Verdict:** RDBMS lags in **real-time velocity** scenarios. **Conclusion (1 mark):** RDBMS is **partially suitable** — it remains the best choice for transactional, structured, low-latency workloads, but for **end-to-end Big Data analytics**, it must be **augmented** (not replaced) by Hadoop/Pig/Spark ecosystems. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:** > 1. **Do NOT write** "Pig is a database" or "Pig stores data." Pig is a *processing platform*; storage is delegated to HDFS. *(-1 mark deduction per occurrence.)* > 2. **Avoid mixing up** "Schema-on-Read" direction — Pig reads with a schema; RDBMS writes with a schema. Confusing the two is the most common error. *(-2 marks in 14-mark questions.)* > 3. **Do not omit ACID** when listing RDBMS strengths — full credit requires mentioning the four properties explicitly. > 4. **For 14-mark questions**, ensure **at least seven** comparison parameters are tabulated; five or fewer yields partial credit (typically 5/7 marks for the comparison sub-part). > 5. **Avoid writing pseudo-code** for Pig Latin in non-coding sub-parts; only use snippets when explicitly asked. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Comparison of Pig with Databases** - ✅ **Pig is a data-flow processing platform, NOT a database.** Storage is delegated to HDFS/HBase/NoSQL stores. - ✅ **Pig uses Schema-on-Read**; RDBMS uses **Schema-on-Write**. This is the *single most important* distinction. - ✅ **Pig Latin is procedural** (data-flow); **SQL is declarative**. - ✅ **RDBMS is preferred for OLTP, ACID transactions, low-latency queries, structured data, GB–TB scale.** - ✅ **Pig is preferred for ETL, log analytics, semi-structured / unstructured data, PB scale, batch processing.** - ✅ **Pig does NOT support ACID properties**; RDBMS does (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability). - ✅ **Pig scales horizontally** on commodity hardware; **RDBMS scales vertically** on enterprise hardware. - ✅ **Pig architecture has three layers:** Parser → Optimizer → Execution Engine (over MapReduce/Tez/Spark). - ✅ **Decision crossover for volume:** Practically, beyond a few hundred GB, **Pig on Hadoop** outperforms single-server RDBMS by orders of magnitude (≈ 500× for 20-node cluster). - ✅ **Pig Latin statement form:** `LOAD → TRANSFORM (FILTER, GROUP, JOIN, FOREACH) → DUMP / STORE`. - ✅ **ETL (Extract-Transform-Load) pattern** is the canonical RDBMS workflow; **ELT (Extract-Load-Transform)** is the Big Data / Pig paradigm. - ✅ **Coexistence pattern:** RDBMS remains the *system of record*; Pig/Hadoop is the *system of analytics*. Sqoop bridges the two. - ✅ **KTU-favoured examples:** MySQL, Oracle, PostgreSQL (RDBMS); Pig on HDFS / HBase (Big Data). - ✅ **High-yield one-liner for viva:** *"Pig trades real-time latency and ACID guarantees for horizontal scalability, schema flexibility, and petabyte handling."* ---

topics.title: Grunt
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT746 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Grunt

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Grunt

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Grunt** is the **interactive command-line shell environment** of **Apache Pig**, the high-level data flow platform built on top of **Hadoop**. It provides a **Read-Eval-Print Loop (REPL)** interface that allows developers, data engineers, and analysts to interactively execute **Pig Latin statements** directly against **HDFS** (Hadoop Distributed File System) without needing to compile a full script or invoke the Hadoop MapReduce job tracker explicitly.

Grunt serves three primary functional roles inside the Hadoop ecosystem:

1. **Shell for HDFS File Operations** — Acts as an HDFS command explorer supporting commands such as `ls`, `cd`, `cat`, `mkdir`, `rm`, `copyFromLocal`, and `copyToLocal`.
2. **Interactive Pig Latin Interpreter** — Accepts Pig Latin statements line-by-line, validates them, builds the logical plan, and when prompted, executes the corresponding MapReduce job.
3. **Pig Utility Command Processor** — Executes built-in Pig commands like `dump`, `describe`, `explain`, `illustrate`, `store`, `run`, and `exec` that control script execution, debugging, and result persistence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Grunt is explicitly listed under Module 3 of PECNT746 — Big Data Analytics. Examiners frequently test knowledge of Grunt's command categories and their syntax.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are travelling to a foreign country where the locals speak only **Pig Latin** (a procedural data-flow language), but you only know English and a bit of HDFS gestures. Without a translator, every sentence must be pre-written, handed over, and translated offline.

**Grunt is your personal, on-the-spot translator sitting beside you at a café table:**

- You speak one sentence ("load my file") → Grunt immediately understands, verifies, and prepares it.
- You ask clarifying questions ("show me the structure") → Grunt responds instantly using `describe`.
- When you finally say "execute" → Grunt translates everything into MapReduce jobs and runs them on the Hadoop cluster.

The name **"Grunt"** itself comes from the pig-like grunting sound — fitting because it is the *voice* through which **Pig** speaks to the user.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Default Pig Storage Mode** | `PigStorage('\t')` | Tab-delimited text by default |
| **Parallelism Reduction Default** | `pig.exec.reducers.bytes.per.reducer` = **1 GB** | Each reducer processes ~1 GB |
| **Max Reduce Tasks Limit** | `pig.exec.reducers.max` = **999** | Hard cap set by Hadoop |
| **Default Join Strategy** | `replicated` (small) / `hash` (large) | Auto-selected by Pig |

> [!NOTE]
> Although these constants are not specific to Grunt, they govern how Grunt-executed Pig Latin statements behave when translated into MapReduce jobs in the background.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Position of Grunt in the Hadoop Stack

Grunt sits **above the Pig Latin parser and logical planner**, providing the I/O channel between the human operator and the Pig runtime. The exact layer ordering is:

$$
\text{User Input} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Grunt Shell (REPL)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Pig Latin Parser} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Logical Plan} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Physical Plan} \;\rightarrow\; \text{MapReduce / Tez / Spark Engine}
$$

When a user types a Pig Latin statement at the `grunt>` prompt, the following pipeline activates:

1. **Tokenization & Lexical Analysis** — Pig Latin keywords (`LOAD`, `FOREACH`, `GROUP`, etc.) are identified.
2. **Syntactic Parsing** — The statement is checked against the Pig Latin grammar.
3. **Semantic Validation** — Schema compatibility, type checking, and field aliasing are verified.
4. **Logical Plan Accumulation** — The parsed statement is appended to the in-memory logical plan DAG.
5. **Deferred Execution** — Unless a triggering command (`DUMP`, `STORE`, `RUN`, `EXEC`, `ILLUSTRATE`) is invoked, the plan is *not* executed.

## 2.2 Command Classification — The Three Pillars

Grunt commands fall into **three well-defined categories**, each with its own syntax, scope, and lifetime. Mastering this classification is the single most important step to scoring well on Grunt-related KTU questions.

### Pillar 1 — HDFS Shell Commands (File System Utilities)

These mirror standard Unix commands but operate on **HDFS paths** instead of the local filesystem. They are essential for inspecting, staging, and managing input/output data without leaving the Pig environment.

| Command | Syntax | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `ls` | `grunt> ls <path>` | List contents of an HDFS directory |
| `cd` | `grunt> cd <dir>` | Change working directory inside HDFS |
| `cat` | `grunt> cat <file>` | Display contents of an HDFS file |
| `mkdir` | `grunt> mkdir <dir>` | Create a new HDFS directory |
| `rm` | `grunt> rm <path>` | Remove HDFS file or directory (`-r` for recursive, `-f` for force) |
| `copyFromLocal` | `grunt> copyFromLocal <local> <hdfs>` | Upload local file to HDFS |
| `copyToLocal` | `grunt> copyToLocal <hdfs> <local>` | Download HDFS file to local filesystem |
| `mv` | `grunt> mv <src> <dst>` | Move/rename HDFS path |
| `pwd` | `grunt> pwd` | Print current working HDFS directory |
| `chmod` | `grunt> chmod <perm> <path>` | Change file permissions on HDFS |

### Pillar 2 — Pig Latin Statements (Data Flow Constructs)

These are the **actual data-processing commands** typed at the Grunt prompt. Each statement either defines a relation, manipulates data, or sets execution parameters.

| Statement Category | Examples | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Loading** | `LOAD`, `STREAM` | Read data into Pig from HDFS or external source |
| **Storage** | `STORE`, `DUMP` | Persist results or stream to terminal |
| **Relational** | `FILTER`, `FOREACH`, `GROUP`, `JOIN`, `ORDER`, `DISTINCT`, `UNION`, `SPLIT`, `LIMIT`, `SAMPLE` | Transform, aggregate, and reshape relations |
| **Diagnostic** | `DESCRIBE`, `EXPLAIN`, `ILLUSTRATE` | Inspect schema, plan, or sample execution |
| **Schema** | `DEFINE`, `REGISTER`, `SET` | Configure UDFs, JARs, and runtime parameters |

### Pillar 3 — Utility / Shell Commands

These are **meta-commands** that control the Grunt environment itself, execute operating-system calls, or manage the in-memory logical plan.

| Command | Syntax | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `help` | `grunt> help` | List all available Grunt commands |
| `quit` / `exit` | `grunt> quit` | Exit the Grunt shell |
| `history` | `grunt> history` | Display previously typed commands |
| `set` | `grunt> set <key> '<value>'` | Configure Pig runtime properties |
| `sh` | `grunt> sh <cmd>` | Spawn a sub-shell to run OS commands |
| `exec` | `grunt> exec <script.pig>` | Run a Pig script (clean environment) |
| `run` | `grunt> run <script.pig>` | Run a Pig script (current environment preserved) |
| `kill` | `grunt> kill <job_id>` | Terminate a running MapReduce job |
| `fs` | `grunt> fs <hdfs_cmd>` | Run an `hdfs dfs` command |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Distinction:** `exec` vs `run`
> - `exec` → script runs in a **fresh, isolated** environment (declared aliases vanish afterwards).
> - `run` → script runs in the **current** Grunt session (aliases defined inside the script remain accessible).

## 2.3 Logical Plan Accumulation — The Deferred Execution Model

A defining feature of Grunt is **lazy evaluation**. Pig Latin statements typed into Grunt are *not* executed immediately. Instead, each statement adds a node to a **logical plan** that is materialized only when a triggering command (`DUMP`, `STORE`, `ILLUSTRATE`) is invoked.

$$
\text{Logical Plan DAG} = \{ \text{LOAD} \rightarrow \text{FILTER} \rightarrow \text{GROUP} \rightarrow \text{FOREACH} \} \;\xrightarrow{\text{DUMP}}\; \text{MapReduce Execution}
$$

This deferral offers two engineering advantages:
1. **Optimization window** — The Pig optimizer can rewrite the entire DAG before any job starts.
2. **Interactive debugging** — A user can incrementally build, inspect, and verify each step using `DESCRIBE` and `EXPLAIN`.

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Syntax Cheat Sheet

| Construct | Exact Syntax |
|---|---|
| Launch Grunt (Local Mode) | `pig -x local` |
| Launch Grunt (MapReduce Mode) | `pig` |
| Load data | `A = LOAD '<path>' USING PigStorage(',') AS (id:int, name:chararray);` |
| Inspect schema | `DESCRIBE A;` |
| View logical + physical plan | `EXPLAIN A;` |
| View sample execution | `ILLUSTRATE A;` |
| Print to terminal | `DUMP A;` |
| Persist to HDFS | `STORE A INTO '<path>' USING PigStorage(',');` |
| List HDFS files | `ls /user/hadoop/data;` |
| Stage local file to HDFS | `copyFromLocal /home/user/input.txt /user/hadoop/data/;` |
| Execute script (clean env) | `exec /home/user/analysis.pig;` |
| Execute script (shared env) | `run /home/user/analysis.pig;` |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of Grunt in Engineering Pipelines

Grunt is the **operational console** used by data engineers worldwide for:

- **Ad-hoc ETL prototyping** — Quickly loading, transforming, and verifying data slices before production deployment.
- **Pipeline debugging** — `EXPLAIN` and `ILLUSTRATE` reveal MapReduce stage details critical for tuning.
- **Data exploration** — `DUMP` of small samples lets analysts eyeball data distributions before scaling.
- **Cluster administration** — `sh` and `fs` allow HDFS and OS commands without leaving Pig.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — End-to-End Grunt Session (Analytical Walkthrough)

The following session demonstrates every pillar of Grunt commands working in concert on a real HDFS-based dataset. Each line is annotated with the conceptual operation it performs.

**Scenario:** A retail analytics team wants to compute total sales per product category from a CSV file `/data/sales.csv` (columns: `txn_id, product, category, amount, date`).

### Step 1 — Launch Grunt in MapReduce Mode

```
$ pig
```

**Explanation:** This opens the Grunt shell connected to the Hadoop cluster, ready to execute jobs that compile down to MapReduce.

### Step 2 — Verify HDFS Working Directory

```
grunt> pwd
/user/hadoop
```

**Explanation:** Confirms the current HDFS location. Mirrors the Unix `pwd` utility.

### Step 3 — Inspect Input Directory

```
grunt> ls /data
hdfs://namenode:9000/data/sales.csv
```

**Explanation:** Lists the contents of the `/data` directory to confirm the input file exists.

### Step 4 — Stage Local Configuration File

```
grunt> copyFromLocal /home/user/categories.txt /data/categories.txt
```

**Explanation:** Uploads a local taxonomy file into HDFS so that subsequent Pig statements can JOIN against it.

### Step 5 — Load the Sales Dataset with Explicit Schema

```
grunt> sales = LOAD '/data/sales.csv'
              USING PigStorage(',')
              AS (txn_id:int, product:chararray, category:chararray,
                  amount:double, date:chararray);
```

**Explanation:** Defines the relation `sales`. The schema `(txn_id:int, product:chararray, category:chararray, amount:double, date:chararray)` is registered but **not yet executed**.

### Step 6 — Verify the Schema

```
grunt> describe sales
sales: {txn_id: int, product: chararray, category: chararray,
        amount: double, date: chararray}
```

**Explanation:** `DESCRIBE` reads the in-memory logical plan and prints the field-by-field schema. Critical for debugging column typos before committing to a full job.

### Step 7 — View the Physical Execution Plan

```
grunt> explain sales
```

**Explanation:** `EXPLAIN` shows both the **logical plan** (operator tree) and the **physical plan** (MapReduce stages, including the auto-generated split conditions, partitioners, and combiners).

### Step 8 — Apply a Filter (No Execution Yet)

```
grunt> high_value = FILTER sales BY amount > 1000.0;
```

**Explanation:** Adds a new `high_value` relation to the logical plan DAG. Still **no MapReduce job is submitted** because no triggering command has been issued.

### Step 9 — Group by Category

```
grunt> grouped = GROUP high_value BY category;
```

**Explanation:** Pig translates this into a MapReduce stage where the map phase emits `(category, record)` key-value pairs, and the reduce phase aggregates records sharing the same category key.

### Step 10 — Aggregate Sales per Category

```
grunt> totals = FOREACH grouped GENERATE
                  group AS category,
                  SUM(high_value.amount) AS total_sales;
```

**Explanation:** For each group, the `SUM()` evaluation function walks through the bag of `amount` values and returns the arithmetic sum. Algebraically:

$$
\text{total\_sales}(c) = \sum_{r \in \text{group}(c)} r.\text{amount}
$$

### Step 11 — Order Results Descending

```
grunt> ranked = ORDER totals BY total_sales DESC;
```

**Explanation:** Pig issues a single reduce-phase sort using the underlying MapReduce shuffle ordering.

### Step 12 — Trigger Materialization via DUMP

```
grunt> dump ranked
```

**Explanation:** This is the **first execution trigger**. Pig compiles the accumulated logical plan into one or more MapReduce jobs, runs them on the cluster, and prints the final output rows to the terminal.

### Step 13 — Persist Results for Downstream Consumers

```
grunt> store ranked into '/data/output/category_totals'
              USING PigStorage(',');
```

**Explanation:** Writes the result relation to `/data/output/category_totals/` as comma-delimited `part-r-00000` files. This is the **production-friendly** termination of a Pig pipeline.

### Step 14 — Inspect the Output Directory

```
grunt> ls /data/output/category_totals
part-r-00000  _SUCCESS
```

**Explanation:** Confirms that the reducer produced the standard Hadoop output layout. `_SUCCESS` is the zero-byte flag file Hadoop writes upon successful completion.

### Step 15 — Preview Output

```
grunt> cat /data/output/category_totals/part-r-00000
Electronics,452300.50
Apparel,238940.00
Grocery,124500.75
```

**Explanation:** The category-wise sales totals are now persisted and readable.

### Step 16 — View Command History

```
grunt> history
```

**Explanation:** Prints every command typed in the session, enabling reproducibility and audit trails.

### Step 17 — Exit Grunt

```
grunt> quit
```

**Explanation:** Gracefully terminates the Grunt session and frees cluster resources.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Critical KTU Distinctions

| Dimension | `DUMP` | `STORE` | `EXPLAIN` | `ILLUSTRATE` |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Triggers Execution?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (plan-only) | ✅ Yes (sample run) |
| **Output Destination** | Terminal (stdout) | HDFS directory | Terminal | Terminal |
| **Side Effect on HDFS** | None | Writes `part-*` files | None | Writes sample to temp dir |
| **Use Case** | Quick verification | Production persistence | Performance debugging | Schema & data preview |
| **KTU Question Frequency** | High | High | Medium | Medium |

| Dimension | `exec` | `run` |
|---|---|---|
| **Environment Scope** | Fresh, isolated | Current session shared |
| **Aliases Survive?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Recommended For** | Production batch jobs | Modular interactive development |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Grunt's Position in the Apache Pig Execution Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Types Pig Latin at grunt Prompt] --> B[Grunt REPL Shell]
    B --> C[Lexer and Parser]
    C --> D[Semantic Validator and Type Checker]
    D --> E[Logical Plan DAG Builder]
    E --> F{Trigger Command Issued?}
    F -->|No| G[Wait for More Statements]
    G --> B
    F -->|Yes DUMP or STORE or ILLUSTRATE| H[Logical Optimizer]
    H --> I[Physical Plan Generator]
    I --> J[MapReduce Job Launcher]
    J --> K[Hadoop Cluster Execution]
    K --> L[Output to Terminal or HDFS]
```

## 4.2 Classification of Grunt Commands

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Grunt Command Categories]
    R --> S1[HDFS Shell Commands]
    R --> S2[Pig Latin Statements]
    R --> S3[Utility Commands]

    S1 --> T1[ls cd cat mkdir rm]
    S1 --> T2[copyFromLocal copyToLocal mv pwd chmod]

    S2 --> U1[LOAD and STORE and DUMP]
    S2 --> U2[FILTER GROUP JOIN FOREACH ORDER]
    S2 --> U3[DESCRIBE EXPLAIN ILLUSTRATE]

    S3 --> V1[help quit history]
    S3 --> V2[set sh kill]
    S3 --> V3[exec run]
```

## 4.3 Deferred Execution Lifecycle of a Grunt Session

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[grunt prompt] --> Q1[Type LOAD Statement]
    Q1 --> Q2[Logical Plan Accumulates Relation A]
    P1 --> R1[Type FILTER Statement]
    R1 --> R2[Logical Plan Accumulates Relation B referencing A]
    P1 --> S1[Type GROUP and FOREACH]
    S1 --> S2[Logical Plan Accumulates Relations C and D]
    P1 --> T1[Type DUMP]
    T1 --> U1[Pig Compiles Entire DAG into MapReduce]
    U1 --> V1[Job Submits to Hadoop]
    V1 --> W1[Output Streams to Terminal]
```

## 4.4 HDFS Data Flow Initiated from Grunt

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Local File system] -->|copyFromLocal| B[HDFS Cluster]
    B -->|LOAD via PigStorage| C[Pig Logical Plan in Grunt]
    C -->|DUMP| D[Standard Output Terminal]
    C -->|STORE| E[HDFS Output Directory part-r files]
    E -->|copyToLocal| A
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is Grunt in Apache Pig? List any four HDFS shell commands supported by Grunt.**

**Model Answer:**

Grunt is the interactive command-line shell of Apache Pig. It provides a Read-Eval-Print Loop (REPL) interface to execute Pig Latin statements interactively against HDFS without requiring a full script compilation. It also supports HDFS file operations, Pig Latin statements, and utility commands such as shell execution and job control.

Four HDFS shell commands supported by Grunt are:

1. **`ls`** — Lists the contents of an HDFS directory.
2. **`cat`** — Displays the contents of an HDFS file on the terminal.
3. **`copyFromLocal`** — Uploads a file from the local filesystem to HDFS.
4. **`mkdir`** — Creates a new HDFS directory.

*(Any four of: `ls`, `cd`, `cat`, `mkdir`, `rm`, `mv`, `pwd`, `copyFromLocal`, `copyToLocal`, `chmod`)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between `DUMP` and `STORE` statements in Pig Latin.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `DUMP` | `STORE` |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Prints relation contents to the terminal (stdout) | Persists relation contents to HDFS as files |
| **Output Location** | Standard output | HDFS directory (`part-r-00000` files) |
| **Use Case** | Quick ad-hoc verification, debugging | Production pipelines, downstream consumers |
| **Side Effect** | None on HDFS | Writes `part-*` files and `_SUCCESS` flag |

Both commands trigger actual MapReduce execution, but `DUMP` is for human inspection while `STORE` is for system-to-system data handoff.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the architecture of Apache Pig and the role of Grunt within it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, demonstrate how `LOAD`, `FILTER`, `GROUP`, `FOREACH`, and `STORE` statements are executed interactively through the Grunt shell. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Apache Pig Architecture and the Role of Grunt**

Apache Pig consists of the following layered components:

1. **Pig Latin Language** — The high-level procedural language used to express data flows.
2. **Grunt Shell** — The interactive REPL that accepts Pig Latin statements and HDFS commands.
3. **Parser** — Performs lexical and syntactic analysis of statements typed at the Grunt prompt.
4. **Optimizer** — Performs logical optimizations such as projection pushdown and predicate pushdown on the accumulated DAG.
5. **Compiler** — Translates the optimized logical plan into a physical MapReduce / Tez / Spark execution plan.
6. **Execution Engine** — Submits the physical plan to the Hadoop cluster for distributed execution.

**Role of Grunt:**
Grunt acts as the **interactive operator console**. It receives user input, forwards it to the parser, accumulates the logical plan in memory, and only triggers execution when commands like `DUMP`, `STORE`, or `ILLUSTRATE` are invoked. Grunt also exposes HDFS file-management commands (`ls`, `cat`, `copyFromLocal`, etc.) and utility commands (`sh`, `set`, `kill`, `history`), making it a one-stop shell for data engineering on Hadoop.

**(b) Interactive Grunt Session Example**

Given a file `/data/students.txt` with columns `(name:chararray, age:int, grade:chararray, marks:int)`, the following Grunt session computes the average marks per grade:

```
grunt> pwd
/user/hadoop

grunt> ls /data
hdfs://namenode:9000/data/students.txt

grunt> students = LOAD '/data/students.txt'
                USING PigStorage(',')
                AS (name:chararray, age:int,
                    grade:chararray, marks:int);

grunt> describe students
students: {name: chararray, age: int, grade: chararray, marks: int}

grunt> passed = FILTER students BY marks >= 50;

grunt> grouped = GROUP passed BY grade;

grunt> avg_marks = FOREACH grouped GENERATE
                      group AS grade,
                      AVG(passed.marks) AS average;

grunt> ordered = ORDER avg_marks BY average DESC;

grunt> store ordered into '/data/output/grade_averages'
              USING PigStorage(',');
```

**Explanation of each step:**

- `LOAD` registers the relation `students` with schema, but no I/O occurs.
- `FILTER` adds a logical node that prunes rows where `marks < 50`.
- `GROUP` introduces a shuffle-and-reduce boundary in the eventual MapReduce plan.
- `FOREACH GENERATE AVG(...)` computes the per-group mean.
- `ORDER` performs a final reduce-side total sort.
- `STORE` triggers the physical execution and persists the result as `part-r-00000` files in `/data/output/grade_averages/`.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain any five HDFS shell commands supported by the Grunt shell with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With an example, illustrate the difference between `exec` and `run` commands in Grunt. Also explain the deferred execution model of Pig. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Five HDFS Shell Commands in Grunt**

1. **`ls`** — Lists files and directories.
   ```
   grunt> ls /user/hadoop/data
   hdfs://namenode:9000/user/hadoop/data/sales.csv
   ```

2. **`mkdir`** — Creates a new HDFS directory.
   ```
   grunt> mkdir /user/hadoop/warehouse
   ```

3. **`copyFromLocal`** — Uploads a file from local disk to HDFS.
   ```
   grunt> copyFromLocal /home/user/input.csv /user/hadoop/data/input.csv
   ```

4. **`cat`** — Displays the contents of an HDFS file.
   ```
   grunt> cat /user/hadoop/data/input.csv
   ```

5. **`rm`** — Removes an HDFS file or directory.
   ```
   grunt> rm -r /user/hadoop/old_data
   ```

**(b) `exec` vs `run` and Deferred Execution**

**Example demonstrating the difference:**

Suppose `module1.pig` defines:

```
A = LOAD '/data/source1.txt' AS (x:int, y:int);
B = FILTER A BY x > 10;
STORE B INTO '/data/output1';
```

**Executing via `exec`:**

```
grunt> exec /scripts/module1.pig;
```

After execution, the aliases `A` and `B` are **not available** in the current Grunt session. The script runs in an isolated environment, suitable for production batch jobs.

**Executing via `run`:**

```
grunt> run /scripts/module1.pig;
```

After execution, `A` and `B` **remain accessible** in the current Grunt session. This enables interactive chaining:

```
grunt> C = FOREACH B GENERATE y * 2;
grunt> DUMP C;
```

**Deferred Execution Model:**

Pig Latin statements typed into Grunt are *not* executed immediately. Instead, each statement appends a node to the **logical plan DAG** held in memory. Execution is deferred until a *triggering* command — `DUMP`, `STORE`, `ILLUSTRATE`, or `RUN`/`EXEC` — is invoked. This design enables the Pig optimizer to perform global rewrites (predicate pushdown, projection pushdown, map-side join selection) before any MapReduce job is launched, reducing both job count and network I/O.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing `exec` with `run`** — Students frequently lose 2–3 marks by stating the opposite (that `exec` shares the environment and `run` isolates it). The correct rule is: **`exec` = clean environment, `run` = shared environment.**
> 2. **Forgetting that `DESCRIBE` and `EXPLAIN` do NOT trigger execution** — These are diagnostic commands; only `DUMP`, `STORE`, and `ILLUSTRATE` force MapReduce job submission.
> 3. **Mixing up HDFS commands with Pig commands** — `ls`, `cat`, `mkdir` are **HDFS shell commands**, not Pig Latin statements. Examiners award marks for clear classification.
> 4. **Omitting the launch syntax** — Always mention `pig -x local` (local mode) vs `pig` (MapReduce mode) when writing Grunt-based answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Grunt = Apache Pig's interactive REPL shell** providing a Read-Eval-Print Loop for Pig Latin.
- ✅ Grunt commands are classified into **three categories**: HDFS shell commands, Pig Latin statements, and utility commands.
- ✅ **Deferred (lazy) execution** is a hallmark of Grunt — logical plans accumulate without running until `DUMP`, `STORE`, `ILLUSTRATE`, `RUN`, or `EXEC` is invoked.
- ✅ **`DESCRIBE`** prints schema; **`EXPLAIN`** prints logical + physical plans; **`ILLUSTRATE`** shows sample execution with mock data.
- ✅ **`DUMP`** outputs to terminal (stdout); **`STORE`** writes to HDFS as `part-r-00000` files.
- ✅ **`exec`** runs a script in a fresh environment (aliases vanish); **`run`** runs in the current shared environment (aliases persist).
- ✅ HDFS shell commands: `ls`, `cd`, `cat`, `mkdir`, `rm`, `mv`, `pwd`, `copyFromLocal`, `copyToLocal`, `chmod`.
- ✅ Utility commands: `help`, `quit`, `history`, `set`, `sh`, `kill`, `fs`.
- ✅ Launch syntax: `pig -x local` (local) vs `pig` (MapReduce / cluster mode).
- ✅ Grunt sits between the user and the Pig parser, making it the **primary debugging and prototyping console** for data engineers working with Hadoop.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pig Latin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT746 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pig Latin

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Apache Pig Latin** is a high-level, data-flow scripting language designed to analyze and transform large data sets in parallel within the **Hadoop ecosystem**. It is one of the core sub-projects of the Apache Software Foundation and is tightly integrated with **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** and the **MapReduce** execution framework.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Course Code: PECNT746), Pig Latin is the procedural language layer sitting on top of the **Apache Pig** platform. It allows developers/engineers to write **data transformation pipelines** without needing to write low-level Java MapReduce code.

The architecture consists of:
- **Pig Latin Script** → The user's source code.
- **Pig Engine (Parser, Optimizer, Compiler)** → Translates the script into physical execution plans.
- **Execution Backends** → Local Mode (single JVM) or **MapReduce Mode (Hadoop cluster)** or **Spark/Tez Mode**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology (KTU Board-Expected Definitions):**
> - **Atom:** A simple atomic data value (string, int, float, long, double, chararray, bytearray).
> - **Tuple:** An ordered set of fields (similar to a row in a table).
> - **Bag:** An unordered collection of tuples (similar to a table).
> - **Map:** A set of key-value pairs.
> - **Relation:** A bag of tuples (the primary data structure manipulated in Pig).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Factory Conveyor Belt" Model

Imagine you have a massive pile of mixed raw materials (Big Data) in a warehouse (HDFS). Writing **MapReduce** is like manually operating every machine on the factory floor using Java code — powerful, but extremely tedious.

**Pig Latin** acts like a **Supervisor's Instruction Sheet** 📋. You write simple English-like commands:
1. *LOAD* the raw materials from the warehouse.
2. *FILTER* out the broken pieces.
3. *GROUP* them by color.
4. *FOREACH* box, COUNT them and STORE back.

The Pig Engine reads your instruction sheet and automatically operates all the factory machines (compiles it to MapReduce jobs) in parallel. The data **flows** through a sequence of operators — hence, Pig is fundamentally a **data-flow language** rather than a query language like SQL.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pig Latin vs SQL:** SQL is **declarative** (you state *what* you want). Pig Latin is **procedural/data-flow** (you state *how* to get it, step-by-step). This makes Pig Latin better suited for ETL pipelines and unstructured/semi-structured data.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Anatomy of a Pig Latin Script

A Pig Latin program is executed in a sequence of logical steps. The high-yield structure is as follows:

### Step 1: Data Loading
The first step in any Pig Latin script is bringing data from **HDFS** (or local FS) into the Pig environment. The `LOAD` operator is used.

```pig
loaded_data = LOAD 'hdfs://path/to/input.txt' USING PigStorage(',') 
              AS (id:int, name:chararray, salary:float);
```

### Step 2: Data Transformation (Operators)
Pig Latin provides a rich set of operators (akin to verbs in English). The major categories are:

**A. Diagnostic Operators**
- `DUMP` — Prints to console (debugging).
- `DESCRIBE` — Shows schema of a relation.
- `EXPLAIN` — Shows logical/physical execution plan.
- `ILLUSTRATE` — Shows sample execution.

**B. Filtering Operators**
- `FILTER` — Removes unwanted tuples based on a condition.
- `DISTINCT` — Removes duplicates.
- `SAMPLE` — Random sampling.
- `LIMIT` — Restricts number of tuples.

**C. Grouping & Joining Operators**
- `GROUP` — Groups tuples by a key.
- `COGROUP` — Groups multiple relations together.
- `JOIN` (Inner, Outer Left, Right, Full) — Combines relations.
- `CROSS` — Cartesian product.

**D. Transformation Operators**
- `FOREACH ... GENERATE` — Applies expressions to each tuple.
- `ORDER BY` — Sorts tuples.
- `MAPREDUCE` — Embeds custom MR jobs.

**E. Input/Output & Store Operators**
- `LOAD` / `STORE` — Read/Write data.
- `REGISTER` — Registers a JAR (for UDFs).

### Step 3: Data Storing
The final transformed relation is stored back to HDFS using `STORE`.

```pig
STORE filtered_data INTO 'hdfs://output/path' USING PigStorage(',');
```

## 2.2 Pig Latin Data Types (Schema & Types)

| Data Type | Category | Example | Description |
|-----------|----------|---------|-------------|
| `int` | Atom | `42` | 32-bit signed integer |
| `long` | Atom | `1000000L` | 64-bit signed integer |
| `float` | Atom | `3.14f` | 32-bit floating point |
| `double` | Atom | `3.14159` | 64-bit floating point |
| `chararray` | Atom | `'hello'` | UTF-8 string |
| `bytearray` | Atom | (binary blob) | Uninterpreted bytes |
| `Tuple` | Composite | `(1, 'A', 2.5)` | Ordered, fixed-length fields |
| `Bag` | Composite | `{(1,'A'), (2,'B')}` | Unordered collection of tuples |
| `Map` | Composite | `['name'#'John', 'age'#30]` | Key-value pair collection |

> [!NOTE]
> A **`Bag`** in Pig can contain **duplicate tuples** and is the primary data structure. A relation is *defined* as a bag. Two important bag subtypes: `Inner Bag` (a bag within a tuple field) and `Outer Bag` (a relation itself).

## 2.3 Schema Definition Mechanics

Schemas can be **explicit** (declared by the user) or **implicit** (inferred at runtime). When schemas are not declared, fields are accessed by **positional notation** (`$0`, `$1`, etc.) — a frequent cause of KTU mark loss if positional mapping is wrong.

## 2.4 Execution Modes

| Mode | Use Case | Cluster Size |
|------|----------|--------------|
| **Local Mode** | Testing / Development on a single machine | Single JVM, local FS |
| **MapReduce Mode** | Production deployment on Hadoop | Full HDFS + YARN cluster |
| **Spark / Tez Mode** | Low-latency execution | Engine-specific |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Pig Latin is extensively used in:
- **ETL Pipelines** at companies like Yahoo, Twitter, LinkedIn.
- **Log Analytics** for web clickstreams (semi-structured logs).
- **Ad-hoc Data Analysis** for data scientists who don't want to write Java MR jobs.
- **Pre-processing** input for **Machine Learning** pipelines (e.g., Mahout, Spark MLlib).

## 2.6 KTU Formula / Operator Cheat Sheet

| Operation | Pig Latin Syntax | SQL Equivalent | Returns |
|-----------|------------------|----------------|---------|
| Load | `LOAD 'path' USING PigStorage(',') AS (...)` | `FROM ...` | Relation |
| Filter | `FILTER X BY condition;` | `WHERE` | Relation |
| Project | `X = FOREACH Y GENERATE $0, $1;` | `SELECT` | Relation |
| Group | `X = GROUP Y BY key;` | `GROUP BY` | Relation (grouped) |
| Join | `X = JOIN A BY id, B BY id;` | `JOIN ... ON` | Relation |
| Order | `X = ORDER Y BY col ASC;` | `ORDER BY` | Relation |
| Distinct | `X = DISTINCT Y;` | `DISTINCT` | Relation |
| Count | `X = FOREACH (GROUP Y ALL) GENERATE COUNT(Y);` | `COUNT(*)` | Bag |
| Store | `STORE X INTO 'path' USING PigStorage(',');` | `INSERT INTO` | (writes to disk) |
| Dump | `DUMP X;` | `PRINT` | (console output) |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Word Count in Pig Latin (KTU Standard)

This is a **guaranteed KTU 2024 question pattern** for Module 3. We will derive the entire script step-by-step.

### Problem Statement
Given an input text file `input.txt` containing lines of text, count the frequency of each word. Output format: `(word, count)`.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — LOAD the data**
The input file has no schema (raw lines of text). We load it as a single field (`chararray`) per line, accessed via `$0`.

```pig
lines = LOAD 'hdfs:///user/ktu/input.txt' AS (line:chararray);
```

*Logic:* The `AS (line:chararray)` clause assigns a schema. Without it, we'd have to use `$0` blindly.

---

**Step 2 — TOKENIZE the lines into words**
The `TOKENIZE` function (built-in UDF) splits each line into a bag of words.

```pig
words = FOREACH lines GENERATE FLATTEN(TOKENIZE(line)) AS word:chararray;
```

*Logic:*
- `TOKENIZE(line)` returns a **bag**: `{('Hello'), ('World')}`.
- `FLATTEN` unwraps the bag so each word becomes a separate tuple.
- The schema is explicitly `word:chararray`.

---

**Step 3 — GROUP the words by word key**

```pig
grouped = GROUP words BY word;
```

*Logic:* This produces a relation with schema `(group: chararray, words: bag { (word: chararray) })`. The inner bag `words` contains all tuples that share the same key.

---

**Step 4 — COUNT occurrences in each group**

```pig
wordcount = FOREACH grouped GENERATE group AS word, COUNT(words) AS count;
```

*Logic:*
- The first field of any grouped relation is named `group` automatically.
- `COUNT(words)` counts the number of tuples in the inner bag.
- We rename `group` back to `word` for cleaner output.

---

**Step 5 — ORDER the results (Optional, common in KTU)**

```pig
ordered = ORDER wordcount BY count DESC;
```

---

**Step 6 — STORE the output**

```pig
STORE ordered INTO 'hdfs:///user/ktu/output/' USING PigStorage('\t');
```

---

### Complete Final Script

```pig
-- 1. Load
lines = LOAD 'hdfs:///user/ktu/input.txt' AS (line:chararray);

-- 2. Tokenize and Flatten
words = FOREACH lines GENERATE FLATTEN(TOKENIZE(line)) AS word:chararray;

-- 3. Group by word
grouped = GROUP words BY word;

-- 4. Count
wordcount = FOREACH grouped GENERATE 
            group AS word, 
            COUNT(words) AS count;

-- 5. Order (descending)
ordered = ORDER wordcount BY count DESC;

-- 6. Store
STORE ordered INTO 'hdfs:///user/ktu/output/' USING PigStorage('\t');
```

### Mathematical Expression of the Operation

The transformation can be expressed formally as:

$$
\text{Output}(w) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}\left[w_i = w\right], \quad \forall w \in \text{UniqueWords}
$$

Where:
- $n$ = total number of word tokens in the input.
- $\mathbb{1}[\cdot]$ is the indicator function (1 if condition is true, 0 otherwise).
- $w$ ranges over the set of unique words extracted via the `DISTINCT` semantic of `GROUP BY`.

$$
\text{Relation}_{\text{grouped}} = \left\{ (w, \{(w, 1)_1, (w, 1)_2, \dots, (w, 1)_k\}) \;\vert\; w \in \Sigma^* \right\}
$$

$$
\text{Relation}_{\text{final}} = \left\{ (w, k) \;\vert\; k = \left| \text{inner\_bag}_w \right| \right\}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Operator Selection Guide

| If you want to... | Use this operator | Common KTU Trap |
|-------------------|------------------|-----------------|
| Remove rows based on condition | `FILTER` | Using `WHERE` (SQL syntax — invalid) |
| Apply a function to each row | `FOREACH ... GENERATE` | Forgetting `GENERATE` keyword |
| Combine two relations by key | `JOIN` | Confusing inner/outer semantics |
| Count in grouped data | `COUNT()` | Using it on an ungrouped relation |
| Select top N | `LIMIT N` after `ORDER BY` | Order is not preserved post-`LIMIT` without explicit order |
| Access field by name | `field_name` | Using `$0` when schema is declared |
| Handle NULL | `IsEmpty()` | Treating NULL as empty string |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pig Latin Execution Pipeline (Block-Level Architecture)

This diagram maps the **data flow** from raw script to executed job — a frequent KTU diagram question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pig Latin Script .pig file] --> B[Grunt Shell / Script Execution]
    B --> C[Parser]
    C --> D[Logical Plan DAG]
    D --> E[Optimizer]
    E --> F[Physical Plan]
    F --> G{Execution Mode}
    G -->|Local| H[Local JVM]
    G -->|MapReduce| I[Hadoop YARN Cluster]
    G -->|Spark/Tez| J[Spark/Tez Engine]
    H --> K[Result Output HDFS / Console]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[STORE or DUMP]
```

## 4.2 Data Flow Through Pig Latin Operators (Word Count Example)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input File HDFS] -->|LOAD| B[lines Relation]
    B -->|FOREACH TOKENIZE FLATTEN| C[words Relation]
    C -->|GROUP BY word| D[grouped Relation]
    D -->|FOREACH COUNT| E[wordcount Relation]
    E -->|ORDER BY count DESC| F[ordered Relation]
    F -->|STORE| G[Output HDFS]
    D -.->|DESCRIBE| H[Schema View]
    E -.->|DUMP| I[Console Debug]
```

## 4.3 Pig Latin Data Type Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Pig Latin Data Model] --> A1[Atom]
    P --> A2[Composite Types]
    A1 --> I1[int]
    A1 --> I2[long]
    A1 --> I3[float]
    A1 --> I4[double]
    A1 --> I5[chararray]
    A1 --> I6[bytearray]
    A2 --> C1[Tuple]
    A2 --> C2[Bag]
    A2 --> C3[Map]
    C2 --> C2a[Outer Bag = Relation]
    C2 --> C2b[Inner Bag = field within tuple]
```

## 4.4 Relation / Tuple / Bag Visual Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Relation wordcount] --> T1[Row 1: hello, 3]
    R --> T2[Row 2: world, 2]
    R --> T3[Row 3: pig, 1]
    T1 --> F1a[Field 0: hello chararray]
    T1 --> F1b[Field 1: count int]
    T2 --> F2a[Field 0: world]
    T2 --> F2b[Field 1: 2]
    T3 --> F3a[Field 0: pig]
    T3 --> F3b[Field 1: 1]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Summary:** The Pig Engine ingests a `.pig` script → Parses it into a logical DAG → Optimizes → Generates a physical plan → Dispatches to the chosen execution backend. The data flows **left-to-right** through operators in the script, with each operator producing a **new relation** (Pig Latin is **immutable** — relations are never modified in place).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**

### Q1. Define Pig Latin. List any four built-in data types supported by Pig Latin.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Pig Latin is a high-level, procedural data-flow language used in Apache Pig for analyzing large data sets in a parallel fashion on the Hadoop platform. It allows developers to write data transformation scripts without using low-level Java MapReduce code.

**Four built-in data types:**
1. **`int`** — 32-bit signed integer.
2. **`chararray`** — String (UTF-8 character array).
3. **`Tuple`** — An ordered set of fields.
4. **`Bag`** — An unordered collection of tuples.
   *(Alternative: `Map`, `float`, `double`, `long`, `bytearray`)*

---

### Q2. Differentiate between `FILTER` and `DISTINCT` operators in Pig Latin.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `FILTER` | `DISTINCT` |
|--------|----------|------------|
| Purpose | Removes tuples based on a Boolean condition | Removes **duplicate** tuples |
| Condition | Requires a predicate (`BY condition`) | No condition needed |
| Use case | Row-level filtering on values | Deduplication |
| Example | `FILTER X BY salary > 50000;` | `X = DISTINCT Y;` |
| Output | Subset of original relation | Unique tuples only |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern — Module Internal Choice]**

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of Apache Pig with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **Parser, Optimizer, and Compiler** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete Pig Latin script to perform the following on a sales dataset (`sales.txt`) with fields `(product:chararray, region:chararray, amount:float)`:
   - Load the data.
   - Group sales by `region`.
   - Calculate the total sales amount per region.
   - Filter regions with total sales > 1,00,000.
   - Store the result. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Apache Pig Architecture (7 marks)

The Apache Pig framework has a layered architecture:

**1. Pig Latin Script (Top Layer):** The user writes a `.pig` script. It is a procedural data-flow language with operators like `LOAD`, `FILTER`, `GROUP`, `STORE`.

**2. Parser:** The parser parses the Pig Latin script and performs syntactic and semantic validation. It checks for:
- Correct operator usage.
- Valid data types.
- Schema consistency.
Output: A **Logical Plan** (a DAG of logical operators).

**3. Optimizer (Logical Optimizer):** Applies logical optimizations like:
- **Projection pushdown** (only required fields are read).
- **Predicate pushdown** (filters pushed closer to LOAD).
- **Map-side join optimization** when possible.

**4. Compiler:** Converts the optimized logical plan into a **Physical Plan** consisting of a series of **MapReduce jobs** (or Spark/Tez tasks).

**5. Execution Engine:** Submits the physical plan to the chosen backend:
- **Local Mode** (single JVM for testing).
- **MapReduce Mode** (Hadoop YARN cluster for production).

```
Script → Parser → Logical Plan → Optimizer → Physical Plan → Execution Engine
```

---

#### Part (b) — Pig Latin Script (7 marks)

```pig
-- Step 1: Load the sales dataset
sales = LOAD 'hdfs:///user/ktu/sales.txt' 
        USING PigStorage(',') 
        AS (product:chararray, region:chararray, amount:float);

-- Step 2: Group by region
grouped_sales = GROUP sales BY region;

-- Step 3: Calculate total sales per region
total_sales = FOREACH grouped_sales GENERATE 
              group AS region, 
              SUM(sales.amount) AS total_amount;

-- Step 4: Filter regions with total > 100000
high_sales = FILTER total_sales BY total_amount > 100000;

-- Step 5: Store the result
STORE high_sales INTO 'hdfs:///user/ktu/high_sales_output/' 
      USING PigStorage(',');
```

**Explanation of each step (mark distribution):**
- **Step 1 (1.5 marks):** `LOAD` with explicit schema and `PigStorage` delimiter.
- **Step 2 (1 mark):** `GROUP BY` syntax.
- **Step 3 (2 marks):** `FOREACH ... GENERATE` with `SUM()` aggregation.
- **Step 4 (1 mark):** `FILTER BY` boolean condition.
- **Step 5 (1.5 marks):** `STORE INTO` with output path and storage function.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the schema in LOAD:** Students often write `LOAD 'path' AS (product, region, amount);` *without data types* — this leads to all fields being `bytearray`, breaking `SUM()`.
> 2. **Misnaming `group`:** In a `GROUP BY` relation, the key field is named `group` automatically. If you write `FOREACH grouped GENERATE region, ...`, it fails. You must use `group AS region`.
> 3. **Aggregating before filtering:** You must `FILTER` *after* aggregation, not before, to filter on the sum.
> 4. **Missing comma in schema:** `AS (product:chararray region:chararray amount:float)` is a syntax error.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Restructured Pattern]**

**(a)** Discuss the data model of Pig Latin with examples. Explain the difference between a **Tuple**, **Bag**, and **Map**. **(7 marks)**
