**(b)** Write a Pig Latin script to:
   - Load a file `employees.txt` with fields `(emp_id:int, name:chararray, dept:chararray, salary:float)`.
   - Find the **top 5 highest paid employees** in the **'CSE'** department.
   - Display the result using `DUMP`. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Pig Latin Data Model (7 marks)

The Pig Latin data model has four primary types — **Atom, Tuple, Bag, Map**.

**1. Atom:** A simple scalar value. Cannot be broken down further.
- Examples: `42` (int), `'Alice'` (chararray), `3.14` (float), `true` (boolean).

**2. Tuple:** An ordered sequence of fields, each of which can be of any data type (including another tuple, bag, or map). Written as `(field1, field2, ...)`.
- Example: `(101, 'Alice', 75000.0)` — represents one employee record.
- Analogous to a **row in a relational table**.
- Tuples are **immutable** in Pig Latin.

**3. Bag:** An **unordered** collection of tuples. Tuples within a bag can be duplicate and can have heterogeneous schemas.
- Example: `{(101, 'Alice'), (102, 'Bob'), (101, 'Alice')}` — duplicates allowed.
- Two types:
   - **Outer Bag** — a relation itself.
   - **Inner Bag** — a bag nested inside a tuple (used in `GROUP` results).
- Analogous to a **table**.

**4. Map:** A set of **key-value pairs**, where keys are `chararray` (must be unique) and values can be any data type. Written as `['key'#'value', ...]`.
- Example: `['name'#'Alice', 'dept'#'CSE']`.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Tuple | Bag | Map |
|---------|-------|-----|-----|
| Order | Ordered | Unordered | Unordered |
| Duplicates | No (within field set) | Yes (tuples) | No (keys unique) |
| Access | Positional (`$0`) | Iteration (`$0.field`) | Key-based (`#'key'`) |
| Analogy | Row | Table | Dictionary/JSON |

---

#### Part (b) — Top 5 Highest Paid CSE Employees (7 marks)

```pig
-- Step 1: Load with schema
employees = LOAD 'hdfs:///user/ktu/employees.txt' 
             USING PigStorage(',') 
             AS (emp_id:int, name:chararray, dept:chararray, salary:float);

-- Step 2: Filter CSE department
cse_employees = FILTER employees BY dept == 'CSE';

-- Step 3: Order by salary descending
ordered = ORDER cse_employees BY salary DESC;

-- Step 4: Limit to top 5
top5 = LIMIT ordered 5;

-- Step 5: Dump to console
DUMP top5;
```

**Explanation:**
- The `FILTER BY dept == 'CSE'` step selects only CSE employees. Note the **`==`** operator (Pig Latin uses `==` for equality, **not** `=`).
- `ORDER BY ... DESC` sorts in descending order of salary.
- `LIMIT 5` retains the top 5 tuples.
- `DUMP` prints to the Grunt console (used here instead of `STORE` since the question asks for display).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> 1. **Using `=` instead of `==`:** Common mistake. `=` is invalid in Pig Latin filter conditions.
> 2. **Order of operations:** `LIMIT` *must* come *after* `ORDER BY`, otherwise you get arbitrary 5 rows.
> 3. **Schema mismatch:** If `salary` is loaded as `chararray` instead of `float`, sorting will be **lexicographic** (e.g., `'99999' > '100000'` alphabetically true). Always declare numerical types.
> 4. **Confusing `DUMP` with `STORE`:** `DUMP` outputs to console; `STORE` writes to disk. The question explicitly asks to "display", so `DUMP` is correct.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Memorize Before Exam**

- ✅ **Pig Latin = Procedural / Data-flow** language; **SQL = Declarative** language. Pig Latin is step-by-step.
- ✅ The **basic data types** are: `int`, `long`, `float`, `double`, `chararray`, `bytearray` (atoms), and **Tuple**, **Bag**, **Map** (composites).
- ✅ A **Relation** in Pig Latin is a **Bag of Tuples** — this is the primary manipulated structure.
- ✅ **Bags are unordered** and can contain **duplicate tuples**; **Tuples are ordered**.
- ✅ Equality in `FILTER` uses `==` (double equals), **never** `=`.
- ✅ Schemas are declared with `AS (field:type, field:type, ...)`. Without types, fields default to `bytearray`.
- ✅ `GROUP BY` produces a relation where the first field is automatically named `group`.
- ✅ `JOIN` in Pig Latin supports **Inner, Left Outer, Right Outer, Full Outer** (similar to SQL).
- ✅ `FOREACH ... GENERATE` is the **projection + transformation** operator — Pig Latin's equivalent of `SELECT`.
- ✅ `FLATTEN` is used to **unwrap bags** (e.g., in tokenization, `FLATTEN(TOKENIZE(...))`).
- ✅ **Built-in aggregation functions**: `COUNT`, `SUM`, `AVG`, `MIN`, `MAX`, `COUNT_STAR`.
- ✅ **Diagnostic operators**: `DUMP` (print), `DESCRIBE` (schema), `EXPLAIN` (execution plan), `ILLUSTRATE` (sample).
- ✅ **Execution modes**: `Local Mode` (single JVM) vs `MapReduce Mode` (Hadoop cluster) vs `Spark/Tez Mode`.
- ✅ `STORE` writes to HDFS; `DUMP` writes to console — **do not interchange**.
- ✅ The **Pig Engine** has Parser → Optimizer → Compiler → Execution Engine.
- ✅ Word Count script: `LOAD → TOKENIZE → FLATTEN → GROUP → FOREACH COUNT → ORDER → STORE`.
- ✅ **Pig Latin is case-sensitive** (`group` ≠ `GROUP`); reserved keywords must be used correctly.
- ✅ For multi-step transformations, each operator produces a **new named relation** (immutability).
- ✅ `REGISTER 'myudf.jar'` is used to register custom **UDFs (User Defined Functions)**.
- ✅ `LIMIT` is applied *after* sorting to get the top N — not before.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: User Defined Functions
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT746 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** User Defined Functions

# User Defined Functions (UDFs) in Big Data Analytics

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **User Defined Function (UDF)** is a custom, user-programmed routine that extends the built-in functional vocabulary of a Big Data processing framework (such as Apache Spark, Apache Hive, or Apache Pig) to perform transformations that are not natively supported by the system's library of operators.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (aligned with **Apache Spark** and **Apache Hive** ecosystems), a UDF is a *first-class reusable function* registered either:
- Programmatically (via API) in a Spark Session, or
- Declaratively (via `CREATE FUNCTION` in HiveQL / Spark SQL),
which can be invoked inside DataFrame operations or SQL queries exactly like a native built-in function.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECNT746 – Module 3):**
> UDFs are categorized into three functional classes:
> 1. **UDF** – Scalar transformation (one input row → one output value)
> 2. **UDAF** – User Defined Aggregate Function (many rows → one value)
> 3. **UDTF** – User Defined Table Function (one row → many rows)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a kitchen with only standard appliances — a stove, oven, and mixer. You can cook most things, but suppose you need to make a specialized traditional Kerala *Ada Pradhaman* dessert. The recipe is not in any built-in cookbook, so **you write your own recipe (UDF)** and register it into your personal kitchen index. Now, every time you say "make Ada Pradhaman", the kitchen knows exactly what to do.

In Spark:
- The **kitchen** = Spark Engine
- The **built-in appliances** = `pyspark.sql.functions.*` (e.g., `upper`, `length`)
- **Your custom recipe** = UDF
- **Registering the recipe** = `spark.udf.register("myUDF", fn)` or `@udf` decorator
- **Cooking the dish** = calling it inside `.withColumn()` or `SELECT myUDF(col)`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Constant (Industry Benchmark):** A pure Python UDF in PySpark runs at approximately **1x to 2x slower** than an equivalent Scala UDF because Python UDFs require **data serialization between the JVM and the Python interpreter** for every row. Use **Pandas UDFs (Vectorized UDFs)** for up to **100x speedup** by exploiting Apache Arrow's columnar transfer.

---

## 1.3 Classification of UDFs

| # | Category | Full Form | Input → Output | Example Use-Case |
|---|----------|-----------|---------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **UDF** | User Defined Function | 1 row → 1 value | Custom string cleaner |
| 2 | **UDAF** | User Defined Aggregate Function | N rows → 1 value | Custom weighted average |
| 3 | **UDTF** | User Defined Table Function | 1 row → N rows | Tokenizing a sentence into words |
| 4 | **Pandas UDF** | Vectorized UDF (PyArrow) | Pandas Series → Pandas Series | ML inference at scale |

Explicit boundary values (KTU expected):
- **Row-in / Row-out cardinality** for a scalar UDF = `1 : 1`
- **Row-in / Row-out cardinality** for an UDTF = `1 : N`
- **Row-in / Row-out cardinality** for a UDAF = `N : 1`

---

## 1.4 Why UDFs are Indispensable in Big Data

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why we need UDFs (Engineering Motivation):**
> 1. **Domain Specific Logic** — Tax computation, regional language processing, custom ML preprocessing
> 2. **Encapsulation** — Code reuse across multiple DataFrame pipelines
> 3. **Extensibility** — Bridge the gap between native SQL/DataFrame operators and proprietary business rules
> 4. **Portability** — A well-registered UDF behaves identically in batch (`df.transform`) and SQL contexts (`spark.sql`)

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The UDF Execution Pipeline (Theoretical Walkthrough)

The lifecycle of a UDF in a Spark cluster consists of **5 ordered stages**:

1. **Definition Stage** — The user writes the function body in Python / Scala / Java.
2. **Registration Stage** — The function is bound to a *string name* inside the `SparkSession`'s `FunctionRegistry`.
3. **Logical Plan Stage** — Catalyst Analyzer parses the SQL/DataFrame code, recognizes the registered name, and replaces the call site with a `FunctionRegistryExpression` or `PythonUDF` node.
4. **Physical Plan Stage** — The Catalyst Optimizer generates execution logic. For Python UDFs, Spark schedules them on `PythonWorker` JVMs; for Scala UDFs, they execute directly in the JVM.
5. **Execution Stage** — The UDF is invoked **row-by-row (scalar UDF)** or **batch-by-batch (Pandas UDF)** on the worker nodes holding the relevant partition data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Insight:** In Spark's architecture, a Python UDF incurs **two serialization round-trips per batch** (Python → JVM pickling and JVM → Python unpickling). Pandas UDFs reduce this to a **single Arrow-based columnar transfer**, dramatically reducing overhead.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Signature | Description |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Scalar UDF Cardinality | $f : \mathbb{R}^{1} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{1}$ | One input row produces one output value |
| 2 | UDAF Cardinality | $f : \mathbb{R}^{n} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{1}$ | n input rows produce one aggregated value |
| 3 | UDTF Cardinality | $f : \mathbb{R}^{1} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{n}$ | One input row produces n output rows |
| 4 | Row-Wise Throughput (Python UDF) | $T_{py} \approx 10^3 \text{ rows/sec}$ | Baseline performance on commodity hardware |
| 5 | Row-Wise Throughput (Pandas UDF) | $T_{arrow} \approx 10^5 \text{ rows/sec}$ | Vectorized performance with Apache Arrow |
| 6 | Speedup Factor | $S = \dfrac{T_{py}}{T_{arrow}} \approx 100\times$ | Empirical speedup when migrating to Pandas UDF |
| 7 | Catalyst Resolution | $R(f) = \text{FuncRegistry.lookup}(name)$ | Where $R$ is the resolution function and $f$ is the function reference |
| 8 | Vectorized Batch Size | $B = 10{,}000$ rows | Default Arrow batch size per worker iteration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Convention:** All throughput figures assume a single CPU core. For cluster-wide throughput, multiply by the number of executor cores: $T_{cluster} = T_{core} \times N_{cores}$.

---

## 2.3 Three Implementation Paradigms (KTU High-Yield)

### Paradigm A — Python UDF (`pyspark.sql.functions.udf`)

$$\text{udf}(\text{func}, \text{returnType}) \rightarrow \text{UserDefinedFunction}$$

### Paradigm B — Pandas UDF (Vectorized, Apache Arrow)

$$\text{pandas\_udf}(\text{func}, \text{returnType}, \text{functionType}) \rightarrow \text{UserDefinedFunction}$$

where `functionType ∈ {SCALAR, GROUPED_AGG, GROUPED_MAP}`

### Paradigm C — SQL Registration

$$\text{CREATE FUNCTION } \text{myUDF AS } '\text{com.example.MyUDF}'$$

The first two are API-based; the third is a DDL operation primarily used in **Apache Hive** and **Spark SQL Thrift Server** deployments.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking** — Custom UDF to mask the first 12 digits of a credit card (`XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-1234`)
- **Healthcare** — UDF to compute Body Mass Index (BMI) with medical domain rules
- **Telecom** — UDTF to split concatenated CDR (Call Detail Record) strings into structured columns
- **Retail** — UDAF to compute a *trimmed mean* (excluding top/bottom 5%) for outlier-robust analytics
- **NLP** — Pandas UDF to invoke a pre-trained spaCy / Transformer model in parallel across partitions

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Implementation, Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — End-to-End UDF Pipeline in PySpark

**Problem Statement (KTU Lab Style):**
Given a DataFrame `employee(id, name, salary, department)`, write a UDF that classifies employees into salary bands (`LOW`, `MID`, `HIGH`) and register it for use in both DataFrame DSL and Spark SQL.

### Step 1 — Initialize Spark Session

```python
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import udf, col
from pyspark.sql.types import StringType

spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName("UDF_Demo") \
    .getOrCreate()
```
*Logic:* A SparkSession is the unified entry point for DataFrame and SQL APIs in Spark 2.x+.

---

### Step 2 — Create Sample DataFrame

```python
data = [
    (1, "Anand",    35000, "IT"),
    (2, "Bhavna",   75000, "HR"),
    (3, "Chitra",  150000, "IT"),
    (4, "Deepak",   95000, "Finance"),
    (5, "Esha",     25000, "HR")
]
columns = ["id", "name", "salary", "department"]
df = spark.createDataFrame(data, columns)
df.show()
```

*Logic:* `createDataFrame` distributes the in-memory list across partitions of the cluster. With 5 rows and default parallelism, all data fits in 1 partition — a *micro-batch* (irrelevant for production but pedagogically clear).

---

### Step 3 — Define the Pure Python Function

```python
def classify_salary(salary):
    if salary < 50000:
        return "LOW"
    elif salary < 100000:
        return "MID"
    else:
        return "HIGH"
```

*Logic:* This is a deterministic, side-effect-free function. Such purity is essential for Spark's parallel execution because rows may be processed in **any order, on any worker**.

---

### Step 4 — Register as UDF (API Method)

$$\text{salary\_band} = \text{udf}(\text{classify\_salary}, \text{StringType}())$$

```python
salary_band = udf(classify_salary, StringType())
```

*Logic:* `udf()` wraps the Python function into a Spark-compatible `UserDefinedFunction` object. The second argument specifies the **return schema** — without it, Catalyst cannot infer output types.

---

### Step 5 — Apply UDF in DataFrame DSL

```python
df_with_band = df.withColumn("band", salary_band(col("salary")))
df_with_band.show()
```

*Logic:* `withColumn` adds a new column whose value is computed by invoking `classify_salary` on each row's `salary` field. This generates a **PythonUDF** node in the Catalyst Logical Plan.

---

### Step 6 — Register UDF for SQL Usage

```python
spark.udf.register("sql_salary_band", classify_salary, StringType())
```

*Logic:* `spark.udf.register` binds the function to the string name `"sql_salary_band"` in the `FunctionRegistry`, making it addressable from `spark.sql()` queries.

---

### Step 7 — Invoke UDF from Spark SQL

```python
df.createOrReplaceTempView("emp")

result = spark.sql("""
    SELECT name, salary, department,
           sql_salary_band(salary) AS band
    FROM emp
    WHERE department = 'IT'
""")
result.show()
```

*Logic:* The Catalyst Analyzer resolves `sql_salary_band` via the FunctionRegistry and substitutes the call with the registered Python function reference. The `WHERE` filter executes **before** the UDF (predicate pushdown), saving computational cost.

---

### Step 8 — Expected Output Trace

| id | name   | salary  | department | band  |
|----|--------|---------|------------|-------|
| 1  | Anand  | 35000   | IT         | LOW   |
| 3  | Chitra | 150000  | IT         | HIGH  |

*Derivation Trace for Chitra:* $\text{salary} = 150000 \geq 100000 \Rightarrow \text{return "HIGH"}$ ✓

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Conversion to Pandas UDF (Performance Upgrade)

$$\text{SCALAR Pandas UDF signature: } f(\text{pd.Series} \rightarrow \text{pd.Series})$$

```python
from pyspark.sql.functions import pandas_udf
import pandas as pd

@pandas_udf(StringType())
def classify_salary_vec(s: pd.Series) -> pd.Series:
    return s.apply(lambda x: "LOW" if x < 50000 else ("MID" if x < 100000 else "HIGH"))
```

*Logic:* Unlike a Python UDF, the input is a `pandas.Series` (columnar, contiguous memory). The function operates on the **entire column vector** in one JVM-Python round-trip per Arrow batch, yielding a **~10-100x** speedup.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What

| Scenario | Recommended UDF Type | Reason |
|----------|---------------------|--------|
| Simple row-wise logic, low latency requirement | Python UDF | Easiest to write and debug |
| ML inference / heavy numerical computation | **Pandas UDF** | Vectorization via Apache Arrow |
| Production Hive warehouse | Hive UDF (Java) | Compiles to JVM bytecode — no serialization |
| Aggregations (sum, custom mean) | UDAF (Scala/Python) | Must follow aggregator contract |
| Row → Table expansion (e.g., tokenization) | UDTF (Hive) or `flatMap` (Spark) | `explode`-like behavior |
| Need broadcast variables / accumulators | Python UDF with closure | Pandas UDFs do NOT support closure capture |
| Group-wise ML scoring | `GROUPED_MAP` Pandas UDF | Returns full DataFrame per group |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse `withColumn` (DataFrame DSL) with `SELECT` (SQL). While both can invoke a UDF, only the **registered** UDF is visible in `spark.sql()`. Unregistered `udf()` wrappers are **DSL-only**.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level UDF Execution Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Application Layer] --> B[SparkSession]
    B --> C[Function Registry]
    
    C --> D1[UDF Definition 1]
    C --> D2[UDF Definition 2]
    C --> D3[UDF Definition 3]
    
    D1 --> E1[Python UDF]
    D1 --> E2[Pandas UDF]
    D1 --> E3[Scala UDF]
    
    E1 --> F1[Logical Plan with PythonUDF node]
    E2 --> F2[Logical Plan with ArrowPythonUDF node]
    E3 --> F3[Logical Plan with ScalaUDF node]
    
    F1 --> G[Catalog Optimizer and Catalyst]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    
    G --> H[Physical Plan]
    H --> I1[Driver JVM]
    H --> I2[Executor JVM]
    
    I1 --> J1[PythonWorker Pickle Pipeline]
    I2 --> J2[Direct JVM Execution]
    
    J1 --> K1[Row by Row Processing]
    J2 --> K2[Vectorized Arrow Batch Processing]
    
    K1 --> L[Result RDD or DataFrame]
    K2 --> L
```

*Interpretation:* The diagram traces how a user-defined function flows from the application layer through Spark's logical/physical planning into either JVM-direct execution (Scala) or Python-via-Arrow execution (Pandas).

---

## 4.2 UDF Classification Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Defined Functions] --> B[Scalar UDF]
    A --> C[UDAF]
    A --> D[UDTF]
    
    B --> B1[Python UDF]
    B --> B2[Pandas UDF SCALAR]
    B --> B3[Scala UDF]
    B --> B4[Hive Java UDF]
    
    C --> C1[Grouped Aggregate Pandas UDF]
    C --> C2[Hive GenericUDAF]
    
    D --> D1[Hive UDTF]
    D --> D2[Spark flatMap and explode]
```

*Interpretation:* Shows the three primary UDF categories (Scalar, Aggregate, Table) and their implementation variants across Python, Scala, and Hive ecosystems.

---

## 4.3 Execution Flow: UDF in DataFrame DSL vs Spark SQL

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([User Code]) --> decision{Use DataFrame DSL or SQL?}
    
    decision -->|DSL| dsl1[Define Python Function]
    dsl1 --> dsl2[Wrap with udf function]
    dsl2 --> dsl3[Apply via withColumn or select]
    dsl3 --> dsl4[Logical Plan contains PythonUDF node]
    dsl4 --> exec[Execute on Cluster]
    
    decision -->|SQL| sql1[Define Python Function]
    sql1 --> sql2[Register via spark.udf.register]
    sql2 --> sql3[Invoke inside spark.sql string]
    sql3 --> sql4[FunctionRegistry lookup]
    sql4 --> sql5[Replace with PythonUDF node]
    sql5 --> exec
    
    exec --> end([Result DataFrame])
```

*Interpretation:* Contrasts the two invocation paths. The DSL path uses an in-memory `UserDefinedFunction` object; the SQL path requires explicit registration to the `FunctionRegistry`.

---

## 4.4 Pandas UDF vs Python UDF — Block-Level Performance Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Traditional[Python UDF Path]
        P1[JVM Batch of Rows] --> P2[Serialize to Pickle]
        P2 --> P3[Python Worker Deserializes]
        P3 --> P4[Apply Function Row by Row]
        P4 --> P5[Serialize Output]
        P5 --> P6[JVM Collects Result]
    end
    
    subgraph Vectorized[Pandas UDF Path]
        V1[JVM Batch of Rows] --> V2[Convert to Arrow Columnar Format]
        V2 --> V3[Python Receives Pandas Series]
        V3 --> V4[Apply Vectorized Operation]
        V4 --> V5[Return Pandas Series]
        V5 --> V6[Arrow Deserialization to JVM]
    end
```

*Interpretation:* The Pandas UDF path consolidates the per-row serialization overhead into a single columnar Arrow transfer, which is the principal source of its 10x–100x performance advantage.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
**Define a User Defined Function (UDF) in Apache Spark. List its three main categories with one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **User Defined Function (UDF)** is a custom function written by the developer to extend the built-in functional library of Apache Spark, enabling transformations not natively available in the SQL or DataFrame API. It is registered either programmatically (via `pyspark.sql.functions.udf`) or via `spark.udf.register()` and can be invoked inside `withColumn()` or `spark.sql()`.

**Three categories:**
1. **Scalar UDF** — Takes one input row and returns one value. *Example:* A function to convert Celsius to Fahrenheit.
2. **UDAF (User Defined Aggregate Function)** — Takes N input rows and returns one aggregated value. *Example:* A function to compute a *trimmed mean* (excluding outliers).
3. **UDTF (User Defined Table Function)** — Takes one input row and returns N rows. *Example:* A function to split a sentence into individual words.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam – Jul 2023]`**
**Explain the difference between a Python UDF and a Pandas UDF. Why is the latter preferred in production?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Python UDF | Pandas UDF |
|--------|-----------|------------|
| Input Type | Single Python scalar | `pandas.Series` (columnar) |
| Execution | Row-by-row | Vectorized (batch-by-batch) |
| Data Transfer | Pickle (JVM ↔ Python) | Apache Arrow (binary columnar) |
| Performance | **Baseline (1x)** | **~10x to 100x faster** |
| Closure Support | Yes (broadcast variables) | **No** (cannot capture closure) |

**Why Pandas UDF is preferred:** Pandas UDFs exploit **Apache Arrow's columnar memory layout**, eliminating the per-row serialization overhead of traditional Python UDFs. This results in dramatically higher throughput for operations like ML inference, statistical aggregation, and group-wise computation. However, they cannot capture closure variables, so the choice depends on the use-case.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> When answering UDF questions, students frequently:
> 1. **Forget the return type** — Every UDF declaration MUST specify `returnType` (e.g., `StringType()`); omitting it loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confuse `udf.register` with `functions.udf`** — The former registers for SQL; the latter creates a DSL-only column expression.
> 3. **Miss Arrow / Pandas UDFs** — If the question says "optimize the UDF", you MUST mention Pandas UDFs and Apache Arrow, not just basic Python UDFs.
> 4. **Don't justify cardinality** — Always state the row-in / row-out mapping (1:1 for scalar, N:1 for UDAF, 1:N for UDTF) when defining the category.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
Explain the architecture of User Defined Functions in Apache Spark with a neat block diagram. Differentiate between row-at-a-time and vectorized execution.

**Model Answer:**

User Defined Functions in Spark follow a **5-stage architectural pipeline**:

1. **Definition** — The user writes the function in Python/Scala/Java.
2. **Registration** — The function is bound to a string name in the `FunctionRegistry` (`spark.udf.register()`).
3. **Logical Plan** — Catalyst's analyzer identifies the function call and creates a `PythonUDF` or `ScalaUDF` expression node.
4. **Physical Plan** — Catalyst's optimizer decides whether to execute via `PythonWorker` (Python UDF) or directly in JVM (Scala UDF).
5. **Execution** — The function is invoked on partition data on the executor nodes.

**Row-at-a-time (Python UDF) execution:**
- Each row is serialized (Pickle) → sent to Python → deserialized → processed → serialized back → deserialized in JVM.
- Result: 2 serialization round-trips per row; slow throughput.

**Vectorized (Pandas UDF) execution:**
- A batch of ~10,000 rows is converted to Apache Arrow columnar format → transferred to Python in a single call → processed as `pandas.Series` → returned as Arrow → deserialized in JVM.
- Result: 1 serialization round-trip per batch; ~10-100x faster.

```
Row-at-a-time:   [r1]→[py]→[r2]→[py]→[r3]→[py]  (slow)
Vectorized:      [r1..r10000]→[py]→[r10001..r20000]→[py]  (fast)
```

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
Write a PySpark program to: (i) create a DataFrame of students with columns `name` and `marks`, (ii) define a Python UDF to assign a **grade** (`A` if marks ≥ 90, `B` if 75–89, `C` if 60–74, `F` otherwise), (iii) register it for SQL usage, and (iv) display the result using both DataFrame DSL and Spark SQL.

**Model Answer:**

```python
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import udf, col
from pyspark.sql.types import StringType

# (i) Create Spark Session and DataFrame
spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("GradeUDF").getOrCreate()

data = [("Anand", 92), ("Bhavna", 78), ("Chitra", 65), ("Deepak", 45), ("Esha", 88)]
df = spark.createDataFrame(data, ["name", "marks"])

# (ii) Define Python function and wrap as UDF
def assign_grade(m):
    if m >= 90: return "A"
    elif m >= 75: return "B"
    elif m >= 60: return "C"
    else: return "F"

grade_udf = udf(assign_grade, StringType())

# (iii) Register for SQL usage
spark.udf.register("sql_grade", assign_grade, StringType())

# (iv) Display using DataFrame DSL
print("=== DataFrame DSL Output ===")
df.withColumn("grade", grade_udf(col("marks"))).show()

# (iv) Display using Spark SQL
df.createOrReplaceTempView("students")
print("=== Spark SQL Output ===")
spark.sql("SELECT name, marks, sql_grade(marks) AS grade FROM students").show()
```

**Expected Output:**

| name   | marks | grade |
|--------|-------|-------|
| Anand  | 92    | A     |
| Bhavna | 78    | B     |
| Chitra | 65    | C     |
| Deepak | 45    | F     |
| Esha   | 88    | B     |

**Explanation of Key Lines:**
- `udf(assign_grade, StringType())` — Wraps the Python function and declares the return schema.
- `spark.udf.register("sql_grade", ...)` — Binds the function to the SQL namespace.
- `.withColumn("grade", ...)` — DataFrame DSL invocation.
- `spark.sql("... sql_grade(marks) ...")` — SQL invocation via the registered name.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**`[KTU University Exam – Jul 2022]`**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
What are the different types of UDFs supported in Apache Hive? Explain each with an example.

**Model Answer:**

Apache Hive supports **three categories** of UDFs:

**1. UDF (User Defined Function) — Scalar**
- Maps one input row to one output value.
- Implemented by extending the `org.apache.hadoop.hive.ql.exec.UDF` class and overriding the `evaluate()` method.
- *Example:* A function to compute the square of an integer.

```java
import org.apache.hadoop.hive.ql.exec.UDF;
public class SquareUDF extends UDF {
    public Integer evaluate(Integer x) { return x * x; }
}
```

**2. UDAF (User Defined Aggregate Function)**
- Maps N input rows to one aggregated output value.
- More complex — requires 5 methods: `init`, `iterate`, `mergePartial`, `merge`, `terminate`.
- *Example:* Computing a custom geometric mean of a column.

**3. UDTF (User Defined Table Function)**
- Maps one input row to N output rows.
- Implemented by extending `GenericUDTF` and overriding `initialize`, `process`, `close`.
- *Example:* Splitting a comma-separated string into multiple rows.

**Usage in HiveQL:**
```sql
ADD JAR /path/to/udf.jar;
CREATE TEMPORARY FUNCTION my_square AS 'com.example.SquareUDF';
SELECT my_square(salary) FROM employees;
```

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
Compare Python UDFs and Pandas UDFs in PySpark. Show the code transformation from a Python UDF to a Pandas UDF for the `assign_grade` function from Question A. Discuss the performance implications.

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Python UDF | Pandas UDF |
|---------|-----------|------------|
| Decorator | `udf()` | `pandas_udf()` |
| Input Argument | Scalar (e.g., `int`) | `pandas.Series` |
| Return Type | Scalar | `pandas.Series` |
| Data Transfer Tech | Pickle | Apache Arrow |
| Throughput | ~1,000 rows/sec | ~100,000 rows/sec |
| Cluster Best Use | Low-volume logic | ML inference, vectorized math |
| Limitation | None major | Cannot capture closure variables |

**Code Transformation:**

**Python UDF Version:**
```python
from pyspark.sql.functions import udf
from pyspark.sql.types import StringType

@udf(returnType=StringType())
def assign_grade(m: int) -> str:
    if m >= 90: return "A"
    elif m >= 75: return "B"
    elif m >= 60: return "C"
    else: return "F"
```

**Pandas UDF Version (Vectorized):**
```python
from pyspark.sql.functions import pandas_udf
import pandas as pd

@pandas_udf(StringType())
def assign_grade_vec(m: pd.Series) -> pd.Series:
    return m.apply(lambda x: "A" if x >= 90 else ("B" if x >= 75 else ("C" if x >= 60 else "F")))
```

**Performance Implications:**

1. **Serialization Overhead** — Python UDFs require per-row Pickle serialization, causing **O(n)** JVM-Python boundary crossings. Pandas UDFs batch this into **O(n/batches)** Arrow transfers, an effective reduction by a factor of 10,000 (the default Arrow batch size).

2. **Vectorized CPU Operations** — Pandas operations (e.g., `Series.apply`, `np.where`) execute as **compiled C code** under the hood, avoiding Python's GIL bottleneck per row.

3. **Memory Locality** — Arrow's columnar format provides better CPU cache utilization compared to row-wise Python objects.

4. **Trade-off** — Pandas UDFs cannot capture external state (broadcast variables, accumulators, or mutable counters) because the function is shipped to executors as a serialized Arrow payload. For stateful logic, Python UDFs remain necessary.

5. **Empirical Speedup** — Benchmarks on commodity hardware (4-core executor) report **15x to 100x speedup** when migrating from Python UDF to Pandas UDF for typical row-wise transformations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Scheme):**

- **UDF** is a *user-programmed function* that extends Spark/Hive's built-in operator library.
- **Three categories:** Scalar UDF (1:1), UDAF (N:1), UDTF (1:N). Always state the cardinality.
- **Two registration paths:** DSL-only via `udf()` function; SQL-visible via `spark.udf.register()`.
- **Pandas UDFs use Apache Arrow** for columnar data transfer, giving **10x–100x** speedup over traditional Python UDFs.
- **Python UDFs support closure** capture (broadcast variables, accumulators); Pandas UDFs do **NOT**.
- **PySpark serialization:** `pickle` (Python UDF) vs `arrow` (Pandas UDF).
- **Hive UDFs** are written in **Java**, extend `org.apache.hadoop.hive.ql.exec.UDF`, and are loaded via `ADD JAR` + `CREATE FUNCTION`.
- **Always specify `returnType`** when registering a UDF; Catalyst cannot infer return types from arbitrary Python functions.
- **Predicate pushdown:** `WHERE` clauses are applied **before** UDF execution, so filter first, then apply UDFs for optimal performance.
- **Three Pandas UDF function types:** `SCALAR`, `GROUPED_AGG`, `GROUPED_MAP` — know the signature of each.
- **Common exam keywords to recognize:** "custom function", "extend built-in", "Apache Arrow", "FunctionRegistry", "PythonWorker", "serialization overhead".
- **Default Arrow batch size** is 10,000 rows per JVM-Python round-trip.
- **For ML inference at scale**, prefer Pandas UDF + pre-trained model broadcast via `pandas_udf` with `FUNCTIONTYPE` parameter.
- **For UDTF-like behavior in Spark**, use `.flatMap()` or `explode()` instead of writing a full Hive UDTF.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Processing Operators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT746 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Data Processing Operators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Data Processing Operators** are the fundamental, composable, and reusable functional primitives (or algebraic transformations) applied to a distributed dataset (an RDD, DataFrame, or a key–value pair collection) in order to filter, transform, aggregate, join, or persist the data across a cluster of computing nodes. In the context of KTU's Big Data Analytics syllabus, these operators form the **logical execution backbone** of distributed processing engines such as **Apache Spark** and **Hadoop MapReduce**.

In formal algebraic terms, a data processing operator is a function:

$$O : D_{in} \rightarrow D_{out}$$

where $D_{in}$ and $D_{out}$ are distributed datasets, and the operator $O$ preserves (or breaks) the partitioning and lineage structure of the input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 3):** Operators are classified into **Narrow (Transformations)** and **Wide (Shuffle Transformations)** depending on whether data movement between partitions is required.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Factory Assembly Line"

Imagine a **bottling plant** producing millions of bottles per day:

- The **raw bottles (dataset)** enter the conveyor belt.
- Each **worker station (operator)** performs exactly one tiny job: one worker fills, another caps, another labels, another inspects, another boxes.
- The **conveyor belt (RDD/DataFrame)** carries the bottles from one station to the next.
- If a worker only works on bottles physically in front of them, that is a **narrow operation** (no cross-belt communication).
- If the worker needs bottles from another belt altogether (e.g., to merge two production lines), that is a **wide operation** — the whole plant must pause and re-route traffic (this is the **shuffle**).

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Tip:** The bottleneck in any big data job is *almost always* the wide operators. Narrow operators scale linearly with cluster size; wide operators do not.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric | Standard Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Default Parallelism** | `2 × cores` in cluster | Number of partitions created when none is specified |
| **Shuffle Block Threshold** | **134217728 bytes (128 MB)** | Spark's spill threshold to disk during shuffle |
| **RDD Lineage Depth** | Unbounded logically, capped by DAG scheduler | Length of operator dependency chain |
| **Lazy Evaluation** | Boolean flag (`true` by default) | Operators form a DAG, not executed until an Action is called |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Data Processing Operators

### A. By Execution Strategy

1. **Transformations (Lazy)** — build a new dataset from an existing one; execution is deferred.
2. **Actions (Eager)** — trigger the actual computation and return a value to the driver or write to storage.

### B. By Data Dependency (Narrow vs. Wide)

| Property | Narrow Transformation | Wide Transformation |
|---|---|---|
| Partition Dependency | 1 → 1 (parent → child) | 1 → N (requires shuffle) |
| Network I/O | None | Heavy cross-node transfer |
| Fault Tolerance | Recompute single partition | Recompute full parent stage |
| Examples | `map`, `filter`, `flatMap`, `union` | `groupByKey`, `reduceByKey`, `join`, `repartition` |

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Operator Execution

The general sequence followed by every distributed operator pipeline is:

- **Step 1 — Input Reading:** Data is loaded into an RDD/DataFrame from HDFS, S3, or an in-memory collection.
- **Step 2 — DAG Construction:** The driver builds a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** of operator stages.
- **Step 3 — Stage Partitioning:** The DAG Scheduler splits the DAG into **Stages** at shuffle boundaries.
- **Step 4 — Task Scheduling:** Each stage is broken into **Tasks** (one per partition) and pushed to executors.
- **Step 5 — Transformation:** Tasks apply the operator function (user lambda) on each record.
- **Step 6 — Shuffle (if wide):** Records are repartitioned by key and moved across the network.
- **Step 7 — Aggregation / Output:** Reducer logic combines shuffled records and emits the final dataset.
- **Step 8 — Action Trigger:** Result is returned to driver (e.g., `collect()`) or persisted (e.g., `saveAsTextFile`).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **high-yield equations** for the KTU ESE. Memorize the column headers, units, and operator semantics.

| Operator | Mathematical Form | Input → Output | Type | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `map(f)` | $y_i = f(x_i)$ | RDD[T] → RDD[U] | Narrow (1-to-1) | Stateless |
| `flatMap(f)` | $Y = \bigcup_i f(x_i)$ | RDD[T] → RDD[U] | Narrow (1-to-N) | Flattens nested collections |
| `filter(p)` | $Y = \{x \in X \mid p(x)\}$ | RDD[T] → RDD[T] | Narrow (subset) | Predicate-based |
| `reduceByKey(f)` | $y_k = \bigoplus_{x \in X_k} f(x)$ | RDD[(K,V)] → RDD[(K,V)] | **Wide** | Combines per key, local + global |
| `groupByKey()` | $y_k = \{v \mid (k,v) \in X\}$ | RDD[(K,V)] → RDD[(K,Iterable[V])] | **Wide** | No pre-aggregation |
| `join(R)` | $Y = \{(k,(v,w)) \mid k \in X_k \cap R_k\}$ | RDD[(K,V)] × RDD[(K,W)] → RDD[(K,(V,W))] | **Wide** | Inner equi-join |
| `distinct()` | $Y = \text{set}(X)$ | RDD[T] → RDD[T] | **Wide** | Implies `reduceByKey` |
| `sortByKey()` | $Y = \text{sorted}(X)$ | RDD[(K,V)] → RDD[(K,V)] | **Wide** | Range partitioner |
| `union(R)` | $Y = X \cup R$ | RDD[T] × RDD[T] → RDD[T] | Narrow | No shuffle |
| `count()` | $n = \vert X \vert$ | RDD[T] → Long | Action | Driver return |
| `collect()` | $Y = \text{list}(X)$ | RDD[T] → Array[T] | Action | **Pulls to driver** |
| `saveAsTextFile(p)` | $X \rightarrow \text{files at } p$ | RDD[T] → HDFS path | Action | Distributed write |

> [!NOTE]
> **Set-Theoretic View:** Many operators (union, intersection, join, subtract) are *relational-algebraic* operations, but their implementation in a distributed engine makes them far more expensive than in classical RDBMS — especially the **wide** ones.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Data processing operators power:

- **ETL pipelines** in enterprises (Informatica → Spark equivalent).
- **Real-time analytics** (Kafka → Spark Streaming operators).
- **Recommendation engines** (`join` + `reduceByKey` for co-occurrence matrices).
- **Log mining & clickstream analysis** (`flatMap` tokenization + `groupByKey`).
- **Fraud detection** in banking (`filter` rules + `window` operators in structured streaming).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation: `reduceByKey(f)` Execution

The `reduceByKey` operator is the **most tested wide operator** in KTU exams. We derive its mathematical behavior step-by-step.

> [!NOTE]
> Let $X = \{(k_1, v_1), (k_2, v_2), \ldots, (k_n, v_n)\}$ be the input key–value RDD with cardinality $n$, and let $f : (V, V) \rightarrow V$ be the user-supplied associative, commutative binary reducer.

### Step 1 — Partition the Dataset

Suppose the cluster has $P$ partitions. The driver assigns records to partitions via a partitioner $\pi$:

$$X = \bigcup_{p=1}^{P} X_p, \quad X_p = \{(k, v) \in X \mid \pi(k) = p\}$$

**Logic:** Each executor receives only its locally assigned key–value pairs.

### Step 2 — Local Combiners (Map-Side Combine)

Within partition $X_p$, the operator performs a local reduction **before** the shuffle. This is the critical optimization that distinguishes `reduceByKey` from `groupByKey`:

$$L_p(k) = \bigoplus_{(k, v) \in X_p} f(v)$$

where $\bigoplus$ denotes left-fold application of $f$ over all values sharing key $k$ within partition $p$.

**Logic:** This drastically reduces the data volume that must traverse the network.

### Step 3 — Shuffle to Reducer Partition

Each locally combined record $(k, L_p(k))$ is shipped to the reducer partition $r$, where:

$$r = \pi(k)$$

All locally reduced records for key $k$ are collected on the same executor.

### Step 4 — Global Reduction

At the reducer, the final value is computed as:

$$Y(k) = \bigoplus_{p \in \text{partitions}(k)} f(L_p(k))$$

**Logic:** The reducer folds across the pre-aggregated values from all partitions.

### Step 5 — Final Output

The output RDD is constructed as:

$$Y = \{(k, Y(k)) \mid k \in \text{keys}(X)\}$$

### 3.1.1 Compact Final Form

The complete `reduceByKey` operator can be expressed as the **two-stage fold**:

$$Y(k) = \bigoplus_{p} \left( \bigoplus_{v \in X_{p,k}} f(v) \right)$$

where $X_{p,k} = \{v \mid (k, v) \in X_p\}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** If $f$ is *not* associative, the local combiner will produce incorrect results. KTU frequently tests this property.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — `reduceByKey` for Word Count

Let the input RDD be:

$$X = \{(\text{"hadoop"}, 1), (\text{"spark"}, 1), (\text{"hadoop"}, 1), (\text{"spark"}, 1), (\text{"spark"}, 1)\}$$

Suppose $P = 2$ partitions:

$$X_1 = \{(\text{"hadoop"}, 1), (\text{"spark"}, 1), (\text{"hadoop"}, 1)\}$$

$$X_2 = \{(\text{"spark"}, 1), (\text{"spark"}, 1)\}$$

The user-defined reducer is $f(a, b) = a + b$.

### Stage 1 — Local Combine (per partition)

- In $X_1$:

$$L_1(\text{"hadoop"}) = 1 + 1 = 2$$
$$L_1(\text{"spark"}) = 1$$

- In $X_2$:

$$L_2(\text{"spark"}) = 1 + 1 = 2$$

### Stage 2 — Shuffle

Records are routed so that all values of the same key land at the same reducer:

- For key `"hadoop"`: only $L_1 = 2$ arrives.
- For key `"spark"`: $L_1 = 1$ and $L_2 = 2$ arrive.

### Stage 3 — Global Reduce

$$Y(\text{"hadoop"}) = 2$$

$$Y(\text{"spark"}) = 1 + 2 = 3$$

### Final Output RDD

$$Y = \{(\text{"hadoop"}, 2), (\text{"spark"}, 3)\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Data Volume Saved:** Without local combine, the reducer would have processed $3$ values for "hadoop" (3 over the wire) and $3$ values for "spark" (3 over the wire) → 6 records shuffled. With local combine, only $2$ records are shuffled. This is the **shuffle amplification factor**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Operator Performance Trade-offs

| Operator | Shuffle Cost | Memory Cost | When to Use | When to Avoid |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `reduceByKey` | **Low** (pre-aggregated) | Low | Large aggregations by key | When reducer is not associative |
| `groupByKey` | **High** (no pre-agg) | High (Iterable grows) | When reducer is non-associative | Default — always prefer `reduceByKey` |
| `aggregateByKey` | **Low** (custom combiner) | Medium | When you need different input/output types | Simple sums/counts |
| `join` | **High** | High | Equi-joins on the same partitioner | Skewed keys |
| `mapPartitions` | None | **High** (per-partition state) | DB connections, ML model loading | When partition is too large |
| `coalesce(n)` | **None / Low** | Low | Reducing partition count | Increasing partitions (use `repartition`) |

## 3.4 Derivation of Shuffle Cost

Let $N$ = total input records, $K$ = distinct keys, $P$ = partitions.

- **Without combiner (e.g., `groupByKey`):** All $N$ records are shuffled:

$$C_{\text{shuffle}}^{\text{no-combine}} = N$$

- **With combiner (e.g., `reduceByKey`):** At most $K$ records are shuffled (one per key per partition):

$$C_{\text{shuffle}}^{\text{combine}} \leq K \cdot P$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Shuffle Reduction Ratio** is defined as:

$$R_{\text{shuffle}} = \frac{C_{\text{shuffle}}^{\text{no-combine}}}{C_{\text{shuffle}}^{\text{combine}}} = \frac{N}{K \cdot P}$$

For typical word-count workloads, $R_{\text{shuffle}}$ is in the order of **10× to 1000×** — this is why `reduceByKey` is the **de-facto** KTU-favoured operator.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Operator Pipeline Block Diagram (Spark DAG)

Below is the functional architecture flow of a typical Spark job illustrating how operators interlink:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HDFS Input File] --> B[textFile Operator - Narrow]
    B --> C[flatMap Tokenization - Narrow]
    C --> D[map Pair Creation - Narrow]
    D --> E[filter Stopwords - Narrow]
    E --> F[reduceByKey Aggregation - Wide Shuffle]
    F --> G[sortByKey - Wide Shuffle]
    G --> H[map Formatting - Narrow]
    H --> I[saveAsTextFile - Action]
    I --> J[HDFS Output Directory]

    stage1[Stage 1 - Narrow Chain] -.covers.-> B
    stage1 -.covers.-> C
    stage1 -.covers.-> D
    stage1 -.covers.-> E
    stage2[Stage 2 - Shuffle Boundary] -.covers.-> F
    stage3[Stage 3 - Shuffle Boundary] -.covers.-> G
    stage4[Stage 4 - Narrow + Action] -.covers.-> H
    stage4 -.covers.-> I
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each "Stage" is bounded by a wide operator. Spark's DAG scheduler physically executes stages; within a stage, operators are *fused* into a single task per partition.

## 4.2 RDD Lineage Graph (Operator Dependency DAG)

The following graph depicts how a complex query is broken into operator vertices, with narrow edges (solid) and wide edges (dashed):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Raw RDD] -->|narrow| node2[Mapped RDD]
    node2 -->|narrow| node3[Filtered RDD]
    node3 -->|narrow| node4[FlatMapped RDD]
    node4 -->|wide shuffle| node5[ReducedByKey RDD]
    node5 -->|wide shuffle| node6[Joined RDD]
    node6 -->|narrow| node7[Final RDD]
    node7 -->|action collect| node8[Driver Result Array]

    style node1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style node5 fill:#ffe1e1
    style node6 fill:#ffe1e1
    style node8 fill:#e1ffe1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:**
> - **Blue nodes** = source/input.
> - **Red nodes** = wide operators (shuffle boundaries).
> - **Green node** = action (eager evaluation).
> - **Solid arrows** = narrow dependency.
> - **Dashed arrows** = wide dependency.

## 4.3 Operator Classification Taxonomy

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Data Processing Operators))
    Transformations
      Narrow
        map
        filter
        flatMap
        mapPartitions
        union
        sample
      Wide
        groupByKey
        reduceByKey
        join
        distinct
        sortByKey
        repartition
        coalesce
    Actions
      Driver
        collect
        count
        first
        take
        reduce
      Storage
        saveAsTextFile
        saveAsSequenceFile
        foreach
```

## 4.4 Shuffle Data Flow — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Executor1
        p1[Partition 1 - Local Combine]
    end
    subgraph Executor2
        p2[Partition 2 - Local Combine]
    end
    subgraph Executor3
        p3[Partition 3 - Local Combine]
    end

    p1 -->|shuffle write| b1[Shuffle Block 1]
    p2 -->|shuffle write| b2[Shuffle Block 2]
    p3 -->|shuffle write| b3[Shuffle Block 3]

    b1 --> r1[Reducer 1]
    b2 --> r1
    b3 --> r1
    b1 --> r2[Reducer 2]
    b2 --> r2
    b3 --> r2

    r1 --> out1[Output Partition A]
    r2 --> out2[Output Partition B]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between narrow and wide transformations in Spark. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Narrow transformations are operators in which each partition of the parent RDD is used by **at most one partition** of the child RDD; therefore, no data movement across executors is required. They are pipelined and highly efficient. **Examples:** `map`, `filter`, `flatMap`, `union`, `sample`.

Wide transformations require data from **multiple parent partitions** to be combined into a single child partition, triggering a **shuffle** across the network. They are expensive and create new stages. **Examples:** `groupByKey`, `reduceByKey`, `join`, `distinct`, `sortByKey`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often state "`join` is narrow" — **NO**. A join on a partitioned key still requires shuffle if the two RDDs are not co-partitioned. Marks lost here are very common.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is lazy evaluation in Spark? Why is it advantageous?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Lazy evaluation means that Spark **does not execute transformations immediately** when they are called. Instead, it builds a **logical DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph)** of pending operations. Execution is triggered only when an **action** (e.g., `count`, `collect`, `saveAsTextFile`) is invoked.

**Advantages:**
- The optimizer can **fuse multiple narrow operators** into a single stage, avoiding intermediate disk I/O.
- Unused RDD branches are **pruned** before execution.
- It enables **pipelining** and **whole-stage code generation** in modern Spark (Tungsten engine).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU ESE 2024 Scheme, **Part B questions carry 14 marks with internal choice**. Each has two sub-parts of **7 marks** each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of the `reduceByKey` operator. Show how a local combiner reduces shuffle cost. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following RDD of (student, marks) pairs:

$$\text{RDD}_{\text{in}} = \{(\text{A}, 80), (\text{B}, 75), (\text{A}, 90), (\text{C}, 60), (\text{B}, 85), (\text{A}, 70)\}$$

Assuming 2 partitions $P_1 = \{A:80, B:75, A:90\}$ and $P_2 = \{C:60, B:85, A:70\}$, compute the output of `reduceByKey` using `max` as the reducer. Show all stages. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — 7 Marks:**

The `reduceByKey` operator follows a two-stage architecture:

- **Stage 1 — Map-side Combine:** Each partition performs a local aggregation using the user-defined associative function. This reduces the volume of data emitted to the shuffle.
- **Stage 2 — Shuffle and Reduce:** Locally combined records are repartitioned by key and globally reduced.

The diagram:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1[P1 Records] --> lc1[Local Combiner on P1]
    p2[P2 Records] --> lc2[Local Combiner on P2]
    lc1 -->|shuffle| r1[Reducer 1]
    lc2 -->|shuffle| r1
    lc2 -->|shuffle| r2[Reducer 2]
    lc1 -->|shuffle| r2
    r1 --> out1[Output RDD Part 1]
    r2 --> out2[Output RDD Part 2]
```

**Why the local combiner matters:** Without it, every record is sent over the network. With it, only one record per (partition, key) tuple is sent, dramatically reducing shuffle I/O. **(2 marks for diagram, 3 marks for explanation, 2 marks for the "why")**

**(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Stage 1: Local Combine per Partition** (using `max` as reducer $f$):

- Partition $P_1$:

$$L_1(\text{A}) = \max(80, 90) = 90$$
$$L_1(\text{B}) = \max(75) = 75$$

- Partition $P_2$:

$$L_2(\text{A}) = \max(70) = 70$$
$$L_2(\text{B}) = \max(85) = 85$$
$$L_2(\text{C}) = \max(60) = 60$$

**Stage 2: Shuffle and Global Reduce** (grouping by key across partitions):

- For key `A`: values $= \{L_1(\text{A}), L_2(\text{A})\} = \{90, 70\}$

$$Y(\text{A}) = \max(90, 70) = 90$$

- For key `B`: values $= \{L_1(\text{B}), L_2(\text{B})\} = \{75, 85\}$

$$Y(\text{B}) = \max(75, 85) = 85$$

- For key `C`: values $= \{L_2(\text{C})\} = \{60\}$

$$Y(\text{C}) = \max(60) = 60$$

**Final Output RDD:**

$$\text{RDD}_{\text{out}} = \{(\text{A}, 90), (\text{B}, 85), (\text{C}, 60)\}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. Marks deducted if student skips the local combine stage.
> 2. If student uses `groupByKey` semantics and just lists values (e.g., `B → [75, 85]`), **deduct 3 marks** — that is `groupByKey`, not `reduceByKey`.
> 3. Ensure the final answer is an RDD of `(Key, AggregatedValue)` pairs, **not** a list of values.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`**

**(a)** Explain the Spark execution model based on DAG, stages, and tasks. How does the operator type (narrow vs. wide) influence stage boundaries? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the input RDD:

$$X = [\text{"big data big"}, \text{"data analytics big"}, \text{"data is big"}]$$

Compute the output RDD after applying the following operator pipeline: `flatMap` (split on whitespace) → `map` (emit `(word, 1)`) → `reduceByKey` (sum). Show each step. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — 7 Marks:**

The Spark execution model consists of three layers:

1. **DAG (Logical Plan):** When transformations are called, Spark constructs a DAG where vertices are RDDs and edges are operator dependencies.
2. **Stages (Physical Plan):** The DAG Scheduler partitions the DAG at **shuffle boundaries**. A stage is a maximal set of narrow operators that can be pipelined.
3. **Tasks (Execution Units):** Each stage is decomposed into tasks, where **one task processes one partition**. Tasks are dispatched to executors by the Task Scheduler.

**Influence of operator type:**
- **Narrow operators** → stay within the same stage (fused into a single task per partition).
- **Wide operators** → create a stage boundary (shuffle write → shuffle read → next stage).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory trick:** "*Narrow = No boundary; Wide = Wall*."

**(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — `flatMap(line => line.split(" "))`:**

$$Y_1 = [\text{"big"}, \text{"data"}, \text{"big"}, \text{"data"}, \text{"analytics"}, \text{"big"}, \text{"data"}, \text{"is"}, \text{"big"}]$$

**Step 2 — `map(word => (word, 1))`:**

$$Y_2 = \{(\text{"big"}, 1), (\text{"data"}, 1), (\text{"big"}, 1), (\text{"data"}, 1), (\text{"analytics"}, 1), (\text{"big"}, 1), (\text{"data"}, 1), (\text{"is"}, 1), (\text{"big"}, 1)\}$$

**Step 3 — `reduceByKey(_ + _)`:**

Group and sum per key:

- "big" → $1+1+1+1 = 4$
- "data" → $1+1+1 = 3$
- "analytics" → $1$
- "is" → $1$

**Final Output RDD:**

$$Y_3 = \{(\text{"big"}, 4), (\text{"data"}, 3), (\text{"analytics"}, 1), (\text{"is"}, 1)\}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Using `groupByKey` instead of `reduceByKey` is incorrect here; `groupByKey` would yield `(word, [1,1,1,...])`, which is **not** the final aggregated RDD. KTU expects the final aggregated pair.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Operator = A Function on Distributed Data:** $O : D_{in} \rightarrow D_{out}$.
- **Transformations are LAZY; Actions are EAGER.** Nothing executes until an action.
- **Narrow = same partition; Wide = cross-partition (shuffle).** Wide operators are *always* stage boundaries.
- **`reduceByKey` ≠ `groupByKey`.** The former pre-aggregates locally; the latter does not. **Prefer `reduceByKey`** when the function is associative and commutative.
- **`join` is wide** unless both RDDs are co-partitioned (use `partitionBy` + same partitioner to avoid shuffle).
- **DAG → Stages → Tasks → Partitions** is the execution hierarchy.
- **Lazy evaluation enables operator fusion** — a key Spark performance advantage.
- **Pipelining** is automatic within a stage for narrow operators; wide operators break the pipeline.
- **`coalesce(n)` reduces partitions without shuffle**; `repartition(n)` increases them and triggers a shuffle.
- **Shuffle cost** for `reduceByKey`: $\leq K \cdot P$ records, where $K$ = distinct keys, $P$ = partitions.
- **Fault tolerance** is via lineage — RDDs can be recomputed by re-running the operator chain from the source.
- **Default parallelism** = `2 × total cluster cores` (approximate, configurable via `spark.default.parallelism`).
- **Shuffle block threshold** = **128 MB** default in Spark (`spark.shuffle.file.buffer` and related configs).
- **`mapPartitions`** allows per-partition state (e.g., DB connections) — efficient when setup cost is high.
- **Caching** (`persist`, `cache`) materializes an RDD in memory (or disk) when reused across multiple actions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hive Shell
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT746 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hive Shell

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 : HIVE SHELL

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Hive Shell** is the command-line interface (CLI) provided by Apache Hive that enables users to interact with the Hive data warehouse system. It acts as a thin client that submits HiveQL (HQL) statements to the HiveServer, receives execution plans, and displays formatted query results.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** terminology, the Hive Shell is defined as the *primary execution environment for submitting SQL-like queries against structured and semi-structured data stored in the Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)*. It serves as the textual front-end for issuing Data Definition Language (DDL), Data Manipulation Language (DML), and Data Query Language (DQL) statements.

> [!NOTE]
> **Official Definition (Apache Hive Documentation)**
> The Hive Shell is a Java-based command-line utility that connects to Hive using either the legacy Thrift protocol (HiveServer1) or the modern JDBC/Thrift protocol (HiveServer2 via Beeline).

There are two principal variants of the Hive Shell:

| Shell Variant | Protocol | Status | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hive CLI** (`$ hive`) | Direct Derby/Thrift | Deprecated (Hive 2.0+) | Local single-user development |
| **Beeline** (`$ beeline`) | JDBC over Thrift | Recommended | Production multi-user environments |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy : "The Receptionist of a Massive Library"**

Imagine a colossal library containing **petabytes** of books scattered across thousands of shelves. As a reader, you cannot wander into the storage halls directly — that would be chaotic and unsafe. Instead, you walk up to a **receptionist** (the Hive Shell) at the front desk. You speak to the receptionist in plain, conversational English:

> *"Please list all books published in 2024."*
> *"How many books mention 'Big Data'?"*
> *"Update the price column of the 'Computer Science' section."*

The receptionist translates your request, walks into the warehouse, gathers the right books, and returns with neatly formatted answers.

In this analogy:
- **The library** = HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)
- **The books** = Data files (CSV, Parquet, ORC, JSON)
- **The receptionist** = Hive Shell
- **Your English question** = HiveQL (HQL) — SQL-like query
- **The librarian in the back** = HiveServer2 + Execution Engine (MapReduce / Tez / Spark)

The Hive Shell is therefore the **conversational gateway** that lets engineers, analysts, and data scientists talk to Hadoop using familiar SQL syntax — without ever writing low-level MapReduce Java code.

### 1.3 Why Does Hive Need a Shell?

Hadoop stores data in flat files on HDFS. Writing **Java MapReduce** programs to query this data is verbose and complex. Hive was created to provide a familiar **SQL abstraction** over Hadoop, and the Shell is the *user-facing layer* through which this abstraction is invoked.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Parameters of the Hive Shell**
> - **Default Port for Beeline connecting to HiveServer2** : `10000` (JDBC: `jdbc:hive2://<host>:10000`)
> - **Default embedded metastore database** : Apache Derby (file-based, single-user)
> - **Default warehouse directory on HDFS** : `/user/hive/warehouse`
> - **Configuration directory** : `$HIVE_HOME/conf`
> - **User initialization file** : `$HOME/.hiverc` (read automatically at shell startup)

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Position of the Hive Shell

The Hive Shell occupies the **presentation tier** of the multi-layered Hive architecture. Understanding this position is essential for KTU exam questions on "Hive components."

| Tier | Component | Role |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Presentation | **Hive Shell (CLI / Beeline)** | User interface — accepts HQL |
| 2. Service | **HiveServer2 / Thrift Server** | Compiles, optimizes, executes |
| 3. Processing | **Execution Engine** | MapReduce / Tez / Spark |
| 4. Metadata | **Metastore** | Schema, partition, column statistics |
| 5. Storage | **HDFS / S3** | Raw data files |

### 2.2 Two Operational Modes of the Hive Shell

The Hive Shell operates in **two distinct modes**, and KTU frequently tests this distinction.

#### Mode 1 : Interactive Mode (REPL)

In this mode, the shell starts, prints a prompt (`hive>` or `0: jdbc:hive2://>`), and waits for the user to type queries one at a time. Each query is compiled and executed immediately, and the result is displayed before the next prompt.

```bash
$ hive
hive> SHOW DATABASES;
hive> USE sales;
hive> SELECT * FROM orders LIMIT 5;
hive> quit;
```

#### Mode 2 : Non-Interactive (Batch) Mode

Here, the shell executes a single query (or a script of queries) and exits, without entering the REPL loop. This is critical for **automation, schedulers (Oozie, Airflow), and CI/CD pipelines**.

```bash
$ hive -e "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM sales.orders;"
$ hive -f /scripts/daily_report.hql
$ hive -S -e "SELECT * FROM users;"   # -S = Silent (suppresses OK/Time taken logs)
```

### 2.3 Categories of Commands Executable in the Hive Shell

KTU expects you to **classify HQL statements by category**:

| Category | Purpose | Example Commands |
|---|---|---|
| **DDL** (Data Definition Language) | Define / alter / drop schema | `CREATE TABLE`, `ALTER TABLE`, `DROP TABLE`, `CREATE DATABASE` |
| **DML** (Data Manipulation Language) | Load, insert, delete data | `LOAD DATA`, `INSERT INTO`, `INSERT OVERWRITE` |
| **DQL** (Data Query Language) | Retrieve data | `SELECT ... FROM ... WHERE ... GROUP BY ...` |
| **DCL** (Data Control Language) | Permissions / roles | `GRANT`, `REVOKE` |
| **Utility / Shell-specific** | Session control | `SET`, `ADD FILE`, `LIST FILES`, `DESCRIBE`, `EXPLAIN`, `SOURCE`, `!` (shell escape) |

### 2.4 Hive Variable System

The Hive Shell supports **three namespaces** for variables — a high-yield KTU topic:

| Namespace | Syntax | Source | Mutability |
|---|---|---|---|
| `hivevar` | `${hivevar:name}` | `SET hivevar:name=value;` | Read-write |
| `env` | `${env:NAME}` | Operating-system environment | Read-only |
| `system` | `${system:name}` | JVM system properties | Read-only |

**Worked Example :**

```sql
hive> SET hivevar:year=2024;
hive> SELECT * FROM sales WHERE order_year = ${hivevar:year};
```

### 2.5 The `.hiverc` Initialization File

When the Hive Shell starts, it automatically looks for a file named `.hiverc` in the user's home directory (`$HOME/.hiverc`). Every HQL command in this file is executed *before* the first interactive prompt. This is the Hive equivalent of the `.bashrc` file in Linux.

**Common uses of `.hiverc` :**
- Set default properties: `SET hive.cli.print.header=true;`
- Register custom UDF jars: `ADD JAR /opt/hive/udfs/myudf.jar;`
- Define frequently used variables: `SET hivevar:env=production;`
- Configure output formats: `SET hive.resultset.use.unique.column.names=false;`

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Command Sheet

The following table is the **single most important cheat-sheet** for KTU viva and written exams on Hive Shell:

| Command | Syntax | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Launch shell | `hive` | Start interactive REPL |
| One-shot query | `hive -e "<HQL>"` | Run a single query, then exit |
| Execute script | `hive -f <file.hql>` | Run all queries in a file |
| Silent mode | `hive -S -e "<HQL>"` | Suppress non-error logs |
| Define variable | `SET hivevar:x=10;` | Create a user variable |
| Use variable | `${hivevar:x}` | Reference a variable |
| Set property | `SET hive.exec.reducers.max=20;` | Configure session property |
| Show property | `SET -v;` | List all properties |
| Add resource | `ADD FILE /path/file.py;` | Register auxiliary file |
| Show databases | `SHOW DATABASES;` | List databases |
| Show tables | `SHOW TABLES;` | List tables in current DB |
| Describe table | `DESCRIBE FORMATTED orders;` | Full schema + metadata |
| Explain query | `EXPLAIN SELECT ...;` | Display execution plan |
| Source file | `SOURCE /path/script.hql;` | Run HQL from inside shell |
| Shell escape | `!ls -la` | Run OS command from Hive |
| JDBC connect | `beeline -u jdbc:hive2://host:10000` | Connect to HiveServer2 |
| Quit shell | `quit;` or `exit;` | Leave interactive mode |

### 2.7 Engineering Utility of the Hive Shell

> [!NOTE]
> **Where the Hive Shell is used in industry :**
> - **ETL pipelines** : cron-driven `hive -f` scripts for nightly aggregations.
> - **Ad-hoc analyst queries** : data scientists using Beeline via JDBC.
> - **BI tool integration** : Tableau, Power BI, Superset connect through Beeline.
> - **DevOps automation** : Ansible, Airflow, Oozie invoke Hive through the shell.
> - **Cloud platforms** : EMR (AWS), Dataproc (GCP), HDInsight (Azure) expose Beeline endpoints.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section derives, step by step, the **execution flow** of an HQL statement submitted through the Hive Shell. This is a high-frequency 14-mark KTU question.

### 3.1 Derivation : The Lifecycle of an HQL Query Submitted via the Hive Shell

Let us trace what happens when a user types `SELECT region, SUM(revenue) FROM sales GROUP BY region;` at the `hive>` prompt.

**Step 1 — Lexical and Syntactic Analysis (Parsing)**

The shell passes the raw text to the Hive **parser**. The parser uses **ANTLR** grammar rules to construct an **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)**. The AST is then traversed by the `SemanticAnalyzer` to resolve table names, column names, and types against the **Metastore**.

**Step 2 — Query Plan Generation (Logical Plan)**

The semantic analyzer produces a **Query Block (QB)** tree. Each `SELECT-FROM-WHERE-GROUP BY` block becomes one QB. The QB tree is the logical plan, e.g., `QB1 → QB2` for nested sub-queries.

**Step 3 — Physical Plan Generation**

The logical plan is converted to a **physical operator tree**. The optimizer applies **predicate pushdown, partition pruning, join reordering, and column pruning** transformations. This yields a DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) of physical operators such as `TableScanOperator`, `ReduceSinkOperator`, `JoinOperator`, and `GroupByOperator`.

**Step 4 — Execution Engine Compilation**

The physical DAG is handed to the configured **execution engine**. This is a critical branch point.

Let the chosen engine be denoted $E \in \{\text{MapReduce}, \text{Tez}, \text{Spark}\}$. The Hive Shell property controlling this is:

$$
\text{Property: } \texttt{hive.execution.engine} = \text{tez}
$$

**Step 5 — Job Submission to the Cluster**

The execution engine translates the DAG into one or more **Hadoop YARN jobs**. For MapReduce, this involves explicit `Map` and `Reduce` phases; for Tez, it is a single optimized DAG of vertices; for Spark, it is RDD transformations.

The shell records the total jobs submitted as $J = \{J_1, J_2, \dots, J_n\}$ and monitors each.

**Step 6 — Result Aggregation and Formatting**

Once all $n$ jobs complete, the **FetchTask** (for simple queries) or the final **Reduce phase** (for aggregations) collects the output records. The shell formats them as a **text table** by default, or as **TSV / CSV / JSON** depending on the property:

$$
\texttt{hive.resultset.output.format} \in \{\text{text}, \text{tsv}, \text{csv}, \text{json}\}
$$

**Step 7 — Display and Prompt Loop**

The shell prints the formatted table, followed by a statistics footer, and re-prints the `hive>` prompt for the next command. The wall-clock time $T$ is recorded as:

$$
T = T_{\text{compile}} + T_{\text{submit}} + T_{\text{execute}} + T_{\text{fetch}}
$$

**Summary of the derivation** :

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{HQL Text} \;&\xrightarrow{\text{Parser}} \;\text{AST} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Semantic}} \;\text{Logical Plan (QB)} \\
&\xrightarrow{\text{Optimizer}} \;\text{Physical DAG} \;\xrightarrow{E} \;\text{YARN Jobs} \\
&\xrightarrow{\text{FetchTask}} \;\text{Formatted Result} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Shell}} \;\text{Console}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix : Hive CLI vs Beeline

This is the most-asked comparison in KTU Module 3. Construct the matrix below when answering.

| Dimension | Hive CLI (`hive`) | Beeline (`beeline`) |
|---|---|---|
| **Protocol** | Direct in-process Derby JDBC | JDBC over Thrift to HiveServer2 |
| **Multi-user** | No (single user only) | Yes (concurrent clients) |
| **Authentication** | None | LDAP, Kerberos, PAM, Custom |
| **SSL/TLS** | Not supported | Supported (`ssl=true`) |
| **HiveServer dependency** | No | Mandatory |
| **Status in Hive 2.0+** | Deprecated | Recommended & actively maintained |
| **Connection string** | N/A (local) | `jdbc:hive2://host:port/db` |
| **Embedded mode** | Yes (default) | No (always client-server) |
| **Use case** | Local dev, quick tests | Production, BI tools, multi-user |
| **Prompt** | `hive>` | `0: jdbc:hive2://>` |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step : Non-Interactive Execution

**Problem :** Run the HQL below without entering the REPL.

```sql
SELECT product_category, COUNT(*) AS total_orders
FROM sales.orders
WHERE order_date >= '2024-01-01'
GROUP BY product_category;
```

**Step-by-step solution :**

1. Save the HQL to a file:
   ```bash
   $ nano /home/hadoop/queries/category_count.hql
   ```

2. Execute the script using the Hive Shell:
   ```bash
   $ hive -f /home/hadoop/queries/category_count.hql
   ```

3. To suppress all but error logs (silent mode):
   ```bash
   $ hive -S -f /home/hadoop/queries/category_count.hql
   ```

4. To embed the query directly without a file:
   ```bash
   $ hive -e "USE sales; SELECT product_category, COUNT(*) FROM orders \
       WHERE order_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY product_category;"
   ```

5. To write the output to a file:
   ```bash
   $ hive -S -e "SELECT * FROM orders;" > /tmp/output.txt
   ```

### 3.4 Step-by-Step : Working with Hive Variables

**Problem :** Parameterize a query by `start_date` and `region`.

**Step 1 — Define variables at shell start (using `-hiveconf`):**

```bash
$ hive --hiveconf start_date='2024-01-01' --hiveconf region='APAC'
```

**Step 2 — Use them in HQL:**

```sql
hive> SELECT * FROM sales.orders
    > WHERE order_date >= '${hiveconf:start_date}'
    >   AND region = '${hiveconf:region}';
```

**Step 3 — Or set them inside the shell:**

```sql
hive> SET hivevar:start_date='2024-01-01';
hive> SET hivevar:region='APAC';
hive> SELECT * FROM sales.orders
    > WHERE order_date >= '${hivevar:start_date}'
    >   AND region = '${hivevar:region}';
```

> [!TIP]
> **`hiveconf` vs `hivevar` difference :** Both syntaxes are interchangeable in `${...}`. The distinction is organizational — `hiveconf` is meant for configuration properties (e.g., `mapreduce.job.reduces`), while `hivevar` is meant for user-defined query parameters.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hive Shell — End-to-End Execution Architecture

The diagram below traces a single HQL query from the user's keystroke to the final output, passing through the parser, optimizer, execution engine, and storage layers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    User1[Data Analyst / Engineer] -->|types HQL| ShellA[Hive Shell - hive prompt]
    User2[Scheduler - Oozie / Airflow] -->|invokes -f / -e| ShellB[Hive Shell - batch mode]
    User3[BI Tool - Tableau / Superset] -->|JDBC Thrift| ShellC[Beeline Client]

    ShellA --> Parser[Parser - ANTLR Grammar]
    ShellB --> Parser
    ShellC -->|via HiveServer2| Parser

    Parser -->|Abstract Syntax Tree| Semantic[Semantic Analyzer + Metastore]
    Semantic -->|Logical Plan QB Tree| Optimizer[Query Optimizer]
    Optimizer -->|Physical DAG| Engine{Execution Engine}

    Engine --> MR[MapReduce on YARN]
    Engine --> Tez[Apache Tez on YARN]
    Engine --> Spark[Apache Spark]

    MR --> Storage[(HDFS / S3 / ADLS)]
    Tez --> Storage
    Spark --> Storage

    Storage -->|Result Records| Fetch[Fetch Task / Final Reducer]
    Fetch -->|Format| Out[Formatted Output - text/TSV/CSV]
    Out --> User1
    Out --> User2
    Out --> User3
```

### 4.2 Hive Shell Command Classification Tree

This block-level architecture diagram organizes the **hierarchy of commands** accepted by the Hive Shell — a frequent 7-mark question in KTU.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Hive Shell Commands]

    Root --> DDL[Data Definition Language - DDL]
    Root --> DML[Data Manipulation Language - DML]
    Root --> DQL[Data Query Language - DQL]
    Root --> Util[Utility Commands]
    Root --> Session[Session Control]

    DDL --> DDL1[CREATE DATABASE]
    DDL --> DDL2[CREATE TABLE]
    DDL --> DDL3[ALTER TABLE]
    DDL --> DDL4[DROP TABLE]
    DDL --> DDL5[TRUNCATE TABLE]

    DML --> DML1[LOAD DATA INPATH]
    DML --> DML2[INSERT INTO]
    DML --> DML3[INSERT OVERWRITE]
    DML --> DML4[DELETE from managed table - Hive 2.0+]

    DQL --> DQL1[SELECT with WHERE / GROUP BY / HAVING / ORDER BY]
    DQL --> DQL2[JOIN - INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL]
    DQL --> DQL3[UNION ALL]
    DQL --> DQL4[Sub-queries and CTEs]

    Util --> U1[DESCRIBE / DESCRIBE FORMATTED]
    Util --> U2[EXPLAIN / EXPLAIN EXTENDED]
    Util --> U3[SHOW DATABASES / SHOW TABLES]
    Util --> U4[ADD FILE / ADD JAR / LIST FILES]

    Session --> S1[SET - configure property]
    Session --> S2[SET hivevar:x = value]
    Session --> S3[SOURCE file.hql]
    Session --> S4[Shell escape - bang command]
    Session --> S5[QUIT / EXIT]
```

### 4.3 Hive CLI vs Beeline — Functional Block Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ClientLayer["CLIENT TIER"]
        CLI1[Legacy Hive CLI]
        BL1[Beeline JDBC Client]
    end

    subgraph ServerLayer["SERVER TIER"]
        HS1[HiveServer1 - Thrift - deprecated]
        HS2[HiveServer2 - Thrift + JDBC - active]
    end

    subgraph BackendLayer["BACKEND TIER"]
        Meta[(Metastore - Derby/MySQL/Postgres)]
        Engine[Execution Engine - Tez/Spark/MR]
        DFS[(HDFS Storage)]
    end

    CLI1 -->|Direct in-process JDBC| HS1
    BL1 -->|Remote Thrift over TCP 10000| HS2
    HS1 --> Meta
    HS2 --> Meta
    HS1 --> Engine
    HS2 --> Engine
    Engine --> DFS
```
