## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Hive Shell Questions**
> - **Do not** confuse `hivevar` with `hiveconf` — they are *syntactically interchangeable* inside `${...}` but conceptually distinct. Marks are deducted if you say "hiveconf is for user variables".
> - **Do not** list `hive -e` and `hive -f` as the *only* two modes. The full answer requires **Interactive (REPL)** vs **Non-Interactive (Batch)** as the primary classification, with `-e` and `-f` as sub-options of the latter.
> - **Do not** forget to mention the `.hiverc` file when discussing shell initialization — it is worth 1–2 marks in nearly every 14-mark answer.
> - **Beeline syntax must be quoted precisely**: `jdbc:hive2://hostname:10000` (note the *two* 2s in `hive2` and the port 10000).

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1**  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`

> **Define the Hive Shell. List any three commands that can be executed from it.**

**Model Answer :**

The **Hive Shell** is a command-line interface that allows users to submit HiveQL (HQL) statements to the Hive data warehouse system running on top of Hadoop. It serves as the textual front-end for interacting with HDFS-resident data using SQL-like syntax.

Three commonly used Hive Shell commands are:

1. `SHOW DATABASES;` — Lists all databases in the metastore.
2. `CREATE TABLE orders (id INT, amount DOUBLE);` — Defines a new managed table.
3. `LOAD DATA INPATH '/data/orders.csv' INTO TABLE orders;` — Loads external HDFS data into a Hive table.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for valid commands)*

---

#### **Question 2**  `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

> **Differentiate between `hive -e` and `hive -f`. When is `-S` used?**

**Model Answer :**

| Aspect | `hive -e "<query>"` | `hive -f <file.hql>` |
|---|---|---|
| **Input** | Query is given inline as a string | Query is read from a `.hql` file |
| **Use case** | Quick ad-hoc commands, cron jobs with short queries | Long, multi-statement production scripts |
| **Example** | `hive -e "SELECT * FROM t;"` | `hive -f /scripts/report.hql` |

The `-S` flag is the **silent mode** flag. It suppresses all non-error log output (e.g., `OK`, `Time taken`, `Map/Reduce` progress lines) and prints **only the result set**. It is preferred when the output is being redirected to a file or piped to another command for further processing.

*(3 marks — 1 each for the difference and 1 for `-S` explanation)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule :** *In Module-end ESE, every 14-mark question carries an internal choice. Two full alternative questions are provided below.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **architecture of the Hive Shell** and describe how an HQL query is processed from submission to result display. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With suitable examples, explain the **Hive variable system** (`hivevar`, `env`, `system`) and the use of the **`.hiverc`** file. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

### Part (a) — Hive Shell Architecture & Query Processing

The Hive Shell sits at the **presentation layer** of Hive's multi-tier architecture. The processing of an HQL statement proceeds through **seven logical stages**:

1. **Submission** — The user types an HQL statement at the `hive>` prompt (or submits it via `hive -e` / `hive -f`). The shell tokenizes the input.

2. **Parsing** — The **Parser** (built on ANTLR grammar) checks the syntax and produces an **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)**.

3. **Semantic Analysis** — The AST is traversed to resolve table and column names against the **Metastore**, producing a **Query Block (QB) tree** — the logical plan.

4. **Optimization** — The **Optimizer** applies transformations such as **predicate pushdown**, **partition pruning**, **join reordering**, and **column pruning**, producing a **physical operator DAG**.

5. **Execution Engine Compilation** — The DAG is converted into YARN jobs. The engine is selected by:

$$
\texttt{hive.execution.engine} \in \{\text{mapreduce}, \text{tez}, \text{spark}\}
$$

6. **Cluster Execution** — MapReduce, Tez, or Spark tasks run on the Hadoop cluster, reading/writing data in HDFS.

7. **Fetch & Display** — The `FetchTask` (or final reducer) collects results. The shell formats them according to:

$$
\texttt{hive.resultset.output.format} \in \{\text{text}, \text{tsv}, \text{csv}, \text{json}\}
$$

and prints them to the console, followed by the next `hive>` prompt.

**Execution-time equation :**

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{parse}} + T_{\text{optimize}} + T_{\text{compile}} + T_{\text{execute}} + T_{\text{fetch}}
$$

*(7 marks — 1 mark for shell position, 5 marks for the 7 stages with equations, 1 mark for the timing equation)*

---

### Part (b) — Hive Variables and `.hiverc`

The Hive Shell supports three variable namespaces:

| Namespace | Declared With | Read From | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| `hivevar` | `SET hivevar:x=10;` | `${hivevar:x}` | `SET hivevar:year=2024;` |
| `env` | OS environment | `${env:NAME}` | `echo $HADOOP_HOME` then `${env:HADOOP_HOME}` |
| `system` | JVM system property | `${system:name}` | `${system:user.name}` |

**Worked Example :**

```sql
hive> SET hivevar:threshold=1000;
hive> SELECT product, revenue FROM sales
    > WHERE revenue > ${hivevar:threshold};
```

**The `.hiverc` File :**

- **Location** : `$HOME/.hiverc`
- **When read** : Automatically executed at Hive Shell startup, *before* the first prompt.
- **Typical contents :**

```sql
-- $HOME/.hiverc
SET hive.cli.print.header=true;
SET hive.resultset.output.format=tsv;
ADD JAR /opt/hive/udfs/date_utils.jar;
SET hivevar:default_db=sales;
```

This file is the Hive equivalent of `.bashrc` and is essential for setting per-user defaults, registering custom UDFs, and defining reusable variables.

*(7 marks — 3 marks for variables with example, 2 marks for `.hiverc` location and behavior, 2 marks for a sample `.hiverc`)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**  `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 1]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Hive CLI and Beeline** with respect to protocol, multi-user support, security, and current usage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **interactive and non-interactive modes** of the Hive Shell with examples. Discuss the role of **`.hiverc`** in shell initialization. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

### Part (a) — Hive CLI vs Beeline Comparison

| Dimension | Hive CLI | Beeline |
|---|---|---|
| **Full command** | `$ hive` | `$ beeline` |
| **Connection** | Direct in-process Derby/Thrift | Remote JDBC over Thrift to HiveServer2 |
| **Multi-user** | Single user only (uses embedded Derby) | Multiple concurrent users |
| **Authentication** | None | LDAP, Kerberos, PAM, Custom |
| **SSL/TLS** | Not supported | Supported via `ssl=true;sslTrustStore=...` |
| **Status** | Deprecated since Hive 2.0 | Actively maintained; recommended |
| **Prompt** | `hive>` | `0: jdbc:hive2://>` |
| **Connection string** | N/A — local | `jdbc:hive2://<host>:10000` |
| **Default port** | N/A | `10000` |
| **Best use** | Quick local development, tutorials | Production BI, ETL, multi-tenant setups |

**Key takeaway :** Beeline is the **modern, secure, multi-user** replacement for Hive CLI. All KTU recommended setups use Beeline + HiveServer2.

*(7 marks — 1 mark per important row, 1 mark for the final summary statement)*

---

### Part (b) — Interactive and Non-Interactive Modes + `.hiverc`

**Interactive Mode (REPL) :**

```bash
$ hive
hive> SHOW DATABASES;
hive> USE sales;
hive> SELECT * FROM orders LIMIT 5;
hive> DESCRIBE FORMATTED orders;
hive> quit;
```

The shell starts a Read-Eval-Print-Loop. Each statement is compiled and executed immediately, and the result is displayed before the next prompt.

**Non-Interactive Mode (Batch) :**

```bash
$ hive -e "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM sales.orders;"
$ hive -f /scripts/daily_report.hql
$ hive -S -e "SELECT * FROM users;" > /tmp/users.tsv
```

The shell runs the query (or script) and **exits without entering the REPL**. This mode is used by:
- **Oozie, Airflow, Cron** — for scheduled ETL.
- **CI/CD pipelines** — for automated data validation.
- **Shell pipelines** — when output is piped to `awk`, `grep`, or files.

**Combining both** : Use `hive -i <file.hql>` to run initialization commands in non-interactive mode and stay in the REPL afterward:

```bash
$ hive -i /opt/hive/init.hql
hive> SELECT * FROM orders;   # now available because init.hql set defaults
```

**Role of `.hiverc` in Initialization :**

When the Hive Shell starts (in either mode), it:
1. Locates `$HOME/.hiverc`.
2. Executes every HQL command in it sequentially.
3. Only then displays the first prompt or processes the `-e` / `-f` input.

**Sample `.hiverc` :**

```sql
SET hive.cli.print.header=true;
SET hive.resultset.output.format=tsv;
SET hive.execution.engine=tez;
SET hivevar:env=production;
ADD JAR /opt/udfs/geo_decoder.jar;
CREATE TEMPORARY FUNCTION geo_decode AS 'com.company.udfs.GeoDecoder';
```

This is the Hive equivalent of `~/.bashrc` and ensures consistent, per-user defaults across every session.

*(7 marks — 3 marks for interactive mode with example, 2 marks for non-interactive with `hive -e` and `hive -f`, 2 marks for `.hiverc`)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Hive Shell (Module 3)**

- **Definition** : Hive Shell is the CLI (CLI = Command-Line Interface) used to submit HiveQL (HQL) to the Hive data warehouse engine on Hadoop.
- **Two variants** : `hive` (legacy CLI, deprecated) and `beeline` (modern JDBC client, recommended).
- **Beeline default port** : `10000` — connection string `jdbc:hive2://<host>:10000`.
- **Two operational modes** : **Interactive** (REPL with `hive>` prompt) and **Non-Interactive** (one-shot via `hive -e` or script via `hive -f`).
- **Silent mode flag** : `-S` suppresses all non-error logs — ideal for piping output.
- **Variable namespaces** : `hivevar` (read-write user vars), `env` (OS env vars, read-only), `system` (JVM properties, read-only).
- **Variable syntax** : `${hivevar:name}`, `${env:NAME}`, `${system:name}`.
- **`SET` command** : Used to define variables and configure session properties (e.g., `SET hive.execution.engine=tez;`).
- **`.hiverc` file** : Auto-executed initialization file at `$HOME/.hiverc` — used for default settings, UDF registration, and per-user variables.
- **Command categories** : DDL, DML, DQL, DCL, and Utility/Session commands — all executable from the shell.
- **Utility commands** : `DESCRIBE FORMATTED`, `EXPLAIN`, `SHOW DATABASES/TABLES`, `ADD FILE/JAR`, `SOURCE`, `!` (OS escape).
- **Execution engine property** : `hive.execution.engine` — values `mapreduce`, `tez`, or `spark`.
- **Output format property** : `hive.resultset.output.format` — values `text`, `tsv`, `csv`, `json`.
- **Architecture position** : Shell is at the **presentation layer**, above HiveServer2, the execution engine, the metastore, and HDFS.
- **Production use** : In real-world deployments, Beeline + HiveServer2 + Tez is the standard stack.
- **KTU mnemonic** : *"HIVE Shell = Hadoop Interface for SQL via Execution Layer"*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hive Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT746 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hive Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hive Services** refer to the suite of client-side and server-side components in the Apache Hive architecture that enable users to submit, compile, optimize, and execute SQL-like queries (HQL) against distributed data stored in HDFS or compatible storage systems. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECNT746)**, Hive Services form the bridge between end-user query submission and the underlying Hadoop/Yarn execution layer.

The primary services in the Hive ecosystem are:

- **HiveServer2 (HS2)** — The principal server-side daemon that accepts client connections, handles authentication, manages sessions, and orchestrates query execution.
- **Beeline** — A lightweight, command-line SQL client used to connect to HiveServer2.
- **Hive Metastore Service (HMS)** — A centralized service that stores table definitions, partition information, column types, and storage locations.
- **Thrift Service** — A high-performance cross-language service framework that allows Hive to expose its query interface to multiple programming languages.
- **Hive CLI (legacy)** — The original shell-based interface, now deprecated in favor of Beeline.
- **HCatalog / WebHCat** — Table and storage management layer for Hadoop.
- **Hive Web Interface (HWI)** — A web-based GUI for query execution (largely deprecated).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 of Big Data Analytics explicitly focuses on **Hive Architecture, Hive Services, HiveQL, and the comparison between Hive and traditional RDBMS**. The most frequently tested services in KTU University Exams are **HiveServer2, Beeline, and Metastore**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **large, multi-lingual library** where books (data) are stored across many floors and wings (distributed HDFS nodes). You, the user, want to find every book written by a specific author between specific years. You cannot wander the aisles yourself.

- **HiveServer2** is the **chief librarian** sitting at the front desk. It accepts your request, checks your library card (authentication), logs your visit (session management), and assigns an assistant to handle your request.
- **Beeline** is the **printed form** you fill out at the desk to submit your query in plain English-like terms.
- **The Hive Metastore** is the **master catalogue** — a notebook that tells the librarian exactly *where* every book, shelf, and section is located.
- **The Driver, Compiler, and Optimizer** are the **assistants** who translate your request into specific instructions, optimize the search path, and then dispatch runners to fetch the books.
- **The Execution Engine (MapReduce / Tez / Spark)** is the **team of runners** who actually go fetch the books from the shelves.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world parallel:** Just as a librarian cannot memorize the entire catalogue, HiveServer2 does not store data — it merely *orchestrates* the operations on data. The **Metastore** is the catalogue; **HDFS** is the bookshelf.

## 1.3 Core Metrics and Standard Parameters

| Parameter | Standard Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Default HS2 Port | **10000** | TCP port for client connections |
| Web UI Port | **10002** | HS2 Web Interface |
| Metastore DB Port | **9083** | Thrift port for Metastore Service |
| Default Query Timeout | **600 seconds** | Maximum execution time |
| Max Open Connections | **100 (default)** | Configurable in `hive-site.xml` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Port Numbers are High-Yield KTU Questions:** Memorize **10000 (HS2)**, **10002 (HS2 Web UI)**, and **9083 (Metastore)**. KTU frequently asks to match services with their default ports.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 HiveServer2 (HS2) — The Primary Service

### 2.1.1 Definition and Role

**HiveServer2** is a server interface that enables remote clients to execute queries against Hive. It is a **rewrite of the original HiveServer** (HiveServer1) and is built on **Apache Thrift**, allowing multi-client concurrency and improved authentication.

### 2.1.2 Why HiveServer2 Replaced HiveServer1

| Feature | HiveServer1 (Legacy) | HiveServer2 (Current) |
|---|---|---|
| Concurrency | Single client at a time | **Multiple concurrent clients** |
| Authentication | None / weak | **Kerberos, LDAP, PAM, custom** |
| Thrift API | Limited | **Full Thrift + JDBC + ODBC** |
| Multi-Client | Not supported | **Supported** |
| Session Management | None | **Per-client sessions** |

### 2.1.3 HiveServer2 Internal Architecture — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Client Connection Initiation:** A client (Beeline, JDBC, ODBC) opens a TCP connection to port **10000**.
2. **Authentication Handshake:** HS2 verifies credentials using configured auth modes (e.g., `NOSASL`, `KERBEROS`, `LDAP`, `PAM`, `CUSTOM`).
3. **Session Creation:** A new session object is allocated, holding session-level parameters, variables, and lock information.
4. **Query Reception:** The HQL query string is received and forwarded to the **Hive Driver**.
5. **Driver Invocation:** The Driver instantiates the **Compiler**, **Optimizer**, and **Execution Engine**.
6. **Result Streaming:** Partial query results are streamed back to the client via the open session.
7. **Session Termination:** On client disconnect, session resources are released.

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Point:** The phrase "**open session / open connection model**" is specific to HiveServer2. The original HiveServer used a "**single connection, no session**" model. This is a common KTU MCQ distractor.

---

## 2.2 Beeline — The Modern CLI Client

### 2.2.1 Definition

**Beeline** is a **JDBC client** based on the SQLLine CLI. It is the official replacement for the deprecated **Hive CLI** in Hive 0.14+ and is the recommended way to issue HQL queries in KTU-lab environments.

### 2.2.2 Connection Modes

**Mode 1: Embedded Mode (Local)**
- Beeline connects to an in-process HiveServer2.
- No port required.
- Command: `!connect jdbc:hive2://`

**Mode 2: Remote Mode (Production)**
- Connects to a separately running HiveServer2.
- Requires host and port.
- Command:
```bash
!connect jdbc:hive2://<hostname>:10000
```

### 2.2.3 Beeline Command Set (High-Yield for KTU Labs)

| Command | Function |
|---|---|
| `!set` | View/Set Beeline configuration |
| `!connect` | Establish JDBC connection |
| `!sql <query>` | Execute SQL/HQL statement |
| `!quit` / `!exit` | Terminate Beeline session |
| `!dbinfo` | Show database metadata |
| `!history` | Show command history |
| `!verbose` | Toggle verbose mode |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Beeline vs. Hive CLI:** In Beeline, every HQL statement must be **terminated with a semicolon `;`** and Beeline does **NOT support `dfs`, `add`, `list` commands** directly — these require the `!` prefix (e.g., `!sh ls /`).

---

## 2.3 Hive Metastore Service (HMS)

### 2.3.1 Definition

The **Metastore** is the **central repository of metadata** for Hive. It stores:
- Table definitions (column names, types, comments)
- Partition information
- Bucketing information
- Storage location URIs
- SerDe (Serializer/Deserializer) information

### 2.3.2 Metastore Configuration Modes

**Mode 1: Embedded Metastore (Default, Local)**
- Uses embedded Derby database.
- Single-session only.
- Suitable for unit testing.

**Mode 2: Local Metastore**
- Metastore runs in the same JVM as HiveServer2.
- Supports multiple sessions.
- Uses external RDBMS (MySQL, PostgreSQL).

**Mode 3: Remote Metastore (Production)**
- Metastore runs as a separate Thrift service on port **9083**.
- Most scalable; supports many HS2 instances pointing to one HMS.
- **KTU-emphasized architecture for enterprise deployments.**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** "Why is Remote Metastore preferred in production?" → Because it supports **horizontal scaling of HiveServer2 instances** sharing a single metadata source, enabling **load balancing and high availability**.

### 2.3.3 Metastore Storage Architecture

The Metastore schema is composed of key tables such as:
- `DBS` — Database information
- `TBLS` — Table metadata
- `COLUMNS_V2` — Column definitions
- `PARTITIONS` — Partition data
- `SDS` — Storage Descriptor (location, SerDe, format)

---

## 2.4 Thrift Service

**Apache Thrift** is a software framework for cross-language service development. Hive exposes its services via Thrift, allowing clients written in **Java, Python, C++, PHP, Ruby, etc.** to communicate with HiveServer2.

- **Thrift IDL (Interface Definition Language)** defines the contract.
- **Thrift Compiler** generates client/server stubs.
- Supports **binary, JSON, and compact protocols** for serialization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Thrift is the underlying RPC mechanism for **HiveServer2 (port 10000)** and **Metastore (port 9083)**. Both are Thrift-based services.

---

## 2.5 HCatalog and WebHCat

- **HCatalog:** A table and storage management service for Hadoop that enables users to read/write data in Hive table format from **Pig, MapReduce, and Spark** without worrying about schema.
- **WebHCat:** A RESTful API server for HCatalog, providing HTTP endpoints to run Hive, Pig, and MapReduce jobs.

> [!NOTE]
> WebHCat has been **deprecated** in newer Hive versions in favor of **Hive LLAP (Live Long and Process)**. KTU 2024 syllabus may still include this for conceptual coverage.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula / Configuration Cheat Sheet

| Service | Default Port | Protocol | Authentication Methods |
|---|---|---|---|
| HiveServer2 | **10000** | Thrift over TCP | Kerberos, LDAP, PAM, NOSASL, CUSTOM |
| HS2 Web UI | **10002** | HTTP | Web-based |
| Metastore (Remote) | **9083** | Thrift over TCP | Kerberos, LDAP, NOSASL |
| WebHCat | **50111** | HTTP/REST | Kerberos, PAM |

**Configuration File: `hive-site.xml`** — All Hive service parameters are configured here.

Key properties:
- `hive.server2.thrift.port` = 10000
- `hive.server2.thrift.bind.host` = 0.0.0.0
- `hive.metastore.uris` = thrift://host:9083
- `hive.server2.authentication` = NOSASL
- `javax.jdo.option.ConnectionURL` = jdbc:mysql://host:3306/metastore

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering

In production big data environments (e.g., **Cloudera, Hortonworks, EMR**):
- **HiveServer2** clusters behind a load balancer (HAProxy, Nginx) for high availability.
- **Metastore** runs on dedicated RDBMS (MySQL HA cluster or AWS RDS) for durability.
- **Beeline** is used by data engineers for ad-hoc analysis; **JDBC/ODBC drivers** are used by BI tools (Tableau, PowerBI, Superset).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 End-to-End Query Execution Flow Through Hive Services

The KTU 2024 Scheme specifically tests the **end-to-end query flow** through Hive services. Below is the exhaustive, step-by-step analytical trace of what happens when a user executes an HQL query.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Reminder:** A 14-mark KTU question often asks: *"Explain the architecture of Hive with a neat diagram. Describe the role of each component in query processing."* The derivation below is your model answer.

### Step 0 — User Initiates Query
The user opens Beeline and types:
```sql
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM employee WHERE dept='CS';
```
**Logical state:** Query string in client buffer.

---

### Step 1 — Beeline Establishes JDBC Connection
Beeline uses the **JDBC driver `org.apache.hive.jdbc.HiveDriver`** to open a connection string:
```
jdbc:hive2://localhost:10000/default
```
**Logical state:** TCP socket open to HS2 on port 10000.

---

### Step 2 — HiveServer2 Authenticates the Client
HS2 reads `hive.server2.authentication` from `hive-site.xml`. Suppose it is `KERBEROS`:
- HS2 sends a Kerberos challenge.
- Client presents a TGT (Ticket Granting Ticket) and service ticket.
- HS2 validates against the KDC.
**Logical state:** Authenticated session created with a unique `SessionHandle`.

---

### Step 3 — Driver Receives Query
The HS2 executor service routes the query to the **Hive Driver**, which is the central control object.

---

### Step 4 — Compiler Parses and Plans
The **Compiler (org.apache.hadoop.hive.ql.parse)` parses the query in stages:

- **Step 4.1:** Lexical analysis (tokens).
- **Step 4.2:** Syntax analysis → **Parse Tree** (AST).
- **Step 4.3:** Semantic analysis → resolved against **Metastore** via Thrift call to port 9083.
- **Step 4.4:** Logical Plan generation → **Operator Graph**.
- **Step 4.5:** Conversion to **Physical Plan** (MapReduce jobs / Tez DAG / Spark job).
- **Step 4.6:** Optimization passes (predicate pushdown, partition pruning, join reordering).

---

### Step 5 — Optimizer Refines the Plan
The **Optimizer** applies transformations:

$$\text{Optimized DAG} = \text{Optimizer}(\text{Physical Plan})$$

Common optimizations:
- **Predicate Pushdown:** filters pushed to scan operators.
- **Partition Pruning:** unnecessary partitions skipped.
- **Join Reordering:** smaller tables streamed first.

---

### Step 6 — Execution Engine Submits Job
The **Execution Engine** converts the physical plan into a **DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph)** and submits to:
- **MapReduce** (legacy, via JobClient to YARN ResourceManager).
- **Tez** (default in Hive 0.13+, runs Tez DAGs on YARN).
- **Spark** (Hive-on-Spark).

---

### Step 7 — YARN Allocates Containers
The YARN ResourceManager (RM) negotiates with NodeManagers (NM) to launch **ApplicationMaster (AM)** containers, which then launch task containers for execution.

---

### Step 8 — HDFS Read Operations
Tasks read source data from HDFS. Each split is processed by a **mapper**, intermediate results shuffled, and reducers aggregate.

---

### Step 9 — Result Aggregation and Streaming
The driver collects results from reducers and HS2 streams them back to the Beeline client through the open Thrift session.

---

### Step 10 — Output to User
Beeline displays the result:
```
_c0
120
```

---

## 3.2 Mathematical Model of Query Throughput

KTU may ask to compute the **query throughput** of a Hive deployment. The standard model:

$$
T = \frac{N_{\text{queries}}}{t_{\text{total}}}
$$

Where:
- $T$ = Throughput in queries per second
- $N_{\text{queries}}$ = Number of queries executed
- $t_{\text{total}}$ = Total wall-clock time

**Average query latency** is given by:

$$
L = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{t_{\text{total}}}{N_{\text{queries}}}
$$

**Concurrency-aware throughput** when $C$ concurrent clients are supported:

$$
T_{\text{effective}} = \frac{N_{\text{queries}} \cdot C}{t_{\text{total}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Application:** A Hive cluster supports 50 concurrent clients and processes 3000 queries in 10 minutes. Compute effective throughput.
>
> **Solution:**
> $$
> T_{\text{effective}} = \frac{3000 \cdot 50}{600 \text{ s}} = 250 \text{ query-client-pairs/sec}
> $$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Hive CLI vs. Beeline

| Property | Hive CLI (Deprecated) | Beeline (Current) |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | Direct connection to Driver | Connects to HiveServer2 |
| Concurrency | None | Yes (via HS2) |
| Multi-user | No | Yes |
| Connection Type | In-process | JDBC over Thrift |
| Semantic | HQL + DFS commands | Pure HQL (with `!` shell) |
| Security | None | Full authentication |
| Status | **Removed in Hive 3.0+** | **Active, supported** |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU often frames the question: *"Why is Beeline preferred over Hive CLI in production?"* Your answer **must** mention: (1) multi-client concurrency, (2) authentication, (3) session isolation, and (4) JDBC/ODBC compatibility with BI tools.

---

## 3.4 Architecture Mapping: Which Service Does What

| Service | Responsibility | Does NOT Do |
|---|---|---|
| **Beeline** | Submit HQL, display results | Store data, manage metadata |
| **HiveServer2** | Authenticate, manage sessions, orchestrate execution | Store data |
| **Metastore** | Store schema, partition info | Execute queries |
| **Compiler** | Parse HQL, generate plans | Store metadata |
| **Execution Engine** | Run jobs on YARN/MapReduce/Tez/Spark | Define schema |
| **HDFS** | Store raw data | Provide SQL interface |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hive Architecture and Service Interaction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Client1["Beeline CLI"]:::client
    Client2["JDBC Application"]:::client
    Client3["ODBC Tool Tableau"]:::client
    Client4["Thrift API Python"]:::client

    HS2["HiveServer2 Service Port 10000"]:::server
    Auth["Authentication Module Kerberos or LDAP"]:::module
    Session["Session Manager"]:::module
    Driver["Hive Driver"]:::module

    Compiler["Compiler Parse and Plan"]:::module
    Optimizer["Optimizer Predicate Pushdown"]:::module
    Exec["Execution Engine Tez or Spark or MapReduce"]:::module

    Meta["Metastore Service Port 9083"]:::metastore
    MetaDB[("MySQL or PostgreSQL Metadata DB")]:::db

    YARN["YARN ResourceManager"]:::yarn
    NM1["NodeManager 1"]:::yarn
    NM2["NodeManager 2"]:::yarn
    HDFS["HDFS DataNodes"]:::storage

    Client1 --> HS2
    Client2 --> HS2
    Client3 --> HS2
    Client4 --> HS2

    HS2 --> Auth
    HS2 --> Session
    HS2 --> Driver
    Driver --> Compiler
    Compiler --> Meta
    Meta --> MetaDB
    Compiler --> Optimizer
    Optimizer --> Exec
    Exec --> YARN
    YARN --> NM1
    YARN --> NM2
    NM1 --> HDFS
    NM2 --> HDFS

    classDef client fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```
