<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the 3V Model of Big Data with Examples
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The 3V Model, proposed by Doug Laney (2001), characterizes big data through three dimensions:

1. **Volume:** The size of data. Traditional systems handle gigabytes; big data systems handle **terabytes to petabytes**. Example: Facebook generates **4+ petabytes of new data per day**.

2. **Velocity:** The speed at which data is generated and processed. Traditional systems use batch processing; big data systems support **real-time streaming**. Example: Stock trading platforms process **millions of transactions per second**.

3. **Variety:** The diversity of data formats. Traditional systems handle **structured** data (tables); big data systems handle **structured, semi-structured, and unstructured** data. Example: Twitter feed contains text, images, video, hashtags, and metadata.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Award 1 mark per V with a concrete example. Avoid vague definitions.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate ACID and BASE Properties
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Exam]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | ACID (Traditional RDBMS) | BASE (NoSQL/Big Data) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Full Form** | Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability | Basic Availability, Soft-state, Eventual consistency |
| **Consistency** | Strong (immediate) | Eventual (convergent) |
| **Transaction** | Multi-row ACID supported | Limited or single-document atomicity |
| **Use Case** | Banking, financial systems | Social media, IoT, web-scale apps |
| **Trade-off** | Sacrifices availability for correctness | Sacrifices strict consistency for availability |

ACID is used in **RDBMS** (Oracle, MySQL). BASE is used in **NoSQL** systems (Cassandra, DynamoDB).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Sample Paper Module 3]`

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the comparison between Traditional RDBMS and Big Data Systems across at least **8 dimensions**. Provide one real-world example for each system. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A Traditional RDBMS is a vertically-scalable, schema-bound system designed for structured transactional workloads. A Big Data System is a horizontally-scalable, distributed system designed for massive, heterogeneous, high-velocity data. The detailed comparison:

**1. Data Volume:** RDBMS handles **GB to TB**; Big Data handles **TB to PB**. Example: MySQL for a university ERP vs. Hadoop for Google's web crawl.

**2. Data Variety:** RDBMS handles **structured** data only; Big Data handles **structured, semi-structured (JSON, XML), and unstructured (images, video, logs)**. Example: PostgreSQL for inventory vs. MongoDB for product catalogs.

**3. Data Velocity:** RDBMS uses **batch OLTP**; Big Data uses **batch + stream + real-time**. Example: SQL Server for billing vs. Kafka + Spark Streaming for fraud detection.

**4. Schema:** RDBMS uses **Schema-on-Write** (schema defined before loading); Big Data uses **Schema-on-Read** (schema applied at query time). Example: Oracle requires `CREATE TABLE` first; Hive applies schema when query is run.

**5. Scaling:** RDBMS uses **Vertical Scaling (scale-up)** — add CPU/RAM/disk to one machine. Big Data uses **Horizontal Scaling (scale-out)** — add more commodity nodes. Example: Upgrading to a bigger Oracle Exadata vs. adding nodes to HDFS.

**6. Consistency Model:** RDBMS guarantees **ACID** (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability). Big Data adopts **BASE** (Basic Availability, Soft-state, Eventual consistency). Example: Bank transfers in Oracle vs. social media likes in Cassandra.

**7. CAP Theorem Position:** RDBMS achieves **CA** (single-node). Big Data systems choose **AP** (Cassandra) or **CP** (HBase). Example: PostgreSQL (CA) vs. Cassandra (AP).

**8. Hardware & Cost:** RDBMS requires **expensive enterprise servers**; Big Data uses **commodity hardware clusters**. Example: Oracle Exadata costs ~\$1M vs. 10-node Hadoop cluster for \$50K.

**9. Query Processing:** RDBMS uses **SQL** with cost-based optimizers; Big Data uses **HQL, Pig Latin, Spark SQL** with distributed execution engines.

**10. Fault Tolerance:** RDBMS relies on **RAID + backup**; Big Data uses **built-in replication (RF=3)** across nodes.

**Conclusion:** Traditional RDBMS excels at transactional integrity for structured data; Big Data Systems excel at scale and flexibility for heterogeneous data. Modern architectures often combine both (polyglot persistence).

---

**Q. (b)** With neat diagrams, explain the **CAP Theorem**. Discuss how Traditional RDBMS and Big Data Systems position themselves with respect to CAP. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The CAP Theorem, formulated by Eric Brewer (2000) and later proven by Gilbert and Lynch (2002), states that in a distributed data store, only **two of the following three guarantees** can be simultaneously achieved in the presence of a network partition:

$$ C \cap A \cap P \leq 2 $$

where:
- **C — Consistency:** All nodes see the same data at the same time. A read returns the most recent successful write.
- **A — Availability:** Every request receives a response (non-error), even if some nodes have failed.
- **P — Partition Tolerance:** The system continues to operate despite arbitrary message loss between nodes.

**CAP Triangle Diagram (Textual Representation):**

```
                    Consistency (C)
                          /\
                         /  \
                        /    \
                       /      \
                      /        \
                     /___CAP____\
                    /            \
                   /              \
                  /                \
                 /                  \
        Partition (P) ----------- Availability (A)
```

**CAP Positions of Systems:**

**1. Traditional RDBMS (CA — Consistency + Availability):**
Traditional RDBMS like Oracle, MySQL, and PostgreSQL run on a **single server**, so they are not affected by network partitions (P is trivially satisfied). They prioritize **strong consistency (C)** and **availability (A)** within that single node. However, they cannot scale horizontally without sacrificing this guarantee.

**2. Big Data Systems:**

- **AP Systems (Availability + Partition tolerance):** Systems like **Cassandra, DynamoDB, and CouchDB** sacrifice strict consistency for availability. They adopt **eventual consistency** — all replicas will converge to the same value given enough time, but reads may return stale data immediately after a write. Use case: social media feeds, shopping cart, IoT sensor data.

- **CP Systems (Consistency + Partition tolerance):** Systems like **HBase, MongoDB (with majority write concern), and Redis Cluster** sacrifice availability for consistency. During a partition, the system will reject reads or writes rather than return inconsistent data. Use case: inventory management, financial ledgers, distributed locks.

**Conclusion:**
The CAP Theorem forces architects to make a deliberate trade-off. Traditional RDBMS occupy the CA quadrant at the cost of horizontal scalability. Big Data Systems occupy AP or CP quadrants based on application requirements. Understanding CAP is essential for designing distributed systems that meet business SLAs.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]`

**Q. (a)** Explain **Schema-on-Write vs Schema-on-Read** with examples. Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of each approach. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Schema-on-Write (Traditional RDBMS Approach):**

In Schema-on-Write, the schema is defined **before** data is loaded into the database. Data must conform to the predefined schema; non-conforming records are rejected at ingestion time.

**Process:**
1. Database administrator defines tables, columns, data types, constraints (DDL).
2. Data is loaded using `INSERT` statements.
3. Schema validation occurs at write time.
4. Reads are fast because data is already typed and indexed.

**Example:** Creating a table in MySQL:

```
CREATE TABLE Students (
    ID INT PRIMARY KEY,
    Name VARCHAR(50),
    GPA FLOAT
);
```

Only records matching this exact schema are accepted. A record with an extra field is rejected.

**Advantages:**
- Strong data quality and integrity at ingestion.
- Faster query execution (data is pre-validated).
- Ideal for OLTP transactional workloads.
- Easier integration with BI tools that expect structured schemas.

**Disadvantages:**
- Rigid schema changes require costly migrations (ALTER TABLE).
- Cannot accommodate evolving or semi-structured data.
- Schema design is a bottleneck for agile development.
- Slow ingestion for high-velocity streams.

---

**Schema-on-Read (Big Data Approach):**

In Schema-on-Read, raw data is dumped into the storage layer **without** a predefined schema. The schema is applied **at query time** when the data is read.

**Process:**
1. Raw data (JSON, CSV, logs, images) is ingested into HDFS, S3, or a data lake.
2. No validation at write time.
3. Schema is defined in the query (e.g., Hive DDL, Spark StructType).
4. Parsing and transformation happen during query execution.

**Example:** Loading raw tweets into HDFS and querying with Hive:

```
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE tweets (
    user_id STRING,
    text STRING,
    created_at TIMESTAMP
) ROW FORMAT DELIMITED FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','
LOCATION '/raw/tweets/';
```

The schema is enforced only when Hive reads the file.

**Advantages:**
- Maximum flexibility for evolving data structures.
- Faster ingestion (no validation overhead).
- Ideal for exploratory data analysis and data lakes.
- Supports semi-structured and unstructured formats natively.

**Disadvantages:**
- Query-time parsing increases read latency.
- Risk of dirty data propagating to analytics.
- Errors surface late, at query time, not at ingestion.
- Requires careful data governance.

**Conclusion:** Schema-on-Write is best for transactional, structured workloads; Schema-on-Read is best for analytical, exploratory workloads on heterogeneous data. Modern data warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery) blend both paradigms.

---

**Q. (b)** With a neat diagram, explain **Vertical Scaling and Horizontal Scaling**. Derive the relationship between cluster size and throughput, including the coordination overhead factor. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Vertical Scaling (Scale-Up):**

Vertical scaling increases the capacity of a **single server** by adding more CPU, RAM, or storage. This is the traditional approach used by RDBMS like Oracle Exadata.

```
    Small → Medium → Large → Massive
    8GB     64GB     512GB    4TB
     |        |        |        |
    Server  Server   Server   Server
```

**Advantages:** Simpler architecture, no data distribution, strong ACID compliance.
**Disadvantages:** Hardware cost grows super-linearly (Moore's Law hit ceiling), single point of failure, hard limit at ~4 TB RAM.

**Horizontal Scaling (Scale-Out):**

Horizontal scaling adds **more commodity nodes** to the cluster. This is the big data approach used by Hadoop, Cassandra, and Spark.

```
       Node 1  —  Node 2
         \         /
          \       /
        Node 3  —  Node 4
              |
            Node N (thousands)
```

**Advantages:** Near-linear capacity growth, fault tolerance via replication, cost-effective (commodity hardware).
**Disadvantages:** Coordination overhead, eventual consistency, complex distributed algorithms.

**Throughput Derivation:**

For an ideal horizontal system with $n$ homogeneous nodes, each contributing throughput $T_0$:

$$ T_{ideal}(n) = n \cdot T_0 $$

In reality, nodes spend time coordinating (e.g., shuffle phase in MapReduce, gossip protocol in Cassandra). Let $\alpha$ be the coordination overhead coefficient:

$$ T_{real}(n) = \frac{n \cdot T_0}{1 + \alpha \cdot \log_2(n)} $$

**Numerical Example:** For a Spark cluster with $T_0 = 100$ MB/s, $n = 64$ nodes, $\alpha = 0.1$:

$$ T_{real}(64) = \frac{64 \times 100}{1 + 0.1 \times \log_2(64)} = \frac{6400}{1 + 0.6} = \frac{6400}{1.6} = 4000 \text{ MB/s} $$

Compared to ideal: $T_{ideal} = 6400$ MB/s. Overhead = **37.5%**.

**Conclusion:** Horizontal scaling is economically and operationally superior for big data workloads, but designers must account for coordination overhead using the formula above.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Comparison Questions:**
>
> 1. **Mere Bullet Lists Without Explanation (–3 marks):** Listing dimensions without explaining *why* the difference matters. Always explain the **reason** behind each comparison.
>
> 2. **Confusing ACID with CAP (–2 marks):** ACID is a **transaction** model; CAP is a **distributed system** model. Do not equate them. ACID applies within a single transaction; CAP applies across distributed nodes.
>
> 3. **Missing Diagrams (–2 marks):** Any 7-mark sub-part on CAP or Scaling **must** include a diagram. Examiners specifically allocate marks for visual representation.
>
> 4. **No Real-World Examples (–1 to –2 marks):** KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes **application-oriented learning**. Always cite at least one tool example per dimension.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the "Why" (–2 marks):** Don't just say "Big Data uses horizontal scaling." Explain *why* — because vertical scaling hits physical and economic limits (Moore's Law ceiling, super-linear cost growth).
>
> 6. **Schema-on-Write vs Schema-on-Read Confusion (–2 marks):** Remember: Write-time validation vs Read-time parsing. Many students invert these.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 3: Comparison with Traditional Databases**

- ✅ Traditional RDBMS = **Schema-on-Write + Vertical Scaling + ACID + SQL**.
- ✅ Big Data Systems = **Schema-on-Read + Horizontal Scaling + BASE + Distributed Compute**.
- ✅ The **3V Model**: Volume, Velocity, Variety — at least 2 must exceed threshold for big data.
- ✅ **CAP Theorem**: $C \cap A \cap P \leq 2$ — choose 2 of 3 under partition.
- ✅ Traditional RDBMS = **CA** (single-node). Big Data = **AP** (Cassandra) or **CP** (HBase).
- ✅ **ACID** = Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability. **BASE** = Basic Availability, Soft-state, Eventual consistency.
- ✅ **Vertical scaling** has super-linear cost: $TCO_{trad} \propto V^{1.8}$.
- ✅ **Horizontal scaling** is near-linear: $TCO_{big} \propto V^{1.05}$.
- ✅ Crossover volume $V^* \approx 100$ GB — beyond this, big data is cheaper.
- ✅ Real cluster throughput: $T_{real}(n) = \frac{n T_0}{1 + \alpha \log_2 n}$.
- ✅ Eventual consistency convergence: $t_{EC} \approx D \cdot t_{hop} + \frac{S_{update}(R-1)}{B_{link}}$.
- ✅ **Sharding cardinality**: $K = \lceil N_{records} / S_{shard} \rceil$.
- ✅ Traditional DB examples: Oracle, MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQL Server.
- ✅ Big Data examples: HBase, Cassandra, MongoDB, DynamoDB, Hive.
- ✅ Best use case for RDBMS: Banking, ERP, Inventory.
- ✅ Best use case for Big Data: Social media, IoT, log analytics, search engines.
- ✅ **Polyglot persistence** = using both paradigms in the same architecture.
- ✅ **ETL** (Extract-Transform-Load) = traditional Schema-on-Write.
- ✅ **ELT** (Extract-Load-Transform) = modern Schema-on-Read in data lakes.
- ✅ Always include a **diagram** in any 7-mark sub-question on CAP or Scaling.
- ✅ Always cite **real tool examples** — KTU rewards application-oriented answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HiveQL
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT746 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** HiveQL

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**HiveQL (Hive Query Language)** is the declarative, SQL-like query language native to Apache Hive, designed to perform data definition, data manipulation, and query operations on massive datasets residing in distributed storage (HDFS) and compatible systems such as HBase and Amazon S3. HiveQL abstracts the underlying complexity of the MapReduce, Tez, or Spark execution engines, allowing analysts familiar with relational databases to express batch analytics over petabyte-scale structured and semi-structured data using familiar SQL constructs.

In the KTU 2024 scheme (Course: PECNT746 – Big Data Analytics), HiveQL is positioned as the **de facto data warehousing and analytical query layer** of the Hadoop ecosystem, bridging the gap between traditional RDBMS users and the MapReduce programming paradigm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** HiveQL supports Data Definition Language (DDL), Data Manipulation Language (DML), built-in operators, user-defined functions (UDFs), partitioning, bucketing, joins, sub-queries, and windowing functions. It is a *declarative* language — the user specifies *what* to retrieve, not *how* to compute it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from SQL:** HiveQL is *not* a complete superset of ANSI SQL. It does not support row-level updates, inserts, or deletes (originally), nor does it enforce transactional integrity in the traditional ACID sense. It is tuned for **Hadoop-scale, write-once, read-many** analytical workloads.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you run a massive **national library** containing billions of books (your data) stored in thousands of warehouses across the country (your HDFS cluster). The readers (analysts) are used to filling out simple request slips in plain English: *"Give me the total sales of Product X in Kerala during the last 3 years."*

Without HiveQL, the librarian would have to assemble a team of workers, give them a step-by-step physical choreography (MapReduce Java code) for *how* to walk through the warehouses, count items, and combine results.

**HiveQL is the smart front-desk interpreter** at this library. You submit an English-like request, and Hive:

1. **Parses** your request (semantic analysis),
2. **Plans** the optimal retrieval path (query optimization),
3. **Dispatches** workers using MapReduce / Tez / Spark (execution engine),
4. **Returns** a clean tabular result — just like SQL.

| Element | Library Analogy | HiveQL Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Books spread in warehouses | Distributed files on HDFS | Raw structured/semi-structured data |
| Plain English request | User-submitted query | HiveQL SELECT statement |
| Front desk interpreter | Librarian | Hive Service + Compiler |
| Workers executing the plan | Search team | MapReduce / Tez / Spark |
| Result sheet | Tabular report | Output table / HDFS file |

> [!NOTE]
> **Founders' Insight (Meta/Facebook context):** Apache Hive was originally developed at Facebook in 2008 to handle multi-petabyte logs and was open-sourced in 2010. It now underpins analytical workflows at Netflix, Uber, Airbnb, and countless enterprise data lakes.

---

## 1.3 Position in the Hadoop Stack

HiveQL sits at the **SQL-on-Hadoop** layer of the Hadoop ecosystem:

$$
\text{Hive} = \underbrace{\text{HiveQL}}_{\text{Query Language}} + \underbrace{\text{Execution Engine}}_{\text{MapReduce / Tez / Spark}} + \underbrace{\text{MetaStore}}_{\text{Schema/Catalog}} + \underbrace{\text{Driver}}_{\text{Compiler/Optimizer}}
$$

This modular design means HiveQL is **engine-agnostic** — the same query can be re-targeted between MapReduce (legacy), Tez (default since Hive 0.13), or Spark (Hive-on-Spark).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** A typical HiveQL query on a 1 TB dataset using Tez completes in **30–120 seconds**, whereas the equivalent hand-written MapReduce job takes **10–30 minutes** to develop and debug — a productivity gain of roughly **15x** for batch analytics.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 HiveQL Architecture — The "Why" and the "How"

The execution of any HiveQL statement follows a strict **5-stage pipeline**:

1. **Parse (ANTLR-based Parser):** The query string is tokenized and converted into a **Parse Tree (AST — Abstract Syntax Tree)**. Syntax errors are caught here.
2. **Semantic Analysis:** The compiler validates column names, table references, and data types against the **MetaStore** catalog.
3. **Query Plan Generation:** A **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** of physical operators (TableScan → Filter → Join → Aggregate → FileSink) is constructed.
4. **Optimization:** The **Optimizer** applies predicate pushdown, partition pruning, join reordering, and column pruning. **Cost-Based Optimization (CBO)** uses table/partition statistics since Hive 0.14.
5. **Execution:** The DAG is serialized into a **physical execution plan** and submitted to the configured engine (Tez, MapReduce, or Spark) which runs as YARN application containers.

**The "Why":** This separation allows HiveQL to remain a *declarative* language while the engine handles parallelism, fault tolerance, and resource negotiation.

**The "How":** Each stage uses well-defined Java interfaces (`SemanticAnalyzer`, `PhysicalPlanner`, `TezCompiler`).

---

## 2.2 HiveQL Data Types — KTU Cheat Sheet

| Category | Data Type | Size / Range | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Numeric | `TINYINT` | 1 byte, $-128$ to $127$ | Small counters, flags |
| Numeric | `SMALLINT` | 2 bytes, $-32\,768$ to $32\,767$ | ID columns |
| Numeric | `INT` | 4 bytes, $-2^{31}$ to $2^{31}-1$ | Standard integers |
| Numeric | `BIGINT` | 8 bytes, $-2^{63}$ to $2^{63}-1$ | Timestamps, large counts |
| Numeric | `FLOAT` | 4 bytes, single-precision | Approximate metrics |
| Numeric | `DOUBLE` | 8 bytes, double-precision | Scientific data |
| Numeric | `DECIMAL(precision,scale)` | Variable | Financial / exact arithmetic |
| String | `STRING` | Up to 2 GB | Free text, VARCHAR substitute |
| String | `VARCHAR(n)` | Up to $n$ chars | Bounded text |
| String | `CHAR(n)` | Fixed $n$ chars | Country codes |
| Date/Time | `DATE` | `YYYY-MM-DD` | Calendar dates |
| Date/Time | `TIMESTAMP` | Nanosecond precision | Event logs |
| Boolean | `BOOLEAN` | `TRUE` / `FALSE` | Flags |
| Complex | `ARRAY<data_type>` | Variable | List of values |
| Complex | `MAP<key_type,value_type>` | Variable | Key-value pairs |
| Complex | `STRUCT<field:type,...>` | Variable | Nested records |
| Complex | `UNIONTYPE<type1,type2,...>` | Variable | Heterogeneous cells |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why `STRING` instead of `VARCHAR`?** Hive records are stored as line-delimited files (text) or columnar files (ORC/Parquet). Using `STRING` avoids fixed-length padding overhead and matches the HDFS block-storage philosophy.

---

## 2.3 HiveQL DDL — Cheat Sheet

| Operation | Syntax Pattern | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Create Database | `CREATE DATABASE [IF NOT EXISTS] db_name;` | Creates HDFS dir `/user/hive/warehouse/db_name.db` |
| Use Database | `USE db_name;` | Sets current DB context |
| Create Table (Internal) | `CREATE TABLE t (col TYPE, ...) ROW FORMAT DELIMITED FIELDS TERMINATED BY ',';` | Data lives in `/user/hive/warehouse/` |
| Create External Table | `CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE t (...) LOCATION '/path/on/hdfs';` | Hive does **not** delete data on `DROP` |
| Create Partitioned Table | `CREATE TABLE t (...) PARTITIONED BY (country STRING);` | Logical sub-directory per partition value |
| Create Bucketed Table | `CREATE TABLE t (...) CLUSTERED BY (id) INTO 4 BUCKETS;` | Hash-based file splits |
| Alter Table — Add Column | `ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMNS (new_col INT);` | Schema evolution supported |
| Drop Table | `DROP TABLE [IF EXISTS] t;` | Removes metadata (+ data for internal) |

---

## 2.4 HiveQL DML — Cheat Sheet

| Operation | Syntax | Execution Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Load Data (Bulk) | `LOAD DATA INPATH '/hdfs/path' INTO TABLE t;` | HDFS **move** (fast) |
| Insert from Query | `INSERT OVERWRITE TABLE t2 SELECT ... FROM t1;` | Triggers Tez job |
| Multi-Insert | `FROM t1 INSERT OVERWRITE TABLE t2 SELECT ... INSERT OVERWRITE TABLE t3 SELECT ...;` | Single scan, multiple outputs |
| CTAS | `CREATE TABLE t2 AS SELECT ... FROM t1;` | Atomic create + populate |
| Export | `EXPORT TABLE t TO '/path';` | Backup to HDFS |
| Import | `IMPORT TABLE t FROM '/path';` | Restore + metadata rebuild |

---

## 2.5 Built-in Operators and Functions

HiveQL supports the following **four operator classes** (matching KTU syllabus verbatim):

| Operator Class | Examples | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Relational Operators** | `=`, `<>`, `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=`, `IS NULL`, `LIKE`, `RLIKE` | Filter predicates (`WHERE`, `JOIN ON`) |
| **Arithmetic Operators** | `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%`, `div` | Numeric computation |
| **Logical Operators** | `AND`, `OR`, `NOT` | Boolean composition |
| **Complex Operators** | `A[n]`, `M[key]`, `S.field`, `UNION ALL` | Access complex type elements |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested:** The difference between `LIKE` (SQL glob: `%` and `_`) and `RLIKE` (Java regex: `.*`, `\d`, `[a-z]+`). Example: `name RLIKE '^A.*son$'` matches `"Anderson"` and `"Adamson"`.

### Aggregations and Window Functions

| Function | Description |
|---|---|
| `COUNT(*)`, `COUNT(DISTINCT col)` | Cardinality and unique counts |
| `SUM(col)`, `AVG(col)`, `MIN(col)`, `MAX(col)` | Standard aggregates |
| `GROUPING SETS`, `ROLLUP`, `CUBE` | Multi-level aggregation (OLAP cube) |
| `RANK()`, `DENSE_RANK()`, `ROW_NUMBER()` | Window ranking |
| `LAG(col)`, `LEAD(col)` | Time-series offset |
| `NTILE(n)` | Bucketize rows |
| `EXPLODE(array)` | Lateral view — flatten arrays |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | HiveQL Application |
|---|---|
| **E-commerce** | Daily sales aggregation, customer cohort analysis |
| **Banking** | Fraud pattern mining over transaction logs |
| **Telecom** | CDR (Call Detail Record) analytics at petabyte scale |
| **IoT/Manufacturing** | Sensor stream archival + batch anomaly detection |
| **Social Media** | User engagement metrics, A/B test result computation |

The strategic value: HiveQL **democratizes Big Data** by allowing the existing SQL workforce (millions of analysts) to operate on Hadoop without learning Java/MapReduce.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Because HiveQL is a *programming/query language* (not a physics/calculus subject), the "derivations" here take the form of **worked query examples** that progressively build competence — exactly the style used in KTU board examinations and lab viva-voce.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Database & Table Creation (DDL)

### Step 1 — Create and Use a Database

```sql
CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS analytics_db;
USE analytics_db;
```

**Logic:** `IF NOT EXISTS` prevents an error if the DB already exists. `USE` sets the **current schema context** so subsequent statements resolve unqualified names against `analytics_db`.

**Resulting HDFS path:**
$$
\text{/user/hive/warehouse/analytics\_db.db}
$$

### Step 2 — Create an Internal Table

```sql
CREATE TABLE employee (
    emp_id      INT,
    emp_name    STRING,
    salary      DECIMAL(10,2),
    department  STRING,
    join_date   DATE
)
ROW FORMAT DELIMITED
FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','
STORED AS TEXTFILE;
```

**Line-by-line logic:**

- `emp_id INT` — fixed 4-byte integer.
- `emp_name STRING` — variable-length text.
- `salary DECIMAL(10,2)` — exact arithmetic: 8 digits before, 2 after decimal.
- `department STRING` — categorical text.
- `join_date DATE` — ISO-formatted `YYYY-MM-DD`.
- `ROW FORMAT DELIMITED FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','` — CSV-style input format.
- `STORED AS TEXTFILE` — default storage; can also be `ORC`, `PARQUET`, `RCFILE`, `SEQUENCEFILE`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internal vs. External — KTU Exam Pitfall:** For an *internal* table, `DROP TABLE` removes **both metadata and HDFS data**. For an *external* table, only metadata is removed; the HDFS data is preserved. Choose **EXTERNAL** in production to avoid accidental data loss.

### Step 3 — Create an External Partitioned Table

```sql
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE sales (
    txn_id      BIGINT,
    product_id  INT,
    amount      DOUBLE
)
PARTITIONED BY (year INT, month INT)
ROW FORMAT DELIMITED FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','
STORED AS TEXTFILE
LOCATION '/data/warehouse/sales';
```

**Logic of partitioning:**
$$
\text{HDFS Path} = \text{/data/warehouse/sales/year=2024/month=11/}
$$

When a query filters `WHERE year=2024 AND month=11`, Hive performs **partition pruning** and reads only that sub-directory — a $10^{3}$ to $10^{6}$ I/O reduction.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Loading and Querying (DML)

### Step 1 — Load Data

```sql
LOAD DATA INPATH '/tmp/employee_raw.csv'
INTO TABLE employee;
```

**Logic:** This is a **HDFS move operation** (not a copy), so it is O(1) time regardless of file size. The file `/tmp/employee_raw.csv` is *removed* from `/tmp/`.

### Step 2 — Filter and Project

```sql
SELECT emp_name, salary
FROM employee
WHERE department = 'Engineering' AND salary > 50000;
```

**Logical Execution Plan (DAG):**

$$
\text{TableScan(employee)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Filter(department='Engineering' AND salary>50000)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Select(emp\_name, salary)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{FileSink}
$$

### Step 3 — Aggregation

```sql
SELECT department,
       COUNT(*)              AS headcount,
       AVG(salary)           AS avg_salary,
       MAX(salary)           AS top_salary
FROM employee
GROUP BY department
HAVING COUNT(*) > 5;
```

**Logic:** `WHERE` runs **before** `GROUP BY` (row-level filter); `HAVING` runs **after** (group-level filter). This is identical to ANSI SQL semantics and a **frequent KTU question**.

### Step 4 — Window Function

```sql
SELECT emp_name, department, salary,
       RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC) AS dept_rank
FROM employee;
```

**Logic:** For each `department` partition, rows are sorted by `salary` descending and assigned a rank. Ties receive the same rank with gaps (use `DENSE_RANK` for no gaps).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Joins

Assume two tables — `orders` and `customers`:

```sql
SELECT c.cust_name, o.order_id, o.amount
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o
  ON c.cust_id = o.cust_id
WHERE o.amount > 1000;
```

### Hive-Specific Join Optimizations

| Join Type | Hint / Syntax | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Inner Join** | `JOIN` | Default, intersection only |
| **Left Outer** | `LEFT OUTER JOIN` | Preserve left side |
| **Right Outer** | `RIGHT OUTER JOIN` | Preserve right side |
| **Full Outer** | `FULL OUTER JOIN` | Preserve both |
| **Broadcast Join** | `/*+ MAPJOIN(o) */` | Small table ≤ 25 MB fits in driver memory |
| **Sort-Merge-Bucket Join** | Both tables `CLUSTERED BY` same key | Massive fact-to-fact joins |
| **Skew Join** | `set hive.optimize.skewjoin=true;` | Highly skewed key distributions |

**Analytical derivation for Map Join decision:**

Let $S_{\text{small}}$ be the size of the smaller table in bytes. Map join is preferred if:

$$
S_{\text{small}} \le S_{\text{threshold}}, \quad S_{\text{threshold}} = \text{hive.mapjoin.smalltable.filesize} \; (\text{default } 25\,\text{MB})
$$

If the inequality holds, Hive loads the small table into a **distributed in-memory hash table** in every mapper, avoiding a shuffle phase on the large table — a $5$–$10\times$ speedup.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Bucketing and Sampling

```sql
CREATE TABLE emp_bucketed (
    emp_id INT,
    emp_name STRING
)
CLUSTERED BY (emp_id) INTO 4 BUCKETS;
```

**Bucket assignment function:**

$$
\text{bucket\_id} = \text{hash}(\text{emp\_id}) \;\bmod\; N_{\text{buckets}}
$$

For $N_{\text{buckets}} = 4$, the hash space $\mathbb{Z}$ is partitioned into 4 equal ranges, and rows are written into 4 physical files. This enables **efficient sampling**:

```sql
SELECT * FROM emp_bucketed TABLESAMPLE (BUCKET 1 OUT OF 4);
```

Reads **exactly 25%** of the data — useful for exploratory data analysis on petabyte tables.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5: User-Defined Function (UDF)

A custom UDF extends HiveQL semantics. A simple length-suffix UDF in pseudo-Java:

```java
public class EndsWithLength extends UDF {
    public String evaluate(String s) {
        return s + "_" + s.length();
    }
}
```

**Register and use in HiveQL:**

```sql
ADD JAR /path/endswithlength.jar;
CREATE TEMPORARY FUNCTION my_suffix AS 'com.example.EndsWithLength';

SELECT my_suffix(emp_name) FROM employee;
```

**Why use UDFs?**
- Encapsulate business logic once and reuse across queries.
- Achieve performance via compiled Java execution inside the Tez container — **10–100x faster** than equivalent HiveQL CASE expressions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **UDF types taxonomy (KTU syllabus):**
> - **UDF** — one input row → one output row (e.g., `my_suffix`).
> - **UDAF** — many rows → one row (e.g., custom aggregate).
> - **UDTF** — one row → many rows (e.g., `EXPLODE`).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hive Query Execution Pipeline (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["USER QUERY IN HIVEQL"] --> B["DRIVER RECEIVES QUERY"]
    B --> C["PARSE PHASE ANTLR PARSER"]
    C --> D{"SYNTAX VALID"}
    D -- NO --> E["RETURN SYNTAX ERROR"]
    D -- YES --> F["SEMANTIC ANALYZER"]
    F --> G["METASTORE QUERY FOR SCHEMA"]
    G --> H["LOGICAL PLAN DAG BUILDER"]
    H --> I["PHYSICAL PLAN OPTIMIZER"]
    I --> J["COST BASED OPTIMIZER CBO"]
    J --> K["EXECUTION ENGINE TEZ MAPREDUCE OR SPARK"]
    K --> L["YARN RESOURCE MANAGER"]
    L --> M["DISTRIBUTED TASK EXECUTION ON HDFS"]
    M --> N["RESULT AGGREGATION"]
    N --> O["OUTPUT TABLE OR FILE"]
    O --> P["RETURN TO USER"]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style P fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style E fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,color:#FFF
    style J fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style K fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 Hive Table Architecture — Internal vs External

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph META["HIVE METASTORE RELATIONAL CATALOG"]
        T1["Table: employee_internal"]
        T2["Table: sales_external"]
    end
    subgraph HDFS["HDFS CLUSTER"]
        WH["/user/hive/warehouse/analytics_db.db"]
        EXT["/data/warehouse/sales external location"]
        WH --> F1["employee emp_id=1.csv"]
        WH --> F2["employee emp_id=2.csv"]
        EXT --> P1["year=2024 month=11 part1.csv"]
        EXT --> P2["year=2024 month=12 part2.csv"]
    end
    T1 -.metadata pointer.-> WH
    T2 -.metadata pointer.-> EXT
    DROP_INT["DROP TABLE employee_internal"] --> DEL_INT["HDFS data DELETED metadata DELETED"]
    DROP_EXT["DROP TABLE sales_external"] --> DEL_EXT["HDFS data PRESERVED metadata DELETED"]
    style META fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style HDFS fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style DROP_INT fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style DROP_EXT fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Partitioning & Bucketing File Layout

```mermaid
graph LR
    ROOT["/sales_root/"] --> Y1["year=2023/"]
    ROOT --> Y2["year=2024/"]
    Y1 --> M1["month=10/"]
    Y1 --> M2["month=11/"]
    Y2 --> M3["month=11/"]
    Y2 --> M4["month=12/"]
    M1 --> B1["bucket_00000"]
    M1 --> B2["bucket_00001"]
    M1 --> B3["bucket_00002"]
    M1 --> B4["bucket_00003"]
    M3 --> B5["bucket_00000"]
    M3 --> B6["bucket_00001"]
    M3 --> B7["bucket_00002"]
    M3 --> B8["bucket_00003"]

    style Y2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,color:#000

    style M2 fill:#D8BFD8,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style M3 fill:#D8BFD8,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style M4 fill:#D8BFD8,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

## 4.4 HiveQL Operator Class Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    HQL["HIVEL OPERATORS"] --> REL["RELATIONAL OPERATORS"]
    HQL --> ARITH["ARITHMETIC OPERATORS"]
    HQL --> LOG["LOGICAL OPERATORS"]

    REL --> R1["= EQUALS"]
    REL --> R2["less than greater than LESS GREATER"]
    REL --> R3["less equals greater equals LE GE"]
    REL --> R4["less greater NOT EQUAL"]
    REL --> R5["IS NULL"]
    REL --> R6["LIKE RLIKE REGEX"]
    ARITH --> A1["PLUS MINUS ASTERISK SLASH PERCENT"]
    LOG --> L1["AND OR NOT"]
    COMP --> C1["ARRAY INDEX A N"]
    COMP --> C2["MAP KEY M KEY"]
    COMP --> C3["STRUCT FIELD S FIELD"]
    COMP --> C4["UNION ALL"]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture Note:** Where physical 3D schematics (e.g., server racks) are not feasible in text/Mermaid, the diagrams above depict the **functional architecture** — the logical data flow, storage layout, and operator taxonomy — which is what KTU examiners expect students to reproduce in board exams.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing `WHERE` and `HAVING`:** `WHERE` filters rows *before* aggregation; `HAVING` filters *groups* after aggregation. Using `WHERE` with an aggregate function costs **3 marks outright**.
> 2. **Forgetting `IF NOT EXISTS` / `IF EXISTS`:** Causes runtime errors in scripts — KTU deducts **2 marks** for unsafe DDL.
> 3. **Using `STRING` for date literals without casting:** `'2024-01-01'` is a string, not a date. Always use `CAST(... AS DATE)`.
> 4. **Not specifying `EXTERNAL` for production tables:** Accidental `DROP TABLE` deletes production data — an automatic **5-mark penalty** in scenario-based questions.
> 5. **Mixing `LIKE` and `RLIKE`:** `LIKE` uses SQL globs; `RLIKE` uses Java regex. Mixing them is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 6. **Forgetting `PARTITION` keyword in `LOAD DATA` for partitioned tables** — data will not land in the right sub-directory.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define HiveQL. List any four data types supported by HiveQL.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

HiveQL is the SQL-like declarative query language of Apache Hive, used to perform data definition, manipulation, and analytical queries on data stored in HDFS. It is converted internally into MapReduce, Tez, or Spark jobs for execution.

Four supported data types:

1. **`TINYINT`** — 1-byte integer ($-128$ to $127$).
2. **`STRING`** — Variable-length text up to 2 GB.
3. **`DATE`** — Calendar date in `YYYY-MM-DD` format.
4. **`ARRAY<STRING>`** — Complex type representing an ordered list of strings.

> *Alternative valid types:* `BIGINT`, `DECIMAL`, `BOOLEAN`, `MAP<INT,STRING>`, `STRUCT`, `TIMESTAMP`.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Internal and External tables in Hive.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Internal Table | External Table |
|---|---|---|
| `LOCATION` clause | Optional (defaults to `/user/hive/warehouse/`) | **Mandatory** (must specify HDFS path) |
| Storage location | Inside Hive's warehouse directory | Outside warehouse — user-controlled |
| `DROP TABLE` behavior | **Deletes both data and metadata** | Deletes only **metadata**; HDFS data preserved |
| Keyword | `CREATE TABLE` (default) | `CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE` |
| Use case | Temporary / scratch data | Production data shared with other tools (Spark, Pig) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### 📘 Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Module 3, Set A

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the architecture of Apache Hive with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of the MetaStore, Driver, and Execution Engine.

**(b) [7 marks]** Write HiveQL statements to:
1. Create an external partitioned table `transactions(txn_id BIGINT, product_id INT, amount DOUBLE)` partitioned by `year INT` and `month INT`, stored as ORC.
2. Load data from HDFS path `/raw/txn/2024/11/` into the appropriate partition.
3. Write a query to find the **top 3 products** (by `product_id`) with the **highest total sales amount** in the year 2024.

---

#### Model Solution — (a) Architecture [7 marks]

**Apache Hive Architecture consists of five major components:**

1. **User Interface (UI):** Thrift Server, CLI, Web UI (HWI), and JDBC/ODBC drivers allow users to submit HiveQL queries.

2. **Driver:** The central controller. It receives the query, creates session handles, and coordinates the Parse → Semantic Analysis → Plan Generation → Optimization → Execution pipeline. It also fetches results.

3. **Compiler:** Comprises the Parser (ANTLR), Semantic Analyzer, and Query Plan Generator. It transforms HiveQL into a logical DAG and then a physical execution plan.

4. **MetaStore:** A separate relational database (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Derby) that stores **schema metadata** — table definitions, column types, partition information, statistics, and serialization properties. It is the catalog of the data warehouse.

5. **Execution Engine:** Submits the physical plan as a YARN application. Supports **MapReduce, Tez, and Spark**. Tez is the default since Hive 0.13 and provides a directed-acyclic-graph execution model that is **5–10× faster** than classic MapReduce for iterative queries.

**Block diagram (text representation for answer sheet):**

$$
\boxed{\text{User Interface}} \;\rightarrow\; \boxed{\text{Driver}} \;\rightarrow\; \boxed{\text{Compiler}} \;\rightarrow\; \boxed{\text{MetaStore}}
$$
$$
\boxed{\text{Compiler}} \;\rightarrow\; \boxed{\text{Execution Engine}} \;\rightarrow\; \boxed{\text{HDFS / HBase}}
$$

**Roles summary:**

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **MetaStore** | Stores table schema, column types, partition keys, statistics |
| **Driver** | Orchestrates the entire query lifecycle; manages sessions |
| **Execution Engine** | Runs physical plan on the cluster (Tez/MapReduce/Spark) |

---

#### Model Solution — (b) HiveQL Statements [7 marks]

**1. Create External Partitioned Table [3 marks]**

```sql
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE IF NOT EXISTS transactions (
    txn_id      BIGINT,
    product_id  INT,
    amount      DOUBLE
)
PARTITIONED BY (year INT, month INT)
STORED AS ORC
LOCATION '/data/warehouse/transactions';
```

**Logic:** `EXTERNAL` ensures HDFS data survives `DROP TABLE`. `STORED AS ORC` gives columnar compression + predicate pushdown.

**2. Load Data into Partition [2 marks]**

```sql
ALTER TABLE transactions
ADD PARTITION (year=2024, month=11)
LOCATION '/raw/txn/2024/11/';
```

**Alternative (if data already on HDFS in sub-directory form):**

```sql
MSCK REPAIR TABLE transactions;
```

This auto-discovers partitions from sub-directory names matching the `key=value` convention.

**3. Top 3 Products by Sales [2 marks]**

```sql
SELECT product_id, SUM(amount) AS total_sales
FROM transactions
WHERE year = 2024
GROUP BY product_id
ORDER BY total_sales DESC
LIMIT 3;
```

**Logic:** Partition pruning on `year=2024` triggers, reading only the `year=2024/` sub-directory tree. Aggregation produces total sales per product; `ORDER BY ... LIMIT 3` extracts the top three.

---

### 📗 Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — Module 3, Set B (Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of **partitioning** and **bucketing** in Hive. How do they differ? Provide a CREATE TABLE example for each.

**(b) [7 marks]** Consider two tables:

- `customers(cust_id INT, cust_name STRING, city STRING)`
- `orders(order_id INT, cust_id INT, amount DOUBLE, order_date DATE)`

Write HiveQL queries to:
1. Display the **total order amount** per city.
2. Find customers who have **not placed any order** (use a suitable outer join).
3. Compute a **running total** of `amount` for each customer ordered by `order_date` (use a window function).

---

#### Model Solution — (a) Partitioning vs. Bucketing [7 marks]

**Partitioning** [3.5 marks]

- Divides a table into **logical sub-directories** based on the value of one or more *partition columns* (e.g., `country`, `year`).
- Stored as HDFS directories of the form `tablename/col1=val1/col2=val2/`.
- Enables **partition pruning**: queries with `WHERE col=val` read only the relevant directory.
- **Limitation:** Cardinality must be low (avoid columns with >10,000 distinct values, e.g., `user_id`).
- **Example:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE sales (
    txn_id BIGINT,
    amount DOUBLE
)
PARTITIONED BY (year INT, month INT)
STORED AS ORC;
```

**Bucketing** [3.5 marks]

- Divides data within a **partition** (or a non-partitioned table) into a fixed number of **buckets** using a hash function on a *clustered-by* column.
- Stored as files `bucket_00000`, `bucket_00001`, ... inside the partition directory.
- Bucket assignment: $\text{hash}(col) \bmod N_{\text{buckets}}$.
- Enables **efficient sampling** (`TABLESAMPLE`) and **sort-merge-bucket joins**.
- **Example:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE customers_bucketed (
    cust_id INT,
    cust_name STRING
)
CLUSTERED BY (cust_id) INTO 8 BUCKETS
STORED AS ORC;
```

**Comparison Table [built into answer]:**

| Aspect | Partitioning | Bucketing |
|---|---|---|
| Granularity | Directory level | File level |
| Mechanism | Discrete column value | Hash on column value |
| Cardinality constraint | Low | High (works with any cardinality) |
| Use case | Date, region, status | User ID, product ID, session ID |
| Pruning benefit | Whole-partition elimination | Within-partition sampling |

---

#### Model Solution — (b) HiveQL Queries [7 marks]

**1. Total order amount per city [2 marks]**

```sql
SELECT c.city, SUM(o.amount) AS total_amount
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o ON c.cust_id = o.cust_id
GROUP BY c.city;
```

**Logic:** Inner join pairs customers with their orders; aggregation sums `amount` grouped by `city`.

**2. Customers with no orders [2 marks]**

```sql
SELECT c.cust_id, c.cust_name
FROM customers c
LEFT OUTER JOIN orders o ON c.cust_id = o.cust_id
WHERE o.order_id IS NULL;
```

**Logic:** `LEFT OUTER JOIN` keeps all customers; customers with no matching order produce a `NULL` in `o.order_id`. The `IS NULL` filter selects only those.

**3. Running total per customer [3 marks]**

```sql
SELECT c.cust_name,
       o.order_date,
       o.amount,
       SUM(o.amount) OVER (
           PARTITION BY c.cust_id
           ORDER BY o.order_date
           ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
       ) AS running_total
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o ON c.cust_id = o.cust_id;
```

**Logic:** The `SUM(...) OVER (...)` window function computes a cumulative sum partitioned by `cust_id` and ordered by `order_date`. The `ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW` frame specifies the cumulative range. (Default frame in Hive is `RANGE UNBOUNDED PRECEDING`, which behaves identically for unique date values.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **HiveQL = SQL for Hadoop:** Declarative, not procedural; engine-agnostic (Tez default).
- **Five-stage execution pipeline:** Parse → Semantic Analysis → Logical Plan → Physical Plan Optimization → Execution.
- **MetaStore** is the central catalog (typically MySQL/PostgreSQL in production) — never Derby in production.
- **Internal vs. External tables** differ only in `DROP` semantics — choose **EXTERNAL** for production data.
- **Partitioning** = directory-level pruning; **Bucketing** = file-level hashing; both can coexist.
- **Operators are 4 classes:** Relational, Arithmetic, Logical, Complex (KTU syllabus verbatim).
- **Complex data types:** `ARRAY`, `MAP`, `STRUCT`, `UNIONTYPE` enable semi-structured analytics.
- **Window functions** (`RANK`, `DENSE_RANK`, `ROW_NUMBER`, `LAG`, `LEAD`, `SUM OVER`) bring OLAP power to HiveQL.
- **UDF / UDAF / UDTF** are the three extension points; written in Java, executed in Tez containers.
- **Storage formats:** TEXTFILE (default), ORC (columnar, best for analytics), PARQUET (cross-engine), RCFILE, SEQUENCEFILE.
- **Cost-Based Optimizer (CBO)** uses table/partition statistics — keep statistics fresh via `ANALYZE TABLE ... COMPUTE STATISTICS`.
- **Map Join hint** `/*+ MAPJOIN(t) */` delivers **5–10× speedup** for small dimension tables.
- **File format order of efficiency (best → worst):** `ORC > Parquet > RCFile > SequenceFile > TextFile`.
- **Always use `IF EXISTS` / `IF NOT EXISTS`** in DDL — it's a **2-mark safer-script** habit in KTU practical exams.
- **Default warehouse path:** `/user/hive/warehouse/<database>.db/` — remember for `LOAD DATA INPATH` answers.
- **Hive does NOT enforce ACID** for non-transactional tables — it is **read-optimized, write-once** in its classic mode.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT746 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are "Tables" in Big Data Analytics?

In the context of **Big Data Analytics** under the KTU 2024 scheme (Module 3), the term **"Tables"** refers primarily to the **distributed, sparse, multi-dimensional sorted map** data structure popularized by Google's **Bigtable** and its open-source counterpart, **Apache HBase (Hadoop Database)**. Unlike traditional RDBMS tables that follow a rigid relational schema with fixed columns and ACID transactions, Big Data tables are designed for **horizontal scalability**, **fault tolerance**, and handling **petabytes of sparse data** across thousands of commodity servers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Bigtable / HBase Table):**
> A *Bigtable* is a **distributed, persistent, multi-dimensional sorted map** indexed by a **row key, column key, and a timestamp**. It is designed to scale to very large sizes (petabytes of data) across thousands of commodity servers while handling hundreds of millions of operations per second. The Apache HBase table is the open-source Hadoop equivalent of Google Bigtable.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Library Card Catalog of the Internet

Imagine a **gigantic, infinitely expandable library** where:
- The **shelves** are the table (instead of a single bookshelf, you have thousands distributed across many buildings).
- The **row keys** are the unique book accession numbers (sorted alphabetically/numerically so you can find a range of books quickly).
- The **column families** are the *types* of drawers attached to each book — e.g., one drawer for "Customer Info", another for "Order History" — and you can add new drawer types whenever you want.
- The **columns** inside a column family are individual slots — e.g., in "Customer Info", you might have slots for `name`, `email`, `phone`.
- The **cells** are the actual values written in those slots, and each time you write, you get a **timestamped version** (like a history log of all edits to that book).
- Most drawers are **empty** (the table is *sparse*), so you don't waste space storing blank slots.

> [!NOTE]
> This sparse, versioned, multi-dimensional structure is what makes HBase/Bigtable fundamentally different from a relational table. A relational table forces you to declare every column up-front and stores `NULL` for missing data; an HBase table simply *does not store* a cell that does not exist.

## 1.3 Core Terminology Snapshot

| KTU Term | One-Line Definition |
|---|---|
| **Bigtable** | Google's proprietary distributed storage system for structured data (2006 whitepaper) |
| **HBase** | Open-source, distributed, non-relational database modeled after Google's Bigtable, runs on HDFS |
| **Row Key** | A unique byte-array identifier for each row; rows are stored in **lexicographic (sorted) order** |
| **Column Family** | A logical grouping of related columns; defined at table creation; supports `n` columns dynamically |
| **Column Qualifier** | The actual column name within a family; need not be predefined |
| **Cell** | The intersection of `{row, column family, column qualifier, timestamp}` — holds a byte-array value |
| **Timestamp** | A 64-bit integer (version identifier); defaults to system time at write |
| **Region** | A horizontal, contiguous range of rows served by a single **RegionServer** |
| **HFile** | The on-disk (HDFS) storage format for HBase data — an immutable, sorted SSTable-like file |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** Think **R-C-C-C-T** — **R**ow key, **C**olumn Family, **C**olumn Qualifier, **C**ell, **T**imestamp. Together these five attributes uniquely identify any data point in a Bigtable.

## 1.4 Why "Tables" Matter in Big Data

Modern workloads — **time-series IoT telemetry, social media feeds, search-engine indexes, financial tick data, recommendation engines** — produce data that is:
1. **Too large** for a single RDBMS (rows in billions, columns in millions).
2. **Sparse** (most users interact with only a tiny fraction of available attributes).
3. **Schema-evolving** (new attributes appear constantly).
4. **Versioned** (you need point-in-time recovery and audit trails).

Bigtable/HBase tables are engineered for precisely these requirements. They are the **storage backbone** of Facebook's Messages infrastructure, LinkedIn's activity logs, Pinterest's graphs, and Yahoo's serving platform.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bigtable Data Model — A Logical View

The Bigtable data model can be visualized as a **3D map**:

$$
\text{BigTable : } (\text{row} : \text{byte}*, \ \text{column} : \text{byte}*, \ \text{time} : \text{int64}) \ \longrightarrow \ \text{byte}*
$$

Where:
- **row** is a variable-length byte string (typically up to **64 KB** in HBase; practically a few hundred bytes).
- **column** is a two-level hierarchical key of the form `family:qualifier`.
- **time** is a 64-bit timestamp in microseconds (HBase) or milliseconds (Bigtable).

### 2.1.1 Row Keys and Sorting

Rows are stored in **strict lexicographic (byte-wise) order** by row key. This is critical because:

1. **Range scans** (`startKey` → `endKey`) are fast sequential reads because rows are physically co-located.
2. **Hotspotting** can occur if row keys are monotonically increasing (e.g., timestamps) — a *must-avoid* design pattern taught in KTU.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule:** For high-write workloads, use **salting, hashing, or field-reversal** on the row key to distribute writes evenly across regions. Example: Instead of `2024-01-15-user123`, use `hash(user123) % N + "-" + 2024-01-15`.

### 2.1.2 Column Families — The Only Schema

A table is partitioned into **column families**, and they are the **only** schema elements you declare at creation:

$$
\text{Table} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} \text{ColumnFamily}_i
$$

Each column family:
- Has its own set of **HFiles** on HDFS (separate storage).
- Has its own **MemStore** and **block cache** in memory.
- Should be created **sparingly** (typically **2 to 4 per table**) because every family adds I/O overhead.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Never put attributes of different access patterns in the same column family. Example: If `profile_pic` is read rarely and `login_count` is read frequently, they should be in **separate families** so the hot family fits in the block cache efficiently.

### 2.1.3 Cells, Versions, and TTL

A cell can hold multiple versions. By default HBase keeps **3 versions**; this is configurable via:

$$
\text{VERSIONS} = n \quad \text{and} \quad \text{TTL (Time-To-Live)} = t \ \text{seconds}
$$

The retention policy: a cell version is discarded when **either** (a) the version count exceeds $n$, or (b) the timestamp is older than $t$ seconds from now.

## 2.2 HBase Architecture — Component Deep Dive

| Component | Role | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **HMaster** | Coordinates the cluster: assigns regions to RegionServers, handles DDL, recovers from RegionServer failure | Single point of metadata coordination; **not** a data-path node |
| **RegionServer** | Serves read/write requests for a set of regions; runs MemStore flushes and compaction | The actual workhorse; typically 10–20 per node |
| **ZooKeeper** | Maintains cluster state, coordinates HMaster election, tracks RegionServer liveness | Mandatory quorum service (odd number of nodes, e.g., 3 or 5) |
| **HDFS** | Underlying distributed file system; stores HFiles, WAL, and root/META table data | Provides durability and replication |
| **WAL (Write-Ahead Log)** | Append-only log on HDFS; ensures write durability before MemStore commit | Recovery mechanism after RegionServer crash |
| **MemStore** | In-memory sorted buffer (per column family) flushed to HFile when full | Latency → throughput tradeoff; default size **128 MB** |
| **HFile** | Immutable, sorted, indexed file format based on Hadoop's **SequenceFile/SSTable** | Read-optimized structure with **block index**, **bloom filter**, **key-value index** |
| **BlockCache** | LRU cache in RegionServer RAM holding recently-read HFile blocks | Default = **40% of RegionServer heap** |
| **Region** | A horizontal shard of a table (contiguous row range) | Splits automatically when it exceeds `hbase.hregion.max.filesize` (**default 10 GB**) |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Default |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Region split trigger** | $\text{RegionSize} > hbase.hregion.max.filesize$ | Default = **10 GB** |
| 2 | **MemStore flush trigger** | $\text{MemStoreSize} > hbase.hregion.memstore.flush.size$ | Default = **128 MB** |
| 3 | **Total MemStore upper bound** | $hbase.regionserver.global.memstore.upperLimit \times Heap$ | Default = **0.4** of heap |
| 4 | **Block cache fraction** | $hfile.block.cache.size$ | Default = **0.4** of heap |
| 5 | **Bloom filter false positive rate** | $\epsilon = (1 - e^{-kn/m})^k$ where $k$ = hash count, $n$ = items, $m$ = bits | HBase uses **ROWCOL** filters |
| 6 | **Cell coordinate** | $(r, c : q, t) \rightarrow v$ | $r$ = row key bytes, $c$ = family, $q$ = qualifier, $t$ = int64 |
| 7 | **Compaction ratio** | $R = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \text{size}(i) \ / \ \text{size}(n)$ | Trigger default $R = 1.2$ |
| 8 | **HFile block size** | $io.hfile.block.size$ | Default = **64 KB** |
| 9 | **WAL roll size** | $hbase.regionserver.hlog.blocksize$ | Default = **~64 MB** |
| 10 | **Version retention** | $\text{VERSIONS} = n \ \text{or} \ \text{TTL} = t$ | $n \in \mathbb{Z}^+$, $t$ in seconds |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bloom filter math:** The optimal number of hash functions is $k = (m/n) \ln 2$. For HBase's default 1% false-positive target with $m/n = 10$ bits per element, $k \approx 7$.

## 2.4 Read/Write Path Analysis

### 2.4.1 Write Path (4 Stages)

1. **Client → RegionServer**: Client consults `-ROOT-` and `.META.` (or `hbase:meta`) to find the RegionServer hosting the target region. Modern clients cache this in `ClientMetaCache`.
2. **Append to WAL**: The new `(row, column, value, timestamp)` entry is appended to the WAL on HDFS.
3. **Write to MemStore**: The same entry is added to the column family's in-memory sorted skip-list.
4. **Acknowledge**: The client receives success. **Flush** to HFile happens asynchronously when MemStore fills.

### 2.4.2 Read Path (Merge of 3 Sources)

1. **Block cache lookup** (HFile blocks) — fastest.
2. **MemStore scan** — captures the most recent writes.
3. **Bloom filter check** on candidate HFiles — eliminates HFiles that definitely don't contain the row.
4. Results from all three sources are **merged** (using timestamp to pick the latest version) and returned.

## 2.5 Bigtable vs. RDBMS — KTU Comparison Table

| Feature | HBase / Bigtable | RDBMS (MySQL, Oracle) |
|---|---|---|
| **Data model** | Wide-column, sparse, multi-dimensional | Fixed-schema relational |
| **Scaling** | Horizontal (add RegionServers) | Mostly vertical |
| **Transactions** | Single-row only (no multi-row ACID by default) | Full ACID, multi-row |
| **Query language** | Get / Scan / Filter API; Hive/Phoenix for SQL | Standard SQL |
| **Joins** | Not supported natively (denormalize) | First-class feature |
| **Indexing** | Only on row key (primary); secondary via separate tables | Multiple B-tree/hash indexes |
| **Consistency** | Strong consistency for single row | Strong consistency |
| **Schema flexibility** | Add column qualifiers anytime | ALTER TABLE required |
| **Best for** | Sparse, write-heavy, large-scale | Structured, transactional, OLTP |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use of Bigtable-style Tables |
|---|---|
| **Search Engines** | Web table, inverted indexes (Google, Bing) |
| **Social Networks** | Facebook Messages — over a trillion messages stored in HBase |
| **Finance** | Tick-data storage, fraud detection, time-series analytics |
| **IoT** | Sensor telemetry with high write throughput |
| **Genomics** | Sequence data with sparse per-sample attributes |
| **Recommendation** | User-item sparse matrices |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sizing a Region

A common KTU-style problem asks: *"Given a table with N rows of average size S bytes, what is the expected number of regions when each region can hold at most R bytes?"*

$$
\text{Number of Regions} = \left\lceil \frac{N \times S}{R} \right\rceil
$$

**Worked Example:**
Suppose a table contains $N = 5 \times 10^9$ rows, the average row size is $S = 4 \ \text{KB}$ (including all column families and versions), and the configured maximum region size is $R = 10 \ \text{GB}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of Regions} &= \left\lceil \frac{5 \times 10^9 \times 4 \times 1024}{10 \times 1024^3} \right\rceil \\[6pt]
&= \left\lceil \frac{5 \times 10^9 \times 4 \times 1024}{10 \times 1.0737 \times 10^{10}} \right\rceil \\[6pt]
&= \left\lceil \frac{2.048 \times 10^{13}}{1.0737 \times 10^{10}} \right\rceil \\[6pt]
&= \left\lceil 1907.35 \right\rceil \\[6pt]
&= 1908 \ \text{regions}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** With the default 10 GB region size, the cluster will need **at least 1908 regions** spread across RegionServers. If each RegionServer can host ~100 regions, we need **~20 RegionServers**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bloom Filter False Positive Probability

A **Bloom filter** is a probabilistic structure used in HFile metadata to determine if a row *might* exist in an HFile, *without* a disk read.

For a Bloom filter with $m$ bits, $n$ inserted items, and $k$ hash functions, the false positive probability is:

$$
\epsilon = \left( 1 - e^{-kn/m} \right)^{k}
$$

**Step-by-step optimization:** To minimize $\epsilon$ for fixed $m$ and $n$:

$$
\frac{\partial \epsilon}{\partial k} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad k = \frac{m}{n} \ln 2
$$

**Worked Example:**
HBase configures a Bloom filter with $m/n = 10$ bits per element. Find the optimal $k$ and resulting $\epsilon$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_{\text{opt}} &= 10 \times \ln 2 \\
&= 10 \times 0.6931 \\
&\approx 6.93 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad k = 7 \ \text{(rounded to integer)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $k = 7$ into the false-positive formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\epsilon &= \left( 1 - e^{-7 \times 10 / 10} \right)^{7} \\
&= \left( 1 - e^{-7} \right)^{7} \\
&= \left( 1 - 0.000912 \right)^{7} \\
&= \left( 0.999088 \right)^{7} \\
&\approx 0.9936 \ (\text{intermediate value}) \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Re-evaluate using } 1 - e^{-kn/m} = 1 - e^{-7} \approx 0.9991
\end{aligned}
$$

A cleaner form using the optimum:

$$
\epsilon_{\min} = \left( 1 - e^{-\ln 2} \right)^{(m/n)\ln 2} = \left( 0.5 \right)^{10 \ln 2} = 2^{-10 \ln 2} = 2^{-6.93} \approx 0.0083
$$

So with $m/n = 10$ and $k = 7$, the false-positive rate is approximately **0.83%**.

> [!NOTE]
> HBase offers three Bloom filter types: **NONE**, **ROW** (key only), and **ROWCOL** (row + column). The default since HBase 0.96 is **ROWCOL** for maximum selectivity.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — MemStore Flush Frequency

Each RegionServer has a global upper bound on total MemStore usage:

$$
\text{MemStore Upper Limit} = hbase.regionserver.global.memstore.upperLimit \times \text{Heap Size}
$$

**Worked Example:**
A RegionServer has $\text{Heap} = 32 \ \text{GB}$ and uses the default `upperLimit = 0.4`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Global MemStore Limit} &= 0.4 \times 32 \ \text{GB} = 12.8 \ \text{GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the cluster has $R$ regions per server, the *average* per-region MemStore threshold is:

$$
\text{Avg. MemStore} = \frac{12.8 \ \text{GB}}{R}
$$

For $R = 100$ regions: $\text{Avg. MemStore} = 128 \ \text{MB}$, which matches the default `hbase.hregion.memstore.flush.size`.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Compaction Selection Ratio

HBase's **RatioBasedCompactionPolicy** selects files for compaction using the ratio $R$:

$$
R_j = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{j} \text{size}(HFile_i)}{\text{size}(HFile_{j+1})}
$$

A compaction is triggered when $R_j \geq hbase.hstore.compaction.ratio$ (default = **1.2**).

**Worked Example:**
Suppose a Store has 5 HFiles with sizes: $[100, 110, 120, 130, 500] \ \text{MB}$.
Compute the cumulative ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= \frac{100}{110} \approx 0.91 \quad (< 1.2) \\
R_2 &= \frac{100 + 110}{120} = \frac{210}{120} = 1.75 \quad (\geq 1.2) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $R_2 \geq 1.2$, HBase selects the **first 3 HFiles (100, 110, 120 MB)** for compaction. After compaction, these are merged into one file of size $\approx 330 \ \text{MB}$.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Scenario | Governing Formula | Critical Constant |
|---|---|---|
| Estimating region count | $N_R = \lceil (N \times S) / R \rceil$ | $R_{\max} = 10 \ \text{GB}$ |
| Bloom filter FPR | $\epsilon = (1 - e^{-kn/m})^k$ | $k_{\text{opt}} = (m/n)\ln 2$ |
| MemStore memory budget | $M_{g} = 0.4 \times H$ | $H$ = RegionServer heap |
| Compaction trigger | $\sum_{i=1}^{j} S_i / S_{j+1} \geq 1.2$ | Default ratio = 1.2 |
| HFile block size | $B = 64 \ \text{KB}$ (default) | Tunable for scan vs. random-read tradeoff |
| TTL-driven retention | $T_{\text{expire}} = t_{\text{write}} + \text{TTL}$ | TTL in seconds |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define the Bigtable data model. List its five primary coordinate components.**

**Model Answer:**

The Bigtable data model is a **distributed, persistent, multi-dimensional sorted map** that is indexed by a row key, column key, and timestamp. The five primary coordinate components that uniquely identify any cell are:

1. **Row Key** — a variable-length byte-array identifier for a row; rows are stored in lexicographic order.
2. **Column Family** — a logical group of related columns; declared at table creation time.
3. **Column Qualifier** — a specific column within a family; need not be predefined.
4. **Timestamp** — a 64-bit integer (microsecond in HBase) that version-stamps every cell write.
5. **Cell Value** — the byte-array payload stored at the intersection of the above four coordinates.

The data model is **sparse**, meaning cells that have never been written do not occupy any storage.

> [!TIP]
> Mentioning "**sparse, multi-dimensional sorted map**" is mandatory in a KTU 3-mark answer — it is the exact phrase from the Google Bigtable whitepaper.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between column-oriented and row-oriented storage. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Row-Oriented Storage | Column-Oriented Storage |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical layout** | All columns of a row stored contiguously | All values of a column stored contiguously |
| **Best for** | OLTP — many rows, few columns (full-row reads) | OLAP — few rows, many columns (aggregations) |
| **Compression** | Poor (mixed data types in a row) | Excellent (homogeneous data in a column) |
| **Read efficiency** | Fast for full-row reads | Fast for column-wise analytics |
| **Examples** | MySQL InnoDB, PostgreSQL, Oracle | Parquet, ORC, HBase (column-family oriented), Vertica |

HBase is technically **column-family-oriented** (a hybrid): data within a column family is stored together on disk, enabling column-family-level compression and selective reads, but it is not purely columnar in the OLAP sense.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — HBase Architecture and Data Model

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3, Q Choice A]**

**(a) Explain the HBase data model with a suitable diagram. Describe the roles of row key, column family, column qualifier, cell, and timestamp. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The HBase data model is a **distributed, multi-dimensional, sparse, sorted map**. Logically, a table is a three-dimensional structure indexed by **(row key, column key, timestamp)**, and the value is an uninterpreted byte array.

**Role of Each Component:**

- **Row Key:** Acts as the primary key. Each row is uniquely identified and stored in **byte-wise sorted (lexicographic) order**, which enables efficient range scans. Example: `user001`, `user002`. The row key is the *only* natively indexed attribute.

- **Column Family:** A logical grouping of columns that share the same access pattern and physical storage. Column families are declared at table creation (e.g., `profile`, `actions`). Each family has its own set of HFiles, MemStore, and block cache. **HBase recommends 2–4 families per table** because each family adds I/O overhead.

- **Column Qualifier:** A specific column within a family. They are **not** declared in advance and may be added dynamically. Example: in family `profile`, qualifiers could be `name`, `email`, `age`.

- **Cell:** The actual data element located at the intersection of `(row key, column family, column qualifier, timestamp)`. It stores an uninterpreted byte-array value.

- **Timestamp:** A 64-bit integer (typically microseconds since epoch) automatically assigned at write time. HBase keeps multiple versions of a cell; clients can read by specifying a timestamp or version count.

**Tabular Example:**

| Row Key | Column Family `profile:name` | Column Family `profile:email` | Column Family `actions:login` |
|---|---|---|---|
| user001 | "Rahul" (t1) | "rahul@x.com" (t1) | "2024-01-15" (t2) |
| user002 | "Anu" (t1) | — (no cell) | "2024-01-16" (t2) |

Note the **sparsity**: user002 has no `email` cell — no storage is consumed.

---

**(b) With a neat block diagram, explain the HBase cluster architecture. Describe the roles of HMaster, RegionServer, ZooKeeper, and HFile. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The HBase cluster architecture has four major layers:

**1. Client Layer:** Talks to ZooKeeper to discover the region location and to the RegionServer for actual read/write operations.

**2. Coordination Layer (ZooKeeper):** Maintains cluster state, manages HMaster election, and tracks RegionServer liveness via ephemeral znodes. A typical deployment uses 3 or 5 ZooKeeper nodes for high availability.

**3. Master Layer (HMaster):** Performs administrative tasks — assigning regions to RegionServers, balancing load, handling DDL operations (`create`, `disable`, `drop`), and recovering regions from a failed RegionServer. It does **not** lie on the data read/write path.

**4. RegionServer Layer:** The workhorse. Each RegionServer:
- Hosts multiple **Regions** (a horizontal shard of a table).
- For each column family, maintains a **MemStore** (in-memory sorted buffer, default 128 MB) and a set of immutable **HFiles** on HDFS.
- Uses a **Block Cache** (LRU, default 40% of heap) to cache frequently-read HFile blocks.
- Appends every write to the **Write-Ahead Log (WAL)** on HDFS for durability. On crash, the WAL is replayed to recover the MemStore.

**5. Storage Layer (HDFS):** Stores HFiles, WAL, and the special `hbase:meta` table (previously `.META.`). Provides 3x replication by default for fault tolerance.

**Roles of Key Components:**

- **HMaster** — Coordinator; manages region assignment, DDL, and recovery.
- **RegionServer** — Serves data; handles MemStore, HFile, BlockCache, WAL.
- **ZooKeeper** — Distributed coordination service for cluster membership and leader election.
- **HFile** — The on-disk file format (based on SSTable); contains sorted key-value pairs with a block index, bloom filter, and trailer for fast random and sequential reads.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the HMaster with a "master node that handles all data traffic." This is **wrong** — the HMaster is a *control-plane* node only. All actual data reads/writes go directly from the client to the RegionServer. Marking deduction of 1–2 marks is common for this misconception.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — HBase Operations and RDBMS Comparison

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3, Q Choice B]**

**(a) Explain the working of the HBase write and read paths. How is data durability guaranteed? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Write Path (4 stages):**

1. **Region Lookup:** The client first queries ZooKeeper for the `-ROOT-`/`hbase:meta` table (or uses its cached `ClientMetaCache`) to find the RegionServer hosting the target region for the row key.
2. **WAL Append:** Before any in-memory change, the new entry `(row, cf:qualifier, value, timestamp)` is **appended to the WAL on HDFS**. This guarantees durability — if the RegionServer crashes, the WAL can be replayed.
3. **MemStore Update:** The entry is inserted into the column family's in-memory **MemStore** (a sorted skip-list, not a hashmap, to enable fast scans).
4. **Client Ack:** Once both the WAL and MemStore are written, the client receives a success response. The **flush to HFile is asynchronous** and happens when the MemStore reaches 128 MB.

**Read Path (3 sources merged):**

1. **Block Cache Lookup:** First, the BlockCache (LRU) is checked. If the HFile block is cached, the result is returned instantly.
2. **MemStore Scan:** The MemStore is scanned for the latest version of the cell.
3. **HFile Scan with Bloom Filter:** A **Bloom filter** check eliminates HFiles that definitely do not contain the row. Surviving HFiles are scanned using the block index.
4. **Merge and Return:** Results from BlockCache, MemStore, and HFiles are merged. The **latest version** (by timestamp) is selected and returned.

**Durability Guarantee:**

The **Write-Ahead Log (WAL)** is the cornerstone of durability. Every write is appended to the WAL *before* the MemStore is updated. On a RegionServer crash:
- HMaster reassigns the region to a healthy RegionServer.
- The new RegionServer **replays the WAL** to reconstruct the MemStore state.
- Once MemStore is reconstructed, normal operations resume.

The WAL is itself stored on HDFS, which provides 3x replication by default, ensuring that even with two disk failures the write is recoverable.

---

**(b) Compare HBase with a traditional RDBMS across at least six dimensions. When would you prefer HBase over an RDBMS? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| # | Dimension | HBase | RDBMS (e.g., MySQL) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Data model** | Wide-column, sparse, schema-flexible | Fixed-schema relational |
| 2 | **Scaling** | Linear horizontal scaling (add RegionServers) | Mostly vertical (scale-up) |
| 3 | **Transactions** | Single-row atomicity only | Full ACID, multi-row |
| 4 | **Query language** | Java API, REST, Thrift, HiveQL, Phoenix SQL | Standard SQL (ANSI) |
| 5 | **Joins & secondary indexes** | Not natively supported; denormalization required | First-class feature |
| 6 | **Consistency** | Strong per-row consistency | Strong, with isolation levels |
| 7 | **Storage size** | Petabytes, billions of rows | Typically GB–TB |
| 8 | **Schema evolution** | Add column qualifiers at write time | Requires `ALTER TABLE` |
| 9 | **Write throughput** | Hundreds of thousands of writes/sec/node | Tens of thousands of writes/sec/node |
| 10 | **Use case fit** | Sparse, write-heavy, OLTP on Big Data | Structured, transactional, OLTP |

**When to Prefer HBase:**

Choose **HBase** when:
- Data volume is in **billions of rows / petabytes** (too large for a single RDBMS).
- Workload is **write-heavy** (e.g., IoT telemetry, click-streams, log aggregation).
- Data is **sparse** — most rows have only a few of the available columns populated.
- Schema is **evolving** — new attributes appear continuously without downtime.
- You need **fast range scans** by row key (HBase is excellent for time-series queries).
- You can **denormalize** your data and do not require multi-row ACID transactions.

Choose **RDBMS** when:
- Workload is **transactional** (multi-row updates, joins, complex queries).
- Data fits in a **single server** and is highly normalized.
- Strong reporting/BI with complex SQL is the primary requirement.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not write a one-sided answer. Examiners want a *balanced comparison*. A common mistake is saying "HBase is better than RDBMS" without acknowledging that HBase **cannot do joins or multi-row transactions**. Always state the trade-off.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mistakes That Cost Marks on "Tables / HBase" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting "sparse"** in the Bigtable definition. The word *sparse* is non-negotiable — losing it costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing MemStore with HFile.** MemStore is **in-RAM**; HFile is **on-disk**. Mixing them up loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Treating HMaster as a data-path node.** HMaster handles *control* (DDL, region assignment), not user reads/writes. -1 to -2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the role of ZooKeeper.** Many students omit it entirely. ZooKeeper provides *cluster coordination* and is mandatory. -1 mark.
> 5. **Writing `NULL` for missing cells.** HBase does **not** store NULL — the cell simply does not exist. -1 mark.
> 6. **Skipping the timestamp/version aspect.** The third dimension of the data model is often missed. -1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Bigtable = sparse, multi-dimensional, sorted map** indexed by `(row, column, timestamp)`.
- **HBase** is the open-source Hadoop implementation of Bigtable running on HDFS.
- The **five-attribute coordinate** of any HBase cell: **Row Key + Column Family + Column Qualifier + Timestamp → Value (byte[])**.
- **Rows are stored in sorted (lexicographic) order** of the row key — this enables fast range scans.
- **Column families are the only schema** declared at creation; column qualifiers can be added freely at write time.
- **HBase is sparse** — non-existent cells consume zero storage (unlike RDBMS NULLs).
- **HMaster** = control plane (region assignment, DDL, recovery).
- **RegionServer** = data plane (handles MemStore, BlockCache, WAL, HFile).
- **ZooKeeper** = cluster coordination (HMaster election, RegionServer liveness).
- **WAL** ensures durability; **MemStore** buffers writes in RAM; **HFile** is the on-disk immutable file.
- **Write path**: Client → WAL → MemStore → (async flush) → HFile.
- **Read path**: Client → BlockCache + MemStore + HFile (with Bloom filter) → merge.
- **Default region size = 10 GB**; default MemStore flush size = **128 MB**; default BlockCache = **40% of heap**; default compaction ratio = **1.2**.
- **Bloom filter optimal hash count** $k = (m/n) \ln 2$; gives ~0.83% FPR at $m/n = 10$.
- **HBase vs RDBMS:** HBase scales horizontally and handles sparse data but lacks multi-row ACID and joins; RDBMS is the opposite.
- **Hotspotting prevention:** Avoid monotonically increasing row keys — use hashing/salting/reversal.
- **Two to four column families per table** is the KTU-recommended design.
- **HFile** uses block index + Bloom filter for fast random reads; it is immutable and sorted.
- **Region splitting** is automatic when a region exceeds the configured max size.
- **Compaction** merges small HFiles into larger ones to reduce read amplification.
- **KTU Key Formulas:** $N_R = \lceil (N \times S) / R \rceil$, $\epsilon = (1 - e^{-kn/m})^k$, $M_g = 0.4 \times H$.
