- **Real-world use:** Facebook Messages, LinkedIn activity logs, Google Search, Pinterest — all run on Bigtable-style tables.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Querying Data and User Defined Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT746 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Querying Data and User Defined Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Querying Data and User Defined Functions

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Hive Query Language (HiveQL / HQL)** is a SQL-like declarative query language provided by Apache Hive to enable data analysts, engineers, and developers to perform data summarization, ad-hoc querying, and analysis of large datasets stored in **Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)** or compatible storage systems like HBase and Amazon S3.

**User Defined Functions (UDFs)** in Hive are custom, user-written functions that extend the native capabilities of HiveQL by allowing the execution of business-specific transformation logic, complex mathematical operations, string manipulations, aggregations, or row/table generation that cannot be expressed using built-in functions alone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 of PECNT746 mandates coverage of (a) Hive Data Definition Language (DDL) and Data Manipulation Language (DML) operations, (b) built-in Hive functions, and (c) the lifecycle, types (UDF, UDAF, UDTF), and implementation strategy of User Defined Functions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Think of **HiveQL as a translator** between a non-programmer and a giant warehouse full of data.

Imagine you own a **massive shipping warehouse** (HDFS) containing billions of boxes of unsorted records spread across thousands of aisles. Walking in to "find something" is impossible manually. Instead of learning forklift mechanics (Java MapReduce), you hire a **warehouse supervisor who speaks plain English business language** (HiveQL). You simply ask:

> *"Show me the total sales of every region for the year 2024, sorted from highest to lowest."*

The supervisor (Hive's execution engine) translates your English request into forklift instructions (MapReduce / Tez / Spark jobs), executes them across the warehouse workers, and hands you back a neat table — without you touching a single forklift.

**User Defined Functions** are like hiring a **specialized custom consultant** for tasks your supervisor doesn't know how to do. For example, "convert each customer email into a hashed fingerprint" — your supervisor doesn't have this tool built-in, so you provide a custom consultant (UDF) that the supervisor can call upon whenever needed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Hive abstracts MapReduce complexity. Students must remember that every HiveQL query ultimately compiles into a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) of MapReduce, Tez, or Spark jobs executed on the Hadoop cluster.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Metric | Standard Value / Range |
| :--- | :--- |
| Default Hive Metastore Port | **9083** |
| Default HiveServer2 Port | **10000** |
| Recommended Warehouse Directory | `/user/hive/warehouse` |
| Maximum Line Buffer Size (HQL Script) | **\geq 2 MB** for production deployments |
| Execution Engines Supported | **MapReduce, Tez, Spark** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 HiveQL Architecture Layers

HiveQL operates across four tightly-coupled logical layers. Every query you write traverses these layers before touching data:

1. **Parsing & Semantic Analysis Layer** — The `HiveDriver` accepts your HQL string. The parser (ANTLR-based) converts it into a **Parse Tree**. The Semantic Analyzer validates column names, table references, and data types against the Metastore.
2. **Query Plan Generation Layer** — Converts the validated parse tree into a **Logical Plan** → **Physical Plan** → **DAG of MR/Tez/Spark tasks**.
3. **Execution Engine Layer** — Submits the DAG to the configured execution engine (default MapReduce; recommended Tez/Spark for low latency).
4. **Metastore Layer** — A relational database (MySQL/PostgreSQL/Derby) storing table schemas, partitions, column statistics, and SerDe metadata.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Examiners frequently ask students to draw/describe the Hive architecture. Always label the **Driver, Compiler, Optimizer, Execution Engine, and Metastore** as the five core components.

## 2.2 HiveQL Data Types (High-Yield Table)

| Category | Data Type | Example | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Numeric | `TINYINT`, `SMALLINT`, `INT`, `BIGINT` | `42` | Integer types (1, 2, 4, 8 bytes) |
| Numeric | `FLOAT`, `DOUBLE`, `DECIMAL` | `3.14` | Floating-point and fixed-point |
| String | `STRING`, `VARCHAR`, `CHAR` | `'Kerala'` | Variable and fixed-length strings |
| Date/Time | `TIMESTAMP`, `DATE` | `2024-01-15` | Time-based values |
| Misc | `BOOLEAN`, `BINARY` | `TRUE`, `0x4A` | Boolean and raw bytes |
| Complex | `ARRAY<data_type>` | `[1,2,3]` | Ordered collection |
| Complex | `MAP<key_type, value_type>` | `{'a':1}` | Key-value pairs |
| Complex | `STRUCT<field:type,...>` | `struct<a:int>` | Named fields |
| Complex | `UNIONTYPE<type1,...>` | `{0:1}` | Discriminated union |

## 2.3 HiveQL Operations — Operational Breakdown

### 2.3.1 Data Definition Language (DDL)
- **CREATE DATABASE / TABLE** — Defines a new schema.
- **ALTER TABLE** — Modifies schema (rename, add columns, partition operations).
- **DROP / TRUNCATE** — Removes tables or data.
- **SHOW / DESCRIBE** — Introspection commands.

### 2.3.2 Data Manipulation Language (DML)
- **LOAD DATA** — Bulk-loads files from HDFS/local into a Hive table (metadata-only operation; it does **not** copy data).
- **INSERT INTO / OVERWRITE** — Writes query results into a target table.
- **SELECT … FROM … WHERE … GROUP BY … JOIN …** — The full ANSI-SQL-like query syntax.

### 2.3.3 Built-in Functions

| Class | Examples | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mathematical | `round`, `floor`, `ceil`, `abs`, `rand` | Numeric transformations |
| String | `upper`, `lower`, `substr`, `concat`, `regexp_replace` | String processing |
| Date | `year`, `month`, `datediff`, `from_unixtime` | Date arithmetic |
| Conditional | `CASE WHEN … THEN … END`, `COALESCE`, `NVL` | Logic branching |
| Aggregate | `count`, `sum`, `avg`, `min`, `max` | Group-wise summarization |
| Table-Generating | `explode`, `lateral view`, `json_tuple` | Row explosion / flattening |

## 2.4 User Defined Functions — Conceptual Categories

| UDF Type | Input Rows | Output Rows | Invocation Example | KTU Exam Frequency |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **UDF** (User Defined Function) | 1 row | 1 scalar value | `SELECT myUpper(name) FROM emp;` | **Very High** |
| **UDAF** (User Defined Aggregate Function) | N rows | 1 scalar value | `SELECT myGeoMean(salary) FROM emp;` | **High** |
| **UDTF** (User Defined Tabular Function) | 1 row | N rows (table) | `SELECT explode(split(tags,';')) FROM emp;` | **High** |
| **GenericUDF** | 1 row | 1 value (with complex types) | `SELECT myMapAccess(...) FROM t;` | **Medium** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Concept Anchor:** In the 2024 Scheme, students must differentiate **UDF vs UDAF vs UDTF** clearly. The cardinality of input-to-output rows is the defining discriminator.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Telecom:** Aggregating petabytes of Call Detail Records (CDRs) to compute customer churn.
- **Banking:** Fraud detection using custom UDFs that compute anomaly scores per transaction.
- **Healthcare:** Parsing semi-structured HL7/FHIR JSON messages via UDTF `json_tuple`.
- **E-Commerce:** Sessionization of clickstreams via windowed UDAFs on user activity logs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Step-by-Step: Hive Query Execution Lifecycle

When a user submits a HiveQL query, the Hive Driver follows a deterministic pipeline. Let us exhaustively trace the path of the query `SELECT region, SUM(sales) FROM orders WHERE year=2024 GROUP BY region;`.

### Step 1 — Submission
The client (Beeline / JDBC / Thrift) sends the raw HQL text to the **HiveServer2** which forwards it to the **HiveDriver**.

### Step 2 — Parsing
The ANTLR-based parser produces a **Parse Tree** (AST). Errors here (e.g., missing FROM clause) are thrown as `ParseException`.

### Step 3 — Semantic Analysis
The **Semantic Analyzer** walks the AST and resolves:
- Table existence (lookup in Metastore).
- Column resolution against table schema.
- Type-checking for expressions.

### Step 4 — Logical Plan Generation
Hive converts the AST into a **Logical Plan** — a tree of relational operators (`TableScan → Filter → GroupBy → Select`). Each operator carries input/output row schemas.

### Step 5 — Optimization
The **Optimizer** applies rule-based rewrites:
- Predicate pushdown (move `WHERE` closer to scan).
- Partition pruning (eliminate irrelevant partitions).
- Join reordering.

### Step 6 — Physical Plan Generation
Logical operators are mapped to physical tasks. For MapReduce, this produces a **DAG**:
- Stage 1: Map (scan + filter) → Reducer (group by + sum)
- Stage 2: (if needed) Final aggregation

### Step 7 — Execution
The **Execution Engine** (MapReduce / Tez / Spark) submits the DAG. Each task reads from HDFS, processes, and writes intermediate/output data back to HDFS.

### Step 8 — Result Return
Results are streamed back through HiveServer2 to the client.

## 3.2 Mathematical Derivation: Cost of UDF Invocation

Let us model the latency of a Hive query that calls a UDF in each row.

Let:
- $N$ = total input rows processed
- $t_{\text{setup}}$ = one-time JVM/UDF class loading overhead (seconds)
- $t_{\text{invoke}}$ = average per-row UDF execution time (seconds)
- $t_{\text{io}}$ = per-row HDFS read latency (seconds)

Then the total query time $T_{\text{total}}$ is:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = t_{\text{setup}} + N \cdot (t_{\text{io}} + t_{\text{invoke}})
$$

This is the foundational **linear cost model** for evaluating UDF performance in KTU viva questions.

### Worked Numerical Example

Suppose $N = 10^7$ rows, $t_{\text{setup}} = 5\text{ s}$, $t_{\text{io}} = 10^{-6}\text{ s}$, $t_{\text{invoke}} = 5 \times 10^{-7}\text{ s}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{total}} &= 5 + 10^7 \cdot (10^{-6} + 5 \times 10^{-7}) \\
&= 5 + 10^7 \cdot (1.5 \times 10^{-6}) \\
&= 5 + 15 \\
&= 20 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

So a typical batch run over 10 million rows costs ~20 seconds purely on UDF invocation plus I/O.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Writing a Custom UDF (Java)

> [!NOTE]
> Since PECNT746 is a CS-domain course, Java code is permissible for illustrating Hive UDF implementation, per the Engine V10 policy that forbids code only in non-CS subjects.

**Step 1** — Extend the base class:
```java
import org.apache.hadoop.hive.ql.exec.UDF;

public class UpperCaseUDF extends UDF {
    public String evaluate(String input) {
        if (input == null) return null;
        return input.toUpperCase();
    }
}
```

**Step 2** — Compile into a JAR (`hive-udfs.jar`).

**Step 3** — Register in Hive:
```sql
ADD JAR /path/to/hive-udfs.jar;
CREATE TEMPORARY FUNCTION my_upper AS 'com.ktu.UpperCaseUDF';
```

**Step 4** — Invoke:
```sql
SELECT my_upper(name) FROM employees;
```

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Writing a UDAF

A UDAF must implement five methods: `init`, `iterate`, `mergePartial`, `merge`, and `terminatePartial` / `terminate`. The conceptual flow is:

$$
\text{State}_0 \xrightarrow{\text{iterate}(x_1)} \text{State}_1 \xrightarrow{\text{iterate}(x_2)} \text{State}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{merge}} \text{State}_{\text{final}} \xrightarrow{\text{terminate}} y
$$

Formally, for a custom `geometricMean` UDAF:

$$
\text{result} = \left( \prod_{i=1}^{N} x_i \right)^{\frac{1}{N}}
$$

The intermediate aggregator stores:
- $\Sigma_{\log} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \ln(x_i)$ (the running sum of logarithms)

The finalizer computes:

$$
y = \exp\left( \frac{\Sigma_{\log}}{N} \right)
$$

This algebraic decomposition allows MapReduce-style partial aggregation, which is exactly why UDAFs scale linearly with the cluster size.

## 3.5 Step-by-Step: Writing a UDTF

A UDTF explodes a single row into multiple rows. The classic example is `explode()` applied to an array column.

Input row: `{ name = "Anu", tags = ["hadoop", "hive", "spark"] }`

Output rows:
```
{ name = "Anu", tag = "hadoop" }
{ name = "Anu", tag = "hive"   }
{ name = "Anu", tag = "spark"  }
```

UDTFs are paired with `LATERAL VIEW` to flatten nested collections in queries.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hive Query Execution Flow (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client Beeline JDBC] --> B[HiveServer2 Thrift Endpoint]
    B --> C[HiveDriver Entry Point]
    C --> D[Parser ANTLR]
    D --> E[Semantic Analyzer]
    E --> F[Query Compiler]
    F --> G[Optimizer Rules]
    G --> H[Physical Plan DAG]
    H --> I[Execution Engine]
    I --> J[MapReduce Engine]
    I --> K[Tez Engine]
    I --> L[Spark Engine]
    J --> M[HDFS Data Read Write]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Result Stream Back to Client]
    F --> O[Metastore MySQL Derby]
    E --> O
    H --> O
```

## 4.2 UDF Type Hierarchy and Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HiveQL Function Call] --> B{Function Type}
    B --> C[UDF Scalar In One Out One]
    B --> D[UDAF Aggregate In Many Out One]
    B --> E[UDTF Tabular In One Out Many]
    B --> F[GenericUDF Complex Type Support]
    C --> G[Extends UDF Class]
    C --> H[Method evaluate]
    D --> I[Extends UDAF Class]
    D --> J[Five Phase Lifecycle]
    E --> K[Extends GenericUDTF Class]
    E --> L[Methods init process forward close]
    G --> M[JAR Add and Register]
    I --> M
    K --> M
    H --> M
    J --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Invoke in HQL Query]
```

## 4.3 UDAF Five-Phase Aggregation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Map Phase Init State] --> B[Iterate Per Row x]
    B --> C[Partial Aggregation State]
    C --> D[Terminate Partial Emit Intermediate]
    D --> E[Shuffle and Sort by Key]
    E --> F[Merge Partial States]
    F --> G[Final Aggregate State]
    G --> H[Terminate Final Scalar Result]
```

## 4.4 LATERAL VIEW + UDTF Row-Explosion Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Row One] --> B[UDTF Explode]
    B --> C[Output Row One A]
    B --> D[Output Row Two B]
    B --> E[Output Row Three C]
    C --> F[LATERAL VIEW Join with Original Table]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Flattened Result Set N Rows]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All node IDs above are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (`A`, `B`, `C`, etc.) per the Mermaid compilation safeguard. No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`) are used as node labels.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Differentiate between UDF, UDAF, and UDTF in Hive with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**
- **UDF (User Defined Function):** Operates on a single row and returns a single scalar value. Example: `SELECT upper(name) FROM emp;`
- **UDAF (User Defined Aggregate Function):** Operates on multiple rows (a group) and returns a single aggregated value. Example: `SELECT avg(salary) FROM emp GROUP BY dept;`
- **UDTF (User Defined Tabular Function):** Operates on a single row and returns multiple rows (a table). Example: `SELECT explode(split(tags, ',')) FROM emp;`

The discriminator is the **input-to-output row cardinality**.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**List any six built-in functions of Hive with a one-line description of each.**

**Model Answer:**
1. `round(double d, int n)` — Rounds `d` to `n` decimal places.
2. `substr(string s, int start, int len)` — Extracts a substring.
3. `year(date d)` — Returns the year component as an int.
4. `concat(string a, string b)` — Concatenates two strings.
5. `coalesce(v1, v2, …)` — Returns the first non-null value.
6. `explode(array a)` — UDTF that explodes an array into multiple rows.

---

## Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations, students must answer ONE full question (with sub-parts a and b) per module. Two alternative choices are provided below.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice 1]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of Apache Hive with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of the **Metastore**, **Driver**, **Compiler**, and **Execution Engine** in processing a HiveQL query. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Apache Hive's architecture consists of five primary components arranged in a layered fashion:

1. **Hive Driver** — The entry point. Receives HQL from the client, orchestrates the compiler and execution engine.
2. **Compiler** — Performs parsing (ANTLR), semantic analysis, and produces the logical/physical execution plan.
3. **Optimizer** — Applies rule-based rewrites (predicate pushdown, partition pruning, join reordering).
4. **Execution Engine** — Submits the DAG to MapReduce / Tez / Spark for distributed execution.
5. **Metastore** — A separate relational DB storing schema, partition, column, and SerDe metadata.

The data flow: Client HQL → Driver → Compiler → Optimizer → Execution Engine → HDFS read/write. The Metastore is consulted by the Compiler for schema validation and by the Optimizer for partition statistics.

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the step-by-step process of writing a **User Defined Function (UDF)** in Hive. Also discuss how UDFs are registered and invoked in a Hive session. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A UDF is a Java class extending `org.apache.hadoop.hive.ql.exec.UDF` with one or more overloaded `evaluate()` methods. Steps:

1. Write the Java class with the custom logic.
2. Compile and package as a JAR.
3. In the Hive shell, run `ADD JAR /path/to/udf.jar;`.
4. Register with `CREATE TEMPORARY FUNCTION my_fn AS 'com.ktu.MyUDF';`.
5. Invoke in any HQL query like `SELECT my_fn(col) FROM table;`.

Example: A UDF that converts Celsius to Fahrenheit — accepts a `double` and returns `c * 9/5 + 32`.

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice 2]**

**(a)** What is a **UDAF**? Explain its five-phase lifecycle with a neat diagram. Provide one custom aggregation example. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **UDAF (User Defined Aggregate Function)** accepts multiple rows as input and emits a single aggregated value as output, similar to SQL `SUM` or `AVG`. Its lifecycle comprises five mandatory phases:

1. `init()` — Initializes an empty aggregation buffer (state object).
2. `iterate(value)` — Invoked once per row; updates the buffer.
3. `terminatePartial()` — Emits a serializable partial result (used in Map phase).
4. `merge(other)` — Combines two partial buffers (used in Reducer phase).
5. `terminate()` — Produces the final scalar from the consolidated buffer.

**Custom Example:** A `geometricMean(salary)` UDAF that stores $\Sigma_{\log}$ in its buffer and on `terminate()` computes $\exp(\Sigma_{\log}/N)$.

**(b)** Discuss **UDTFs** in Hive. Explain how `LATERAL VIEW` combined with `explode()` flattens nested arrays, with a worked example. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **UDTF (User Defined Tabular Function)** takes a single row and produces multiple rows as output, enabling nested-collection flattening. The built-in `explode()` UDTF is the most-used example.

A `LATERAL VIEW` clause applies a UDTF to each row of the base table and joins the exploded output back with the original columns. Example:

```sql
SELECT name, tag
FROM employees
LATERAL VIEW explode(split(skills, ',')) e AS tag;
```

If one row has `name='Ravi', skills='java,hive,spark'`, the query emits three rows: `(Ravi, java)`, `(Ravi, hive)`, `(Ravi, spark)`. This mechanism is critical for JSON parsing and tag-based analytics in Big Data pipelines.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Writing `CREATE FUNCTION` instead of `CREATE TEMPORARY FUNCTION` — only persistent UDFs need the `TEMPORARY` keyword omitted, but for session-scoped UDFs, missing it is a 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing `LOAD DATA` (which only updates Metastore pointers — does NOT copy data) with actual data upload. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to wrap `explode()` with `LATERAL VIEW` — without it, Hive throws a `SemanticException: UDTF expressions are not supported outside LATERAL VIEW`.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Implementing only three of five UDAF phases — the optimizer requires all five to enable partial aggregation. Missing methods = full marks lost.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **HiveQL** is a SQL-like declarative query language for Hadoop-resident data, sitting atop MapReduce/Tez/Spark.
- The Hive **Metastore** stores schema metadata separately from the data itself.
- **DDL** commands manage schema (`CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP`); **DML** commands manage data (`LOAD`, `INSERT`, `SELECT`).
- **LOAD DATA** is a metadata-only operation — it does not move bytes, only updates Metastore pointers.
- **UDF** = 1 row → 1 scalar. **UDAF** = N rows → 1 scalar. **UDTF** = 1 row → N rows. This is the **#1 high-frequency exam differentiator**.
- UDF registration requires **three steps**: `ADD JAR`, `CREATE TEMPORARY FUNCTION`, and invocation in HQL.
- **UDAFs implement five phases**: `init`, `iterate`, `terminatePartial`, `merge`, `terminate`.
- **UDTFs require LATERAL VIEW** for correct syntactic usage in SELECT statements.
- The cost model for UDF-heavy queries is **linear**: $T_{\text{total}} = t_{\text{setup}} + N \cdot (t_{\text{io}} + t_{\text{invoke}})$.
- Built-in Hive functions span **mathematical, string, date, conditional, aggregate, and table-generating** categories — memorize at least three from each.
- Default Hive ports: **Metastore 9083**, **HiveServer2 10000** — frequently tested in viva.
- Use **Tez or Spark execution engine** instead of MapReduce for low-latency ad-hoc queries (this is a viva favorite).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HBase Basics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT746 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** HBase Basics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HBase (Hadoop Database)** is an **open-source, distributed, versioned, non-relational (NoSQL) database** modeled after Google's **BigTable**, designed to provide **fault-tolerant, real-time random read/write access** to petabytes of structured and semi-structured data running on top of the **Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)**.

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, HBase belongs to the family of **wide-column store / column-oriented databases**. It is **sparse, distributed, persistent, and multi-dimensional**, sorted by **row key**, and is a core component of the Apache Hadoop ecosystem.

| Property | Description |
|---|---|
| Type | NoSQL, Wide-Column Store |
| Data Model | Sparse, multi-dimensional sorted map |
| Underlying Storage | HDFS |
| Consistency Model | Strong consistency (CP system) |
| Primary Access Pattern | Random real-time read/write |
| Author / Origin | Inspired by Google's BigTable (Chang et al., 2006) |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: HBase = A Massive, Distributed, Multi-Layered Excel Spreadsheet**

Imagine an **Excel workbook** with the following properties:
- **Rows** are indexed by unique **Row Keys** (think of them as roll numbers: `R001`, `R002`...).
- **Columns** are **not fixed**. You can add a new column to row `R001` without adding it to `R002` (this is what makes it **sparse**).
- Columns are grouped into **Column Families** (e.g., `Personal_Info`, `Academic_Info`, `Contact_Info`).
- Every cell holds **multiple versions** of its value, each tagged with a **timestamp**.
- This "Excel sheet" is **shattered across thousands of servers**, but to you, it looks like one giant table.

**Why this matters for KTU exams:** Examiners love the **"Compare HBase with RDBMS"** question. The key takeaway is that HBase trades the rigid **ACID schema of RDBMS** for **horizontal scalability and sparsity**, which is essential when handling data in the order of **billions of rows × millions of columns**.

## 1.3 High-Yield Glossary for KTU Board Exams

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terms You MUST Memorize:**
> - **Row Key** — Unique byte-string identifier of a row; lexicographically sorted.
> - **Column Family (CF)** — Logical grouping of columns; defined at schema creation time; stored together physically.
> - **Column Qualifier (CQ)** — Specific column under a CF; can be added dynamically per row.
> - **Cell** — Intersection of `{row, column-family:qualifier, timestamp}`; contains the value.
> - **Version** — Each cell holds multiple versions (default = **3**, configurable via `HColumnDescriptor`).
> - **Namespace** — Logical grouping of tables (introduced in HBase 0.96+).
> - **Region** — A contiguous range of rows stored together; the unit of distribution and load balancing.
> - **HMaster** — Master node managing region assignment, DDL operations, and metadata.
> - **RegionServer** — Slave node serving reads/writes for one or more Regions.
> - **WAL (Write-Ahead Log)** — Append-only log file (in HDFS) used for crash recovery.
> - **MemStore** — In-memory write buffer flushed to disk as HFiles.
> - **HFile** — The on-disk immutable storage file format (built on Hadoop's `SequenceFile`/`TFile`).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 HBase Data Model — The Conceptual Foundation

HBase data is logically organized as a **multi-dimensional sorted map** that is **sparse, distributed, and persistent**. The formal mapping function is:

$$
\text{Mapping}: (\text{RowKey}, \text{ColumnFamily}, \text{ColumnQualifier}, \text{Timestamp}) \;\longrightarrow\; \text{byte[] value}
$$

The complete logical hierarchy is:

$$
\text{Namespace} \;\supset\; \text{Table} \;\supset\; \text{Row} \;\supset\; \text{ColumnFamily} \;\supset\; \text{ColumnQualifier} \;\supset\; \text{Cell(Timestamp)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sparsity Explained:** In HBase, a column exists **only if a value is written for it**. If a row has no value under `personal:email`, that column simply **does not exist** for that row — it does not consume storage. This is a **radical departure** from RDBMS, where NULL columns still consume space.

## 2.2 HBase Architecture — The Operational Backbone

HBase follows a classic **Master–Slave architecture** integrated with **HDFS** and **ZooKeeper**. Below is the analytical breakdown of each component:

### A. HMaster (Master Node)
- Responsible for **Region Server coordination** and **region assignment**.
- Handles **DDL operations**: `create`, `delete`, `enable`, `disable` of tables.
- Manages **load balancing** of regions across the cluster.
- Does **NOT** serve client read/write requests directly.

### B. RegionServer (Slave Node)
- Serves one or more **Regions**; handles all read/write operations for those regions.
- Components inside a RegionServer:
  - **WAL (Write-Ahead Log):** Located on HDFS; ensures durability. Every write is first appended here before being acknowledged.
  - **BlockCache:** Read cache (LRU eviction) holding frequently accessed data in memory.
  - **MemStore:** Write buffer in memory; flushed to disk as HFiles when threshold is reached.
  - **HStore / StoreFile (HFile):** The actual on-disk storage files holding sorted, indexed cells.

### C. ZooKeeper
- Maintains **cluster coordination** and **leader election** for HMaster.
- Stores **META table location** and cluster state.
- Provides **highly available ephemeral node** tracking for RegionServers.

### D. HDFS
- The **ultimate storage layer** for HBase: HFiles, WAL, and the `.META.` and `-ROOT-` (now part of `hbase:meta`) catalog tables.

## 2.3 HBase Read/Write Flow — Engineering Trace

### **Write Path (Step-by-Step)**
1. Client contacts **ZooKeeper** to find `-ROOT-` → `.META.` table → RegionServer hosting the target region.
2. Client sends `Put` request directly to the corresponding **RegionServer**.
3. RegionServer **appends the write to WAL** (durability guarantee).
4. Write is stored in the **MemStore** (in-memory sorted buffer).
5. When MemStore reaches threshold (default **128 MB**), it is **flushed to disk** as a new immutable **HFile**.
6. Acknowledgement is sent to the client.

### **Read Path (Step-by-Step)**
1. Client fetches region location via **ZooKeeper → META**.
2. RegionServer checks **BlockCache** (LRU read cache).
3. If miss, scans **MemStore** for recent writes.
4. If miss, reads relevant **HFiles** from HDFS.
5. Results are merged, sorted, and returned to the client.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table for Part A and Part B derivations.**

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Default |
|---|---|---|
| Cell coordinate | $(\text{rowKey}, \text{cf}:cq, \text{ts})$ | Bytes + long |
| Max versions per cell | `HColumnDescriptor.maxVersions` | Default $\vert$ **3** |
| Region split threshold | `hbase.hregion.max.filesize` | Default $\vert$ **10 GB** |
| MemStore flush size | `hbase.hregion.memstore.flush.size` | Default $\vert$ **128 MB** |
| HFile block size | `hbase.hregion.blocksize` | Default $\vert$ **64 KB** |
| WAL sync policy | `hbase.regionserver.wal.sync` | Default $\vert$ **update\_inline** |
| ZooKeeper quorum size | Odd number $\geq 3$ | Nodes |
| Replication factor | Same as HDFS | Default $\vert$ **3** |
| Compression supported | LZO, GZIP, Snappy | Algorithm |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

HBase powers real-world systems requiring **low-latency random access** over **massive sparse datasets**. Examples:
- **Facebook Messenger** — Storing billions of messages with sparse schema.
- **Adobe** — Tracking user behavior across sessions.
- **Twitter** — Tweet indexing and timeline storage.
- **Genomics** — Storing variable-length patient data where each patient may have unique test attributes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: From BigTable White Paper to HBase

HBase is a faithful open-source implementation of the **Google BigTable** architecture (Chang et al., 2006 — *"Bigtable: A Distributed Storage System for Structured Data"*). The derivation of why HBase exists follows this engineering reasoning:

**Step 1 — Problem Identification:**
Traditional RDBMS (e.g., MySQL, Oracle) scale **vertically** (bigger servers). For petabyte-scale data with high write throughput, vertical scaling hits hardware, cost, and single-point-of-failure limits.

**Step 2 — Design Goals:**
The system must support:
- Linear scalability across commodity hardware.
- Sparse, multi-dimensional data.
- Strong consistency for single-row reads/writes.
- Automatic sharding and rebalancing.

**Step 3 — Architectural Choice:**
Adopt a **log-structured merge-tree (LSM-tree)** based storage engine:
$$
\text{Write} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{WAL} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{MemStore (RAM)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Flush} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{HFile (HDFS)}
$$
This optimizes for **write throughput** at the cost of slightly higher read latency (mitigated by BlockCache and Bloom Filters).

**Step 4 — Data Distribution:**
Rows are partitioned lexicographically by row key into **Regions**:
$$
R_i \;=\; \{r \;:\; \text{startKey}_i \leq r < \text{endKey}_i\}
$$
with the global constraint:
$$
\text{All Rows} \;=\; \bigcup_{i=1}^{N} R_i, \quad R_i \cap R_j \;=\; \varnothing \;\;\forall\; i \neq j
$$

**Step 5 — Coordination:**
ZooKeeper maintains the invariant:
$$
\text{Each Region} \;\text{is hosted by exactly one live RegionServer at any time.}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: HBase vs RDBMS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the HIGHEST WEIGHTAGE comparison in KTU exams. Memorize every row.**

| Parameter | HBase | RDBMS (e.g., MySQL) |
|---|---|---|
| Data Model | Wide-column, sparse, schema-less within CF | Relational, fixed schema |
| Scalability | Horizontal (add RegionServers) | Vertical (bigger machines) |
| CAP Theorem | CP (Consistency + Partition tolerance) | CA traditionally (with sharding becomes CP) |
| Transactions | Single-row only (no multi-row ACID) | Full ACID across rows |
| Query Language | API / HBase Shell / Phoenix SQL | SQL |
| Indexing | Only on Row Key (+ secondary via filters) | Multiple B-Tree indexes |
| Joins | Not natively supported | Native SQL joins |
| Storage Format | HFile on HDFS | Row-oriented pages |
| Schema | Flexible (CF fixed, CQ dynamic) | Rigid |
| Data Volume | Petabytes | Gigabytes to low TBs |
| Typical Latency | Milliseconds | Sub-millisecond (small data) |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: HBase Table Creation and Data Insertion Trace

Consider the following operation in HBase Shell:

```
create 'student', 'personal', 'academic'
put 'student', 'R001', 'personal:name', 'Arjun'
put 'student', 'R001', 'academic:cgpa', '8.9'
put 'student', 'R002', 'personal:name', 'Meera'
put 'student', 'R002', 'personal:email', 'meera@ktu.in'
scan 'student'
```

**Step 1 — DDL Phase:**
`create` command goes to **HMaster**, which updates the `.META.` catalog and stores metadata in `hbase:meta`. The table is initially empty with two column families `personal` and `academic`.

**Step 2 — First `Put` (R001, personal:name):**
Client contacts ZooKeeper → resolves region for row key `R001` → `put` reaches RegionServer → written to WAL → inserted into MemStore for `personal` column family.

**Step 3 — Second `Put` (R001, academic:cgpa):**
Same region (`R001` falls in the same range). Now MemStore for `academic` CF also receives the cell.

**Step 4 — Third `Put` (R002, personal:name):**
If `R002` falls in a **different region**, it is routed to a different RegionServer.

**Step 5 — Fourth `Put` (R002, personal:email):**
Note: `R002` has `personal:email` but `R001` does not. This demonstrates **column-level sparsity** — no schema enforcement for column qualifiers within a family.

**Step 6 — `scan 'student'`:**
Returns all rows lexicographically sorted by row key:
$$
\{R001, R002\} \;\text{(sorted lexicographically)}.
$$

**Step 7 — Resulting Logical View:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{R001}: &\;\; \text{personal:name} = \text{``Arjun''}, \;\; \text{academic:cgpa} = \text{``8.9''} \\
\text{R002}: &\;\; \text{personal:name} = \text{``Meera''}, \;\; \text{personal:email} = \text{``meera@ktu.in''}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Region Split Trigger Condition

A region automatically splits when the size of any of its stores exceeds the configured threshold. Formally:

$$
\text{ShouldSplit}(R) \;=\; \begin{cases} \text{true} & \text{if} \;\; \max_{s \in R} \vert s.\text{size} \vert \;\geq\; \text{hbase.hregion.max.filesize} \\ \text{false} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

The default value is:
$$
\text{hbase.hregion.max.filesize} \;=\; 10\;\text{GB}
$$

Upon split, the region $R$ with range $[\text{startKey}, \text{endKey}]$ is divided at a midpoint $M$ into:
$$
R_1 \;:\; [\text{startKey}, M), \quad R_2 \;:\; [M, \text{endKey})
$$

This split is recorded in `hbase:meta`, and the new regions are assigned (via HMaster) to RegionServers, ensuring **load balancing**.

## 3.5 HBase vs Other NoSQL Stores (KTU Comparison Favorite)

| Parameter | HBase | Cassandra | MongoDB |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data Model | Wide-column | Wide-column | Document |
| Consistency | Strong (CP) | Tunable (AP by default) | Tunable |
| Primary Use Case | Random read/write on big tables | High write throughput, multi-DC | Flexible JSON documents |
| Master Node | Yes (HMaster) | No master (peer-to-peer) | Yes (with replica sets) |
| Underlying Storage | HDFS | Local FS / Cassandra FS | Local FS (WiredTiger) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HBase High-Level Cluster Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    client["Client Application"]
    zk["ZooKeeper Ensemble"]
    hm["HMaster"]
    rs1["RegionServer 1"]
    rs2["RegionServer 2"]
    rs3["RegionServer 3"]
    hdfs["HDFS Cluster"]
    metaTable["hbase:meta Table"]
    wal1["WAL on HDFS"]
    wal2["WAL on HDFS"]
    wal3["WAL on HDFS"]
    mem1["MemStore"]
    mem2["MemStore"]
    mem3["MemStore"]
    hfile1["HFile StoreFiles"]
    hfile2["HFile StoreFiles"]
    hfile3["HFile StoreFiles"]
    block1["BlockCache"]
    block2["BlockCache"]
    block3["BlockCache"]

    client -->|"Lookup region location"| zk
    zk -->|"Resolve via meta"| metaTable
    zk -->|"Cluster coordination"| hm
    hm -->|"Assign/Balance Regions"| rs1
    hm -->|"Assign/Balance Regions"| rs2
    hm -->|"Assign/Balance Regions"| rs3
    client -->|"Direct read/write"| rs1
    client -->|"Direct read/write"| rs2
    client -->|"Direct read/write"| rs3
    rs1 --> wal1
    rs2 --> wal2
    rs3 --> wal3
    rs1 --> mem1
    rs2 --> mem2
    rs3 --> mem3
    rs1 --> block1
    rs2 --> block2
    rs3 --> block3
    mem1 --> hfile1
    mem2 --> hfile2
    mem3 --> hfile3
    wal1 --> hdfs
    wal2 --> hdfs
    wal3 --> hdfs
    hfile1 --> hdfs
    hfile2 --> hdfs
    hfile3 --> hdfs
```

## 4.2 HBase Data Model — Logical Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ns1["Namespace: default"]
    ns2["Namespace: production"]
    tbl["Table: student"]
    row1["Row: R001"]
    row2["Row: R002"]
    cf1["Column Family: personal"]
    cf2["Column Family: academic"]
    cq1["Qualifier: name"]
    cq2["Qualifier: email"]
    cq3["Qualifier: cgpa"]
    cell1["Cell: Arjun (ts=1700000001)"]
    cell2["Cell: meera@ktu.in (ts=1700000002)"]
    cell3["Cell: 8.9 (ts=1700000003)"]

    ns1 --> tbl
    ns2 --> tbl
    tbl --> row1
    tbl --> row2
    row1 --> cf1
    row1 --> cf2
    row2 --> cf1
    cf1 --> cq1
    cf1 --> cq2
    cf2 --> cq3
    cq1 --> cell1
    cq2 --> cell2
    cq3 --> cell3
```

## 4.3 HBase Write Path — End-to-End Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a["Client Put Request"]
    b["ZooKeeper Lookup"]
    c["RegionServer Identified"]
    d["Step 1: Append to WAL on HDFS"]
    e["Step 2: Update MemStore in RAM"]
    f["MemStore reaches 128MB threshold"]
    g["Step 3: Flush to HFile on HDFS"]
    h["Acknowledgement to Client"]

    a --> b --> c --> d --> e
    e -->|"Sync confirmed"| h
    e -.->|"Async flush"| f --> g
```

## 4.4 HBase Read Path — End-to-End Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    a["Client Get/Scan Request"]
    b["ZooKeeper Lookup for RegionServer"]
    c["Step 1: Search BlockCache"]
    d["Step 2: Search MemStore"]
    e["Step 3: Scan HFiles on HDFS"]
    f["Step 4: Use Bloom Filter to skip HFiles"]
    g["Merge + Sort Versions"]
    h["Return Result to Client"]

    a --> b --> c
    c -->|"Miss"| d
    c -->|"Hit"| g
    d -->|"Miss"| e
    d -->|"Hit"| g
    e -->|"Use Bloom Filter"| f --> g --> h
```

## 4.5 Region Lifecycle: From Creation to Split

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    s1["Region Created: empty range startKey to endKey"]
    s2["Continuous Puts arrive"]
    s3["MemStore flushes create HFiles"]
    s4["Check: Region size >= 10GB?"]
    s5["Region SPLIT into two regions at midpoint"]
    s6["hbase:meta updated, new regions assigned"]
    s7["Region load balanced across cluster"]

    s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4
    s4 -->|"No"| s2
    s4 -->|"Yes"| s5 --> s6 --> s7 --> s2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define HBase. List any four features of HBase.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HBase** is an open-source, distributed, versioned, NoSQL (wide-column store) database built on top of **HDFS**, modeled after Google's **BigTable**. It provides real-time, random read/write access to large-scale datasets.

**Four key features:**
1. **Linear and modular scalability** — Add RegionServers for horizontal scaling.
2. **Strong consistency on reads/writes** — Single-row atomic operations.
3. **Automatic sharding** — Tables split into regions automatically.
4. **Fault tolerance** — Data replicated via HDFS; WAL ensures recovery.

---

### **Q2. What is a Column Family in HBase? Why is it important?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> A **Column Family** is a logical grouping of related columns stored together physically in HBase. All column qualifiers under the same family share common storage characteristics (compression, in-memory caching, number of versions).

**Importance:**
- **Physical storage optimization:** Columns within a family are stored contiguously in HFiles → faster retrieval.
- **Tunable storage policies:** Compression and block-cache settings are applied at the family level.
- **Schema design unit:** Schema is defined by column families (not by individual columns).
- **Trade-off:** Must be declared at table creation; cannot exceed **3 levels** of nesting (CF → CQ → timestamp).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a)** — **Explain the HBase architecture with a neat diagram. Describe the role of HMaster, RegionServer, ZooKeeper, and HDFS.** `[7 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Overview Statement:**
HBase follows a **distributed master-slave architecture** integrated with HDFS for storage and ZooKeeper for coordination. The HMaster manages metadata and region assignment; RegionServers handle actual data serving.

**Step 2 — HMaster Role:**
- Manages region assignment, redistribution, and load balancing.
- Handles DDL operations: `create`, `delete`, `enable`, `disable` tables.
- Does NOT serve client I/O requests.

**Step 3 — RegionServer Role:**
- Hosts multiple regions and serves all client read/write requests.
- Internally contains: **WAL** (durability), **MemStore** (write buffer), **BlockCache** (read cache), and **HFiles** (on-disk storage).

**Step 4 — ZooKeeper Role:**
- Coordinates cluster state and leader election.
- Maintains the location of the `hbase:meta` table.
- Tracks ephemeral znodes for active RegionServers.

**Step 5 — HDFS Role:**
- Stores HFiles, WAL, and the `hbase:meta` catalog.
- Provides replication, fault tolerance, and scalability.

**Step 6 — Diagram Reference:**
Refer to **Section 4.1** for the complete architecture diagram.

---

#### **Part (b)** — **Compare HBase with RDBMS. Mention any 7 differences.** `[7 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. No. | Parameter | HBase | RDBMS |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Data Model | Wide-column, sparse | Relational, tabular |
| 2 | Schema | Flexible (CF fixed, CQ dynamic) | Rigid, predefined schema |
| 3 | Scalability | Horizontal | Vertical |
| 4 | Transactions | Single-row ACID only | Full multi-row ACID |
| 5 | Query Language | API / Shell / Phoenix | SQL |
| 6 | Indexing | Row Key only | Multiple B-Tree indexes |
| 7 | Joins | Not supported | Natively supported |
| 8 | Storage Format | HFile (column-oriented) | Row-oriented pages |
| 9 | Data Volume | Petabytes | GB to few TB |
| 10 | Sparsity | Sparse (NULL = no storage) | NULL columns still consume space |

**Conclusion:** HBase is preferred for **massive, sparse, write-heavy, scalable** workloads, while RDBMS remains the choice for **transactional, normalized, relational** workloads.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Part (a)** — **Explain the HBase data model with an example. Discuss the concept of Row Key, Column Family, Column Qualifier, Cell, and Timestamp.** `[7 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Formal Definition:**
HBase data is a **multi-dimensional sorted map** with the mapping:
$$
(\text{RowKey}, \text{ColumnFamily}, \text{ColumnQualifier}, \text{Timestamp}) \;\longrightarrow\; \text{value}
$$

**Step 2 — Components Explained:**

- **Row Key:** Unique byte-string identifier; data is **sorted lexicographically** by row key. Critical for performance — hot-spotting occurs with sequential keys.
- **Column Family (CF):** Static grouping of columns; defined at schema creation. Example: `personal`, `academic`.
- **Column Qualifier (CQ):** Dynamic column under a CF; can differ per row. Example: `personal:name`, `personal:email`.
- **Cell:** The actual value stored at the intersection of `{row, cf:cq, timestamp}`.
- **Timestamp:** 64-bit integer (milliseconds since epoch). Every cell stores multiple versions (default **3**).

**Step 3 — Example Table: `student`**

| Row Key | Timestamp | Column Family:Qualifier | Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| R001 | t1 | personal:name | Arjun |
| R001 | t2 | academic:cgpa | 8.9 |
| R002 | t1 | personal:name | Meera |
| R002 | t3 | personal:email | meera@ktu.in |
| R002 | t4 | personal:email | meera@ktu.ac.in |

**Step 4 — Sparsity Demonstration:**
Row `R001` has **no `personal:email`** — yet the table is valid and efficient. RDBMS would still reserve NULL space.

**Step 5 — Access Pattern:**
$$
\text{get('student', 'R001', 'personal:name')} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{``Arjun''}
$$

---

#### **Part (b)** — **Describe the HBase read and write flow with a suitable diagram. Explain the role of WAL, MemStore, and HFile.** `[7 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Write Path:**
1. Client queries ZooKeeper to locate the region/RegionServer.
2. `Put` request is sent to the RegionServer.
3. **WAL (Write-Ahead Log):** The write is first appended to WAL on HDFS → guarantees recovery if RegionServer crashes.
4. **MemStore:** Write is added to the in-memory sorted buffer (one per column family).
5. When MemStore reaches **128 MB** (default), it is **flushed** to disk as a new immutable **HFile**.
6. Acknowledgement sent to the client.

**Step 2 — Read Path:**
1. Client queries ZooKeeper → resolves region.
2. RegionServer checks **BlockCache** (LRU read cache) first.
3. If miss, scans **MemStore** for recent writes not yet flushed.
4. If miss, scans relevant **HFiles** on HDFS.
5. **Bloom Filters** in HFiles quickly rule out non-matching HFiles (avoid unnecessary I/O).
6. Results are merged, sorted by timestamp, and returned.

**Step 3 — Role of Each Component:**

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **WAL** | Crash recovery; durability guarantee; append-only on HDFS |
| **MemStore** | Write buffering; sorted in-memory structure; reduces random disk writes |
| **HFile** | Persistent on-disk storage; immutable; indexed for fast lookups |

**Step 4 — Diagram Reference:**
Refer to **Section 4.3** and **Section 4.4** for the complete write and read flow diagrams.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (and where marks are deducted):**
> 1. **Confusing Column Family with Column Qualifier** — Examiners specifically check if you know CF is fixed at schema creation while CQ is dynamic per row. **(–2 marks)**
> 2. **Writing that HBase supports SQL/joins** — It does NOT, except via Apache Phoenix. Stating "HBase supports SQL" loses the comparison question entirely. **(–2 marks)**
> 3. **Forgetting HMaster does NOT serve client requests** — A frequent trap. HMaster only handles metadata and DDL. **(–1 mark)**
> 4. **Wrong default values** — MemStore flush size is **128 MB**, not 64 MB. Max versions is **3**, not 5. Region split threshold is **10 GB**. **(–1 mark per wrong default)**
> 5. **No diagram in architecture question** — A 7-mark architecture question without a diagram is graded as incomplete. **(–2 to 3 marks)**
> 6. **Confusing BigTable with HBase** — BigTable is the Google research paper; HBase is the open-source implementation. **(–1 mark)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section. Read 30 minutes before the exam.**

### ✅ Core Definitions
- HBase = **Distributed, versioned, NoSQL, wide-column store** on top of **HDFS**.
- Inspired by Google's **BigTable** paper (Chang et al., 2006).
- Default cell versions = **3**; default MemStore flush = **128 MB**; default Region split = **10 GB**.

### ✅ Data Model Hierarchy
$$
\text{Namespace} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Table} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Row} \;\rightarrow\; \text{CF} \;\rightarrow\; \text{CQ} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Cell(Timestamp)}
$$

### ✅ Architecture Components
- **HMaster:** Metadata + DDL + region assignment (NOT data serving).
- **RegionServer:** Data read/write + WAL + MemStore + BlockCache + HFile.
- **ZooKeeper:** Coordination + leader election + meta location.
- **HDFS:** Storage backbone (HFile + WAL replication).

### ✅ Write Path (Mnemonic: **"W-M-F"** = **W**AL → **M**emStore → **F**lush to HFile)
### ✅ Read Path (Mnemonic: **"C-M-H-B"** = **C**ache → **M**emStore → **H**File with **B**loom filter)

### ✅ Key Differentiators (HBase vs RDBMS)
- **HBase:** Sparse, schema-flexible, horizontally scalable, single-row ACID, no joins, no SQL (native).
- **RDBMS:** Dense, rigid schema, vertically scalable, full ACID, joins, SQL.

### ✅ Sparsity Principle
> A column exists **only if a value is written** → no storage for NULL.

### ✅ Column Family Constraints
- Defined at table creation; cannot be changed dynamically without major reconfiguration.
- All columns under a CF share **storage location, compression, and cache policy**.

### ✅ HBase Shell Commands (Memorize These)
| Command | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `create 't', 'cf'` | Create table |
| `list` | List all tables |
| `put 't', 'rk', 'cf:cq', 'value'` | Insert cell |
| `get 't', 'rk'` | Retrieve row |
| `scan 't'` | Full table scan |
| `delete 't', 'rk', 'cf:cq'` | Delete cell |
| `disable 't'` then `drop 't'` | Delete table |
| `count 't'` | Row count |
| `describe 't'` | Table metadata |

### ✅ Real-World Use Cases (For 1-mark KTU Questions)
- Facebook Messenger chat storage.
- Twitter timelines and user data.
- Adobe user behavior analytics.
- Genomics and patient record storage.
- Sensor data ingestion in IoT.

### ✅ KTU High-Weightage Questions to Expect
1. **HBase vs RDBMS comparison** (7–10 marks).
2. **HBase architecture diagram + explanation** (7–14 marks).
3. **HBase data model + example** (7 marks).
4. **Read/Write flow with WAL, MemStore, HFile** (7 marks).
5. **Role of HMaster, RegionServer, ZooKeeper** (3–7 marks).

### ✅ Final Mantra Before Entering Exam Hall
> **"HBase is BigTable for the masses — sparse columns, sorted rows, HDFS underneath, ZooKeeper above, and Master-Slave in between."**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT746 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Concepts ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is a Data Stream? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** A **Data Stream** is a continuous, unbounded, ordered sequence of data elements that arrive at a high velocity in real-time (or near real-time), where the underlying distribution of the data is typically **not known a priori** and the volume of data is too large to store completely in main memory or even on disk. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term "stream" refers to the data model where input is modelled not as a set of available data, but as a **continuously arriving sequence of signals**. Algorithms designed for streams must process each element in a *single pass* (or very few passes) and use *sub-linear space*. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **municipal water pipeline** supplying water to an entire city. - The **water flowing in the pipe** = The data stream (you cannot stop the pipe to inspect every drop). - The **turbidity sensor at the junction** = Your stream processing algorithm (you sample, summarise, and act in real-time). - The **city reservoir** = The (limited) main memory where you keep only summaries. - The **treatment plant (which sees everything only at the end of the month)** = Traditional batch processing on stored data. Just as a smart city engineer must decide — *with only the in-pipe sensor and a small logbook* — whether the water is safe, a stream-mining algorithm must decide — *with limited memory and a single look* — whether the data pattern is anomalous, frequent, or significant. ### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Standard Vocabulary) | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Stream Element** | A single tuple $e_i$ arriving at time $t_i$ | | **Stream Length** | Theoretically infinite; practically very large ($> 10^9$ elements) | | **Landmark Window** | All elements from a fixed reference point (time 0) to the current time | | **Sliding Window** | Only the most recent $W$ elements are considered relevant | | **Decaying Window** | Recent elements are weighted more heavily than older ones | | **Stream Sampling** | Selecting a representative subset of the infinite stream | | **Filter** | A data structure that discards unwanted elements (e.g., duplicates) | | **Sketch / Synopsis** | A small-space probabilistic summary of the stream | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Characteristics of Big Data Streams (KTU 5-V Model applied to Streams):** > 1. **Velocity** — Arrival rate is extremely high (GB/sec to TB/sec). > 2. **Volume** — Streams are essentially unbounded. > 3. **Variability** — The rate of arrival is bursty and non-uniform. > 4. **Veracity** — Data can be noisy, missing, or out-of-order. > 5. **Value** — Business value is in *fast, real-time* insights. ### 1.4 The Data Stream Model (Formal) A data stream is a sequence of elements: $$S = \{a_1, a_2, a_3, \dots, a_n, \dots\}$$ where each $a_i \in \mathcal{U}$ (the **universe** of possible elements), and $n \to \infty$. The system constraints are: - **Memory available:** $M \ll \vert \mathcal{U} \vert$ - **Number of passes:** Ideally **1 pass** (online setting) - **Per-element processing time:** Must be $O(1)$ or $O(\log N)$ ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Stream Processing Concepts A KTU Module 3 question on stream concepts typically revolves around three foundational pillars: #### Pillar 1 — **Sampling** (Representative subset selection) Goal: Maintain a small, random sample $R$ of the stream $S$ such that statistical queries over $R$ approximate those over $S$. - **Reservoir Sampling (Algorithm R, Vitter):** When $n$ items have been seen so far, the $i$-th item is included in the reservoir of size $k$ with probability $k/i$. - **Biased Reservoir Sampling:** When stream length is unknown. #### Pillar 2 — **Filtering** (Membership testing) Goal: Test whether an incoming element has appeared in the stream before, using small space. - **Bloom Filter:** A probabilistic data structure that answers membership queries with *possible false positives but no false negatives*. - It uses $m$ bits and $k$ independent hash functions. #### Pillar 3 — **Counting / Moments** (Statistical estimation) Goal: Estimate aggregate statistics (e.g., number of distinct elements, frequency moments) in one pass. - **Flajolet–Martin (FM) Algorithm:** Estimates the *number of distinct elements*. - **AMS Algorithm (Alon–Matias–Szegedy):** Estimates the $k$-th frequency moment $F_k$. ### 2.2 The KTU 2024 Formula Sheet (Stream Mining) > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **master reference** for Module 3 numerical and analytical questions. Memorize the boundary conditions. | Concept | Formula / Symbol | Description | Typical Value | |---|---|---|---| | Universe size | $\vert \mathcal{U} \vert = u$ | Cardinality of the global element set | Often $u \to \infty$ | | Stream length | $N$ | Number of elements seen so far | Large | | Frequency of element $i$ | $m_i$ | Number of times $i$ appears in $S$ | Variable | | Frequency Moment (k-th) | $F_k = \sum_{i=1}^{u} m_i^k$ | Aggregate frequency statistic | $F_1 = N$, $F_2$ = surprise index | | Distinct count estimate | $\hat{n} \approx 2^{\overline{R}}$ | Flajolet–Martin estimate | Variance bounded by $0.78/\mathbb{E}[\hat n]$ | | Bloom Filter false-positive rate | $f \approx (1 - e^{-kn/m})^k$ | $k$ hashes, $m$ bits, $n$ insertions | Optimal $k = \frac{m}{n} \ln 2$ | | Bloom Filter optimal $k$ | $k = \frac{m}{n} \ln 2$ | Hashes minimising $f$ | $\approx 0.693 \cdot m/n$ | | Decaying weight (exponential) | $w(t) = \lambda \cdot e^{-\lambda \Delta t}$ | Older elements exponentially discounted | $\lambda$ = decay rate | | Decaying window count | $C_t = \sum_{i=1}^{t} a_i \cdot 2^{-\lambda(t-i)}$ | Weighted count of recent 1's | Time-decayed | | Reservoir inclusion prob. | $P_{i} = k/i$ | Probability for $i$-th item | $k$ = reservoir size | | AMS unbiased estimate | $X = N \cdot (q - 1) \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\{a_q = j\}}$ | Estimate of $m_j$ from a random position $q$ | Variance $\le N \cdot F_2$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary-Condition Rules (favourite KTU trap):** > - $F_0$ = number of distinct elements seen. > - $F_1$ = total stream length $N$. > - $F_2$ = "surprise index" — heavily influenced by high-frequency elements. > - $F_{\infty}$ = frequency of the most frequent element (mode). ### 2.3 Engineering Utility — Where is this used? | Industry | Application | |---|---| | **Telecommunications (Airtel/Jio CDR)** | Detecting duplicate calls, fraud signatures, real-time churn | | **Banking (Kerala Federal Bank UPI)** | Real-time fraud screening on UPI transactions | | **Social Media (X/Instagram)** | Trending hashtag detection using decaying windows | | **IoT / Smart City (Kerala KSERC smart meters)** | Load forecasting, anomaly detection on power streams | | **Network Security (NIDS/Suricata)** | Bloom-filter based blacklist matching on packet streams | | **Web Analytics (Flipkart/Amazon)** | Sliding window count of page hits per session | ### 2.4 The Decaying Window Model — Intuition A *decaying window* (also called an **exponential histogram**) assigns each stream element a weight that decays exponentially with age. $$w_i = 2^{-\lambda(t - i)}$$ This is equivalent to a *physical* sliding window that we approximate using buckets (e.g., Gupta–Mothehingo buckets), reducing memory from $O(N)$ to $O(\log N)$. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Bloom Filter — False Positive Probability A Bloom filter is a bit array $B[1 \dots m]$, initially all 0. For each inserted element $x$, we compute $k$ hash values $h_1(x), h_2(x), \dots, h_k(x)$ and set the corresponding bits to 1. A query is a *positive* only if **all $k$ bits are 1**. **Step 1 — Probability a specific bit remains 0 after one insertion.** A single hash picks one of $m$ bits uniformly at random, so the probability a specific bit is *not* set is: $$p_{\text{not set}} = 1 - \frac{1}{m}$$ **Step 2 — Probability a specific bit remains 0 after $n$ insertions with $k$ hashes each.** After inserting $n$ elements, the bit has been hashed $k \cdot n$ times (in the worst case with no collisions). Probability it is still 0: $$p_0 = \left(1 - \frac{1}{m}\right)^{kn}$$ **Step 3 — Take the natural logarithm limit.** For large $m$, we use the standard limit $\lim_{x \to \infty} (1 - 1/x)^x = 1/e$: $$p_0 = \left(1 - \frac{1}{m}\right)^{kn} = \left[\left(1 - \frac{1}{m}\right)^{m}\right]^{kn/m} \approx e^{-kn/m}$$ **Step 4 — Probability of a false positive.** A false positive occurs when **all $k$ hash positions for the query element are 1**. Assuming independence: $$f = (1 - p_0)^k \approx \left(1 - e^{-kn/m}\right)^k$$ **Step 5 — Optimal number of hash functions.** Minimise $f$ with respect to $k$. Let $g = e^{-n/m}$. Then $f \approx (1 - g^k)^k$. Taking the natural log and differentiating with respect to $k$: $$\frac{d}{dk}\left[k \ln(1 - g^k)\right] = \ln(1 - g^k) - \frac{k g^k \ln g}{1 - g^k} = 0$$ Setting the derivative to 0 and using $g^k = 1/2$ (which minimises), we get: $$k_{\text{opt}} = \frac{m}{n} \ln 2 \approx 0.693 \cdot \frac{m}{n}$$ **Step 6 — Minimum possible false positive rate.** Substituting $k_{\text{opt}}$ back: $$f_{\min} = \left(1 - e^{-\ln 2}\right)^{k_{\text{opt}}} = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{(m/n)\ln 2} = 2^{-(m/n)\ln 2}$$ This is the *theoretical floor* — any other choice of $k$ strictly increases $f$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering takeaway:** To achieve false-positive rate $f \le 0.01$ for $n = 10^7$ elements, you need $m \ge 9.6 \times 10^7$ bits $\approx 11.5$ MB. Bloom filter never gives a false *negative*. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Flajolet–Martin (FM) Algorithm for Distinct Count **Problem:** Given a stream $S$ of length $N$ over universe $\mathcal{U}$, estimate the number of distinct elements $\vert\{a_1, \dots, a_N\}\vert$ using $O(\log N)$ space. **Step 1 — Hash to a uniform binary string.** Define a hash function $h: \mathcal{U} \to \{0, 1\}^{L}$ such that the output is approximately uniform. **Step 2 — Define the position of the rightmost 1 (i.e., trailing zeros count).** For a hash value $h(x)$, let $\rho(x)$ = the number of trailing zeros in the binary representation, ending at the first 1 (right-to-left scan). For a uniform hash, the probability that $\rho(x) \ge r$ is exactly: $$P(\rho(x) \ge r) = \frac{1}{2^{r}}$$ because exactly one out of $2^r$ strings ends with $r$ trailing zeros. **Step 3 — Maintain the maximum $\rho$ seen.** Define the random variable: $$R = \max_{1 \le i \le N} \rho(a_i)$$ **Step 4 — Expected value of $2^R$.** By linearity of expectations (well, by the integral identity): $$\mathbb{E}[2^R] = \sum_{r=0}^{L} 2^r \cdot P(R = r)$$ Using the relation $P(R \le r) = (1 - 1/2^r)^N \approx e^{-N/2^r}$: $$\mathbb{E}[2^R] \approx \int_0^{L} 2^r \cdot \frac{d}{dr}\left(1 - e^{-N/2^r}\right) dr$$ After substitution $t = 2^r$, the integral evaluates to a quantity tightly concentrated around the true distinct count $\mathbb{E}[2^R] \approx \mathbb{E}[D]$ where $D$ is the number of distinct elements. **Step 5 — The FM estimator.** $$\hat{D} = 2^R$$ The variance of this estimator is bounded: $$\text{Var}(\hat{D}) \le \frac{0.78}{(\mathbb{E}[\hat D])^2} \cdot (\mathbb{E}[\hat D])^2 \approx 0.78$$ **Step 6 — Improving accuracy: Median of $H$ hashes + Mean of groups (FM-improved).** Use $H$ independent hash functions, take the median of $2^{R_j}$ to reduce variance by a factor of $\sim 1/H$: $$\hat{D}_{\text{final}} = \text{median}\left(2^{R_1}, 2^{R_2}, \dots, 2^{R_H}\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > **The "median trick"** converts a high-variance unbiased estimator into a very-low-variance estimator — this trick is reused in AMS estimation. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: AMS Estimator for Second Moment $F_2$ **Problem:** Estimate $F_2 = \sum_{i=1}^{u} m_i^2$ in one pass with sub-linear space. **Step 1 — Choose a random position $q \in \{1, 2, \dots, N\}$ uniformly.** **Step 2 —Define the estimator $X$:** $$X = N \cdot (2q - 1) \cdot v$$ where $v$ is the value at position $q$ in the stream (more precisely, the current count of that element at position $q$). **Step 3 — Show $X$ is unbiased.** For each element $j$ with frequency $m_j$, the probability that position $q$ is occupied by element $j$ is $m_j / N$. Given that, the count $v$ at that position is $m_j$. So: $$\mathbb{E}[X] = \sum_{q=1}^{N} \frac{1}{N} \cdot N \cdot (2q - 1) \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\{a_q = j\}} \cdot v$$ For a specific element $j$ with frequency $m_j$, summing over its $m_j$ occurrences in the stream, the *expected* contribution is: $$\mathbb{E}[X \mid a_q = j] = N \cdot (2q - 1) \cdot m_j$$ Summing over all positions of $j$ and dividing by $N$: $$\mathbb{E}[X] = \sum_j m_j \cdot m_j = \sum_j m_j^2 = F_2$$ **Step 4 — Bounded variance using median-of-means.** The variance of a single estimator is bounded by $N \cdot F_2$. By averaging independent copies in groups and taking the median of group means, the variance reduces exponentially. **Final AMS estimator:** $$\hat{F}_2 = \text{median}\left(\bar{X}_1, \bar{X}_2, \dots, \bar{X}_G\right)$$ where each $\bar{X}_g$ is the mean of $T$ independent $X$ estimators, giving an $(\epsilon, \delta)$ approximation. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Comparison-Favourite) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The single most-likely comparison question in KTU ESE for Module 3:** "Distinguish between Bloom Filter, FM, and AMS." This table is your ready-reckoner. | Parameter | Bloom Filter | Flajolet–Martin | AMS Estimator | |---|---|---|---| | **Purpose** | Membership test | Count distinct elements | Estimate $k$-th moment | | **Output** | Yes / No / Maybe | $\hat{D}$ (a real number) | $\hat{F}_k$ (a real number) | | **Space** | $O(m)$ bits | $O(\log N)$ bits | $O(1/\epsilon^2 \cdot \log(1/\delta))$ | | **False positive** | Yes (tunable) | N/A (estimate has variance) | N/A (estimate has variance) | | **False negative** | **Never** | N/A | N/A | | **Determinism** | Deterministic structure | Probabilistic | Probabilistic | | **Key trick** | Bit array + multiple hashes | Trailing zeros in hash | Random position + median of means | | **Use case** | URL/email deduplication | Number of unique visitors | Skewness of stream distribution | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Stream Processing Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture) This diagram captures the complete stream processing conceptual architecture — from data ingestion to real-time output, including the placement of Bloom filters, samplers, and synopsis structures. ```mermaid flowchart LR srcA[Source A: Web Logs] --> ing[Stream Ingestor] srcB[Source B: IoT Sensors] --> ing srcC[Source C: Transactions] --> ing ing --> buf[In-Memory Buffer] buf --> bf[Bloom Filter
Membership Test] bf -- "Definite Duplicate" --> drop[Discard] bf -- "Possibly New" --> sam[Reservoir Sampler
k slots] sam --> syn[Synopsis Store
FM + AMS + Decaying] syn --> win[Windowing Layer
Landmark / Sliding / Decaying] win --> qry[Query Engine
Continuous SQL] qry --> alert[Alerts and Dashboards] qry --> store[Long-Term Cold Storage] ``` ### 4.2 Bloom Filter Internal Architecture The diagram below shows how a single element $x$ is mapped through $k=3$ hash functions into a bit array of size $m=12$, used for membership queries. ```mermaid flowchart TB inputx["Element x"] --> h1["h1(x) mod 12 = 4"] inputx --> h2["h2(x) mod 12 = 7"] inputx --> h3["h3(x) mod 12 = 2"] h1 --> b1["B[4] = 1"] h2 --> b2["B[7] = 1"] h3 --> b3["B[2] = 1"] subgraph bitarr["Bit Array B[1..12]"] direction LR b01["0"] --- b02["0"] --- b03["1"] --- b04["0"] --- b05["1"] --- b06["0"] --- b07["0"] --- b08["1"] --- b09["0"] --- b10["0"] --- b11["0"] --- b12["0"] end b1 -.set.-> b05 b2 -.set.-> b08 b3 -.set.-> b03 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** When a new element $y$ arrives, the same $k$ hash functions are computed. If **all three** positions in $B$ are already 1, the element is reported as *possibly seen*; if even one is 0, it is *definitely new*. ### 4.3 Windowing Models Compared ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph land["Landmark Window: from time 0 to now"] l1["All elements ever seen"] end subgraph slid["Sliding Window: last W elements only"] s1["t-N+1 ... t-2 t-1 t"] end subgraph decay["Decaying Window: exponential weights"] d1["Newest: weight 1"] d2["1 step back: weight 1/2"] d3["2 steps back: weight 1/4"] d4["k steps back: weight 1/2^k"] end ``` ### 4.4 FM Algorithm Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR start[Stream Element arrives] --> hash["Compute h(x)"] hash --> trail["Count trailing zeros to get rho(x)"] trail --> update["Update R = max(R, rho(x))"] update --> check{End of stream?} check -- No --> start check -- Yes --> estimate["Return D-hat = 2 to the power R"] estimate --> median["(Optional) Median over H independent hashes"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Bloom Filter trap:** Students frequently claim Bloom Filters have *no false positives*. They have **no false negatives** — false *positives* exist. Easy 1–2 mark loss. > 2. **FM estimator:** Writing the formula as $\hat{D} = 2^R$ is correct, but students often confuse the *position of the rightmost 1* with the *leftmost 1*. Examiner will deduct 1 mark for ambiguity. > 3. **Reservoir sampling:** The probability of the $i$-th item entering the reservoir is $k/i$, **not** $k/N$. Many students misquote this. > 4. **Moments:** Confusing $F_0$ (distinct count) with $F_1$ (stream length) is a recurring error in KTU scripts. > 5. **Windowing:** "Decaying window = sliding window" is *wrong*. A decaying window is a *weighted* version where older elements have exponentially less influence. --- ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** *Define a data stream. List any four characteristics that distinguish stream data from traditional stored data.* **Model Answer (3 marks — allocate ~1 mark per point):** A data stream is a real-time, continuous, ordered sequence of data elements arriving at high velocity, where it is infeasible to store the entire stream and processing must occur online. Distinguishing characteristics: 1. **Continuous and unbounded:** No defined end; new elements arrive indefinitely. 2. **High velocity:** Arrival rate is too fast for traditional DBMS to handle per-record. 3. **Single-pass constraint:** Each element is typically seen and processed only once. 4. **Bounded memory:** Algorithms must use sub-linear space relative to stream length. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** *What is a Bloom filter? Why is it said to have "no false negatives"?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Bloom filter** is a space-efficient probabilistic data structure used to test whether an element *may be* or *definitely is not* a member of a set. It consists of a bit array of size $m$ and $k$ independent hash functions. It has **no false negatives** because: - When an element $x$ is inserted, $k$ bits in the array are set to 1. - When the same $x$ is later queried, *all $k$ bits* that were set to 1 must still be 1 (bits are never reset to 0). - Therefore, if the structure ever reports $x$ is absent, $x$ is truly absent; if all bits are 1, the report is *definitely correct* for positives. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has an **internal choice** — answer either Q(A) or Q(B). Both choices are full 14-mark questions with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Flajolet–Martin algorithm** for estimating the number of distinct elements in a data stream. Derive the estimator and discuss how the **median trick** improves its accuracy. **(b) [7 marks]** With a worked example, illustrate the operation of a **Bloom filter** with $m = 12$ bits and $k = 2$ hash functions for the input stream: `{x, y, x, z, y}`. #### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks 1. **Problem statement (1 mark):** Given stream $S$ of length $N$, estimate distinct count $D = \vert \{a_1, \dots, a_N\} \vert$ in one pass, using $O(\log N)$ space. 2. **Hashing setup (1 mark):** Choose a hash function $h: \mathcal{U} \to \{0, 1\}^L$ producing uniform binary strings. Define $\rho(x)$ = the number of trailing zeros in $h(x)$, with the convention $\rho = 0$ for "0" and counting only up to the rightmost 1. 3. **Probability derivation (2 marks):** $P(\rho(x) \ge r) = 1/2^r$. The expected maximum of $N$ i.i.d. draws of $2^{\rho(x_i}}$ is approximately the distinct count $D$. Therefore, the FM estimator is: $$\hat{D} = 2^R, \quad \text{where } R = \max_i \rho(a_i)$$ 4. **Variance bound (1 mark):** $\text{Var}(\hat{D}) \le 0.78 \cdot (\mathbb{E}[\hat D])^2$. A single estimator has high relative variance. 5. **Median-of-hashes trick (2 marks):** Run $H$ independent FM estimators in parallel using different hash families. Final estimate is the **median** of $\{2^{R_1}, 2^{R_2}, \dots, 2^{R_H}\}$. By Chernoff bound, the probability that the median deviates from the truth by a factor $> \epsilon$ decreases exponentially in $H$, giving an $(\epsilon, \delta)$ approximation with $H = O(\log(1/\delta))$ hashes. #### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks Assume $h_1(x) = (3x + 1) \mod 12$ and $h_2(x) = (5x + 2) \mod 12$. For simplicity, treat `x=1, y=2, z=3`: | Stream | $h_1$ | $h_2$ | Bits set to 1 | |---|---|---|---| | `x` | $h_1(1) = 4$ | $h_2(1) = 7$ | B[4], B[7] | | `y` | $h_1(2) = 7$ | $h_2(2) = 0$ | B[0], B[4], B[7] | | `x` | 4 | 7 | (already 1) | | `z` | $h_1(3) = 10$ | $h_2(3) = 5$ | B[0], B[4], B[5], B[7], B[10] | | `y` | 7 | 0 | (already 1) | **Final bit array state:** B = `[1,0,0,0,1,1,0,1,0,0,1,0]` **Query `w` (assume $h_1(w)=4, h_2(w)=7$):** Both bits are 1 → *possibly seen* (false positive risk). **Query `v` (assume $h_1(v)=1, h_2(v)=3$):** B[1]=0 → *definitely not seen* (no false negative). **(3 marks for state evolution, 2 marks for hash logic, 2 marks for query analysis)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **Reservoir Sampling Algorithm (Algorithm R)** for stream sampling. Prove that every element of the stream has *equal probability* of being in the final reservoir of size $k$. **(b) [7 marks]** What are **decaying windows**? Show that the **exponential decay** approximation uses $O(\log N)$ memory, while a true sliding window would need $O(N)$. #### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks 1. **Algorithm (3 marks):** - Maintain reservoir $R$ of size $k$. - Fill $R$ with the first $k$ stream elements. - For each subsequent element $a_i$ ($i > k$): - Choose a random integer $r \in \{1, 2, \dots, i\}$ uniformly. - If $r \le k$, replace $R[r] = a_i$; else discard $a_i$. 2. **Correctness proof by induction (4 marks):** - **Base case:** $i = k$. Each of the first $k$ elements is in $R$ with probability $1 = k/k$. ✓ - **Inductive step:** Assume element $a_j$ ($j < i$) is in $R$ with probability $k/j$. At step $i$: - Probability $a_j$ survives: it is *not* displaced at step $i$, which happens with probability $1 - (1/i) = (i-1)/i$ (since replacement happens only if random $r$ equals $j$'s slot, prob $1/i$). - New probability for $a_j$ in $R$ after step $i$: $$P(\text{a}_j \in R \text{ after step } i) = \frac{k}{j} \cdot \frac{i-1}{i} = \frac{k(i-1)}{ji} \le \frac{k}{j+1}$$ - For the $i$-th element itself, probability of being in $R = k/i$. ✓ - Thus, by induction, every element has equal probability $k/i$ of being in $R$ at any step $i$. #### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks 1. **Decaying window definition (1 mark):** A decaying window assigns exponentially decreasing weights to older elements so that recent events dominate the aggregate count. 2. **Mathematical formulation (2 marks):** Define the count at time $t$ as: $$C_t = \sum_{i=1}^{t} a_i \cdot 2^{-\lambda(t - i)}$$ where $a_i \in \{0, 1\}$ and $\lambda$ is the decay rate. Equivalently: $C_t = 2C_{t-1} + a_t$. 3. **Memory analysis (2 marks):** - **True sliding window of size $N$:** Requires storing all $N$ elements, hence $O(N)$ memory. - **Exponential histogram (bucket-based):** Group bits into buckets of size 1, 2, 4, 8, …, $2^j$. There are at most $O(\log N)$ buckets for $N$ timestamps. Each bucket stores (timestamp, count) — total $O(\log N)$ memory. The bucket whose count halved is merged with the previous one, maintaining the invariant. 4. **Error bound (2 marks):** The bucket-based approximation guarantees that the true decaying count $C_t$ and the estimated count $\hat{C}_t$ satisfy: $$\hat{C}_t \le C_t \le \hat{C}_t + \frac{1}{2\lambda} \cdot 2^{-\lambda W}$$ so the error is bounded by a small constant, independent of stream length. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (5-minute Module 3 refresher)** - [ ] A **data stream** is unbounded, real-time, single-pass, and sub-linear space. - [ ] The **5-V model** applied to streams emphasises **velocity, variability, and veracity**. - [ ] **Reservoir sampling** gives every element a uniform $k/i$ probability of being in the sample. - [ ] **Bloom filters** use $m$ bits and $k$ hashes; **no false negatives**, tunable false positives. - [ ] Optimal $k$ for a Bloom filter: $k = (m/n) \ln 2 \approx 0.693 \cdot (m/n)$. - [ ] **Flajolet–Martin** estimates distinct count via the **max trailing zeros** of hashed elements: $\hat{D} = 2^R$. - [ ] FM variance is bounded; the **median-of-hashes trick** reduces it exponentially. - [ ] **AMS estimator** estimates the **$k$-th frequency moment** $F_k = \sum_i m_i^k$. - [ ] $F_0$ = distinct count, $F_1$ = stream length, $F_2$ = surprise index, $F_\infty$ = mode. - [ ] **Landmark window**: from time 0 to now; **Sliding window**: last $W$ only; **Decaying window**: exponential weight. - [ ] Decaying windows use $O(\log N)$ memory via **exponential histograms**; true sliding uses $O(N)$. - [ ] **Applications** in Kerala: KSEB smart meter anomaly detection, UPI fraud screening, BSNL CDR duplicate detection. - [ ] The "median trick" is the **single most reused variance-reduction technique** in streaming algorithms — remember it for both FM and AMS. - [ ] Always quote the **space complexity** in a stream question — it is the *defining* performance metric. - [ ] KTU frequently asks **comparison** questions: Bloom vs FM vs AMS — memorize the comparison matrix.

topics.title: Clients
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT746 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Clients # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition In the context of the **Apache Hadoop / Big Data Ecosystem**, a **Client** is a software entity (process, application, library, or tool) that runs *outside* the Hadoop cluster master/daemon processes but interacts with the cluster services (HDFS, YARN, MapReduce, HBase, Spark, etc.) by invoking their **public APIs** or **wire protocols**. According to the KTU 2024 PECNT746 syllabus, clients in Big Data frameworks encompass: - **HDFS Clients** (DFSClient, WebHDFS, HttpFS) - **MapReduce / YARN Job Clients** (JobClient, ApplicationClient, YarnClient) - **REST / Web-based Clients** (WebHDFS, WebHBase, Spark REST) - **Command Line Interface (CLI) Tools** (`hdfs dfs`, `yarn jar`, `mapred job`) - **Native Libraries** (libhdfs, libhdfs3) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A client is *NOT* a node in the cluster. It is any user-facing application (running on the user's laptop, edge server, or a gateway machine) that uses the Hadoop client-side libraries to talk to the cluster's master/worker daemons over a network using RPC (typically Protobuf-RPC) or HTTP/REST. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Analogy — "The Food Delivery Customer":** > > Imagine a **Zomato Customer** ordering food from a restaurant. > > - The **Customer (Client)** sits at home — he is *outside* the restaurant kitchen. > - The **Restaurant Kitchen (Hadoop Cluster)** does the actual cooking on its stoves (DataNodes) and is managed by the head chef (NameNode). > - The customer never enters the kitchen.
