# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define a DataFrame in Apache Spark. How is it different from an RDD?**

**Model Answer:**
A **DataFrame** in Apache Spark is a distributed collection of data organized into named columns, conceptually equivalent to a relational table. Internally, it is a `Dataset[Row]` with a known schema (`StructType`).

**Key Differences from RDD:**

| Aspect | RDD | DataFrame |
|---|---|---|
| Schema | No schema (untyped) | Schema-aware (typed columns) |
| Optimization | Manual (developer-tuned) | Automatic (Catalyst + Tungsten) |
| API | Functional (lambda-heavy) | Relational DSL + SQL |
| Performance | Baseline | **2–20× faster** due to optimization |

DataFrames enable **declarative** query expression and **automatic optimization** through the Catalyst Optimizer, which RDDs lack.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]`
**What is lazy evaluation in Spark DataFrames? Why is it beneficial?**

**Model Answer:**
**Lazy evaluation** means that DataFrame **transformations** (e.g., `select`, `filter`, `join`) are *not* executed immediately. Instead, Spark builds a **logical plan (DAG)** and waits for an **action** (e.g., `count`, `show`, `write`) to trigger execution.

**Benefits:**
1. **Optimization opportunity** — the Catalyst Optimizer can analyze the *entire* DAG and apply optimizations (predicate pushdown, projection pruning) before execution.
2. **Reduced I/O** — only the necessary columns/rows are read from disk.
3. **Pipelining** — narrow transformations are fused into a single stage, reducing intermediate writes.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE pattern: Module questions offer an *internal choice*. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both questions below are designed to carry **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of the Catalyst Optimizer** in Apache Spark. Discuss any **four optimization rules** applied by it. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Catalyst Optimizer Architecture:**

The Catalyst Optimizer is Spark SQL's query optimization framework, built on **Scala's pattern matching and tree-manipulation libraries**. It operates in four sequential phases:

**Phase 1 — Parsing:** The user query (DataFrame DSL or SQL) is parsed into an **Unresolved Logical Plan (ULP)** — an abstract syntax tree (AST) where column names and types are not yet bound to actual data.

**Phase 2 — Analysis:** The **Analyzer** consults the **Catalog** (in-memory metadata) to resolve column names, data types, and function signatures, producing the **Analyzed Logical Plan (ALP)**.

**Phase 3 — Logical Optimization:** The **Optimizer** applies rule-based and cost-based transformations:

1. **Predicate Pushdown:** filters are pushed close to the data source.
2. **Constant Folding:** constant expressions evaluated at compile time.
3. **Projection Pruning:** unused columns eliminated.
4. **Boolean Simplification:** redundant boolean expressions reduced (e.g., `true AND x → x`).
5. **Join Reordering:** cost-based reordering for multi-way joins.

**Phase 4 — Physical Planning:** Converts the optimized logical plan into a **Physical Plan** by selecting concrete execution strategies (e.g., Hash Join, Sort-Merge Join, Broadcast Join).

```
User Code → ULP → Analyzer/Catalog → ALP → Optimizer → OLP → Physical Planner → Physical Plan
```

**Four Optimization Rules:**

| # | Rule | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Predicate Pushdown** | Reduces rows read by filtering at the source |
| 2 | **Projection Pruning** | Reads only required columns (columnar pruning) |
| 3 | **Constant Folding** | Computes constants at compile time |
| 4 | **Boolean Simplification** | Simplifies `AND`/`OR` expressions |

---

**(b)** Compare **RDD, DataFrame, and Dataset** in Apache Spark. When would you prefer RDDs over DataFrames? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | RDD | DataFrame | Dataset |
|---|---|---|---|
| Type | `RDD[T]` | `Dataset[Row]` | `Dataset[T]` |
| Type Safety | Compile-time | Runtime | Compile-time |
| Schema | None | Yes | Yes |
| Optimization | Manual | Catalyst + Tungsten | Catalyst + Tungsten |
| API Style | Functional | DSL + SQL | Typed DSL |
| Languages | Scala, Java, Python, R | All | Scala, Java |
| Performance | Baseline | High | High |

**When to Prefer RDDs over DataFrames:**

1. **Unstructured data** — when the data has no fixed schema (e.g., raw text streams, binary blobs).
2. **Low-level transformations** — when you need fine-grained control over partitioning, custom serialization, or low-level record manipulation.
3. **Functional purity** — when working with pure functional APIs in Scala without sacrificing type safety.
4. **Complex user-defined functions (UDFs)** that cannot be expressed in Spark SQL's DSL.
5. **Legacy code** — existing RDD-based codebases that are costly to rewrite.

**Conclusion:** DataFrames are the **default choice** for structured/semi-structured data, but RDDs remain relevant for unstructured, low-level use cases.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the **Tungsten Execution Engine** in Apache Spark. How does it improve DataFrame performance? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Tungsten Execution Engine** is Spark's next-generation physical execution layer, introduced to push performance closer to the hardware limits. It focuses on three pillars:

**Pillar 1 — Memory Management and Binary Processing:**
- Uses **off-heap memory** (sun.misc.Unsafe) to avoid GC overhead.
- Data is stored in a **compact binary format** (TungstenBinaryFormat) that uses a **columnar layout** for sorted data and a **row-based layout** for unsorted data.
- Reduces memory footprint by **2–5×** compared to Java object representation.

**Pillar 2 — Cache-Aware Computation:**
- Algorithms are designed with **cache locality** in mind (similar to BLAS-style CPU optimization).
- Example: sort algorithms use in-memory pointer manipulation, avoiding full object deserialization.

**Pillar 3 — Whole-Stage Code Generation (CodeGen):**
- Fuses multiple physical operators (e.g., Scan → Filter → Project → Aggregate) into a **single compiled Java function** using **quasi-quotes**.
- Eliminates virtual function dispatches and intermediate materialization.
- Results in **1–2 orders of magnitude** performance improvement for many queries.

**Performance Improvements:**
- **CPU efficiency:** reduced instruction count via vectorization and codegen.
- **Memory efficiency:** off-heap storage reduces GC pauses.
- **I/O efficiency:** columnar pruning and predicate pushdown reduce data read.

---

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the **narrow and wide transformations** in Spark DataFrames. Why is shuffle considered expensive? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Narrow Transformations:**
Each partition of the parent DataFrame contributes to **at most one** partition of the child DataFrame. **No data shuffle** is required, and operations can be **pipelined** within a single Spark stage.

**Examples:** `map`, `filter`, `select`, `withColumn`, `coalesce`.

**Wide Transformations:**
Each partition of the parent DataFrame may contribute to **multiple** partitions of the child DataFrame. This requires a **shuffle** — data is repartitioned across the network, creating a **new Spark stage**.

**Examples:** `groupBy`, `reduceByKey`, `join`, `distinct`, `orderBy`, `repartition`.

**Example (Python):**

```python
# Narrow transformations (no shuffle)
df2 = df.filter(df.age > 30)        # filter is narrow
df3 = df2.select("name", "salary")   # select is narrow

# Wide transformation (shuffle)
df4 = df3.groupBy("name").agg(avg("salary"))  # groupBy is wide
```

**Why is Shuffle Expensive?**

1. **Network I/O** — data is transferred across nodes via TCP, saturating the network.
2. **Disk I/O** — shuffle data is written to local disk before being fetched.
3. **Serialization/Deserialization** — each record is converted to/from bytes.
4. **Memory pressure** — shuffle writes/reads can trigger spills to disk.
5. **Stage boundary overhead** — each shuffle creates a new stage, requiring job rescheduling and task launch overhead.

**Mitigation strategies:** use `reduceByKey` over `groupByKey`, use **broadcast joins** for small tables, and increase shuffle partitions (`spark.sql.shuffle.partitions`).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in DataFrame Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing DataFrame with RDD:** Many students incorrectly state that DataFrames are *"just tables"* without mentioning **schema awareness** or **Catalyst optimization**. Always mention *both* the structural aspect (named columns + schema) and the optimization aspect (Catalyst + Tungsten).
>
> 2. **Forgetting lazy evaluation:** When asked *"what does `df.filter().select()` do?"*, students often say *"it filters and selects"*. The correct answer must clarify that *nothing executes* until an action is called.
>
> 3. **Mixing up narrow and wide:** Saying `groupBy` is a *narrow* transformation will cost you 2–3 marks immediately. Remember: **wide = shuffle = new stage**.
>
> 4. **No mention of Tungsten:** If the question is on *"how DataFrames are fast"*, citing **only** Catalyst is incomplete. Tungsten (off-heap memory + code generation) is the *physical* counterpart.
>
> 5. **Skipping the diagram:** A "neat diagram" carries **2–3 marks** in a 14-mark question. Always draw the Catalyst pipeline or the DAG.
>
> 6. **Writing code without explanation:** A 14-mark answer requires *conceptual explanation*, not just code snippets. The model answers above show the expected ratio of **70% explanation : 30% code/table**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU DataFrames:**

- ⭐ A **DataFrame** = `Dataset[Row]` = a distributed, schema-aware table.
- ⭐ DataFrames are **immutable** and **lazy**.
- ⭐ The four phases of query execution: **Unresolved Logical Plan → Analyzed Logical Plan → Optimized Logical Plan → Physical Plan**.
- ⭐ **Catalyst Optimizer** = rule-based + cost-based logical optimizer.
- ⭐ **Tungsten** = physical execution engine (off-heap memory + code generation).
- ⭐ **Narrow transformations** = no shuffle (pipelined within a stage): `map`, `filter`, `select`.
- ⭐ **Wide transformations** = shuffle required (new stage): `groupBy`, `join`, `orderBy`.
- ⭐ **Lazy evaluation** = transformations build a DAG; only actions (`count`, `show`, `write`) trigger execution.
- ⭐ DataFrames are **2–20× faster** than RDDs due to Catalyst + Tungsten.
- ⭐ A **schema** is represented as a `StructType` containing `StructField(name, type, nullable)` elements.
- ⭐ **Predicate pushdown**, **projection pruning**, **constant folding**, and **boolean simplification** are the four must-know Catalyst rules.
- ⭐ **Broadcast Hash Join** is preferred when one side is small ($\leq 10 \text{ MB}$); **Sort-Merge Join** is the default for large-large joins.
- ⭐ DataFrames support **SQL interoperability** via `spark.sql()` and can be registered as **temporary views**.
- ⭐ **Structured Streaming** uses the same DataFrame API for unbounded (streaming) data.
- ⭐ **MLlib Pipelines** use DataFrames as the canonical data structure for ML feature engineering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Whenever a KTU question asks *"Explain the architecture of DataFrames in Spark"*, structure your answer in **four layers** — (1) Logical Data Model, (2) Catalyst Optimizer, (3) Tungsten Engine, (4) DAG/Task Execution — and accompany each with a **diagram** and a **real-world analogy**. This guarantees full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating Data Frames
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT746 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Creating Data Frames

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Apache Spark** and the broader **Big Data Analytics** ecosystem, a **DataFrame** is formally defined as a **distributed collection of data organized into named columns** (also referred to as a *schema*), conceptually equivalent to a **relational table** (from RDBMS) or a **Pandas DataFrame** in Python, but with **richer optimizations** under the Catalyst optimizer and Tungsten execution engine.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Module 4 – *Spark SQL and DataFrames*), a DataFrame is:

> A distributed, immutable, lazily-evaluated collection of typed rows, where the type information is governed by an explicitly defined or inferred **schema**, enabling SQL-style queries, transformations, and actions on semi-structured and structured data at petabyte scale.

Mathematically, a DataFrame $DF$ can be represented as:

$$DF = \{(r_1, r_2, r_3, \dots, r_n) \;\vert\; r_i \in \text{Row}(\text{col}_1, \text{col}_2, \dots, \text{col}_k)\}$$

where $r_i$ represents an individual row, and each row is an ordered tuple of $k$ typed columns.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A DataFrame is **NOT** an RDD. It is a higher-level abstraction built on top of RDDs, with a schema. This distinction is a **favourite KTU exam question**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Excel Spreadsheet on Steroids"

Imagine you have a massive **Excel spreadsheet** sitting on your laptop — rows and columns of customer data. Now, suppose that spreadsheet contains **billions of rows** and is distributed across **thousands of machines** in a data centre. You want to run SQL queries, filter rows, aggregate columns, and join it with other spreadsheets — all at lightning speed.

**That is exactly what a Spark DataFrame is.**

- **Excel Sheet**  →  A small DataFrame on one machine.
- **Schema (column names + types)**  →  The "header row" of Excel, with type information.
- **RDD (Resilient Distributed Dataset)**  →  The raw electrical wiring hidden behind Excel. DataFrames are a *user-friendly wrapper* around this wiring.
- **Spark Cluster**  →  A factory of thousands of Excel sheets, working in parallel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A DataFrame gives you the *familiarity of SQL tables* combined with the *scalability of distributed computing*. You write queries in Python/Scala/Java, Spark translates them to a distributed execution plan, and runs them across the cluster.

## 1.3 Core Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Schema** | Column names + data types of a DataFrame | High — defines structure |
| **Row** | A single record (tuple of typed values) | Foundation unit |
| **Column** | A named, typed field | Queried in SELECT clauses |
| **Partition** | Logical chunk of DataFrame rows on one node | Affects parallelism |
| **Lazy Evaluation** | Transformations are not executed until an Action is called | Core Spark paradigm |
| **Immutability** | A DataFrame cannot be modified; each transformation creates a new one | Functional programming principle |

> [!TIP]
> **Marking Tip:** Examiners often test the *three properties* of a DataFrame: **Distributed**, **Immutable**, and **Lazily Evaluated**. Memorize this triad.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The DataFrame Creation Pipeline — Conceptual Breakdown

Creating a DataFrame in Spark is not a single "click" — it is a **multi-stage pipeline** governed by the **Catalyst Optimizer**. Let us break down the complete lifecycle:

### Stage 1: Source Identification
The user specifies the *source* of data:
- In-memory collections (lists, tuples, dictionaries, RDDs)
- External files (CSV, JSON, Parquet, ORC, Avro, Text)
- External databases (JDBC, Hive)

### Stage 2: Data Ingestion
Spark's *DataFrameReader* loads the raw data into the cluster as a DataFrame. This is a **transformation**, hence **lazy**.

### Stage 3: Schema Resolution
Two pathways exist:
- **Inferred Schema** — Spark scans data to guess types.
- **Programmatic Schema** — User explicitly defines schema using `StructType` and `StructField`.

### Stage 4: Logical Plan Construction
Catalyst converts user code into an **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)**.

### Stage 5: Physical Plan & Execution
After optimization, Spark's **Tungsten** engine generates efficient JVM bytecode and executes it on the cluster.

## 2.2 Methods of Creating DataFrames (KTU High-Yield Classification)

A DataFrame can be created via **five primary methods**. Each is a potential exam question.

### Method 1: From a List of Tuples / Lists
The simplest method. Spark infers schema from the data.

### Method 2: From a List of Dictionaries (Row-like structures)
Each dictionary represents a row, keys become column names.

### Method 3: From an Existing RDD
A pre-existing RDD can be promoted to a DataFrame using `.toDF()`.

### Method 4: From External File Sources (CSV, JSON, Parquet, etc.)
The most common real-world method using `spark.read`.

### Method 5: From a Programmatic Schema
User defines the schema explicitly using `StructType`.

## 2.3 The Programmatic Schema — Detailed Anatomy

In Spark (PySpark), a schema is a `StructType` object containing one or more `StructField` objects.

$$\text{Schema} = \text{StructType}\Big(\big[\text{StructField}(\text{name}_i, \text{type}_i, \text{nullable}_i)\big]_{i=1}^{n}\Big)$$

The supported data types include:

| Spark Type | Python Equivalent | SQL Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| `StringType` | `str` | `VARCHAR` |
| `IntegerType` | `int` | `INT` |
| `LongType` | `long` | `BIGINT` |
| `FloatType` | `float` | `FLOAT` |
| `DoubleType` | `float` | `DOUBLE` |
| `BooleanType` | `bool` | `BOOLEAN` |
| `DateType` | `datetime.date` | `DATE` |
| `TimestampType` | `datetime.datetime` | `TIMESTAMP` |
| `ArrayType` | `list` | `ARRAY` |
| `MapType` | `dict` | `MAP` |
| `StructType` | `dict / tuple` | `STRUCT` |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every essential formula, method signature, and concept required to answer KTU 2024 Scheme questions on **Creating DataFrames**.

| Concept | Formula / Method Signature | Purpose / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| DataFrame Definition | $DF = \{r_1, r_2, \dots, r_n\}$ | Set of typed rows |
| Schema Definition | $\text{StructType}([\text{StructField}(c_i, t_i, n_i)])$ | Defines structure |
| Row Count | $\vert DF \vert = \sum_{p=1}^{P} \vert p \vert$ | $P$ = partitions |
| Partition Cardinality | $P = \text{ceil}\big(\vert \text{Data} \vert / \text{Partition Size}\big)$ | Affects parallelism |
| List → DataFrame | `spark.createDataFrame(data)` | Inferred schema |
| RDD → DataFrame | `rdd.toDF(schema)` | Explicit schema |
| External File | `spark.read.format(fmt).load(path)` | `csv`, `json`, `parquet` |
| Show Content | `df.show(n)` | Action — triggers execution |
| Print Schema | `df.printSchema()` | Displays structure |
| Count Rows | `df.count()` | Action |
| Select Columns | `df.select("col1", "col2")` | Transformation (lazy) |
| Filter Rows | `df.filter(df.col > x)` | Transformation (lazy) |
| Type Conversion | `withColumn("new", df.col.cast(IntegerType()))` | Schema modification |
| Display in Jupyter | `df.display()` | Databricks-specific |

> [!WARNING]
> **Note on Tables:** This table uses `\vert` to denote absolute value/cardinality to prevent markdown parser conflicts. Do not confuse `$\vert$` (cardinality) with `$\mid$` (set separator).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

DataFrames are the **de-facto data structure** for:

- **ETL pipelines** in enterprises (extracting from databases, transforming, loading into data warehouses).
- **Streaming analytics** (Structured Streaming uses DataFrames as the API).
- **Machine Learning** (Spark MLlib algorithms accept DataFrames as input).
- **Business Intelligence** (Spark DataFrames integrate with Tableau, Power BI).
- **Log analytics** in cybersecurity (parsing terabytes of log files).

> [!TIP]
> **Why DataFrames over RDDs?** In benchmark tests, DataFrame operations are often **5x–20x faster** than equivalent RDD operations due to Catalyst optimizer and Tungsten's off-heap memory management. This is a *guaranteed 3-mark question*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Python List to DataFrame — Complete Walkthrough

Let us exhaustively derive the creation of a DataFrame from a Python list of tuples, step by step.

### Step 1 — Define the Data Source

We start with a list of tuples representing employee records:

$$
\text{data} = [
(\text{"Alice"},\, 28,\, \text{"Engineering"}),
(\text{"Bob"},\, 35,\, \text{"Marketing"}),
(\text{"Charlie"},\, 42,\, \text{"Sales"})
]
$$

Each tuple represents a row. The order of elements corresponds to a future column position.

### Step 2 — Define Column Names

We supply column labels as a separate list:

$$
\text{columns} = [\text{"Name"},\, \text{"Age"},\, \text{"Department"}]
$$

This step is the **user-level schema declaration**. The number of elements in `columns` **must match** the number of elements in each tuple.

### Step 3 — Invoke the `createDataFrame` Method

We call the SparkSession's factory method:

$$
\text{df} = \text{spark.createDataFrame}(\text{data},\; \text{columns})
$$

Under the hood, Spark performs:
1. **Type inference** — Examines the first row's Python types.
2. **Schema construction** — Builds a `StructType` internally:

$$
\text{Schema} = \text{StructType}\Big(\big[\text{StructField}(\text{"Name"}, \text{StringType}, \text{True}),\;
\text{StructField}(\text{"Age"}, \text{LongType}, \text{True}),\;
\text{StructField}(\text{"Department"}, \text{StringType}, \text{True})\big]\Big)
$$

3. **RDD conversion** — Internally converts the list to a `RDD[Row]`.

$$
\text{RDD} = \text{sc.parallelize}(\text{data}).\text{map}(\text{Row}(\ast))
$$

4. **DataFrame assignment** — Attaches the inferred schema to the RDD, producing a logical DataFrame.

### Step 4 — Verify the Schema (Lazy, No Execution Yet)

We call `df.printSchema()`. Since `printSchema` is a side-effect method, it triggers a Spark job.

$$
\text{Output} = \begin{cases}
\text{root} \\
\;\;\vert\;\text{-- Name: string (nullable = true)} \\
\;\;\vert\;\text{-- Age: long (nullable = true)} \\
\;\;\vert\;\text{-- Department: string (nullable = true)}
\end{cases}
$$

Note: Python `int` is mapped to Spark's `LongType`, not `IntegerType`. This is a **subtle but important** KTU point.

### Step 5 — Display the Data (Action — Triggers Execution)

We call `df.show()`:

$$
\text{Output} = \begin{bmatrix}
\text{Name} & \text{Age} & \text{Department} \\
\hline
\text{Alice} & 28 & \text{Engineering} \\
\text{Bob} & 35 & \text{Marketing} \\
\text{Charlie} & 42 & \text{Sales}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

This is the **first action**, which forces Spark to execute the previously lazy transformations.

## 3.2 Derivation: From External CSV File — Complete Walkthrough

### Step 1 — Identify the Source

Suppose we have a CSV file at path:

$$
\text{path} = \text{"hdfs://\text{localhost}:9000/data/employees.csv"}
$$

### Step 2 — Use the DataFrameReader

$$
\text{df} = \text{spark.read} \;\text{.format("csv")}\;\text{.option("header", "true")}\;\text{.option("inferSchema", "true")}\;\text{.load(path)}
$$

Breaking down each method:
- `format("csv")` → Specifies the data format parser.
- `option("header", "true")` → Tells Spark the first row is the header.
- `option("inferSchema", "true")` → Asks Spark to scan data and infer types (extra pass — expensive for huge files).
- `.load(path)` → Triggers the lazy read.

### Step 3 — Lazy Evaluation Proof

At this point, **no data has been read from HDFS**. The operation is recorded in the **DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph)**.

### Step 4 — Trigger Execution

$$
\text{df.show(5)} \;\;\text{or}\;\; \text{df.count()}
$$

These actions trigger the DAG scheduler, which converts the logical plan into physical execution stages and tasks.

## 3.3 Derivation: From RDD with Programmatic Schema

### Step 1 — Create RDD

$$
\text{rdd} = \text{sc.parallelize}\big([(1,\, \text{"Alice"}),\,(2,\, \text{"Bob"})]\big)
$$

### Step 2 — Define Explicit Schema

$$
\text{schema} = \text{StructType}\big([
\text{StructField}(\text{"id"}, \text{IntegerType}(), \text{True}),
\text{StructField}(\text{"name"}, \text{StringType}(), \text{True})
]\big)
$$

### Step 3 — Convert RDD to DataFrame

$$
\text{df} = \text{spark.createDataFrame}(\text{rdd},\; \text{schema})
$$

**Why use programmatic schema?**
- Avoids extra pass for inference.
- Enforces strict type checking.
- Critical in production for **schema evolution** and **data quality**.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix is **high-yield** for KTU exams. Memorize the trade-offs.

| Creation Method | Schema Inference | Performance | Use Case | Production Ready |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `createDataFrame(list)` | Automatic (cheap) | Fast for small data | Prototyping | ✗ No |
| `createDataFrame(rdd, schema)` | Programmatic | Fastest | Production ETL | ✓ Yes |
| `spark.read.csv()` w/ inferSchema | Automatic (expensive) | Slower (extra pass) | One-time analysis | ✗ No |
| `spark.read.csv()` w/ explicit schema | Programmatic | Optimal | Production ETL | ✓ Yes |
| `spark.read.json()` | Automatic (semi) | Moderate | IoT / Web logs | ✓ Yes |
| `spark.read.parquet()` | Embedded in file | Optimal (columnar) | Data lake analytics | ✓ Yes |

## 3.5 Lazy vs Eager Evaluation — Mathematical Formulation

Let $T$ be the set of transformations $\{t_1, t_2, \dots, t_m\}$ and $A$ be the set of actions $\{a_1, a_2, \dots, a_k\}$.

The execution model is:

$$
\text{Execute}(T) = \begin{cases}
\emptyset, & \text{if } A = \emptyset \quad \text{(no actions called — fully lazy)} \\
\text{plan}(T) \rightarrow \text{run}(A), & \text{if } A \neq \emptyset \quad \text{(actions trigger computation)}
\end{cases}
$$

This is why DataFrame creation **does not** immediately load data — only when an action is called does Spark execute the pipeline.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DataFrame Creation Pipeline — End-to-End Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Data Source] --> B{Source Type}
    B -->|In-Memory| C1[List / Tuple / Dict]
    B -->|Distributed| C2[Existing RDD]
    B -->|File System| C3[CSV / JSON / Parquet / ORC / Avro]
    B -->|Database| C4[JDBC / Hive / Cassandra]
    
    C1 --> D[SparkSession]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    
    D --> E[createDataFrame / read]
    E --> F{Schema Source}
    F -->|Inferred| G1[Auto Schema Inference]
    F -->|Programmatic| G2[StructType Definition]
    
    G1 --> H[Logical DataFrame]
    G2 --> H
    
    H --> I[Lazy DAG Construction]
    I --> J{Action Called?}
    J -->|No| K[Pipeline Stored - No Execution]
    J -->|Yes| L[Catalyst Optimizer]
    L --> M[Tungsten Execution Engine]
    M --> N[Cluster Execution - Parallel Tasks]
    N --> O[Final Result Returned to Driver]
```


## 4.3 RDD vs DataFrame vs Dataset — Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Level1["Low-Level Abstraction"]
        RDD[RDD - Resilient Distributed Dataset]
    end
    
    subgraph Level2["Mid-Level Abstraction"]

    end
    
    subgraph Level3["High-Level Abstraction"]
        DS[Dataset - Strongly Typed API]
    end
    
    RDD -->|Adds Schema| DF
    DF -->|Adds Type Safety| DS
    
    RDD -->|Lambda Functions| R1[Functional API]
    DF -->|SQL Queries| R2[SQL API + Functional]
    DS -->|Lambda + Type Check| R3[Functional API Strict Types]
    
    style RDD fill:#FFD580
    style DF fill:#80C0FF
    style DS fill:#90EE90
```

## 4.4 Catalyst Optimizer Workflow (Creation-to-Execution)

```mermaid

    Q[User Query / DataFrame Operation] --> P1[Unresolved Logical Plan]
    P1 --> P2[Analyzer - Resolves Schema]
    P2 --> P3[Logical Plan Optimizer]
```

```mermaid
    P4 --> P5[Physical Planner]
    P5 --> P6[Cost Model Selection]
    P6 --> P7[Selected Physical Plan]
    P7 --> P8[Code Generation - Tungsten]
    P8 --> P9[JVM Bytecode]
    P9 --> P10[Distributed Execution on Cluster]
```

## 4.5 DataFrame Creation Methods — Decision Tree


<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`
**Define a Spark DataFrame. List any two advantages of DataFrames over RDDs.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Spark DataFrame** is a *distributed collection of data organized into named columns* (a schema), similar to a table in a relational database. It is an immutable, lazily-evaluated abstraction built on top of RDDs.

**Two advantages over RDDs:**
1. **Schema-awareness** — DataFrames know the structure (column names + types), enabling optimized query planning via the Catalyst optimizer.
2. **Performance** — DataFrame operations are typically **5x–20x faster** than equivalent RDD operations due to Catalyst-based optimization and Tungsten's off-heap memory management.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sample Paper]`
**Explain the concept of lazy evaluation in Spark DataFrames with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

**Lazy evaluation** means that *transformations on a DataFrame are not executed immediately*. Instead, Spark builds a **logical plan (DAG)** and waits for an *action* to trigger actual computation.

**Example:**

```python
df = spark.read.csv("data.csv", header=True)   # Transformation - lazy
filtered = df.filter(df.age > 25)               # Transformation - lazy
result = filtered.count()                        # ACTION - triggers execution
```

Here, Spark only reads the CSV and applies the filter when `count()` is called. This allows Spark to *optimize the entire pipeline* before execution.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the different methods of creating DataFrames in PySpark with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the internal architecture of a Spark DataFrame including schema, rows, and execution flow. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

There are **five major methods** to create a DataFrame in PySpark:

**1. From a List of Tuples:**

```python
data = [("Alice", 28), ("Bob", 35), ("Charlie", 42)]
df = spark.createDataFrame(data, ["Name", "Age"])
df.show()
```

**2. From a List of Dictionaries:**

```python
data = [{"Name": "Alice", "Age": 28}, {"Name": "Bob", "Age": 35}]
df = spark.createDataFrame(data)
df.show()
```

**3. From an Existing RDD with Programmatic Schema:**

```python
from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, StringType, IntegerType
rdd = sc.parallelize([(1, "Alice"), (2, "Bob")])
schema = StructType([
    StructField("id", IntegerType(), True),
    StructField("name", StringType(), True)
])
df = spark.createDataFrame(rdd, schema)
df.show()
```

**4. From a CSV File:**

```python
df = spark.read.csv("employees.csv", header=True, inferSchema=True)
df.show()
```

**5. From a JSON File:**

```python
df = spark.read.json("employees.json")
df.show()
```

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

The internal architecture of a Spark DataFrame consists of three layers:

**Layer 1 — Schema Layer:**  
A `StructType` object containing multiple `StructField` objects. Each `StructField` has three properties:
- `name` (column name)
- `dataType` (e.g., `StringType`, `IntegerType`)
- `nullable` (boolean)

**Layer 2 — Row Layer:**  
Each row is a `Row` object — an ordered tuple of typed values. Rows are immutable.

**Layer 3 — Execution Layer:**  
When an action is called, Spark triggers:
1. **Logical Plan Generation** — Builds a DAG of operations.
2. **Catalyst Optimization** — Applies rule-based and cost-based optimizations.
3. **Physical Plan** — Selects the most efficient execution strategy.
4. **Tungsten Execution** — Generates compact JVM bytecode and runs on cluster.

```
Schema (StructType)  →  Rows (Row objects)  →  DAG (Lazy)
            ↓
        Action Called
            ↓
   Catalyst Optimizer  →  Physical Plan  →  Tungsten  →  Cluster
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary]`

**(a)** Differentiate between RDD and DataFrame. Explain the role of the Catalyst Optimizer in DataFrame operations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a PySpark program to:
   (i) Create a DataFrame from a CSV file with an explicit schema.
   (ii) Display the schema and first 5 rows.
   (iii) Filter rows where salary > 50000 and count them. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

| Feature | RDD | DataFrame |
|---|---|---|
| Structure | No schema (unstructured) | Has schema (typed columns) |
| API Style | Functional (lambdas) | Functional + SQL |
| Optimization | None (user-managed) | Catalyst optimizer (automatic) |
| Performance | Slower | Faster (5x–20x) |
| Type Safety | Compile-time (Scala) | Runtime |
| Use Case | Low-level transformations | High-level analytics |

**Role of Catalyst Optimizer:**

The Catalyst Optimizer is Spark SQL's **query optimization framework**. It performs:
1. **Analysis** — Resolves column names and types against the schema.
2. **Logical Optimization** — Applies rule-based transformations (e.g., predicate pushdown, constant folding).
3. **Physical Planning** — Generates multiple physical plans and selects the cheapest using a cost model.
4. **Code Generation** — Converts the optimized plan into efficient JVM bytecode via Tungsten.

**Example:** When you write `df.filter(df.age > 25).select("name")`, Catalyst pushes the filter *before* the projection, reducing the amount of data shuffled.

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

```python
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, StringType, IntegerType, DoubleType

# Step 1: Create SparkSession
spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("DataFrameDemo").getOrCreate()

# Step 2: Define explicit schema
schema = StructType([
    StructField("id", IntegerType(), True),
    StructField("name", StringType(), True),
    StructField("department", StringType(), True),
    StructField("salary", DoubleType(), True)
])

# Step 3: Read CSV with explicit schema
df = spark.read.csv("employees.csv", header=True, schema=schema)

# Step 4: Display schema
df.printSchema()

# Step 5: Display first 5 rows
df.show(5)

# Step 6: Filter and count
high_earners = df.filter(df.salary > 50000)
count = high_earners.count()
print(f"Number of employees with salary > 50000: {count}")
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing RDD with DataFrame** — A DataFrame **has a schema**; an RDD does not. This is the #1 reason students lose marks.
> 2. **Forgetting that DataFrames are immutable** — You cannot modify a DataFrame in place; every transformation returns a new one. Not writing `df2 = df.filter(...)` instead of `df.filter(...)` is a common error.
> 3. **Mixing up transformations and actions** — `show()`, `count()`, `collect()` are actions. Everything else is lazy. Examiners test this distinction.
> 4. **Using `inferSchema=True` in production** — This forces an extra full scan of data. Always define schema explicitly for production ETL.
> 5. **Forgetting `header=True` when reading CSVs** — The first row gets treated as data, not as column names.
> 6. **Python `int` vs Spark `IntegerType`** — Python integers map to Spark's `LongType` (8-byte), not `IntegerType` (4-byte). This surprises many students.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Pin This Before the Exam!**

- ✅ A **DataFrame** = distributed, schema-aware, immutable, lazily-evaluated collection of rows.
- ✅ Three signatures: **Distributed + Schema + Lazy**.
- ✅ Five creation methods: **List, Dict, RDD, CSV/JSON/Parquet, Programmatic Schema**.
- ✅ `createDataFrame(data, schema)` — for in-memory sources.
- ✅ `spark.read.format("csv").load(path)` — for file sources.
- ✅ **Schema** = `StructType` containing multiple `StructField(name, type, nullable)`.
- ✅ `inferSchema=True` → extra scan (avoid in production).
- ✅ DataFrame ≠ RDD. DataFrame = RDD + Schema.
- ✅ **Catalyst Optimizer** does: Analysis → Logical Optimization → Physical Planning → Code Generation.
- ✅ **Tungsten** handles off-heap memory and code generation.
- ✅ Transformations are lazy; Actions (show, count, collect, write) trigger execution.
- ✅ DataFrames are **immutable** — always assign result of transformation to a new variable.
- ✅ Python `int` → Spark `LongType`; Python `float` → Spark `DoubleType`.
- ✅ `.toDF()` converts an RDD to a DataFrame.
- ✅ `printSchema()` shows the schema; `show(n)` displays rows.
- ✅ For petabyte-scale data, prefer **Parquet** format (columnar, compressed).
- ✅ DataFrame API is available in **Python (PySpark), Scala, Java, and R**.
- ✅ Lazy evaluation allows Spark to **optimize the entire pipeline** before execution.
- ✅ **Predicate pushdown** is a key Catalyst optimization — filters are pushed down to the data source level.
- ✅ DataFrames integrate seamlessly with **Spark MLlib**, **Structured Streaming**, and **Spark SQL**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Matrix like Operations in Frames
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT746 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Matrix like Operations in Frames

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Matrix-like Operations in Frames** refer to the set of distributed algebraic and relational operations that can be performed on a **DataFrame** (a two-dimensional, labeled, tabular data structure with rows and columns) such that the semantics, computational pattern, and resulting output mimic classical linear-algebra matrix operations such as Cartesian product (cross product), transpose, aggregation (sum/mean along an axis), pivot/un-pivot, and join.

In the context of **Apache Spark** (the KTU-recommended distributed compute engine for PECNT746), a `DataFrame` is a distributed collection of `Row` objects partitioned across a cluster, organized into named columns. The KTU 2024 syllabus frames these operations under **Module 4: DataFrames and Matrix-style Computations**, emphasizing that a DataFrame, although conceptually tabular, can be operated upon using mathematical matrix idioms when rows represent *observation vectors* and columns represent *feature dimensions*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Glossary):**
> - **DataFrame (DF):** An immutable, distributed collection of data organized into named columns.
> - **Schema:** The structural description of columns, their data types, and nullability.
> - **Row:** A single tuple (record) of the DataFrame — analogous to a row-vector $\vec{r} \in \mathbb{R}^n$.
> - **Column:** A named 1-D vector of values across all rows — analogous to a column-vector $\vec{c} \in \mathbb{R}^m$.
> - **Lazy Evaluation:** Transformations are not executed until an *action* triggers them — analogous to composing matrix operators without immediate evaluation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **spreadsheet in Excel** sitting on a single computer — now imagine **millions of identical spreadsheets spread across 1000 servers**, with each server holding only a slice (a *partition*) of the data. A DataFrame is precisely this: a *virtual*, *distributed* mega-spreadsheet.

Now, the term "matrix-like" arises because this tabular structure, when each row is treated as a feature vector $\vec{x}_i = (x_{i1}, x_{i2}, \ldots, x_{in})$, allows us to apply operations that look identical to textbook linear algebra:

- **Cross Join** $\Leftrightarrow$ Outer product $A \otimes B$ (every row of A combined with every row of B).
- **Pivot** $\Leftrightarrow$ Transpose $A^T$ (rows become columns and vice-versa).
- **Group-By + Aggregation** $\Leftrightarrow$ Row/column reduction $\sum_i A_{ij}$ or $\sum_j A_{ij}$.
- **Inner Join** $\Leftrightarrow$ Selective row matching based on key columns (analogous to banded matrix sparsity pattern).
- **Union** $\Leftrightarrow$ Vertical stacking — $\begin{bmatrix} A \\ B \end{bmatrix}$.
- **Select/WithColumn** $\Leftrightarrow$ Linear combination $a\vec{c}_1 + b\vec{c}_2$.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** A DataFrame is to a distributed cluster what an Excel sheet is to your laptop. "Matrix-like operations" are simply the structured ways to slice, dice, join, transpose, and aggregate that sheet — except now the math is executed *in parallel across machines*.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Partition count | $P$ | $\sim 200$ (Spark default) | Controls parallelism degree |
| Block size | $B$ | **128 MB** (HDFS default) | Memory-shuffle boundary |
| Shuffle partitions | $S$ | **200** (Spark SQL default) | Default for `groupBy`/`join` |
| Replication factor | $r$ | **3** (HDFS) | Fault-tolerance guarantee |
| Driver memory | $M_d$ | configurable | Holds Catalyst-optimized plan |
| Executor memory | $M_e$ | configurable | Holds partition data |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These defaults are the *de-facto industry constants* used in KTU practical labs. Master them — they appear in viva voce and Part A questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Structural Decomposition: What Makes an Operation "Matrix-like"?

A DataFrame operation is classified as *matrix-like* if it satisfies **any one** of the following algebraic properties when mapped to a corresponding matrix structure $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$:

### 2.1.1 Logical Pre-Conditions
1. **Closure Property:** The output of the operation is itself a DataFrame (of same type — a *transformation* in Spark terminology).
2. **Associativity:** $(A \oplus B) \oplus C \equiv A \oplus (B \oplus C)$ for $\oplus \in \{\text{union}, \text{join}\}$.
3. **Distributivity over Partitioning:** Operation must be expressible as a DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) of `narrow` and `wide` dependencies.

### 2.1.2 Categories of Matrix-like Operations

The KTU 2024 Module 4 syllabus groups these into **four canonical categories**:

| Category | Matrix Analogue | DataFrame Operation | Result Cardinality |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Product family** | Outer product $A \otimes B$ | `crossJoin(B)` | $\vert A \vert \cdot \vert B \vert$ rows |
| **Transpose family** | Transpose $A^T$ | `pivot()`, `stack()`, `unpivot()` | $\vert A \vert$ rows, swapped schema |
| **Reduction family** | Sum / Mean along axis | `groupBy().agg(sum/mean/stddev)` | $\le \vert A \vert$ rows |
| **Selection family** | Row/column projection | `select()`, `filter()`, `where()` | $\le \vert A \vert$ rows, $\le n$ cols |

> [!NOTE]
> Each transformation in Spark is **lazy** — it constructs a *logical plan* in the Catalyst optimizer but does not materialize results until an *action* (`count`, `collect`, `show`, `write`) is invoked.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every equation/formula you must memorize for KTU ESE Module 4 questions. Strict KTU notation has been preserved.

| # | Concept | Formula / Operation | Units / Cardinality |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DataFrame as a matrix | $DF \equiv A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ | $m$ = rows, $n$ = cols |
| 2 | Cross Join cardinality | $\vert A \cross B \vert = m_A \cdot m_B$ | rows |
| 3 | Inner Join cardinality | $\vert A \bowtie_K B \vert = \sum_{k} f_A(k) \cdot f_B(k)$ | rows ($f_X(k)$ = frequency of key $k$) |
| 4 | GroupBy reduction | $\text{agg}(A, K, f) = \{f(g_k) \;\vert\; g_k = \sigma_{K=k}(A)\}$ | $\le m$ rows |
| 5 | Pivot transformation | $P_{A}(i,j) = A_{j,i}$ | $m \leftrightarrow n$ |
| 6 | Union (vertical stack) | $\begin{bmatrix} A \\ B \end{bmatrix}$, requires $n_A = n_B$ | $m_A + m_B$ rows |
| 7 | Shuffle cost | $C_{\text{shuffle}} = O\!\left(\frac{m \cdot n}{P}\right)$ | I/O bytes |
| 8 | Stage count (DAG) | $S = N_{\text{wide}} + 1$ | integer |
| 9 | Broadcast join threshold | $\vert B \vert \le \text{spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold}$ (default **10 MB**) | bytes |
| 10 | Catalyst optimization gain | $\text{speedup} = \frac{T_{\text{naive}}}{T_{\text{optimized}}}$ | ratio |

> **Notation Convention:** $\vert X \vert$ denotes cardinality (row-count), never absolute value here — using `\vert` to avoid markdown-table pipe conflicts. The symbol $\sigma$ denotes relational *selection* (SQL `WHERE`) and $\pi$ denotes *projection* (SQL `SELECT`).

## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Where This Matters

Matrix-like operations on Frames are not academic curiosities — they are the **backbone of**:

1. **Feature Engineering Pipelines (MLlib):** Each ML-ready feature vector $\vec{x}_i$ is a DataFrame row; aggregations and pivots build the feature matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$.
2. **OLAP Cube Construction:** `pivot` + `groupBy` produces multi-dimensional aggregations identical to SQL cubes.
3. **Recommender Systems:** User–Item matrix is built as a join of two DataFrames on a *rating key*.
4. **Graph Analytics:** Edge-list to adjacency matrix conversion uses a self-cross-join.
5. **Time-Series Decomposition:** `pivot` converts long-format sensor data into a wide-format matrix for matrix factorization.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1: Row Cardinality of Cross Join

> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU numerical):** Given DataFrame `orders` with $m_A = 100{,}000$ rows and DataFrame `products` with $m_B = 50$ rows, compute the output cardinality of `orders.crossJoin(products)`. State the spark-config parameter that would auto-optimize this to a broadcast join.

### Step 1 — Recall the Cross-Product Definition

For two matrices $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m_A \times n_A}$ and $B \in \mathbb{R}^{m_B \times n_B}$, the **Kronecker product** (which generalizes the outer product) is defined as:

$$
A \otimes B = \begin{bmatrix} a_{11} B & a_{12} B & \cdots & a_{1,n_A} B \\ a_{21} B & a_{22} B & \cdots & a_{2,n_A} B \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ a_{m_A,1} B & a_{m_A,2} B & \cdots & a_{m_A,n_A} B \end{bmatrix}
$$

The result has dimensions $(m_A \cdot m_B) \times (n_A \cdot n_B)$.

### Step 2 — Map to DataFrame Semantics

In DataFrame semantics, a *cross join* produces every possible pairing of rows from the two frames — equivalent to $\vert A \vert \cdot \vert B \vert$ rows. Schema concatenates all $n_A + n_B$ columns. There is no mathematical distinction between column or row vectors here because Spark treats DataFrames as *unordered sets of rows*.

### Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_A &= 100{,}000 \text{ rows (orders)} \\
m_B &= 50 \text{ rows (products)} \\
\vert \text{crossJoin output} \vert &= m_A \cdot m_B \\
&= 100{,}000 \times 50 \\
&= 5{,}000{,}000 \text{ rows}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Identify Optimization Opportunity

Since `products` has only **50 rows**, its serialized size (≈ 50 × ~200 bytes = ~10 KB) is well below the default **broadcast threshold of 10 MB**. Spark will automatically broadcast `products` to every executor — a *map-side* (no-shuffle) join — yielding massive speedup.

$$
\text{broadcast eligible if } \vert B \vert_{\text{bytes}} \le \text{spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold} = 10 \text{ MB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Output = **5,000,000 rows**. The cross join should be auto-optimized to a **BroadcastHashJoin**, eliminating the shuffle phase entirely. This is a *high-frequency KTU 14-mark question pattern*.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2: Inner-Join Cardinality Bound

> **Problem:** Given `customers(cid, name)` with $m_A = 1000$ rows and `orders(oid, cid, amount)` with $m_B = 10{,}000$ rows, where each customer places on average **5 orders**, compute the expected cardinality of `customers.join(orders, "cid")`.

### Step 1 — Write the Inner-Join Cardinality Formula

For an inner join on key $K$, the cardinality is:

$$
\vert A \bowtie_K B \vert = \sum_{k \in K} f_A(k) \cdot f_B(k)
$$

where $f_X(k)$ is the frequency of key $k$ in relation $X$.

### Step 2 — Substitute Known Quantities

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of distinct customers who ordered} &= \frac{m_B}{\text{avg orders/customer}} \\
&= \frac{10{,}000}{5} = 2{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

But wait — customers with zero orders (the **dangling tuples**) do **not appear** in an inner join. Of the 1000 customers, only those who placed at least one order contribute.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Active customers} &= \frac{m_B}{\text{avg orders per active customer}} = \frac{10{,}000}{5} = 2{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

This contradicts — meaning some customers placed more than 5. Let us assume the average *applies to active customers only*:

### Step 3 — Final Card Calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert A \bowtie_K B \vert &= \sum_{k} f_A(k) \cdot f_B(k) \\
&\approx 2{,}000 \text{ active customers} \times 5 \text{ orders each} \\
&= 10{,}000 \text{ rows in the joined output}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Sanity Check

The join cannot exceed $\min(m_A, m_B) \cdot \max(f_B) = 1000 \times \infty$ — but practically equals $m_B = 10{,}000$ rows when *every* order has a valid `cid`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The inner join preserves **all orders** (since each has a matching customer), but discards **customers without orders**. This is *exactly* the relational algebra $\sigma$ (selection) projected onto the join-key domain — a high-yield exam point.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3: Pivot Operation (Transpose Analogue)

> **Problem:** Convert the long-format `sales(region, quarter, revenue)` DataFrame to a wide-format where `region` becomes the row-key and `quarter` becomes column-headers. Show the algebraic mapping.

### Step 1 — Long-Format Matrix Representation

Let $L \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times 3}$ represent the long format with columns $(R, Q, V)$ where $R$ = region, $Q$ = quarter, $V$ = revenue.

Sample data: 4 regions × 4 quarters = 16 rows.

$$
L = \begin{bmatrix}
\text{North} & Q1 & 100 \\
\text{North} & Q2 & 150 \\
\text{North} & Q3 & 200 \\
\text{North} & Q4 & 250 \\
\text{South} & Q1 & 80 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2 — Pivot Algebra

Pivot maps the tuple $(r, q, v) \mapsto$ a cell $(r, q)$ containing $v$. Algebraically, this is a *reshaping*:

$$
P: \mathbb{R}^{m \times 3} \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}^{\vert R \vert \times \vert Q \vert}
$$

where the pivot cell value is the *last* (or aggregated) value per $(r,q)$ group. For aggregation `sum`:

$$
P(r, q) = \sum_{(r_i, q_i, v_i) \in L, \; r_i = r, \; q_i = q} v_i
$$

### Step 3 — Resulting Wide Matrix

$$
W = \begin{bmatrix}
 & Q1 & Q2 & Q3 & Q4 \\
\text{North} & 100 & 150 & 200 & 250 \\
\text{South} & 80 & 90 & 100 & 110 \\
\text{East} & 70 & 75 & 80 & 85 \\
\text{West} & 60 & 65 & 70 & 75
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Notice that the number of rows shrunk from **16 → 4** (the distinct regions), and the schema grew from **3 → 5** columns (region + 4 quarters). This is a structural *transpose + aggregation* — the canonical matrix-like pivot operation.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always state the pivot aggregate function explicitly in your answer — KTU examiners award 2 marks for naming the aggregator (`sum`, `mean`, `first`, `count`).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Spark DataFrame Operation

The diagram below maps the **end-to-end execution flow** when a matrix-like transformation (e.g., `groupBy().sum()`) is invoked on a DataFrame. This is the standard Catalyst-optimizer DAG taught in KTU Module 4.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Code: df.groupBy.region.sum.revenue] --> B[Unresolved Logical Plan]
    B --> C[Analyzer: Resolve Names and Types]
    C --> D[Logical Plan: Optimized DAG]
    D --> E[Physical Planner: Cost-Based Selection]
    E --> F1[Option A: HashAggregate + Shuffle]
    E --> F2[Option B: SortAggregate]
    F1 --> G[Stage 0: Shuffle Read]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[Stage 1: Map-Side Aggregation]
    H --> I[Action Triggered: show or write]
    I --> J[Result DataFrame Returned to Driver]

    style A fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e
    style D fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af
    style F1 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b
    style J fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46
```

### Reading the Diagram

- **Yellow block (A):** The lazy expression — no computation yet.
- **Blue block (D):** Optimized *logical* plan (predicate push-down, constant folding).
- **Red block (F1):** Chosen *physical* plan based on cost.
- **Green block (J):** Final materialized result returned to the driver.

## 4.2 Cross-Join DAG Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Cluster_Executor_1
        A1[Partition 0: rows 1-1000]
        A2[Partition 1: rows 1001-2000]
    end
    subgraph Cluster_Executor_2
        B1[Partition 0: rows 1-10]
    end
    A1 --> C{Broadcast Join Map Side}
    A2 --> C
    B1 --> C
    C --> D[Output Partition: 1M rows each]

    style C fill:#fef08a,stroke:#854d0e
    style D fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534
```

### Key Insight from Diagram

Because the small DataFrame (B1 with 10 rows) is **broadcast** to every executor, the entire cross-join executes as a *map-only* task — **no shuffle stage is required**, saving I/O and network latency.

## 4.3 Pivot Transformation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Long Format: region, quarter, revenue] --> S1[GroupBy: region, quarter]
    S1 --> S2[Pivot: quarter as new columns]
    S2 --> S3[Aggregate: sum revenue]
    S3 --> R2[Wide Format: region x quarter matrix]

    style R1 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e
    style R2 fill:#a7f3d0,stroke:#065f46
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**
> Define a DataFrame in Apache Spark. How does it relate to a matrix in linear algebra?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A DataFrame is a **distributed collection of data organized into named columns**, conceptually equivalent to a matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ where each row represents an observation vector $(x_{i1}, x_{i2}, \ldots, x_{in})$ and each column represents a feature dimension. Like a matrix, it supports relational and algebraic operations — `select` (projection), `filter` (selection), `join` (row-matching), `pivot` (transpose), and `groupBy` + `agg` (axis-wise reduction). However, unlike a numeric matrix, DataFrame columns may hold heterogeneous types (string, int, struct), and operations are evaluated lazily via the Catalyst optimizer. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for matrix mapping, 1 for distinguishing characteristic.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
> List any three matrix-like operations supported by Spark DataFrames. State their relational algebra equivalents.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Operation | Matrix Analogue | Relational Algebra |
|---|---|---|
| `crossJoin(B)` | Outer product $A \otimes B$ | $A \times B$ (Cartesian) |
| `pivot()` | Transpose $A^T$ | Re-structuring projection |
| `groupBy().agg(sum())` | Column-reduction $\sum_j A_{ij}$ | Aggregation $\mathcal{G}_{\text{sum}(V)}(K)$ |

*(3 marks: 1 per row.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Pattern]** Choose **ONE** of the following questions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the concept of matrix-like operations in DataFrames with reference to Apache Spark. Discuss **any four** such operations in detail, illustrating each with the resulting schema transformation. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider two DataFrames:
- `employees(eid INT, dept STRING, salary INT)` with $50{,}000$ rows.
- `departments(dept STRING, location STRING)` with $20$ rows.

Each department has on average $1000$ employees. Compute:
   (i) The row cardinality of `employees.crossJoin(departments)`. *(3 marks)*
   (ii) The row cardinality of `employees.join(departments, "dept")` assuming every employee has a valid department. *(4 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a)** [7 marks]

A DataFrame is the KTU-recommended abstraction for **structured, semi-structured, and distributed data**. It can be viewed as a matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ where each row is an observation vector. Spark supports matrix-like operations that respect relational algebra semantics while leveraging distributed execution via the Catalyst optimizer.

**Four operations in detail:**

**1. `select` (Projection $\pi$):** Selects a subset of columns — analogous to choosing a column subset $A[:, \{j_1, j_2, \ldots\}]$. Schema cardinality reduces from $n \to k$, rows unchanged.

**2. `filter` (Selection $\sigma$):** Row-wise predicate — analogous to masked extraction. Schema unchanged, row-count decreases by the filter selectivity $\alpha$ where $\vert \sigma(A) \vert = \alpha \cdot m$.

**3. `groupBy().agg()` (Axis-reduction):** Groups rows by key(s) and reduces via `sum`, `mean`, `count`. Schema expands to include the aggregate column. Row-count = number of distinct keys.

**4. `join` (Row matching):** Combines rows from two frames on a key. Inner join cardinality bounded by $\min(m_A, m_B) \cdot \max(f)$; outer joins preserve dangling tuples.

**Bonus 5th:** `pivot()` (Transpose): Rows → columns via aggregation; cardinality $m \to \vert K \vert$ rows, $n \to n + \vert Q \vert$ cols.

**(b)** [7 marks]

**(i) Cross Join cardinality:** *(3 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert \text{crossJoin output} \vert &= m_A \cdot m_B \\
&= 50{,}000 \times 20 \\
&= 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ rows}
\end{aligned}
$$

The schema becomes `(eid, dept, salary, dept, location)` with the duplicate `dept` resolved via renaming.

**(ii) Inner Join cardinality:** *(4 marks)*

Every employee has a valid `dept`, and each department has ~1000 employees. Thus every row of `employees` survives the join, paired with the single matching `departments` row.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert \text{innerJoin output} \vert &= \sum_{d \in D} f_{\text{emp}}(d) \cdot f_{\text{dept}}(d) \\
&= 50{,}000 \times 1 \\
&= 50{,}000 \text{ rows}
\end{aligned}
$$

The output schema is `(eid, dept, salary, location)`.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **Catalyst optimizer pipeline** that executes a matrix-like transformation such as `df.groupBy("region").pivot("quarter").sum("revenue")`. Label each stage clearly. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Differentiate between **narrow** and **wide** dependencies in Spark. How does this distinction affect the **execution plan** and **shuffle cost** when performing a `join` versus a `filter` operation? *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a)** [7 marks]

The Catalyst optimizer executes the pipeline in **four sequential stages**:

1. **Unresolved Logical Plan:** The user expression `df.groupBy("region").pivot("quarter").sum("revenue")` is parsed into an *unresolved* abstract syntax tree — column names not yet validated.

2. **Analysis Stage:** The **Analyzer** resolves names against the catalog and infers types. Each node is tagged with schema information.

3. **Logical Optimization:** Rules such as *predicate push-down*, *constant folding*, and *projection pruning* are applied. The output is an **optimized logical plan**.

4. **Physical Planning:** The optimizer generates multiple physical plans (e.g., `HashAggregate` vs `SortAggregate`), estimates cost via statistics, and selects the cheapest.

5. **Execution:** The chosen plan is broken into **stages** (separated by shuffle boundaries) and **tasks** (one per partition), executed on the cluster.

**Block diagram** (reproduce the Mermaid architecture from SECTION 4.1 — recommended for full marks).

**(b)** [7 marks]

| Aspect | Narrow Dependency | Wide Dependency |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Each parent partition contributes to **at most one** child partition | Each parent partition contributes to **multiple** child partitions |
| Spark name | *Map-only* stage | *Shuffle* stage |
| Examples | `filter`, `select`, `map` | `groupByKey`, `reduceByKey`, `join`, `repartition` |
| Shuffle? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Fault tolerance | Recompute single partition | Recompute full stage |

**Effect on `filter`:** Narrow → no shuffle → single stage → executed as map task on each partition locally.

**Effect on `join`:** Wide → requires data to be co-located by key → shuffle stage → group data by join-key, then merge. Cost:

$$
C_{\text{shuffle}} = O\!\left(\frac{m_A + m_B}{P} \cdot \log\!\left(\frac{m_A + m_B}{P}\right)\right)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing `crossJoin` with `join`:** `crossJoin` produces $m_A \times m_B$ rows; `join` (default inner) is bounded by $\min(m_A, m_B)$ — students often lose **2 marks** by interchanging them.
> 2. **Forgetting the broadcast threshold:** When asked to *optimize* a join, students often skip mentioning `autoBroadcastJoinThreshold` (default 10 MB) — this omission costs **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Lazy evaluation oversight:** Writing "the operation executes immediately" instead of "the operation constructs a logical plan and waits for an action" — costs **1 mark** in conceptual questions.
> 4. **Misidentifying pivot direction:** Stating that pivot increases row count — actually, pivot *reduces* row count by aggregating repeated keys — costs **2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read before entering the exam hall:**

- ☐ A **DataFrame** = distributed, named-column table = conceptual matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$.
- ☐ **Cross Join** cardinality = $m_A \cdot m_B$ (Kronecker-style).
- ☐ **Inner Join** cardinality = $\sum_k f_A(k) \cdot f_B(k)$.
- ☐ **Pivot** = transpose + aggregation; rows shrink, columns grow.
- ☐ **Union** = vertical stack; requires identical schema.
- ☐ **Transformations are lazy**, **actions trigger execution** — this is the central Spark paradigm.
- ☐ **Narrow dependency** → no shuffle (e.g., `filter`, `select`).
- ☐ **Wide dependency** → shuffle (e.g., `groupBy`, `join`, `repartition`).
- ☐ Default **broadcast threshold** = **10 MB**; default **shuffle partitions** = **200**.
- ☐ **Catalyst pipeline stages:** Unresolved → Analyzed → Optimized Logical → Physical Plan → Execution.
- ☐ Always state the **aggregate function** (`sum`, `mean`, `first`) when describing a pivot — KTU awards marks for specificity.
- ☐ Cost formula for shuffle: $C_{\text{shuffle}} = O\!\left(\frac{m \cdot n}{P}\right)$ — quote it whenever justifying optimization choices.
- ☐ Distinguish **inner join** (preserves matched rows only) from **outer joins** (preserve dangling tuples from at least one side).

> **End of Module 4 Topic Notes — Matrix-like Operations in Frames**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Standards (NEP 2020 Aligned)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applying Functions to Data Frames
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT746 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applying Functions to Data Frames

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Pandas** library (the de-facto data manipulation toolkit used inside the **PySpark**, **Dask**, and **Modin** Big Data ecosystems), a **DataFrame** is a **two-dimensional, size-mutable, heterogeneous tabular data structure with labeled axes (rows and columns)**. *Applying functions to a DataFrame* refers to the systematic process of **vectorizing, broadcasting, or mapping** a callable (user-defined, anonymous/lambda, or built-in) across one or more of its structural axes — elements, rows, columns, or the entire table — to perform transformation, aggregation, or feature-engineering operations.

The four primary function-application primitives in Pandas are:

| Primitive | Scope | Axis |
|---|---|---|
| `df.apply()` | Row-wise or Column-wise | `axis=0` or `axis=1` |
| `df.applymap()` *(legacy)* / `df.map()` | Element-wise (entire DataFrame) | Cell-by-cell |
| `Series.map()` | Element-wise (single column/Series) | Cell-by-cell |
| `df.agg()` / `df.aggregate()` | Aggregation (multiple) | Row/Column summary |
| `df.transform()` | Element-wise broadcast of an aggregated value | `axis=0` or `axis=1` |
| `df.pipe()` | Whole-DataFrame, function-chaining | Top-level |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the **2024 Scheme**, Module 4 of *Big Data Analytics (PECNT746)* explicitly tests the candidate's ability to *apply user-defined and library functions* to DataFrames for cleaning, transformation, and aggregation — the foundational skill required before progressing to **Spark DataFrames (pyspark.sql.DataFrame)** in higher semesters.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Spreadsheet Workshop

Imagine a **factory assembly line** where wooden pallets (your raw data) arrive on a conveyor belt (the DataFrame).

* **Column-wise application (`axis=0`)** is like a **quality inspector standing at the end of the belt** who *sums* or *averages* the entire column of weights — one *vertical* pass per column.
* **Row-wise application (`axis=1`)** is like a **customizer** who takes each individual pallet (row) and stamps it with the company logo on the *front face* based on its dimensions — one *horizontal* pass per row.
* **Element-wise application** is like a **spray-paint robot** that paints *every single cell* of every pallet individually — slow but precise, used for normalization, scaling, or type conversion.
* **`pipe()`** is the **foreman** who re-routes the entire assembly line through a brand-new station without dismantling the belt.

This mental model helps in instantly selecting the correct function when solving KTU problems under timed examination conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for Big Data:** When you migrate from Pandas to **PySpark** later, the same `apply()` semantics exist in `pyspark.sql.functions` as `F.col().udf()` and `F.aggregate()`. Mastering these in Pandas is therefore a *prerequisite* for distributed Big Data processing.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Function Application

#### 2.1.1 `DataFrame.apply(func, axis=0)` — The Workhorse

* **Operational logic**: Pandas decomposes the DataFrame into either **columns (Series)** when `axis=0` (the default) or **rows (Series whose values come from each column)** when `axis=1`.
* The function `func` receives one such Series at a time and must return a **scalar** (for aggregation) or a **Series** (for row/column-wise transformation).
* **Why use it?** It is the only built-in that natively supports **row-wise composite transformations** (e.g., computing a derived column from three existing columns in one pass).

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" behind `axis`:** In NumPy/Pandas, `axis=0` means *iterate over the first index (rows) collapsing them* — i.e., the result has *one value per column*. `axis=1` means *iterate over the second index (columns) collapsing them* — i.e., the result has *one value per row*.

#### 2.1.2 `DataFrame.applymap(func)` — Legacy Element-wise (Pandas ≤ 2.1)

* **Operational logic**: Visits *every cell* in the DataFrame, applies `func`, and returns a new DataFrame of the same shape.
* **Deprecation Warning**: In modern Pandas (≥ 2.1), `applymap` is **deprecated** in favor of `DataFrame.map()`. KTU examiners may still use the older name in questions; understand both.
* **Why use it?** Type conversion (e.g., `str.upper`), rounding floats, stripping whitespace — operations where broadcasting via arithmetic operators is inconvenient.

#### 2.1.3 `DataFrame.map(func)` — Modern Element-wise (Pandas ≥ 2.1)

* **Operational logic**: Functionally identical to `applymap`, but with a *unified API* that matches `Series.map()`. Accepts a **callable, dict, or Series** for substitution.
* **Why use it?** It is the future-proof, KTU-2024-Scheme recommended primitive for cell-level transformations.

#### 2.1.4 `DataFrame.agg(func, axis=0)` — The Multi-Aggregator

* **Operational logic**: Accepts a *list of function names* (strings), *callables*, or a *dict mapping column → function*. Returns aggregated results.
* **Why use it?** When you need `mean`, `std`, `min`, `max` of multiple columns in a *single* call — a classic KTU 14-marker scenario.

#### 2.1.5 `DataFrame.transform(func, axis=0)` — The Shape-Preserver

* **Operational logic**: Like `agg`, but the returned array **must be broadcastable back** to the original shape. Used inside `groupby` for group-wise normalization (e.g., z-score within group).
* **Why use it?** `agg` *reduces* dimensionality; `transform` *preserves* it. This distinction is a high-frequency KTU short-answer question.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Function Call Signature | Returns | Shape After | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| `df.apply(f, axis=0)` | Scalar per column | `(n_columns,)` or `(n_rows, k_new_cols)` | Column mean, row-wise custom logic |
| `df.apply(f, axis=1)` | Scalar per row | `(n_rows,)` | Create derived column from N existing |
| `df.applymap(f)` | Cell-mapped DataFrame | Same as input | String cleanup, type coercion |
| `df.map(f)` | Cell-mapped DataFrame | Same as input | Modern replacement of `applymap` |
| `df.agg(['mean','std'])` | Aggregated DataFrame | `(n_aggs, n_columns)` | Statistical summary |
| `df.transform(lambda x: x - x.mean())` | Same-shape DataFrame | Same as input | Within-group centering |
| `df.pipe(f, *args)` | Whichever `f` returns | Depends on `f` | Method-chaining pipelines |
| `np.sqrt(df)` | Vectorized DataFrame | Same as input | Ufunc broadcasting (fastest) |
| `df['col'].map(dict)` | Mapped Series | `(n_rows,)` | Categorical label encoding |
| `df['col'].apply(f)` | Mapped Series | `(n_rows,)` | Element-wise on single column |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In *production Big Data pipelines*, applying functions incorrectly (e.g., using Python `apply` over a million-row Spark DataFrame instead of a built-in UDF) causes severe *Out-of-Memory (OOM)* errors and *job re-computation*. Hence KTU places heavy emphasis on choosing the **right primitive**.

### 2.3 Why These Functions Exist — The Theoretical Justification

Pandas DataFrames are built atop **NumPy ndarrays**. NumPy offers **vectorized universal functions (ufuncs)** — element-wise C-level operations that bypass Python's interpreter overhead. When a transformation can be expressed as a ufunc (e.g., `np.log(df)`, `df + 5`, `df > 10`), it is the **fastest** path.

However, when the transformation involves **conditional branching, multi-column logic, or string manipulation**, ufuncs are insufficient. The `apply` family fills this gap by lifting the operation into a Python-level loop, accepting the performance cost in exchange for *expressiveness*. The hierarchy from fastest to slowest is:

$$\text{Vectorized Ufunc} \;\;>\;\; \text{Series.map} \;\;>\;\; \text{DataFrame.apply} \;\;>\;\; \text{DataFrame.applymap}$$

A KTU-2024 examiner loves asking: *"Which is faster, `df.apply(np.sqrt)` or `np.sqrt(df)`?"* — the answer is always the latter, by an order of magnitude.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Column-wise Aggregation with `apply(axis=0)`

**Problem Setup:** Given a DataFrame `df` containing the marks of B.Tech students in three subjects, compute the **average marks per subject** (column-wise mean).

**Step 1 — Data Construction:**

```python
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Maths'   : [85, 90, 78, 92, 88],
    'Physics' : [72, 80, 75, 89, 70],
    'CS'      : [95, 88, 82, 91, 94]
}, index=['Anu','Binu','Cyril','Diya','Eshan'])

print(df)
```

**Step 2 — Applying a NumPy function via `apply` (column-wise):**

```python
column_means = df.apply(np.mean, axis=0)
print(column_means)
```

**Step 3 — Trace of execution (this is what KTU examiners want to see in the answer sheet):**

When `axis=0` is invoked, Pandas internally performs the following logic:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{For each column } c \in \{\text{Maths, Physics, CS}\}: \quad
\text{result}[c] &= \text{np.mean}\bigl(\text{df}[c]\bigr) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{df}[c][i] \\[4pt]
\text{For Maths: } \quad \text{result}[\text{Maths}] &= \frac{85+90+78+92+88}{5} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{433}{5} = 86.6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Output verification:**

```text
Maths      86.6
Physics    77.2
CS         90.0
dtype: float64
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Explanation:** `axis=0` collapses *along the row index*. The result Series has **one entry per column** — the length of the result equals `df.shape[1]`.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Row-wise Composite Feature with `apply(axis=1)`

**Problem:** Create a new column `'Total'` that stores the *sum of the best two subjects* for each student.

**Step 1 — Define a callable that takes a row and returns a scalar:**

```python
def best_two_total(row):
    return row.nlargest(2).sum()

df['Total'] = df.apply(best_two_total, axis=1)
```

**Step 2 — Internal trace for the first student (Anu):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{row} &= [\text{Maths}=85,\;\text{Physics}=72,\;\text{CS}=95] \\[4pt]
\text{nlargest}(2) &= [95, 85] \\[4pt]
\text{row.nlargest}(2).\text{sum}() &= 95 + 85 = 180
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Vectorized (faster) alternative using NumPy:**

```python
df['Total_Vec'] = np.sort(df.values, axis=1)[:, -2:].sum(axis=1)
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** In a 14-mark question, *always* show **both** approaches — the `apply` version (for *clarity*) and the vectorized version (for *efficiency*). Examiners explicitly award marks for performance-aware coding.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Element-wise Cleaning with `map()`

**Problem:** Standardize the names in the index to uppercase and round all numeric cells to 2 decimal places.

```python
df.index = df.index.map(str.upper)

df_rounded = df.map(lambda x: round(x, 2) if isinstance(x, (int, float)) else x)
```

**Trace for a single cell** `df_rounded.loc['ANU', 'Maths']`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= 85 \\[4pt]
\text{isinstance}(x, (\text{int}, \text{float})) &= \text{True} \\[4pt]
\text{return} \; \text{round}(85, 2) &= 85.0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the `isinstance` check?** The `map` function visits *every* cell, including string index labels that may have been propagated. The guard prevents `TypeError` when `round` receives a non-numeric.

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Multi-Aggregation with `agg()`

**Problem:** Generate a single statistical summary table containing `min`, `max`, `mean`, and `std` for every numerical column.

```python
summary = df.agg(['min', 'max', 'mean', 'std'])
print(summary.round(2))
```

**Trace of the diagonal cell** `summary.loc['mean', 'Maths']`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{\text{Maths}} &= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \\[4pt]
&= \frac{85+90+78+92+88}{5} = 86.6
\end{aligned}
$$

The resulting DataFrame has shape `(4, 3)` — a 2D summary table, *not* a flat Series. This is the canonical output of `agg`.

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Function Do I Use?

| Your Goal | Recommended Function | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Compute column/row means, sums, stds | `df.apply(np.func, axis=...)` | Returns clean Series/Series of scalars |
| Build a *new column* from N existing | `df['new'] = df.apply(f, axis=1)` | Row-wise access via `row['col1']` |
| Modify *every cell* (e.g., strip strings) | `df.map(f)` | Cell-by-cell traversal |
| Get min/max/mean in one call | `df.agg(['min','max','mean'])` | Reduces Python function-call overhead |
| Group-wise standardization | `df.transform(f)` | Preserves shape for broadcasting |
| Chain 5+ transformations | `df.pipe(f1).pipe(f2)...` | Readable, debuggable pipelines |
| Encode categorical labels | `df['col'].map(dict)` | Dict-based lookup is O(1) on average |
| Apply math like `log`, `exp`, `sqrt` | Use NumPy directly: `np.log(df)` | Vectorized C-speed execution |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Function-Application Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: DataFrame df] --> B0{What is the scope?}
    B0 -->|Entire row or column| C0[Use df.apply func axis]
    B0 -->|Every single cell| D0[Use df.map func]
    B0 -->|Multiple stats at once| E0[Use df.agg list of funcs]
    B0 -->|Shape must be preserved| F0[Use df.transform func]
    B0 -->|Chain many steps| G0[Use df.pipe func]

    C0 --> C1{Aggregate or transform?}
    C1 -->|Return one value per row col| C2[Set axis equals 0 or 1]
    C1 -->|Return Series per call| C3[Define row wise logic]

    D0 --> D1{Modern or legacy Pandas?}
    D1 -->|Pandas less than 2.1| D2[Use df.applymap func]
    D1 -->|Pandas greater than or equal 2.1| D3[Use df.map func]

    E0 --> E1{Function per column different?}
    E1 -->|Yes| E2[Pass a dict]
    E1 -->|No| E3[Pass a list of strings]

    F0 --> F1{Usecase}
    F1 -->|Group-wise centering| F2[df.groupby col .transform f]
    F1 -->|Window normalization| F3[Combine with rolling]

    G0 --> G1[Build method chain]
    G1 --> G2[df.pipe f1 .pipe f2 .pipe f3]
```

### 4.2 Axis-Resolution Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AxisZero[axis equals 0 Column-wise collapse]
        I1A[Row 1] --> I1B[Column Aggregator]
        I2A[Row 2] --> I1B
        I3A[Row 3] --> I1B
        I4A[Row 4] --> I1B
        I1B --> O1[One Scalar per Column]
    end

    subgraph AxisOne[axis equals 1 Row-wise collapse]
        J1A[Col 1] --> J1B[Row Aggregator]
        J2A[Col 2] --> J1B
        J3A[Col 3] --> J1B
        J1B --> O2[One Scalar per Row]
    end
```

### 4.3 Function-Application Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw DataFrame Input] --> B{Stage 1 Validation}
    B -->|Missing values present| B1[Apply imputation map]
    B -->|Clean| C[Stage 2 Transformation]
    B1 --> C

    C --> C1{Numeric columns?}
    C1 -->|Yes| C2[Vectorized np ufunc OR apply axis 0]
    C1 -->|No| C3[Row-wise apply axis 1 for composite features]

    C2 --> D[Stage 3 Aggregation]
    C3 --> D
    D --> D1{Need summary stats}
    D1 -->|Yes| D2[agg with min max mean std]
    D1 -->|No| D3[transform for group normalization]

    D2 --> E[Stage 4 Output]
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Refined Big Data Ready DataFrame]
```

### 4.4 Performance Comparison Pyramid (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Apex: NumPy Ufunc — C-level speed] --> P1
    P1[Layer 2: Vectorized arithmetic on Series] --> P2
    P2[Layer 3: df.apply with built-in NumPy function] --> P3
    P3[Layer 4: df.apply with custom Python function] --> P4
```
