```mermaid
    P4[Base: df.applymap OR df.map cell-by-cell traversal]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Differentiate between `DataFrame.apply()` and `DataFrame.applymap()` in Pandas. When would you prefer one over the other?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

* **`DataFrame.apply(func, axis)`** traverses the DataFrame *one row at a time* (when `axis=1`) or *one column at a time* (when `axis=0`). Each call to `func` receives an entire row or column as a `Series`. It is best used for *composite transformations* that need access to multiple cells of a row simultaneously, or for *aggregations* that reduce a row/column to a scalar.

* **`DataFrame.applymap(func)`** (legacy) / **`DataFrame.map(func)`** (modern) visits *every individual cell* of the DataFrame. The function `func` receives a *scalar* and must return a *scalar*. It is best used for *element-wise operations* such as rounding, type conversion, or string cleanup that do not require cross-cell logic.

* **Selection criterion:** Use `apply` when the transformation *depends on other cells in the same row*; use `applymap`/`map` when the transformation is *purely local to each cell* and the same function is applied uniformly. **(3 marks — 1 mark per bullet)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is the difference between `df.agg()` and `df.transform()`? Can both return a DataFrame of the same shape as the input?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

* `df.agg()` **reduces** the dimensionality of the input along the specified axis. Its output shape is *smaller* than the input — e.g., aggregating 100 rows into 1 mean value. It supports multiple aggregation functions in a single call.
* `df.transform()` **preserves** the original shape. The function passed to `transform` must return a value broadcastable to the size of the input group. It is used for operations like *group-wise z-score normalization* or *filling missing values with the group mean*.
* **Answer to the direct question:** *No*, only `transform()` can return a DataFrame of the **same shape** as the input. `agg()` returns a smaller, aggregated shape. **(3 marks — 1 mark per point)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing `axis` direction** — writing `axis=1` when the question demands column-wise operation costs 1–2 marks. Always *re-read* the question for "row-wise" vs "column-wise".
> 2. **Forgetting `import numpy as np`** — most `apply` solutions need NumPy functions; missing the import is a common deduction.
> 3. **Returning wrong shape** — `apply` on `axis=1` that returns a `Series` per row creates extra columns. State the expected output shape explicitly.
> 4. **Not showing the trace** — for 7-mark sub-parts, KTU examiners expect at least *one* line of algebraic or pseudo-code trace.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Given the following DataFrame of employee data, write a Pandas program that uses `apply()` to:

(i) Compute the **column-wise total salary** (1 mark for the call, 2 marks for the trace).
(ii) Compute the **row-wise experience-weighted bonus**, defined as `Bonus = (Salary × 0.10) + (Experience × 5000)`, producing a new column `'Bonus'` (3 marks for the function, 1 mark for the output verification).

```python
df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Name'      : ['Anu', 'Binu', 'Cyril', 'Diya'],
    'Salary'    : [60000, 75000, 50000, 90000],
    'Experience': [3, 5, 2, 8]
})
```

**(b)** Using the same DataFrame, demonstrate **element-wise rounding** of the `Salary` column to the nearest thousand using both `applymap`/`map` *and* a vectorized NumPy alternative. Compare the two approaches in 4–5 lines (4 marks: 2 for code, 2 for comparison).

---

**Model Solution — Question A:**

**(a) (i) Column-wise total salary — 3 marks:**

```python
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Name'      : ['Anu', 'Binu', 'Cyril', 'Diya'],
    'Salary'    : [60000, 75000, 50000, 90000],
    'Experience': [3, 5, 2, 8]
})

total_salary = df[['Salary']].apply(np.sum, axis=0)
print(total_salary)
```

**Trace:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{total\_salary}[\text{'Salary'}] &= \sum_{i=1}^{4} \text{Salary}_i \\[4pt]
&= 60000 + 75000 + 50000 + 90000 \\[4pt]
&= 275000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Output:** `Salary    275000` (3 marks)

**(a) (ii) Row-wise bonus — 4 marks:**

```python
def calc_bonus(row):
    return (row['Salary'] * 0.10) + (row['Experience'] * 5000)

df['Bonus'] = df.apply(calc_bonus, axis=1)
print(df)
```

**Trace for Anu:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bonus}_{\text{Anu}} &= (60000 \times 0.10) + (3 \times 5000) \\[4pt]
&= 6000 + 15000 = 21000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Trace for Diya:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bonus}_{\text{Diya}} &= (90000 \times 0.10) + (8 \times 5000) \\[4pt]
&= 9000 + 40000 = 49000
\end{aligned}
$$

(4 marks)

**(b) Element-wise rounding with comparison — 4 marks:**

```python
# Approach 1: Using DataFrame.map (element-wise, modern Pandas)
df_round_map = df[['Salary']].map(lambda x: round(x / 1000) * 1000)

# Approach 2: Vectorized NumPy (C-speed)
df_round_vec = (df['Salary'] // 1000) * 1000

print("Map approach:\n", df_round_map)
print("Vectorized approach:\n", df_round_vec)
```

**Comparison:**

| Aspect | `df.map()` approach | Vectorized NumPy approach |
|---|---|---|
| Speed | Slow (Python-level loop) | Fast (C-level operation) |
| Memory | Higher (intermediate objects) | Lower (in-place arithmetic) |
| Flexibility | Can host any custom callable | Limited to ufuncs/operators |
| Best for | String ops, complex per-cell logic | Arithmetic, scaling, rounding |

(4 marks — 2 for code, 2 for comparison)

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of `axis` in `DataFrame.apply()` with reference to a *concrete* numeric example involving a 4×3 DataFrame of student marks. Show, with traces, how the output shape changes when `axis=0` vs `axis=1`. **(7 marks: 2 for theory + 5 for worked example + trace)**

**(b)** What is the role of `df.pipe()` in building Big Data analytics pipelines? Write a 3-stage pipeline that takes a raw DataFrame, applies (i) a missing-value imputer, (ii) a column-wise standardizer `(x - mean) / std`, and (iii) a column-wise summary aggregator. Show the final output for a 5×3 numeric DataFrame. **(7 marks: 2 for theory + 5 for code + output)**

---

**Model Solution — Question B:**

**(a) The `axis` parameter in `DataFrame.apply()` — 7 marks:**

* **Theoretical Basis (2 marks):** In NumPy/Pandas, `axis=0` refers to operations that *collapse* along the row index (i.e., aggregate *across* rows, producing one value per column). `axis=1` collapses along the column index (i.e., aggregate *across* columns, producing one value per row). The function passed to `apply` is invoked once per *slice* along the chosen axis.

* **Worked Example with Trace (5 marks):**

```python
df = pd.DataFrame({
    'M1': [80, 70, 90, 85],
    'M2': [75, 88, 72, 95],
    'M3': [92, 65, 80, 78]
}, index=['S1','S2','S3','S4'])
```

**Case 1 — `axis=0` (column-wise mean):**

```python
col_means = df.apply(np.mean, axis=0)
```

**Trace:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{col\_means}[\text{M1}] &= \frac{80+70+90+85}{4} = 81.25 \\[4pt]
\text{col\_means}[\text{M2}] &= \frac{75+88+72+95}{4} = 82.50 \\[4pt]
\text{col\_means}[\text{M3}] &= \frac{92+65+80+78}{4} = 78.75
\end{aligned}
$$

Result shape: `(3,)` — one scalar per **column**.

**Case 2 — `axis=1` (row-wise mean):**

```python
row_means = df.apply(np.mean, axis=1)
```

**Trace for S1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{row\_means}[\text{S1}] &= \frac{80+75+92}{3} = 82.33
\end{aligned}
$$

Result shape: `(4,)` — one scalar per **row**.

* **Key Takeaway (implied):** Always state the resulting shape in your answer; KTU examiners award a *bonus mark* for shape awareness.

---

**(b) `df.pipe()` and the 3-stage analytics pipeline — 7 marks:**

* **Theoretical Basis (2 marks):** `df.pipe(func, *args, **kwargs)` is a *method-chaining helper* that allows you to apply a function taking the DataFrame as its *first argument* without breaking the chain. It is the Pandas equivalent of Unix pipes (`|`) and is invaluable in Big Data pipelines where the same DataFrame must flow through many stages (cleaning → transforming → aggregating) while remaining readable and testable.

* **Three-Stage Pipeline Code (3 marks):**

```python
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

def impute_missing(df, strategy='mean'):
    return df.fillna(df.mean() if strategy == 'mean' else 0)

def standardize(df):
    return (df - df.mean()) / df.std()

def summarize(df):
    return df.agg(['mean', 'std', 'min', 'max'])

raw = pd.DataFrame({
    'A': [10, 20, np.nan, 40, 50],
    'B': [5, np.nan, 15, 20, 25],
    'C': [1, 2, 3, np.nan, 5]
})

processed = (
    raw
    .pipe(impute_missing, strategy='mean')
    .pipe(standardize)
    .pipe(summarize)
)

print(processed.round(3))
```

* **Final Output (2 marks):**

```text
         A      B      C
mean  0.000  0.000  0.000
std   1.000  1.000  1.000
min  -1.414 -1.414 -1.414
max   1.414  1.414  1.414
```

**Verification of standardization (1 mark for the trace):** After imputation, the mean of each column becomes exactly 0 and the standard deviation becomes exactly 1, by the *definition* of z-score standardization.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **`df.apply(f, axis=0)`** → collapses *rows*, returns **one value per column**.
* **`df.apply(f, axis=1)`** → collapses *columns*, returns **one value per row**; ideal for building a new column from multiple existing columns.
* **`df.map(f)`** (Pandas ≥ 2.1) / **`df.applymap(f)`** (legacy) → element-wise; visit every *cell*; slower due to Python-level loop.
* **`df.agg([...])`** → multi-function aggregation; **reduces** shape; supports list, dict, or callable inputs.
* **`df.transform(f)`** → **preserves** shape; the returned array must be broadcastable to the group size; used for *group-wise normalization*.
* **`df.pipe(f)`** → method-chaining helper; the function `f` receives the DataFrame as its *first* argument.
* **Performance Hierarchy (fastest → slowest):** *NumPy ufunc → Series vectorized arithmetic → df.apply with NumPy function → df.apply with custom Python function → df.map / df.applymap*.
* **Never** use Python-level `apply` when a NumPy ufunc exists; *vectorize first*, apply only when unavoidable.
* **For Big Data (Spark migration):** Pandas `apply` does *not* parallelize; it is **single-threaded**. Always prefer `pyspark.sql.functions` UDFs with `F.pandas_udf` for distributed computation.
* **Common 3-marker question pattern:** *"Differentiate between `apply` and `applymap`"* or *"What is the role of `axis` in `apply`?"* — prepare crisp 3-bullet answers.
* **Common 14-marker question pattern:** A composite problem involving **data construction → function application (apply/map/agg) → trace → vectorized alternative** — practice the 5-step derivation pattern shown in Section 3.
* **Always include a shape comment** in your code: `# shape: (4, 3) → (3,)` — this single line often earns the *extra* one mark in KTU valuation.
* **Deprecation alert:** `DataFrame.applymap` is deprecated in Pandas 2.1+. KTU answers should mention `DataFrame.map` as the modern equivalent for forward compatibility.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reading and Writing Files
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT746 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reading and Writing Files

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Big Data Analytics**, **Reading and Writing Files** refers to the systematic process of **Input/Output (I/O) operations** that allow distributed computing frameworks to ingest raw data from persistent storage (such as **HDFS — Hadoop Distributed File System**, local file systems, Amazon S3, or NoSQL databases) and persist processed results back to storage. These operations form the **data ingress and egress layer** of any big data pipeline.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, this topic encompasses:

- **File I/O APIs in Hadoop MapReduce** (`FileInputFormat`, `FileOutputFormat`)
- **Sequence Files** (binary key-value pair storage)
- **Reading/Writing in Apache Spark** (RDD `textFile`, `saveAsTextFile`, `saveAsSequenceFile`)
- **Compression codecs** (Gzip, Snappy, LZO, Bzip2)
- **File formats** (Text, CSV, JSON, Avro, Parquet, ORC)
- **Custom RecordReader / RecordWriter** implementations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECNT746 – Module 4):** Reading and Writing Files is positioned as a foundational pillar for data ingestion in distributed systems. Students must master the conceptual flow from **HDFS block-level read/write** → **InputSplit** → **RecordReader** → **Mapper/Reducer** → **OutputCommitter**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine a **massive library** that stores millions of books spread across **1000 different warehouses** (this is your HDFS cluster). You, as a librarian, are asked to:

1. **Read** — Find every book written in 2024 and count how many times the word "data" appears.
2. **Write** — Create a new summary report and store it back in the library's catalog.

You obviously cannot carry the entire library to your desk. Instead, you hire **100 helpers** (these are your *Map tasks*), each going to a nearby warehouse, picking up a **box of books** (a *block*, typically **128 MB** or **256 MB**), and reading only the relevant pages (a *record*). They each write a small note with their findings (a *map output*), then a senior librarian (*reducer*) combines all notes into the final report and files it back into a specific warehouse shelf (*HDFS path*).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Reading = **Data Ingestion** (HDFS → Memory). Writing = **Data Persistence** (Memory → HDFS). The frameworks (Hadoop/Spark) handle the **distribution, fault-tolerance, and parallelization** automatically, but the developer must understand *which API to invoke* and *which format to choose*.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bolded Standard Metrics)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **HDFS Block Size** | **128 MB** (default) or **256 MB** in modern Hadoop |
| **Replication Factor** | **3** (default) |
| **InputSplit** | A chunk of data (typically one HDFS block) processed by a single Mapper |
| **RecordReader** | Converts a byte-oriented InputSplit into key-value records |
| **OutputCommitter** | Manages the commit/cleanup protocol for task outputs |
| **Compression Codec** | Algorithm (Gzip, Snappy, Bzip2, LZO) used to shrink I/O footprint |
| **SequenceFile** | Hadoop's binary flat-file format consisting of serialized key-value pairs |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiners' Tip:** Always mention the **default HDFS block size (128 MB)** and **default replication factor (3)** when describing file I/O in Hadoop. This shows depth of understanding.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hadoop File I/O Operational Pipeline

The file reading/writing process in Hadoop MapReduce follows a **well-defined layered architecture**. Below is the complete operational flow broken into logical steps.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**A. File Reading Pipeline (Input Path)**

1. **Client Submission** — The user invokes `job.setInputFormatClass(TextInputFormat.class)` to declare the input file format.
2. **InputFormat.getSplits()** — Hadoop contacts the **NameNode** to retrieve the file's **block locations** across the cluster. Each block becomes one `InputSplit`.
3. **RecordReader Initialization** — A `LineRecordReader` (for text) or `SequenceFileRecordReader` (for binary) is created for each split. It tracks the current byte offset within the block.
4. **Block Transfer** — The **DataNode** holding the relevant block streams the bytes to the Mapper's JVM over the network (data locality is preferred, but rack-locality is the fallback).
5. **Key-Value Emission** — The `RecordReader.nextKeyValue()` method parses the byte stream and emits `(K, V)` pairs. For `TextInputFormat`:
   - **Key** = byte offset of the line (LongWritable)
   - **Value** = the line content (Text)
6. **Mapper.map()** — Each `(K, V)` is processed by user-defined logic, producing zero or more intermediate `(K, V)` pairs.
7. **Partitioner & Shuffle** — Intermediate pairs are partitioned, sorted, and shuffled to Reducers.

**B. File Writing Pipeline (Output Path)**

1. **Reducer.reduce()** — Aggregated `(K, list<V>)` pairs are processed.
2. **OutputCollector.write(K, V)** — The reducer emits final `(K, V)` pairs.
3. **RecordWriter** — The format's writer (e.g., `LineRecordWriter`) serializes the pairs to a byte stream.
4. **OutputCommitter.commitJob()** — A `_SUCCESS` marker file is written; temporary output directories are promoted to the final HDFS path.
5. **Block Replication** — The newly written blocks are replicated to **2 additional DataNodes** (default RF = 3).

---

## 2.2 Spark File I/O Operations

Apache Spark provides a **lazy, RDD/DataFrame-based abstraction** for file I/O. Reading and writing are *transformations* and *actions* respectively.

### 2.2.1 Reading Files in Spark

**Scala/Python API Examples:**

```scala
// Text File
val lines = sc.textFile("hdfs:///data/input.txt")

// Whole Text Files (filename, content)
val files = sc.wholeTextFiles("hdfs:///data/folder/")

// Sequence File
val seqData = sc.sequenceFile[String, Int]("hdfs:///data/seq")

// Multiple formats
val csvDF = spark.read.csv("hdfs:///data/*.csv")
val parquetDF = spark.read.parquet("hdfs:///data/*.parquet")
val jsonDF = spark.read.json("hdfs:///data/*.json")
```

### 2.2.2 Writing Files in Spark

```scala
// Save as Text File
lines.saveAsTextFile("hdfs:///data/output/")

// Save as Sequence File
seqData.saveAsSequenceFile("hdfs:///data/seq_output/")

// Save in columnar formats
parquetDF.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("hdfs:///data/parquet_out/")
csvDF.write.mode("append").csv("s3://bucket/csv_out/")
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** Spark's `saveAsTextFile` produces a **directory containing multiple part-files** (`part-00000`, `part-00001`, ...), one per partition. This is a frequent exam question.

---

## 2.3 Compression Codecs — The I/O Performance Lever

| Codec | Algorithm | Splittable? | Compression Ratio | Speed | Hadoop Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Gzip** | DEFLATE | **No** | High | Slow | `GzipCodec` |
| **Snappy** | LZ77 variant | **No** | Medium | **Very Fast** | `SnappyCodec` |
| **LZO** | LZ77 | **Yes** (with index) | Medium | Fast | `LzoCodec` |
| **Bzip2** | BWT + RLE + Huffman | **Yes** | **Very High** | Slowest | `Bzip2Codec` |
| **Zstd** | Modern Zstandard | **No** | High | Fast | `ZStandardCodec` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Splitability is critical.** A *splittable* compressed file allows multiple Mappers to read disjoint ranges in parallel, preserving MapReduce's parallel-processing advantage. Gzip and Snappy files are **not splittable**, so the entire file becomes a single InputSplit — this can create severe data skew.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression / Value | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of Mappers** | $M = \lceil \dfrac{\text{File Size}}{\text{HDFS Block Size}} \rceil$ | The count of InputSplits equals the number of Mappers |
| **HDFS Block Size** | $B = 128 \text{ MB}$ (default) | Physical chunking unit of files on DataNodes |
| **Replication Factor** | $RF = 3$ (default) | Number of block copies across cluster |
| **Storage Overhead** | $S_{total} = \text{File Size} \times RF$ | Total disk usage with replication |
| **Read Throughput (Theoretical)** | $T_r = \dfrac{B \times M_{local}}{t_{disk}}$ | Throughput with data-local Mappers |
| **Compression Ratio** | $C_r = \dfrac{\text{Original Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}}$ | Higher $C_r$ = better compression |
| **Effective Splits (Compressed)** | $S_{eff} = S_{splittable} \cdot N_{blocks}$ | Splittable codecs allow $N_{blocks}$ parallel Mappers |
| **SequenceFile Record Size** | $R_{size} = \vert K \vert + \vert V \vert + \text{Header}$ | Each record has length-prefixed binary header |

> **Note on LaTeX:** Absolute value expressions are written as $\vert x \vert$ to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking:** Daily transaction logs (terabytes) are read from HDFS using `TextInputFormat`, processed by MapReduce, and written back as **Parquet** for OLAP queries.
- **Healthcare:** Patient EHR files in **JSON/Avro** are read, transformed, and persisted in columnar formats for fast analytics.
- **Social Media Analytics:** Clickstream data is streamed in real-time (Kafka) and written to **SequenceFiles** for batch MapReduce processing.
- **IoT:** Sensor readings compressed with **Snappy** are written to HDFS for energy-efficient storage and rapid reading.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation 1: Number of Mappers from File Size

**Problem Statement:** A log file of size **2.7 GB** is stored on HDFS. The default HDFS block size is **128 MB**. Determine the number of Mappers (`InputSplits`) that will be created.

**Given:**
- File Size: $F = 2.7 \text{ GB} = 2.7 \times 1024 \text{ MB} = 2764.8 \text{ MB}$
- Block Size: $B = 128 \text{ MB}$

**Step 1 — Convert Units (SI compliance):**

$$
F = 2.7 \text{ GB} \times \frac{1024 \text{ MB}}{1 \text{ GB}} = 2764.8 \text{ MB}
$$

This step ensures uniform units (MB) for the division that follows.

**Step 2 — Apply the Mapper Count Formula:**

$$
M = \left\lceil \frac{F}{B} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Numerical Values:**

$$
M = \left\lceil \frac{2764.8 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 4 — Perform the Division:**

$$
\frac{2764.8}{128} = 21.6
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Ceiling Function:**

Since we cannot have a fractional Mapper, we round **up** to the next whole integer:

$$
M = \lceil 21.6 \rceil = 22
$$

**Final Answer:** The file will be split into **22 InputSplits**, hence **22 Mappers** will be launched.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ceiling and not floor?** Hadoop splits a file at block boundaries. If the last block is only partially filled, it still requires its own dedicated Mapper. Hence, ceiling is the correct mathematical operation.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation 2: Storage Overhead with Replication

**Problem Statement:** A company stores **500 GB** of raw data in HDFS with the default replication factor. Calculate the total physical storage required.

**Given:**
- Logical Data Size: $D = 500 \text{ GB}$
- Replication Factor: $RF = 3$

**Step 1 — Identify the Storage Formula:**

$$
S_{total} = D \times RF
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Values:**

$$
S_{total} = 500 \text{ GB} \times 3
$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$
S_{total} = 1500 \text{ GB} = 1.5 \text{ TB}
$$

**Final Answer:** The cluster consumes **1.5 TB** of physical storage to durably hold 500 GB of logical data.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation 3: Compression Ratio Analysis

**Problem Statement:** A 400 MB CSV file is compressed with Gzip to 80 MB. Calculate the compression ratio. If the same file were stored on HDFS with a default block size of 128 MB, how many mappers would run for the **uncompressed** vs. **compressed** versions?

**Step 1 — Compute Compression Ratio:**

$$
C_r = \frac{\text{Original Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}} = \frac{400 \text{ MB}}{80 \text{ MB}} = 5
$$

A ratio of **5:1** means the compressed file is 5 times smaller.

**Step 2 — Mappers for Uncompressed File:**

$$
M_{uncomp} = \left\lceil \frac{400}{128} \right\rceil = \lceil 3.125 \rceil = 4 \text{ Mappers}
$$

**Step 3 — Mappers for Compressed (Gzip) File:**

Since Gzip is **non-splittable**, the entire 80 MB compressed file becomes a single InputSplit:

$$
M_{comp} = 1 \text{ Mapper}
$$

**Step 4 — Performance Implication:**

The compression saved **320 MB** of disk space but reduced parallelism from **4 to 1 Mapper**, increasing processing time for this single file.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: File Format Comparison

| Format | Type | Splittable? | Schema Evolution | Read Speed | Write Speed | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Text (.txt)** | Row-based, Plain | **Yes** | No | Slow | Fast | Logs, raw dumps |
| **CSV** | Row-based, Plain | **Yes** | No | Slow | Fast | Tabular exports |
| **JSON** | Row-based, Semi-structured | **Yes** | No | Slow | Medium | API payloads, NoSQL exports |
| **SequenceFile** | Row-based, Binary (K-V) | **Yes** | No | Fast | Fast | Intermediate MapReduce output |
| **Avro** | Row-based, Binary, Schema-in-data | **Yes** | **Yes** | Fast | Fast | Streaming, schema evolution |
| **Parquet** | **Columnar**, Binary | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Very Fast (analytics)** | Medium | OLAP, analytics workloads |
| **ORC** | **Columnar**, Binary | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Very Fast** | Fast | Hive-optimized analytics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Row vs. Columnar Trade-off:** Row-based formats (Text, Avro) are optimized for **write-heavy** workloads. Columnar formats (Parquet, ORC) are optimized for **read-heavy analytical queries** because only the required columns are read from disk (predicate pushdown).

---

## 3.5 Detailed Walkthrough: Writing a SequenceFile in Spark

**Scenario:** You have an RDD of `(String, Int)` representing word counts and wish to persist it as a `SequenceFile` on HDFS.

**Step 1 — Create the RDD:**

```scala
val wordCounts = sc.parallelize(Seq(("hadoop", 5), ("spark", 3), ("hdfs", 7)))
```

**Step 2 — Save as SequenceFile:**

```scala
wordCounts.saveAsSequenceFile("hdfs:///user/data/wordcounts_seq")
```

**Step 3 — Verify the Output Directory Structure:**

```
hdfs:///user/data/wordcounts_seq/
├── _SUCCESS                 (marker file, written by OutputCommitter)
├── part-00000               (binary record 1, 2, ...)
├── part-00001
└── part-00002
```

**Step 4 — Read It Back:**

```scala
val readBack = sc.sequenceFile[String, Int]("hdfs:///user/data/wordcounts_seq")
readBack.collect().foreach(println)
// Output: (hadoop,5), (spark,3), (hdfs,7)
```

**Step 5 — Internal Record Layout (Binary):**

Each record in a `SequenceFile` has the structure:

$$
\text{Record} = \underbrace{\text{Length Prefix}}_{\text{4 bytes}} + \underbrace{\text{Key Bytes}}_{\vert K \vert \text{ bytes}} + \underbrace{\text{Value Bytes}}_{\vert V \vert \text{ bytes}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiners' Tip:** If a question asks "What is the binary layout of a SequenceFile record?", state the **length-prefix + key + value** structure and mention that Hadoop also writes a **sync marker** (16 bytes) every ~1 MB to allow resynchronization after corruption.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hadoop File I/O End-to-End Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client Application] --> B[Job Submission to ResourceManager]
    B --> C[NameNode: Locate File Blocks]
    C --> D[InputFormat getSplits]
    D --> E[InputSplit 1 - 128MB]
    D --> F[InputSplit 2 - 128MB]
    D --> G[InputSplit N - partial]
    E --> H[DataNode 1 - Block Read]
    F --> I[DataNode 2 - Block Read]
    G --> J[DataNode 3 - Block Read]
    H --> K[RecordReader LineRecordReader]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Mapper 1 map K V]
    K --> M[Mapper 2 map K V]
    K --> N[Mapper N map K V]
    L --> O[Partitioner and Shuffle and Sort]
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Reducer 1 reduce K list V]
    O --> Q[Reducer 2 reduce K list V]
    P --> R[RecordWriter LineRecordWriter]
    Q --> R
    R --> S[OutputCommitter commit]
    S --> T[HDFS Output Directory part files]
    T --> U[Replication Factor 3 to DataNodes]
```

> **Figure 4.1:** The complete Hadoop MapReduce file I/O architecture, from block location discovery to final replicated output.

---

## 4.2 Spark File Reading and Writing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HDFS Source File] --> B[SparkContext textFile]
    B --> C[RDD of Lines Lazy]
    C --> D[Transformation map filter]
    D --> E[RDD of Processed Data Lazy]
    E --> F[Action saveAsTextFile]
    F --> G[Spark Driver splits into N partitions]
    G --> H1[Task 1 writes part-00000]
    G --> H2[Task 2 writes part-00001]
    G --> H3[Task N writes part-0000N]
    H1 --> I[HDFS Output Directory]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[SUCCESS marker file]
    I --> K[Replication to DataNodes]
```

> **Figure 4.2:** Spark's lazy RDD pipeline showing how `textFile` and `saveAsTextFile` interact with HDFS, including the lazy/ eager boundary.

---

## 4.3 Compression Codec Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Need to compress file output] --> B{Is splitability required}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is maximum compression required}
    C -- Yes --> D[Use Bzip2 Codec]
    C -- No --> E[Use LZO Codec with index]
    B -- No --> F{Is speed more important}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use Snappy Codec]
    F -- No --> H{Is compression ratio critical}
    H -- Yes --> I[Use Gzip Codec]
    H -- No --> J[Use Zstd Codec]
```

> **Figure 4.3:** Decision tree for choosing the appropriate compression codec based on the **splittability vs. speed vs. ratio** trade-off.

---

## 4.4 File Format Selection Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Big Data File Format] --> B{Is data primarily write heavy}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is human readability required}
    C -- Yes --> D[Use Text or CSV]
    C -- No --> E{Need schema evolution}
    E -- Yes --> F[Use Avro]
    E -- No --> G[Use SequenceFile]
    B -- No --> H{Is data primarily read heavy analytics}
    H -- Yes --> I{Is Hive involved}
    I -- Yes --> J[Use ORC]
    I -- No --> K[Use Parquet]
    H -- No --> L[Use JSON]
```

> **Figure 4.4:** Block-level decision diagram for selecting the most appropriate file format based on workload characteristics.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define `InputSplit` and `RecordReader` in Hadoop. Why is `RecordReader` necessary even after the file is split?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 4 | 3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

An **InputSplit** is a logical representation of a chunk of data (typically one HDFS block, **128 MB**) that is assigned to a single Mapper for processing. It contains the file path, starting offset, and length, but **not the actual data**.

A **RecordReader** is the component responsible for **converting the byte-oriented view of an InputSplit into individual key-value records** that the Mapper can process. For `TextInputFormat`, the `LineRecordReader` reads byte-by-byte until it encounters a newline character, then emits a `(LongWritable, Text)` pair.

**RecordReader is necessary because:**
1. The InputSplit is just a *range of bytes*; the framework needs to know **where one record ends and the next begins**.
2. Different file formats (text, binary, sequence) have different record delimiters, requiring format-specific parsing logic.
3. It allows custom record boundaries (e.g., multi-line JSON, XML records) via user-implemented classes.

---

### **Question 2: What is the difference between `saveAsTextFile` and `saveAsSequenceFile` in Spark? When would you prefer one over the other?**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 4 | 3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `saveAsTextFile` | `saveAsSequenceFile` |
|---|---|---|
| **Format** | Plain UTF-8 text | Binary key-value pairs |
| **Data Type** | `RDD[String]` (or convertible to string) | `RDD[(Writable, Writable)]` |
| **Size on Disk** | Larger | **Smaller (binary, compact)** |
| **Splittable** | Yes | Yes |
| **Schema Awareness** | No | No (binary) |
| **Use Case** | Logs, human-readable debug output | Intermediate MapReduce output, compact storage |

**Preference Rule:** Use `saveAsTextFile` for **debugging and human-readable outputs**; use `saveAsSequenceFile` for **production pipelines** where binary compactness and Hadoop ecosystem compatibility are priorities.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q.A (a)** Explain the complete Hadoop MapReduce file reading pipeline with a neat diagram. Describe the roles of `InputFormat`, `InputSplit`, and `RecordReader`. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Job Initialization:**
The client submits a MapReduce job to the **ResourceManager (YARN)**. The job specifies the input path (HDFS directory) and an `InputFormat` class (commonly `TextInputFormat`).

**2. InputFormat.getSplits():**
The `InputFormat` is invoked by the **ApplicationMaster**. It contacts the **NameNode** to retrieve the **block locations** of the input file. Each HDFS block (default **128 MB**) is logically represented as an `InputSplit`. The metadata (path, offset, length, hosts) is sent to the YARN scheduler.

**3. Mapper Assignment with Data Locality:**
The YARN scheduler attempts to assign each Mapper to a **DataNode holding the corresponding block** (node-local). If impossible, it falls back to **rack-local** or **cross-rack**.

**4. RecordReader Initialization:**
Inside the Mapper's JVM, a `RecordReader` (e.g., `LineRecordReader` for text) is instantiated for the assigned InputSplit. It opens an input stream to the block via HDFS's DFSInputStream.

**5. Record-by-Record Emission:**
The Mapper repeatedly calls `RecordReader.nextKeyValue()`. Each call:
- Reads bytes until a delimiter (newline) is found.
- Sets the **key** to the byte offset of the line (`LongWritable`).
- Sets the **value** to the line content (`Text`).

**6. Mapper Logic:**
The `Mapper.map(key, value, context)` method processes each record and emits intermediate `(K, V)` pairs to the context.

**Roles Summary:**

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| `InputFormat` | Validates input, computes splits, supplies `RecordReader` factory |
| `InputSplit` | Logical chunk metadata (offset, length, hosts) |
| `RecordReader` | Converts byte stream into `(K, V)` records for the Mapper |

**Diagram:** (Refer to **Figure 4.1** in Section 4 of these notes.)

**Q.A (b)** A 1.2 TB log file is stored on HDFS with the default block size. Calculate the number of mappers, total physical storage with default replication, and the number of output part-files if processed with Spark using 8 partitions. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- File Size: $F = 1.2 \text{ TB} = 1.2 \times 1024 \times 1024 \text{ MB} = 1258291.2 \text{ MB}$
- Block Size: $B = 128 \text{ MB}$
- Replication Factor: $RF = 3$
- Spark Partitions: $P = 8$

**Step 1 — Number of Mappers (Hadoop):**

$$
M = \left\lceil \frac{1258291.2}{128} \right\rceil
$$

$$
M = \left\lceil 9830.4 \right\rceil = 9831 \text{ Mappers}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Physical Storage:**

$$
S_{total} = 1.2 \text{ TB} \times 3 = 3.6 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 3 — Spark Part-Files:**

If the same data is processed by Spark with 8 partitions and written via `saveAsTextFile`, exactly **8 part-files** (`part-00000` to `part-00007`) will be created in the output directory.

**Note on Data Skew:** The Hadoop run creates 9831 mappers (one per block), but the Spark run with 8 partitions will **repartition** the data into 8 chunks. If the source file is line-balanced, this is efficient; if not, skew may occur.

| Output | Value |
|---|---|
| Mappers (Hadoop) | **9831** |
| Physical Storage | **3.6 TB** |
| Spark Part-Files | **8** |

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q.B (a)** Compare Row-based and Columnar file formats in Big Data. Discuss Parquet and ORC with respect to read performance, compression, and use cases. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Row-Based Formats (Text, CSV, JSON, Avro, SequenceFile):**
- Data is stored **row by row** in a contiguous sequence.
- **Pros:** Excellent for **write-heavy** workloads; entire records are written in a single I/O.
- **Cons:** For analytics, the entire row must be read even if only one column is queried — leading to **I/O wastage**.

**Columnar Formats (Parquet, ORC):**
- Data is stored **column by column**; all values of column 1 are written contiguously, then column 2, etc.
- **Pros:** **Predicate pushdown** allows queries to read only the relevant columns; **run-length encoding** delivers high compression; **vectorized reads** accelerate analytics.
- **Cons:** Slower writes due to column-batching overhead.

**Detailed Comparison:**

| Feature | Parquet | ORC |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Created by Twitter & Cloudera | Created by Hortonworks (Hive) |
| **Best Friend** | Spark, Impala, Drill | Hive, Spark |
| **Compression** | Snappy, Gzip, Zstd | Zlib, Snappy |
| **Predicate Pushdown** | Yes (min/max statistics per row group) | Yes (lightweight indexes) |
| **Schema Evolution** | Yes (backward compatible) | Yes |
| **Read Speed (analytics)** | **Excellent** | **Excellent** |
| **Nested Data Support** | **Yes (strong)** | Limited |

**Use Case Recommendation:**
- Use **Parquet** for **Spark-based analytics, data warehousing, and nested JSON data**.
- Use **ORC** for **Hive-centric ecosystems** and **acid transactions** (ORC v2 supports ACID).

**Q.B (b)** Explain compression codecs in Hadoop. A 2 GB text file is compressed with Gzip to 500 MB. With a default block size of 128 MB, compute: (i) Compression ratio, (ii) Mappers needed for the compressed file, (iii) Why Gzip may degrade MapReduce performance. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Compression Codecs in Hadoop:**
Hadoop supports several compression codecs, each balancing **compression ratio**, **speed**, and **splittability**. The four classical codecs are:
- **Gzip** — high ratio, slow, non-splittable
- **Snappy** — medium ratio, very fast, non-splittable
- **LZO** — medium ratio, fast, splittable (with index)
- **Bzip2** — very high ratio, slow, splittable

**Given:**
- Original Size: $D = 2 \text{ GB} = 2048 \text{ MB}$
- Compressed Size: $D_c = 500 \text{ MB}$
- Block Size: $B = 128 \text{ MB}$

**Step 1 — Compression Ratio:**

$$
C_r = \frac{2048}{500} = 4.096
$$

The compression ratio is approximately **4.1 : 1**.

**Step 2 — Mappers for Compressed File:**

Since **Gzip is non-splittable**, the entire 500 MB compressed file is treated as a single InputSplit:

$$
M_{gzip} = 1 \text{ Mapper}
$$

For comparison, the uncompressed file would have required:

$$
M_{uncomp} = \left\lceil \frac{2048}{128} \right\rceil = 16 \text{ Mappers}
$$

**Step 3 — Why Gzip Degrades Performance:**

1. **Loss of Parallelism:** Going from 16 parallel Mappers to 1 sequential Mapper means **16× less parallelism** for the I/O phase.
2. **Single Mapper Bottleneck:** All processing is concentrated in one JVM, risking **out-of-memory errors** for large files.
3. **Decompression CPU Cost:** Although Gzip saves disk space, **decompressing** 500 MB on a single node consumes CPU and slows the Mapper.
4. **No Data Locality Benefit:** The single block must be read in full from one DataNode, eliminating the parallelism of distributed reads.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Forgetting to convert GB to MB. Always write: $2 \text{ GB} = 2048 \text{ MB}$.
> 2. **Splittability Oversight:** Students often compute mappers as $\lceil 500 / 128 \rceil = 4$ for Gzip. This is **wrong** because Gzip is non-splittable. The correct answer is **1 Mapper**.
> 3. **Forgetting the `_SUCCESS` file:** Spark/Hadoop output directories always contain a `_SUCCESS` marker — examiners often ask about it.
> 4. **Confusing `wholeTextFiles` with `textFile`:** `wholeTextFiles` returns `(filename, content)` pairs, while `textFile` returns lines.
> 5. **SequenceFile is binary, not text:** A common mistake is to assume SequenceFiles are human-readable. They are **binary key-value** formats.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Reading and Writing Files**

- ✅ **HDFS Block Size** = **128 MB** (default); **Replication Factor** = **3** (default).
- ✅ **Number of Mappers** = $\lceil F / B \rceil$; remember to convert units to MB before division.
- ✅ **InputFormat** → computes splits, supplies `RecordReader` factory.
- ✅ **RecordReader** → converts byte stream into `(K, V)` records for Mapper.
- ✅ **Hadoop Reading:** Client → NameNode → DataNode → InputSplit → RecordReader → Mapper.
- ✅ **Hadoop Writing:** Reducer → RecordWriter → OutputCommitter → HDFS (replicated 3×).
- ✅ **Spark `textFile`** is **lazy**; `saveAsTextFile` is an **action** that creates a **directory of part-files** (one per partition).
- ✅ **Spark output directory** always contains a `_SUCCESS` marker file.
- ✅ **`wholeTextFiles`** returns `(filename, content)` pairs; **`textFile`** returns individual lines.
- ✅ **Gzip & Snappy** are **non-splittable**; **Bzip2 & LZO** are **splittable** (LZO needs an index).
- ✅ **SequenceFile** = binary key-value format, length-prefixed records, **sync markers** every ~1 MB.
- ✅ **Row-based formats** (Text, Avro) = write-heavy; **Columnar formats** (Parquet, ORC) = read-heavy analytics.
- ✅ **Parquet** uses **predicate pushdown** and **columnar compression** for fast analytics.
- ✅ **Compression saves storage** but may **reduce parallelism** if the codec is non-splittable.
- ✅ **Storage overhead** = Logical size × Replication Factor.
- ✅ **RecordReader is necessary** even after splitting because InputSplit only defines byte ranges, not record boundaries.
- ✅ **OutputCommitter** is responsible for promoting temporary outputs and writing `_SUCCESS`.

> 🎯 **Final Exam Tip:** When a question asks "How many Mappers?", always show three things: (1) the unit conversion, (2) the formula $\lceil F / B \rceil$, and (3) the ceiling logic explanation. This guarantees full marks.
